questions.question: A multinational company has a subsidiary in India that provides software development services to its foreign parent under a cost-plus arrangement. The subsidiary receives reimbursement for salaries, infrastructure costs, and a 10% markup. The foreign parent subsequently sublicenses the resulting software to Indian customers. Analyze: (1) whether the subsidiary's supply qualifies as 'export of services' under Section 2(6) of the IGST Act given the place of supply rules under Section 13(2), (2) the transfer pricing implications under the Income Tax Act on the cost-plus markup, (3) whether the Indian customers' license payments are subject to GST under the 'online information and database access or retrieval services' (OIDAR) classification, and (4) the customs duty / BCD applicability if the software is delivered on physical media versus electronically.

An Indian EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme duty-free with an export obligation of 6x the duty saved over 6 years. After 4 years, the unit converts to a domestic tariff area (DTA) unit. At the time of conversion: (1) compute the proportional duty demand and interest liability under Customs Act Section 28 read with EPCG guidelines, accounting for depreciation on capital goods under Customs Valuation Rules; (2) determine whether the outstanding export obligation can be offset against deemed exports under Para 7.02 of the FTP 2023; (3) assess FEMA implications if the original import was partly financed by an ECB (External Commercial Borrowing) that remains outstanding; and (4) evaluate whether converting to a 100% domestic unit triggers any reversal of ITC under GST Section 18(6) on the capital goods whose useful life has not expired.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) to a SEZ unit without payment of IGST. The SEZ unit partially re-exports these goods to a foreign buyer and partially clears them to the DTA after paying applicable duties. The original manufacturer later receives a SCN alleging ineligibility of zero-rated supply benefits on the DTA-cleared portion, contending the supply never constituted an 'export' under Section 16(1)(b) of the IGST Act. Simultaneously, the GST officer invokes Rule 96A(3) demanding IGST with interest for the portion cleared to DTA. Analyze the validity of the SCN, the correct treatment of ITC reversal under Rule 42/43, and whether the manufacturer can invoke the doctrine of unjust enrichment as a defence given that the DTA buyer had already paid IGST on its customs clearance.

An Indian importer purchases specialty chemicals from its wholly-owned foreign parent. The declared transaction value is accepted by customs for three years. In year four, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) under Income Tax Act makes an upward adjustment to the ALP, increasing the arm's length price of the same imports by 18%. The Customs department, upon receipt of the TP order, issues a demand under Section 28 of the Customs Act re-assessing the value of the prior three years' imports under Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation Rules, adding a penalty under Section 114AA for alleged misdeclaration. The importer argues: (a) TP adjustment is not an admission of undervaluation; (b) limitation period under Section 28 has expired for the first two years; (c) Rule 10(1)(e) additions for royalties were already included. Evaluate the legal sustainability of the customs demand, the interplay between TP adjustments and customs valuation law post the SC judgment in Eicher Motors, and the correct computation of interest if any partial demand survives.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (wholly-owned subsidiary) at a price below cost. The recipient uses these goods as inputs for zero-rated exports. The supplier has claimed ITC on all inputs. Determine: (a) the applicable valuation method under Rule 28 of the CGST Rules and whether the open market value or cost-plus method applies given the recipient's zero-rated status; (b) the GST liability on the differential value if any; (c) whether the ITC chain is disrupted at the subsidiary level; and (d) the anti-profiteering implications if the subsidiary subsequently supplies to an unrelated third party at market price.

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a foreign parent company. The transaction value is USD 500,000. Post-importation, the parent charges a 'technology access fee' of USD 50,000 per year for the right to operate the machinery using proprietary software embedded in it. Customs proposes to load this fee onto the transaction value under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation Rules. The importer argues the fee is post-importation and relates to a service, not the goods. Analyze: (a) whether the royalty/licence fee is includible under the Customs Valuation Rules and the conditions precedent under Rule 10(1)(c); (b) the 'condition of sale' test as interpreted by the CESTAT and Supreme Court; (c) the correct treatment if the software is separately licensed and the machinery can physically operate without the annual fee; and (d) the interplay with GST on the import of service (reverse charge) and whether double-taxation can be avoided.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) but the foreign buyer refuses delivery and the goods are re-imported within 6 months. The exporter had already claimed IGST refund on zero-rated supply. Analyze: (a) whether re-importation triggers reversal of the IGST refund already granted, (b) the applicable customs duty treatment under re-import provisions vs. deemed export, (c) whether the original LUT bond liability gets revived, and (d) how input tax credit originally availed on inputs consumed in manufacture should be treated post re-import, with specific reference to Rule 96, Rule 16(2), and Section 74 of CGST Act.

A holding company in India provides shared IT infrastructure, treasury management, and brand licensing services to its wholly-owned subsidiary. The subsidiary is registered under GST; the holding company is not (turnover below threshold). AO proposes to treat inter-company charges as undervalued under Rule 28 of CGST Valuation Rules and re-determines the value using comparable uncontrolled price method, resulting in additional GST demand on the subsidiary as recipient under RCM (Section 9(3)/9(4)). Critically examine: (a) legal validity of applying Rule 28 when the supplier is unregistered, (b) whether RCM applies at all when supplier is below registration threshold, (c) interplay with Income Tax transfer pricing adjustments already accepted by ITAT for the same transactions, and (d) availability of ITC to the subsidiary on any GST paid under RCM on such re-determined value.

title: Seeks to extend the due date for filing of FORM GSTR - 1 for taxpayers having aggregate turnover above Rs 1.5 crores.

(i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs

Notification No. 44/2018 – Central Tax

New Delhi, the 10th September, 2018

G.S.R……(E). -  In exercise of the powers conferred by the second proviso to sub-section (1) 
of section 37 read with section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 
2017) (hereafter in this notification referred to as the said Act), and in supercession of –

(i) Notification No. 18/2017 – Central Tax dated 8th August, 2017 published in the

Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R     
994 (E), dated the 8th August, 2017;

(ii) Notification No. 58/2017 – Central Tax dated 15th November, 2017 published in the

Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R     
1414 (E), dated the 15th November, 2017;

(iii)Notification No. 18/2018 – Central Tax dated 28th March, 2018 published in the

Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R     
296 (E), dated the 28th March, 2018; and

(iv) Notification No. 32/2018 – Central Tax dated 10th August, 2018 published in the

Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R     
759 (E), dated the 10th August, 2018,

except as respects things done or omitted to be done before such supercession, the 
Commissioner, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby extends the time limit for 
furnishing the details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1 of the Central Goods and 
Services Tax Rules, 2017, by such class of registered persons having aggregate turnover of 
more than 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding financial year or the current financial year, for the 
months from July, 2017 to September, 2018 till the 31st day of October, 2018 and for the 
months from October, 2018 to March, 2019 till the eleventh day of the succeeding month:

Provided that the time limit for furnishing the details of outward supplies in FORM

GSTR-1 for the months from July, 2017 to November, 2018 for the taxpayers who have 
obtained Goods and Services Tax Identification Number (GSTIN) in terms of notification 
No. 31/2018 – Central Tax dated 6th August, 2018 published in the Gazette of India, 
Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R 742 (E), dated the 6th 
August, 2018, shall be extended till the 31st day of December, 2018. 
2.  
The time limit for furnishing the details or return, as the case may be, under sub-

section (2) of section 38 and sub-section (1) of section 39 of the said Act, for the months of 
July, 2017 to March, 2019 shall be subsequently notified in the Official Gazette.

[F. No. 349/58/2017-GST (Pt.)]

(Gunjan Kumar Verma)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under a long-term contract where the price is subject to revision based on commodity indices. The revised price is determined 6 months after the original supply. Under Section 15 of the CGST Act read with Rule 88C, when does the time of supply crystallise for the differential amount — at the date of original invoice, the date of price revision agreement, or the date of revised invoice — and what are the ITC reversal obligations on the recipient if the revision crosses a financial year boundary?

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign entity. The transaction value is provisionally accepted by customs pending a Transfer Pricing order from the Income Tax Authority. The final TP order arrives 18 months after clearance and results in an upward revision of the ALP. Under Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007, specifically Rules 3(3)(a) and 10(1)(e), is the importer liable to pay differential customs duty on the revised value, and does the limitation period under Section 28 of the Customs Act run from the date of original assessment or from the date the TP order was communicated to customs?

questions.question: A registered taxpayer under GST received an advance payment in March 2024 for a supply of services to be rendered in June 2024. The taxpayer issued a receipt voucher but did not file GSTR-1 for March 2024 within the due date. Subsequently, the taxpayer's registration was cancelled suo motu by the proper officer in April 2024 under Section 29(2)(c) for non-filing of returns. The taxpayer applied for revocation under Section 30 within 30 days but the officer rejected it citing pendency of dues. The taxpayer now seeks to apply for fresh registration. Identify the complete legal path available, the tax treatment of the advance received, and whether Input Tax Credit (ITC) reversal obligations survive cancellation where no tax invoice was ever issued.

An importer declared a consignment of mixed goods under a single Bill of Entry, claiming a concessional rate under a Free Trade Agreement (FTA) using a Certificate of Origin (CoO) issued by the exporting country. During assessment, the proper officer found that 30% of the goods by value did not satisfy the Regional Value Content (RVC) threshold under the FTA rules of origin, while the remaining 70% did. The officer proposes to deny the FTA benefit for the entire consignment on grounds that the CoO is a single document covering all goods. Analyze whether the partial denial of FTA benefit is permissible under the Customs Act 1962 and relevant FTA rules, the correct procedure for re-assessment, and the importer's remedies including the applicable limitation period for any refund claim if excess duty is deposited under protest.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat) — operating under the same PAN. Unit A transfers semi-finished goods to Unit B for further processing under a job-work arrangement. Unit B then exports the finished goods to a buyer in Germany under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). The ITC claimed by Unit A on inputs used for the transferred goods has not been reversed. Customs raises an objection at Unit B's port citing non-payment of IGST on the inter-unit stock transfer. GST department simultaneously initiates proceedings against Unit A for alleged wrongful ITC retention. Identify all applicable provisions under CGST Act 2017, the relevant circular(s), and the sequence of legal remedies available to the company to defend both proceedings simultaneously while protecting its working capital.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US-based MNC provides IT-enabled services exclusively to its parent (USCo). The ALP for these services was benchmarked using TNMM with an operating margin of 18% in the original TP study. During assessment, the TPO rejects the comparables set, selects new comparables with a median margin of 26%, and proposes an upward adjustment of ₹42 crore. Simultaneously, the AO disallows ₹8 crore of royalty payments made by IndCo to USCo as 'excessive' under Section 40A(2)(b), treating it as a separate transaction from the TP adjustment. IndCo is also subject to secondary adjustment provisions under Section 92CE. Determine the correct order of adjustments, whether the secondary adjustment applies to the TPO's adjustment or the AO's Section 40A(2)(b) disallowance or both, and the precise MAP/APA strategy available to prevent double taxation given the US-India DTAA, citing the relevant OECD guidelines and CBDT circulars.

questions.question: A manufacturing company in Maharashtra has a registered unit in an SEZ and another in the DTA. The SEZ unit procures raw materials from the DTA unit and pays IGST. The DTA unit later receives a credit note from its supplier due to post-supply price reduction. Simultaneously, the SEZ unit exports finished goods to a foreign buyer under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) without payment of IGST. Trace the complete ITC adjustment chain across both units, identify which GST returns are affected (GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, GSTR-6, GSTR-ISD if applicable), and determine whether the reversal obligation under Rule 42/43 applies to either unit, citing relevant CGST Act sections and IGST Act provisions.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (wholly owned by a US parent) provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using TNMM. During AY 2023-24, the AO proposes a transfer pricing adjustment arguing the tested party's operating margin falls below the arithmetic mean of comparables selected by the TPO, who replaced two comparables from the assessee's set with functionally dissimilar high-margin companies. The assessee invokes the +/- 3% range under the second proviso to Section 92C(2). Simultaneously, the AO also invokes Section 9(1)(vii) to tax an allocation of shared services costs recharged by the parent as FTS. Analyze the hierarchy of TP adjustments, the validity of the TPO's comparable selection under Rule 10B, the applicability of the 3% safe harbor, and whether the shared services recharge can simultaneously be characterized as FTS under Section 9(1)(vii) given the 'make available' test under the India-US DTAA Article 12.

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a Bill of Entry dated March 28, 2026. The IGST paid at customs is ₹18,40,000. The goods are partially used in the manufacture of exempt supplies (40%) and taxable supplies (60%) during April 2026. The company also has a pending SCN for ₹6,20,000 relating to inadmissible ITC claimed in FY 2023-24 which is under adjudication. In GSTR-3B for April 2026, what is the exact ITC amount admissible under Rule 42 read with Section 17(2) of CGST Act, and does the pendency of the SCN under Section 73 create any restriction on utilisation of the current month's ITC under Section 86A? Cite the precise legal provision and any relevant circular.

An Indian exporter invoices goods at USD 1,00,000 FOB to a related party in Singapore. The AO under Customs proposes to enhance the declared value to USD 1,35,000 based on contemporaneous export data under Rule 8 of Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Export Goods) Rules 2007, invoking the relationship between parties under Rule 3(3)(ii). Simultaneously, the RBI's AD Bank flags a potential FEMA violation for under-invoicing under Section 4 read with FEMA Notification No. 23(R). (a) What is the correct legal sequence of proceedings — can both Customs and ED proceed simultaneously? (b) Does a final Customs order on value operate as res judicata for the FEMA adjudication? (c) What precedent, if any, governs the evidentiary standard for 'influence on price' in related-party export transactions under Indian law?

questions.question: A manufacturing company operating under the composition scheme has been exporting goods directly to a foreign buyer under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). Midway through the financial year, they cross the aggregate turnover threshold and are compulsorily migrated to the regular GST regime. What are the implications for: (a) the zero-rated supply treatment on exports already dispatched under the LUT during the composition period, (b) the reversal of ITC availed on inputs used in those exports, (c) filing obligations under GSTR-10, and (d) any penal exposure under Section 122 for the period of non-compliance between crossing the threshold and the date of suo motu migration?

A private limited company enters into a slump sale of an undertaking to its wholly-owned subsidiary for a lump sum consideration of ₹15 crore. The net book value of the undertaking is ₹8 crore, but an independent valuation report places the fair market value at ₹22 crore. The undertaking has held depreciable assets (WDV ₹3 crore) and goodwill (self-generated, WDV ₹0 post-2021 amendment). Analyze: (a) the applicability of Section 50B versus Section 45 and whether Rule 11UAE FMV overrides the agreed consideration, (b) the tax treatment of self-generated goodwill under the current depreciation disallowance regime, (c) whether the subsidiary can claim Section 47(iv) exemption given the slump sale structure, and (d) transfer pricing exposure under Section 92 if the AO treats the transaction as not at arm's length.

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline ingests 108,000 chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single GPU. During peak query load, you observe P95 latency of 4.2 seconds per query, with profiling showing 60% of time in Qdrant vector search and 40% in LLM generation. The embedding model occupies GPU 5 (Vulkan), and the LLM (Qwen3-14B) runs on GPU 2 (ROCm). You have 2 additional idle GPUs. Propose a concrete multi-phase optimization strategy that addresses both the retrieval bottleneck and generation latency, specifying: (a) which Qdrant index parameters to tune and why, (b) how to restructure the async FastAPI request pipeline to overlap retrieval and prompt construction, and (c) whether and how to shard or replicate across the idle GPUs given the Vulkan/ROCm constraint.

A practitioner is building a retrieval-augmented system over 471 image-only PDFs of CBIC circulars. The current plan uses Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF via llama-server for OCR with RapidOCR CPU fallback. During evaluation, they discover that: (i) 23% of pages contain dense tabular data with merged cells that Qwen2.5-VL hallucinates, (ii) section numbering in scanned documents is inconsistent, causing chunk boundary errors that split legal provisions mid-sentence, and (iii) query recall for numeric GST rate lookups is 41% vs 78% for prose questions. Without changing the embedding model or vector database, design a preprocessing and chunking strategy that addresses all three failure modes, explaining the tradeoffs of each design decision in the context of a 4-core CPU / multi-GPU Vulkan system under memory pressure.

title: The notification exempts specified goods imported for the manufacture and the development of electronics hardware etc. under 100% EOU Scheme.
text:
alled or used in the unit and that the import of
such goods has been authorised by the Committee;

(7) Without prejudice to any other provisions contained in this notification, the Assistant Commissioner of Customs or Deputy
Commissioner of Customs may, subject to such conditions and limitations as he may deem fit to impose under the
circumstances of the case for the proper safeguard of the revenue interest and also subject to such permission of the
designated officer or the Committee where it is exclusively required under Export and Import Policy, allow the said unit to clear
any of the said goods for being taken to any other place in India in accordance with the Export and Import Policy, -

(a) such clearance of capital goods, material handling equipments, office equipments and captive power plants may be
allowed on payment of an amount equal to the customs duty leviable on such goods on the depreciated value thereof and at the
rate in force on the date of payment of such duty;

(b) such clearance of goods (including containers, suitable for repeated use) other than those specified in clause (a) may be
allowed on payment of customs duty on the value at the time of import and at rates in force on the date of payment of such
customs duty;

(c) such clearance of used packing materials such as cardboard boxes, polythelene bags of a kind unsuitable for repeated use
may be allowed without payment of any customs duty;

Provided that the importer shall not be eligible to avail of the exemption applicable to goods falling under heading number
98.01 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), or the exemption available to the imported goods
under any Export Promotion scheme other than the Export Promotion Capital Goods scheme permitting import of capital
goods at the rate of duty of 5% ad valorem in terms of notifications in force at the time of debonding.

Explanation. - The depreciation in respect of goods covered by clause (a) above shall be allowed for the period from the date
of commencement of commercial production of the unit or where such goods have been imported after such commencement

questions.question: A manufacturing company supplies goods to its 100% subsidiary under a cost-plus-5% pricing arrangement. The subsidiary is registered in a different state. During an audit, the GST officer contends that the transaction must be valued at 'open market value' under Rule 28 of the CGST Rules, 2017, since the parties are 'related persons' under Section 15(5) read with Explanation to Section 15. The company argues that since the recipient (subsidiary) is eligible for full ITC, the invoice value should be the permissible value. The officer further alleges that the company's failure to maintain a Transfer Pricing study (as prepared for Income Tax purposes) constitutes suppression of material facts, justifying invocation of the extended limitation period of 5 years under Section 74. Analyze: (a) whether the related-party valuation rule under Rule 28 mandatorily overrides the transaction value when the recipient has full ITC entitlement; (b) whether a TP study prepared under the Income Tax Act has any legal standing or evidentiary value in GST valuation disputes; and (c) whether invocation of Section 74 on the sole ground of absence of a TP study constitutes a valid allegation of 'fraud or willful misstatement or suppression of facts.'

An Indian importer procures specialized machinery from its foreign parent company. The transaction value declared is USD 2,00,000. The customs officer rejects this value under Rule 12 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, citing that the parties are related and the declared price appears to be influenced by the relationship, invoking Rule 3(3). The officer sequentially applies Rule 4 (transaction value of identical goods), Rule 5 (similar goods), and Rule 6 (deductive value), but fails to find comparable data at each stage. The officer then applies Rule 8 (residual method) by referencing a foreign manufacturer's published list price and arrives at a value of USD 3,50,000, levying differential duty plus interest under Section 28AA of the Customs Act. The importer challenges the assessment contending: (i) the officer never gave an opportunity to present evidence under Rule 3(3)(b) that the relationship did not influence the price; (ii) the sequential application of Rules 4–6 was not adequately documented; and (iii) the 'list price' used under Rule 8 is not a 'computed value' and violates the hierarchy mandated by the Rules. Analyze each ground, addressing the procedural safeguards under Rule 12, the mandatory sequential application doctrine, the admissibility of published list prices under Rule 8 residual methodology, and the applicability of interest under Section 28AA when the original assessment itself is contested.

title: Warehousing - Grant of extension of warehousing period by the Chief Commissioners under section 61 of the Customs Act, 1962-regarding
text:
7. Chief Commissioner of Customs are, therefore, requested to decide the requests for grant of extension of warehousing
period after taking into consideration all aspects referred to above. Issues other than those related to extension of warehousing
period, as contained in the Board's instructions referred in para 2 above, shall continue to be governed by the said instructions.
Board's Circular No. 12/98-Cus., dated 06.03.1998, may be treated as subsumed in the present instructions. The contents of
this Circular may also be brought to the notice of the field formations and the trade under your jurisdiction suitably.

8. It may be clarified here that though the powers under statute now vest with Chief Commissioner of Customs, the above
guidelines are warranted in order to have uniform approach throughout the country.

questions.question: A manufacturer imports capital goods under EPCG authorization and avails exemption from Basic Customs Duty and IGST. Subsequently, they supply the finished goods to an SEZ unit under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) without payment of IGST, claiming zero-rated supply. However, the export obligation under EPCG is calculated on the CIF value of imports. The jurisdictional authority contends that supplies to SEZ do not count toward fulfillment of EPCG export obligation since no foreign exchange was realized. Simultaneously, the Input Tax Credit (ITC) on inputs used in such zero-rated supplies is being disputed on the grounds that IGST was exempted at import stage, invoking Section 17(2) read with Rule 42. Analyze the legal tenability of both contentions, identifying the relevant EPCG scheme provisions, GST law sections, and the reconciliation between the Foreign Trade Policy and GST Act in such hybrid supply chains.

An Indian subsidiary of a multinational group provides software development services to its foreign parent under a cost-plus arrangement. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) proposes an upward adjustment arguing the tested party margin is below the arithmetic mean of comparables selected under TNMM. The assessee challenges three comparables as functionally incomparable (one provides KPO services, one is a captive unit with single customer, one has abnormal profits due to a one-time asset sale) and invokes the benefit of +/- 3% range under the second proviso to Section 92C(2). Simultaneously, the assessee has a secondary adjustment demand under Section 92CE and is disputing whether interest imputation under Rule 10CB applies given repatriation of excess money was completed within the prescribed timeline. Examine the cumulative interaction of Section 92C, 92CE, Rule 10CB, and the OECD BEPS Action 8–10 guidance as adopted under Indian TP regulations, and determine the defensible legal position on both the comparable rejection and secondary adjustment.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) and later discovers that the foreign buyer remitted payment in INR through an Authorized Dealer under the RBI's rupee trade settlement mechanism. The jurisdictional officer contends this does not qualify as 'receipt of payment in convertible foreign exchange' under Rule 96A of CGST Rules, 2017, thereby invalidating the LUT and demanding IGST with interest. Analyze the legal validity of this demand, citing relevant circulars, the RBI Master Direction on Trade Settlements, and any judicial precedents that have ruled on whether rupee-denominated export receipts satisfy the zero-rated supply conditions under Section 16(1)(b) of the IGST Act, 2017.

An Indian importer purchases specialized machinery from its wholly-owned subsidiary in Germany. The customs valuation is determined under Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007 using the transaction value. The Transfer Pricing Officer simultaneously proposes an upward adjustment under Section 92CA of the Income Tax Act, 1961, concluding the declared price is below arm's length. The importer argues the customs-accepted transaction value should bind the TPO under the consistency principle; the department argues the two statutes operate independently. Examine this conflict, the precedential weight of Supreme Court and CESTAT rulings on cross-statute valuation consistency, and the practical exposure for the importer if both adjustments are sustained concurrently.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. The overseas buyer subsequently rejects 30% of the shipment due to quality defects and returns the goods to India. The manufacturer re-imports the rejected goods, pays applicable customs duties, and then re-exports after rectification within 6 months. Simultaneously, the original export invoice had already been reported in GSTR-1 and the shipping bill was linked for IGST refund processing (even though no IGST was paid). What are the exact GST compliance obligations at each stage — re-import, rectification, re-export — and how must the manufacturer amend GSTR-1, handle the LUT continuity, and deal with the already-filed shipping bill linkage to avoid refund complications or bond forfeiture?

An Indian subsidiary of a multinational provides both (a) software development services to its foreign parent under a cost-plus arrangement, and (b) receives management support services from a Singapore group entity for which it pays a fee. The AO proposes to treat the management fee as non-arm's length under TNMM by benchmarking the subsidiary's overall operating margin, arguing the fee inflates costs and depresses the margin below the tested-party range. The taxpayer contends the management fee should be benchmarked independently using CUP, citing identifiable third-party agreements. Simultaneously, the AO invokes Section 40A(2)(b) to disallow 40% of the management fee as excessive. Analyze whether TNMM applied at the entity level legally subsumes the CUP analysis at the transaction level, whether 40A(2)(b) disallowance can coexist with a transfer pricing adjustment on the same transaction, and what the correct sequence of adjustments and appellate strategy should be under the Income Tax Act read with the Transfer Pricing Rules.

this Circular may also be brought to the notice of the field formations and the trade under your jurisdiction suitably.

8. It may be clarified here that though the powers under statute now vest with Chief Commissioner of Customs, the above
guidelines are warranted in order to have uniform approach throughout the country.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a dealer under a principal-to-principal arrangement. The dealer, acting as a del credere agent, provides a guarantee to the manufacturer for payment by sub-dealers. The manufacturer issues a credit note to the dealer when sub-dealers return goods. Determine: (a) whether the del credere agent's guarantee service attracts GST and under which SAC code, (b) how the credit note impacts the manufacturer's output tax liability and the dealer's ITC under Section 34 read with Rule 53 of CGST Rules, and (c) whether the guarantee constitutes a 'supply' under Schedule II Entry 5(e) or falls under the exemption for services by way of guarantee to a subsidiary by a holding company.

An Indian importer procures goods from a related foreign supplier. The transaction value is Rs. 50 lakhs CIF. The Customs officer suspects undervaluation and invokes Rule 4(2) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, rejecting the declared value. The importer offers computed value under Rule 8 but the officer insists on residual value under Rule 9. Simultaneously, DGFT has issued an advance authorization covering 80% of the imported inputs. Analyze: (a) the correct sequential application of valuation rules under Section 14 of Customs Act and whether the officer can skip to Rule 9 without exhausting Rules 5–8, (b) the impact of the advance authorization on assessable value and whether IGST exemption under Notification 18/2015-Customs applies to the entire consignment or only the authorized quantity, and (c) the importer's remedies including provisional assessment under Section 18 and the evidentiary standard required to rebut the officer's comparable price data under Rule 4(2)(b).

title: Warehousing - Grant of extension of warehousing period by the Chief Commissioners under section 61 of the Customs Act, 1962-regarding
text:
7. Chief Commissioner of Customs are, therefore, requested to decide the requests for grant of extension of warehousing
period after taking into consideration all aspects referred to above. Issues other than those related to extension of warehousing
period, as contained in the Board's instructions referred in para 2 above, shall continue to be governed by the said instructions.
Board's Circular No. 12/98-Cus., dated 06.03.1998, may be treated as subsumed in the present instructions. The contents of
this Circular may also be brought to the notice of the field formations and the trade under your jurisdiction suitably.

8. It may be clarified here that though the powers under statute now vest with Chief Commissioner of Customs, the above
guidelines are warranted in order to have uniform approach throughout the country.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two registered units — Unit A in Maharashtra and Unit B in Gujarat. Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit B as a stock transfer under a delivery challan. Unit B processes the goods and sells the finished product to a buyer in Karnataka. The buyer later returns 40% of the goods citing quality defects, but the return happens after the 180-day ITC reversal window has elapsed. Unit A had originally claimed full ITC on the inward supply used for the stock transfer. Analyse: (i) whether the stock transfer constitutes a taxable supply and on what valuation basis GST should be charged, (ii) the ITC reversal obligations at each unit upon the buyer's return, (iii) whether a credit note can be issued after the September deadline of the subsequent financial year, and (iv) how the place of supply is determined for the original sale and the return leg.

An Indian importer is purchasing specialised CNC machinery from a Swiss parent company at a declared transaction value of USD 850,000. The customs officer suspects undervaluation and proposes to reject the transaction value on the ground that the buyer and seller are related persons. The importer contends that the price is consistent with arm's length pricing supported by a transfer pricing study accepted by the Indian income tax authorities for the same financial year. Separately, the machinery attracts a safeguard duty that was notified mid-shipment — the goods were loaded before the notification date but arrived after it. Additionally, the importer is seeking an SVB (Special Valuation Branch) order. Analyse: (i) the legal standard for rejecting transaction value in related-party imports under the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 and whether the accepted TP study is binding on customs, (ii) the point of levy for the safeguard duty given the mid-shipment notification, (iii) the procedural requirements and interim assessment implications of SVB scrutiny, and (iv) the interaction between ADD, CVD, and safeguard duty stacking obligations on the same consignment.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat) — operating under the same PAN. Unit A transfers semi-finished goods to Unit B for further processing, which are then sold to an unregistered buyer in Rajasthan. The goods were never invoiced to any external party until the final sale. Unit A claims the transfer is a 'stock transfer' exempt from GST. The GST officer contends it constitutes a 'supply between distinct persons' under Section 25(4) read with Schedule I, Entry 2, and raises a demand on Unit A for the full transaction value. Analyze: (a) whether the officer's contention is legally sustainable, (b) how the valuation should be determined under Rule 28 of CGST Rules if supply is established, and (c) what ITC entitlement Unit B would have and how it would be affected if Unit A's invoice was not raised at market value.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax & Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US multinational provides software development services to its parent (USCo) under a cost-plus arrangement at cost + 15%. The AO during TP assessment determines the ALP at cost + 22% based on TNMM using net profit margin of comparable companies. IndCo argues that (i) it is a 'low-risk captive' and comparables selected by the AO include companies bearing entrepreneurial risk, (ii) the working capital adjustment was not applied, and (iii) the parent bears all IP risk per the DAPE agreement. Simultaneously, the AO invokes Section 9(1)(vii) to tax a portion of USCo's receipts as FTS in India, arguing the services involve 'make available' of technical knowledge. Critically examine: (a) the validity of the AO's comparable selection and the captive-vs-entrepreneurial risk distinction under OECD TP Guidelines as adopted in Indian jurisprudence, (b) whether working capital adjustment is mandatory or discretionary under Rule 10B, and (c) the legal tension between the TP adjustment increasing IndCo's income and the simultaneous FTS characterization of the same transaction for USCo — can both stand concurrently under domestic law and the India-US DTAA?

questions.question: A holding company (H) incorporated in India holds 100% equity in a wholly-owned subsidiary (S). H provides corporate guarantee to S's lender bank without charging any consideration. The GST department has issued a SCN alleging this is a 'supply of service' under Schedule I Entry 2 (supply between related persons without consideration) and has demanded GST on the open market value of the guarantee as determined under Rule 28. H contends: (a) it is not 'regularly engaged' in financial guarantees, so Schedule I does not apply; (b) even if it does, Rule 28 proviso should apply since S is eligible for full ITC; and (c) the transaction lacks the 'furtherance of business' element required under Section 7(1)(a). Analyze each contention with reference to relevant CBIC circulars, AAR rulings, and the Finance Act 2024 amendments. Which contention has the strongest legal basis post-2024, and what is the residual exposure?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian IT company (IndCo) licenses software IP to its Singapore AE (SGP-AE) under a cost-sharing arrangement. IndCo bears 60% of R&D costs and receives a cost-contribution reimbursement. The TPO recharacterizes the arrangement as an outright IP transfer and benchmarks using CUP, arriving at a royalty rate of 12% on SGP-AE's revenue, resulting in a Rs. 800 Cr adjustment. IndCo appeals arguing: (a) Rule 10B(1)(a) CUP is inapplicable due to product uniqueness; (b) the TNMM with PLI = Berry ratio is the most appropriate method; (c) the cost-sharing arrangement should be respected under the new safe-harbor rules for intra-group services; and (d) Article 9(2) MAP relief is available via the India-Singapore DTAA. Simultaneously, GSTN flags the software license fee repatriation as a potential 'import of service' requiring RCM payment. Evaluate the TP grounds on merits under Chapter X, identify the strongest appellate strategy through ITAT to High Court, and determine whether the GSTN RCM demand is legally sustainable given the existing AP-level advance ruling.

title: Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
text:
(1) 1 [Where] any person-

(i) supplies or receives any goods in contravention of any of the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder with intent to
evade payment of tax; or

(ii) does not account for any goods on which he is liable to pay tax under this Act; or

(iii) supplies any goods liable to tax under this Act without having applied for registration; or

(iv) contravenes any of the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder with intent to evade payment of tax; or

(v) uses any conveyance as a means of transport for carriage of goods in contravention of the provisions of this Act or the rules
made thereunder unless the owner of the conveyance proves that it was so used without the knowledge or connivance of the
owner himself, his agent, if any, and the person in charge of the conveyance,

then, all such goods or conveyances shall be liable to confiscation and the person shall be liable to penalty under section 122 .

(2) Whenever confiscation of any goods or conveyance is authorised by this Act, the officer adjudging it shall give to the owner of
the goods an option to pay in lieu of confiscation, such fine as the said officer thinks fit:

Provided that such fine leviable shall not exceed the market value of the goods confiscated, less the tax chargeable thereon:

Provided further that the aggregate of such fine and penalty leviable shall not be less than the 2 [penalty equal to hundred per
cent. of the tax payable on such goods]

Provided also that where any such conveyance is used for the carriage of the goods or passengers for hire, the owner of the
conveyance shall be given an option to pay in lieu of the confiscation of the conveyance a fine equal to the tax payable on the
goods being transported thereon.

3 [****]

(4) No order for confiscation of goods or conveyance or for imposition of penalty shall be issued without giving the person an
opportunity of being heard.

(5) Where any goods or conveyance are confiscated under this Act, the title of such goods or conveyance shall thereupon vest in
the Government.

(6) The proper officer adjudging confiscation shall take and hold possession of the things confiscated and every officer of Police,
on the requisition of such proper officer, shall assist him in taking and holding such possession.

(7) The proper officer may, after satisfying himself that the confiscated goods or conveyance are not required in any other
proceedings under this Act and after giving reasonable time not exceeding three months to pay fine in lieu of confiscation,
dispose of such goods or conveyance and deposit the sale proceeds thereof with the Government.

* Enforced w.e.f . 1st July, 2017 .

1. Substituted (w.e.f. 1st January, 2022 vide Notification No. 39/2021-C.T. , dated 21st December, 2021) by s. 118(a) of The Finance Act, 2021 (No. 13 of 2021)
dated 28th March, 2021 for "Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, if".

2. Substituted (w.e.f. 1st January, 2022 vide Notification No. 39/2021-C.T. , dated 21st December, 2021) by s. 118(b) of The Finance Act, 2021 (No. 13 of 2021)
dated 28th March, 2021 for "amount of penalty leviable under sub-section (1) of section 129".

3. Omitted (w.e.f. 1st January, 2022 vide Notification No. 39/2021-C.T ., dated 21st December, 2021) by s. 118(c) of The Finance Act, 2021 (No. 13 of 2021) dated
28th March, 2021 for "(3) Where any fine in lieu of confiscation of goods or conveyance is imposed under sub-section (2), the owner of such goods or conveyance
or the person referred to in sub-section (1), shall, in addition, be liable to any tax, penalty and charges payable in respect of such goods or conveyance."

questions.question: A manufacturing company supplies goods to its 100% subsidiary under a cost-sharing arrangement where no markup is charged, claiming it falls outside the scope of 'supply' under Schedule I of the CGST Act. The GST officer contends that since the subsidiary is a 'related person' under Section 15(5) read with Rule 28, the open market value must be determined and GST levied accordingly. The company argues that its subsidiary is not a 'distinct person' as no separate GST registration exists in the same PAN. Critically analyze the legal position, identify which transactions under Schedule I would still attract GST even without consideration, discuss the valuation methodology applicable under Rule 28(1)(a) vs Rule 28(2), and advise on the availability of full ITC credit to the recipient as a mitigating factor.

An Indian importer purchases capital goods from its foreign parent company at a declared transaction value of USD 500,000. The customs officer rejects the declared value under Rule 12 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, alleging that the buyer-seller relationship has influenced the price, citing that the importer consistently declares values 30% below comparable arm's-length transactions identified in NIDB data. The importer contends that (a) NIDB data cannot be used as a standalone benchmark without disclosing the specific entry relied upon, (b) the Tribunal precedent in Sanjivani Non-Ferrous Trading requires the department to first attempt sequential application of Rules 4 through 9, and (c) contemporaneous export declarations to unrelated buyers in third countries establish that the declared price reflects true market value. Analyze the sequential valuation hierarchy, the evidentiary burden under Rule 12(2), the legal validity of using NIDB data without disclosure, and the scope of the importers right to a test value under Rule 6 (computed value method), with reference to WTO Customs Valuation Agreement Article 7 residual method constraints.

title: Customs Tariff (Determination of Origin of Goods under the Preferential Trade Agreement between the Governments of the Republic of India and the Republic of Korea) Rules, 2009
text:
Annexure-II

(See first proviso to rule 13)

Exemption from the Principle of Territoriality

PART-I

1. List of Goods.- (1) Each State party shall apply exemption from the principle of 
territoriality under rule 13 to goods listed in PART-II of this Annexure; and,

(2) A State party may request amendment of the list referred to in sub-paragraph 
(1) which shall be considered by the other State party in good faith and any such 
amendment to the list shall be adopted when mutually agreed by both the State 
parties.

2. Conferring of origin.- (1) Goods referred to in sub-paragraph (1) of paragraph 
1 and any subsequent amendments, which are re-imported as the goods that do not 
undergo any process beyond operations within the territory of the re-importing 
State party for export as set out in rule 7 shall be deemed to be originating in the 
territory of that State party:

Provided that,-

(i) the total value of non-originating input does not exceed forty per cent. of the 
FOB value of the final good for which originating status is claimed; and,

(ii) the value of originating materials exported from the State party is not less 
than sixty per cent of the total value of materials used in manufacturing the re-
imported material or good.

(2) Except as otherwise provided for in this Annexure, provisions of these rules 
shall be applied mutatis mutandis to the origin conferring of the goods to which 
exemption from the principle of territoriality under rule 13 applies.

Explanation:- For the purposes of this paragraph, “total value of non-originating 
input” means the value of any non-originating materials added inside as well as 
any materials added and all other cost accumulated outside the State party 
concerned, including transport costs.

3. Specific implementation procedure.- (1) The Certificate of Origin for goods 
covered by exemption from the principle of territoriality under rule 13 shall be 
issued by the issuing authorities of the exporting State party in accordance with 
Annexure-III to the rules;

(2) The issuing authority of the exporting State party shall indicate in the 
Certificate of Origin that the good is covered by exemption from the principle of 
territoriality under rule 13;

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a Bill of Entry where the supplier has availed input tax credit on inputs used in the manufacture of those goods. The company subsequently exports these goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) without payment of IGST. However, during audit, the department alleges that since the supplier's ITC was reversed under Rule 42 due to partial exemption, the recipient's own ITC on the inward supply is proportionately inadmissible under Section 17(5). Is this interpretation correct? Cite the relevant provisions, circulars, and the Kerala High Court decision on cascading ITC restrictions.

A private limited company incorporated in India has a Permanent Establishment (PE) in Germany. The German PE generates income that is taxed in Germany under the India-Germany DTAA. The company claims a foreign tax credit (FTC) in India under Rule 128. However, the Assessing Officer disallows the FTC on the ground that the income attributable to the PE is also taxable in India under Section 5(1) read with Section 9, and that the 'tax payable' in India on that income after applying Article 7 of the DTAA is Nil — making FTC computation meaningless. Analyze whether the AO's position is sustainable, referencing the DTAA tie-breaker mechanism, the MAP procedure, and the Supreme Court's interpretation of 'doubly taxed income' in the context of FTC eligibility.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three distinct business verticals under a single GSTIN: (a) sale of goods attracting 18% GST, (b) export of services under LUT (zero-rated), and (c) exempt supply of agricultural produce. During FY 2024-25, the company procures a shared ERP software license (₹50 lakh, 18% GST) used across all three verticals. The CFO proposes using the turnover-based apportionment method under Rule 42 to determine the common credit, but the tax manager argues that a usage-log-based special method under the second proviso to Rule 42(1) would yield a more favorable ITC position. Simultaneously, the company has ₹12 lakh of blocked credit under Section 17(5) on motor vehicles used partly for business demos. Compute the maximum permissible ITC, the reversal obligations, and evaluate whether the special method application requires prior intimation to the department and what happens if the chosen method changes mid-year.

A wholly-owned Indian subsidiary (Importer) purchases specialized industrial machinery from its German parent (Seller) under a long-term supply agreement. The transaction value declared is EUR 2,00,000. Post-importation, the Importer pays: (i) EUR 15,000 as 'technical know-how royalty' tied specifically to the use of the imported machine, (ii) EUR 8,000 as post-sale support charges stipulated as a condition of sale, and (iii) receives a year-end volume rebate of EUR 10,000 credited to the Importer's account but not deducted from the invoice. A customs audit also reveals that the Importer provided free-of-charge tooling worth ₹9,00,000 to the German parent for use in manufacturing the machine. Applying Rule 10 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007, determine the assessable value, identify which additions are mandatory inclusions, whether the rebate can reduce the transaction value, and what related-party test under Rule 3(3) must be satisfied for the declared price to be accepted.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two registered units — Unit A in Maharashtra and Unit B in Gujarat. Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit B under a stock transfer arrangement. Unit B then processes the goods and exports them under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). The company claims ITC on inputs at Unit A, transfers it via ITC-02 during the stock transfer, and Unit B claims a refund of accumulated ITC under Rule 89 on zero-rated supply. The GST officer rejects the refund claim on the ground that the stock transfer from Unit A to Unit B is a taxable supply between distinct persons under Section 25(4), and the ITC utilized for that supply is not eligible for refund under the zero-rated supply route. Evaluate the legal correctness of the officer's position, the interplay between Section 25(4), Section 16(1), and Section 54(3), and the correct mechanism for the company to recover its ITC burden.

An Indian importer purchases specialized CNC machinery from a German parent company under a transfer pricing agreement that has been accepted by the Indian APA (Advance Pricing Agreement) authority under the Income Tax Act. The SVB (Special Valuation Branch) of Customs rejects the declared transaction value and proposes enhancement under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, citing a royalty and technical know-how agreement between the same parties. The importer argues that (a) the APA-accepted price is arm's length and therefore satisfies the 'not influenced by relationship' test under Rule 3(3), and (b) the royalty is for post-importation use of IP and is not a condition of sale. The department counters that APA acceptance under income tax does not bind customs authorities, and that the royalty is structurally inseparable from the import transaction. Analyze the jurisdictional independence of customs valuation vis-à-vis transfer pricing APAs, the conditions under which royalties must be included in assessable value, and the evidentiary standard the importer must meet to rebut the SVB's enhancement.

questions.question: A manufacturer procures capital goods (CNC machine) in April 2024 under a financial lease arrangement where the lessor retains title until the final installment. The machine is used 60% for taxable supply and 40% for exempt supply (sale of land). The ITC claimed in GSTR-3B is subsequently reversed in GSTR-9 after a departmental audit flags it under Rule 42/43. The manufacturer argues that under Section 17(5)(d) the block credit applies only to 'motor vehicles', and that Rule 43 apportionment — not outright denial — is the correct treatment. The department counters that since ownership did not vest, the 'used in course of business' threshold under Section 16(1) is not met. Cite the applicable sections, rules, and any AAAR/AAR rulings that resolve the ownership-vs-possession nexus for ITC eligibility on leased capital goods.
expected_answer_elements: Section 16(1) read with Section 16(2)(b) — tax actually paid by supplier
Rule 43 — ITC apportionment formula for capital goods used partly for exempt supplies
Section 17(5)(d) block credit scope — immovable property vs movable capital goods distinction
Financial lease vs operating lease — AAAR Karnataka ruling on ITC on leased assets
Whether 'possession' substitutes 'ownership' — Section 2(19) definition of capital goods
CGST Circular 172/04/2022 on ITC availment timelines post-amendment

questions.question: An Indian importer (subsidiary) purchases specialty APIs from its Swiss parent at a declared transaction value of USD 120/kg. The Customs department rejects this value under Rule 12 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, citing a contemporaneous import by an unrelated Indian buyer at USD 85/kg for the same HS code and substantially similar specification. The importer invokes the 'related party' safe harbour by demonstrating that the transfer price was accepted by the APA (Advance Pricing Agreement) with the CBDT for AY 2022–26. The assessing officer argues that (a) an APA is an income-tax instrument with no statutory force under the Customs Act, and (b) the identical goods comparison must survive all four sequential rules before a residual method is used. The importer alternatively offers a TNMM-based cost-plus computation showing a 12% operating margin consistent with industry benchmarks. Analyse: (i) the hierarchy of Customs Valuation Rules 3–9, (ii) whether a CBDT APA can serve as corroborating evidence for customs value acceptance, and (iii) the admissibility of TNMM as a 'computed value' proxy under Rule 8.
expected_answer_elements: Customs Valuation Rules 2007 — sequential hierarchy Rules 3→4→5→6→7→8→9
Rule 3(3)(a)–(e) — conditions under which related-party transaction value is acceptable
Supreme Court — Eicher Tractors, Garden Silk Mills — on burden of proof for value rejection
APA (Section 92CC of Income Tax Act) — binding only on income tax, not customs; no statutory nexus
Rule 8 (Computed Value) — requires actual cost of production data from manufacturer, not TNMM proxy
CESTAT rulings on whether transfer pricing studies constitute 'sufficient reason' under Rule 12
CBIC Circular 39/2016-Customs on related-party valuation guidelines

questions.question: A composite manufacturer supplies a machine under a single contract that includes design, fabrication, installation, commissioning, and a 3-year AMC. The buyer is a Special Economic Zone unit. Analyze the GST treatment across all supply components, determine whether the transaction qualifies as a 'works contract', evaluate the zero-rated supply eligibility under Section 16 of the IGST Act, and identify the conditions under which input tax credit on inputs and input services used in the zero-rated supply can be claimed by the supplier without payment of integrated tax under the LUT route versus the refund-of-tax route. What documentation failures would cause retrospective denial of the zero-rated status?

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign seller. The declared transaction value is accepted provisionally. Post-clearance, a Customs audit reveals: (a) the foreign seller paid a marketing support fee to the importer's parent company, (b) royalties are paid by the importer to the foreign seller under a separate licensing agreement, and (c) a portion of the proceeds of subsequent resale accrues to the seller. Under Rule 10 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007, identify each element that must be added to the declared price, the burden-of-proof standards for the department versus the importer, the sequential application of fallback valuation methods if transaction value is rejected, and the limitation period and interest implications if the final re-assessed value exceeds the provisionally assessed value.

questions.question: A manufacturing company registered in Maharashtra supplies goods to a SEZ unit in Gujarat under a zero-rated supply. The supplier claims refund of accumulated ITC under Rule 89(4) of CGST Rules. The GST officer rejects the refund on grounds that the Net ITC used in the formula should exclude ITC attributable to exempt supplies under Rule 42, even though the company has no exempt supplies — only zero-rated. The officer cites Circular 135/05/2020-GST. Is the officer's rejection legally tenable? Cite the relevant provisions, Supreme Court or High Court precedents if any, and the correct computation methodology under Rule 89(4) read with Section 54(3) of CGST Act, 2017.

An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services exclusively to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement at cost + 15% markup, benchmarked using TNMM as the most appropriate transfer pricing method. During scrutiny assessment for AY 2023-24, the TPO proposes to treat the subsidiary as a 'limited risk entity' and rejects the assessee's functional analysis, arguing the subsidiary bears significant economic risk because it holds workforce-related intangibles (trained employees, proprietary processes). The TPO applies a PLI of OP/TC = 22% instead of 15%. The assessee argues this is contrary to OECD BEPS Action 8-10 guidance and the functionally-separate entity approach. Analyze: (a) whether trained workforce constitutes a DEMPE function under Indian TP regulations, (b) the legal validity of the TPO's risk re-characterization under Rule 10B(1)(e) read with Rule 10TA, and (c) the correct appellate strategy before DRP and ITAT.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) but subsequently discovers that 15% of the exported consignment was rejected by the foreign buyer and re-imported into India within 6 months. The original IGST refund on input tax credit has already been processed and credited. What are the exact reversal obligations under Rule 96A of the CGST Rules, the applicable interest rate and computation period under Section 50, whether the re-imported goods attract fresh customs duty and IGST under the Customs Act read with Section 3(7) of the Customs Tariff Act, and how should the taxpayer reconcile the mismatch this creates in GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, and the ICEGATE export records?

An Indian subsidiary of a foreign holding company imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme with a 6-year export obligation, then undergoes a court-approved amalgamation where the transferee company does not hold an EPCG license. Simultaneously, the RBI compounding window is invoked for a delayed ECB reporting by the same entity. Analyze: (1) whether the export obligation survives the amalgamation and how it transfers under DGFT Policy Circular norms, (2) the customs duty redemption fine and penalty exposure under Section 114A of the Customs Act if the obligation lapses, (3) whether compounding under FEMA Section 15 for the ECB violation has any preclusive effect on the parallel customs adjudication, and (4) the GST implications on the deemed domestic clearance of capital goods if the EPCG license is ultimately surrendered.

title: E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 (Responsibilities of Port authority under Indian Ports Act, 1908 (15 of 1908) and Customs Authority)
text:
[भाग II—खण् ड 3(i)] 
भारत का राजपत्र : ऄसाधारण  
21

MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST AND CLIMATE CHANGE

NOTIFICATION

New Delhi, the 2nd November 2022

G.S.R. 801(E).—Whereas the draft rules, namely the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 were

published by the Government of India in the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, vide 
notification number S.O. 360 (E), dated the 19th May, 2022 in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, 
section 3, sub-section (i), inviting objections and suggestions from all persons likely to be affected thereby, 
before the expiry of the period of sixty days from the date on which copies of the Gazette containing the 
said notification were made available to the public;

AND WHEREAS, the copies of the Gazette containing the said notification were made available to

the public on the 19th day of May, 2022;

AND WHEREAS, the objections and suggestions received from the public in respect of the said

draft notification within the said period have been duly considered by the Central Government;

NOW, THEREFORE, in exercise of the powers conferred by sections 6, 8 and 25 of the

Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (29 of 1986) read with sub-rule (3) of rule 5 of the Environment 
(Protection) Rules, 1986, and in supersession of the E-waste (Management) Rules, 2016, except as respects 
things done or omitted to be done before such supersession, the Central Government hereby makes the 
following rules, namely: -

CHAPTER I

PRELIMINARY

1. 
Short title and commencement. - (1) These rules may be called the E-Waste (Management) Rules,

2022.

(2) They shall come into force from the 1st day of April, 2023.

2. 
Application. -   These   rules   shall   apply   to   every   manufacturer, producer refurbisher,

dismantler and recycler involved in manufacture, sale, transfer, purchase, refurbishing, dismantling, 
recycling and processing of e-waste or electrical and electronic equipment listed in Schedule I, including 
their components, consumables, parts and spares which make the product operational but shall not apply to 
-

(a) waste batteries as covered under the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022;

(b) packaging plastics as covered under the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016;

(c) micro enterprise as defined in the Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises       Development Act,

2006 (27 of 2006); and

(d) radio-active wastes as covered under the provisions of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962 (33 of

1962) and rules made there under.

3. 
Definitions.  (1) In these rules, unless the context otherwise requires, -

(a) 'Act' means the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (29 of 1986);

(b) 'bulk consumer' means any entity which has used at least one thousand units of electrical and

electronic equipment listed in Schedule I, at any point of time in the particular Financial Year 
and includes e-retailer;

(c) ‘business’ means manufacturing, production, assembling and import of electrical and electronic

equipment as listed in Schedule I and refurbishing, recycling, disposal and treatment of e-waste;

(d)  ‘component’ means one of the parts of a sub-assembly or assembly of which a manufactured

product is made up of and into which it may be resolved and  includes an accessory or 
attachment to another component;

(e) ‘consumables’ means an item, which participates in or is required for a manufacturing process

or for functioning of the electrical and electronic equipment and may or may not form part of

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a Bill of Entry dated March 28, 2024, but the IGST paid on import is reflected in ICEGATE only on April 3, 2024. The company's GSTR-3B for March 2024 was already filed on April 20, 2024 without claiming this ITC. Can the company claim this ITC in April 2024's GSTR-3B, and does the restriction under Section 16(4) of the CGST Act (ITC claim deadline being the earlier of the due date of September return or annual return for that financial year) apply here, given the 2023-24 annual return deadline? Cite the relevant circular or notification, if any, that addresses import ITC timing mismatches.

A private limited company entered into a slump sale under Section 50B of the Income Tax Act, 1961, transferring an undertaking for ₹18 crore. The net worth of the undertaking as computed under the Explanation to Section 50B was ₹6 crore, but the stamp duty value of the immovable property forming part of the undertaking was ₹14 crore (higher than the book value of ₹4 crore for that property alone). Does Section 50C or Section 43CA apply to substitute the stamp duty value for computing capital gains on the slump sale, or does the specific override of Section 50B exclude the deeming provisions? Additionally, how does the computation change if the undertaking had unabsorbed depreciation of ₹2 crore — is it deducted in net worth computation or treated separately?

I agree to maintain adequate and accurate records and to make those records available for audit or 
inspection by the Sponsor, ethics committee, Central Licencing Authority or their authorised representatives, in

reason for the delay to the satisfaction of the Central Licencing Authority along with the report of the serious 
adverse event.

questions.question: Your production RAG system using BGE-M3 embeddings on a 4-core CPU-bound rig is showing P99 query latency of 8–12 seconds. Profiling reveals 60% of time is spent in Qdrant vector search across 108k chunks, 25% in LLM prefill, and 15% in reranking. You cannot add hardware. Walk through your optimization strategy prioritizing correctness preservation, and explain the trade-offs of each intervention you would apply in sequence.

You are ingesting a 10,000-page legal corpus where 471 PDFs are image-only scans (no embedded text layer), 2,300 PDFs have mixed text+image pages, and the remainder are clean digital PDFs. Your embedding pipeline runs at 5–9 chunks/sec on a single Vulkan GPU. Design a triage and routing architecture that maximizes throughput without degrading retrieval quality for the scanned documents, and specify exactly where OCR errors will propagate into your vector index and how you would detect them post-ingestion.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a distributor under a principal-to-principal arrangement. The distributor later returns 40% of the goods beyond the 6-month window due to quality disputes, while the remaining 60% were already sold to end-customers with GST charged and remitted. The original invoice date falls in one financial year, the return falls in the next, and the manufacturer has already filed GSTR-9 for the original year without reversals. What is the precise mechanism and documentary trail required to claim ITC reversal, issue credit notes within the time-limit constraints under Section 34, and reconcile mismatches in GSTR-2B vs GSTR-3B for both parties — and at what point does the manufacturer risk losing the right to adjust output tax liability entirely?

An importer brings in capital goods under the EPCG scheme at nil/concessional duty, books them at transaction value inclusive of freight and insurance. Three years into the export obligation period, the importer partially re-exports the same goods to a related-party overseas subsidiary at a transfer price 30% below comparable uncontrolled price, triggering a Customs valuation dispute under Rule 10 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007. Simultaneously, the DGFT initiates a show-cause for proportionate duty redemption on the unutilised export obligation. Analyze: (a) how the re-export transaction value is determined and whether the residual depreciated value is admissible, (b) the interaction between the EPCG redemption liability and the enhanced customs duty demand, (c) whether payment of the differential duty on re-export extinguishes or merely pauses the export obligation shortfall, and (d) the jurisdictional overlap between the Customs authority and DGFT in adjudicating these concurrent proceedings.

title: Cargo - Consolidation of cargo at gateway port
text:
minate the shipping line destination-wise where such choice has been left to the
custodian.

3. The consignment of one shipping bill should not be allowed to be moved in part.
4. After examination and clearance of LCL cargo, the packages opened for customs examination shall be sealed by

Customs. the shipping line will  use identification mark in each package clearly indicating serial number of package,
description of the goods and the total number of packages covered under that particular shipping bill, exporters identity
and their own codified identity. As far as possible all the shipping lines will use such identification marks in different
colours, so that these can be easily identified.

5. After completion of Customs formalities, the packages would be handed over by the Customs to the custodian along with

two transference copies of Shipping Bill, certified copy of invoice, packing list and other documents in sealed cover. The
concerned shipping line will issue the Bill of Lading, a copy of which will be handed over to the custodian.

6. The custodian would consolidate the cargo irrespective of the shipping lines and transport the same in their containers,

which will be stuffed and sealed in the presence of Customs. The container movement will be organised by the
custodians either by rail and / or road depending on the availability of services.

7. At the gateway port, or some CFS near gateway port, both the transference copies of shipping Bill would be presented

by the Custodian"s representative to the proper officer of Customs who would verify the genuineness of documents and
check the marks and numbers of individual packages. The customs officer would inspect the packages and endorse the
transference copies of Shipping Bill and AR4 in the following manner, "inspected and found in order". The custodian
would hand over the cargo to respective shipping lines/steamer agents. Such packages received from inland ICDs/CFSs
would be stuffed in containers by shipping lines in the presence of Customs.

8. The custodian of the gateway port of CFS near gateway port would maintain a tally sheet containerwise, giving details of

the export consignments, the previous Container No. Shipping Bill No. AR-4 No. and the details of new container in which
goods have been restuffed. After the reworking/restuffing of the containers, tally sheet should be signed by the
representatives of the custodian of the CFS and shipping line as well as by the supervising Customs Officers.

9. After signing the tally sheet the Customs officer would indicate the container number in which such cargoes are stuffed on

both the transference copies of shipping bill and AR-4, and retain one copy of such shipping bill. The other transference
copy would be returned  to ICDs/CFSs from which the container originated.

10. Custodians would, thereafter, use the same containers to bring back LCL imports to various ICDs and make

arrangements to deliver these consignments to the consignees.

11. As earlier, the drawback would be paid at the inland ICDs/CFSs immediately after the clearance of LCL cargo by

Customs at inland ICDs/CFSs without waiting for actual shipment of cargo from the gateway port.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units: Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat). Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit B for processing under a job-work arrangement. Unit B then supplies the finished goods directly to an export-oriented unit (EOU) located in Tamil Nadu. The EOU claims that the supply to them is 'deemed export' under Section 147 of the CGST Act. Unit A claims ITC on the raw material procurement. Analyze: (1) whether the stock transfer from Unit A to Unit B attracts GST and at what value, (2) whether the supply by Unit B to the EOU qualifies as deemed export and what refund mechanism applies, (3) how ITC flows across this chain, and (4) what happens to accumulated ITC if the EOU later converts to a DTA unit mid-year.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent (USCo) under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15%. IndCo also has a DPIIT-recognized startup certificate and claims Section 80-IAC deduction. During AY 2024-25, the Transfer Pricing Officer proposes an upward adjustment of ₹40 crore, arguing the arm's length price should be cost+28% using the TNMM method against USCo's internal comparables. IndCo challenges this citing the 'bright line test' under safe harbour rules. Simultaneously, the AO disallows the 80-IAC deduction arguing that the TP adjustment inflates profits beyond the threshold. Analyze: (1) the legal validity of using the parent's internal comparables in TNMM, (2) whether safe harbour rates under Rule 10TD override the TPO's determination, (3) the interaction between a TP adjustment and 80-IAC deduction eligibility, and (4) what appellate strategy and alternate dispute resolution options (MAP, APA) are available and their practical sequencing.
