questions.question: A multinational company has a wholly-owned Indian subsidiary that acts as a 'pure agent' procuring third-party logistics services on behalf of the foreign parent. The subsidiary charges the parent a management fee plus reimbursement of logistics costs. Under GST, analyze: (1) whether the subsidiary qualifies as a 'pure agent' under Rule 33 of CGST Rules and can exclude logistics reimbursements from its taxable value, (2) whether the management fee constitutes an 'intermediary service' under Section 13(8)(b) of IGST Act making the Place of Supply India regardless of the parent being overseas, and (3) how the Export of Service exemption under Section 16(1)(a) of IGST Act interacts with the intermediary classification — specifically whether a single contract combining pure agent and intermediary elements can be bifurcated for tax treatment or must be treated as a composite supply.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian company holds a patent developed entirely in India and licenses it to its Singapore AE (Associated Enterprise) for royalty. The Singapore AE sub-licenses the same patent to unrelated third parties globally at 5x the royalty charged to the Indian parent. Transfer Pricing Officer invokes Section 92C and proposes to apply the CUP method using the Singapore AE's third-party sub-licensing rate as the comparable. Analyze: (1) whether the TPO's use of the Singapore AE's own sub-licensing rates as an 'external CUP' is legally tenable under Rule 10B(1)(a) given the functional and risk differences, (2) whether the DEMPE (Development, Enhancement, Maintenance, Protection, Exploitation) framework under OECD BEPS Action 8-10 as adopted in India's TP regulations affects the arm's length determination, (3) how the secondary adjustment provisions under Section 92CE and deemed dividend implications under Section 2(22)(e) interact if the TP adjustment exceeds the primary adjustment threshold, and (4) whether the taxpayer can invoke the corresponding adjustment mechanism under Article 9(2) of the India-Singapore DTAA to avoid double taxation.

title: Amends Notification No.69/2004 dated 9.7.2004

questions.question: A pharmaceutical manufacturer operates three distinct business verticals under a single GSTIN: (1) manufacture of Schedule H drugs (exempt supply), (2) manufacture of OTC consumer healthcare products (taxable at 12%), and (3) contract R&D services for foreign pharma companies (zero-rated export of services). During FY 2024-25, the company incurred ₹4.2 crore on a centralized ERP upgrade (SAP S/4HANA) that supports all three verticals, ₹85 lakh on a clean-room HVAC system used exclusively for Schedule H drug manufacturing, and ₹1.1 crore on analytical lab equipment used 60% for exempt drug QC and 40% for export R&D. Under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of the CGST Rules, detail the exact ITC apportionment methodology, compute the ineligible ITC quantum for each asset category, specify which reversals must be reported in GSTR-3B Table 4(B) versus GSTR-9C reconciliation, and identify any ITC that can be claimed as a refund under the inverted duty structure provisions.

An Indian subsidiary of a Swiss luxury watchmaker imports watch movements (HS 9108) from its Singapore-based related party at declared transaction value of USD 320 per unit. The CBIC Special Valuation Branch (SVB) has issued a provisional assessment order. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) under Section 92CA of Income Tax Act has determined the arm's length price at USD 410 per unit using the Comparable Uncontrolled Price (CUP) method, citing three external comparables. The company disputes the SVB's rejection of the transaction value under Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, arguing that the royalty arrangement (2.5% of Indian sales payable to Swiss HO) does not influence the customs value. Analyze: (a) the legal standard for 'related party influence' under WTO Customs Valuation Agreement Article 1.2 as codified in Indian law, (b) whether the TPO's ALP determination is binding or merely persuasive evidence for SVB purposes under the Hon'ble Supreme Court's ruling in Wipro Ltd., (c) the correct sequential application of fallback valuation methods under Rules 5–9, and (d) the mechanism to avoid double taxation where both customs duties and transfer pricing adjustment apply to the same undervaluation quantum.

the additional duty leviable under the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, as the case may be, has 
already been paid.

[F.No.334/3/2006-TRU] 
 
 
(Ajay) 
Under Secretary to Government of India

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) unit under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). The SEZ unit subsequently re-exports the goods after minimal processing. The tax authority contends that the activity constitutes 'supply' within India since title never left domestic territory and the SEZ is a 'deemed foreign territory' only for customs purposes, not GST. Simultaneously, the manufacturer's input tax credit on raw materials is being challenged on the grounds that the final supply is 'zero-rated' and not 'exempt', but that the ITC reversal rules under Rule 42 apply because a portion of inputs were used in exempt activities during the same tax period. Analyse the legal validity of both contentions, the correct treatment of ITC, and the evidentiary burden on the taxpayer before the Adjudicating Authority.

An Indian importer purchases goods from its 100%-owned foreign parent at a declared transaction value. Customs rejects the declared value under Rule 12 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, invoking the 'related party' test, and proposes enhancement based on computed value. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer under the Income Tax Act accepts the same inter-company price as arm's length under the Comparable Uncontrolled Price method. The importer argues that the TP Officer's acceptance creates res judicata or at minimum a strong presumption in favour of the declared customs value. Evaluate: (a) whether the TP acceptance binds the Customs authority, (b) the evidentiary weight of a TP order in customs valuation proceedings, (c) the correct appellate forum and sequence if both orders are challenged concurrently, and (d) whether the importer can seek a pre-import advance ruling to prevent this divergence prospectively.

title: Import OF SELENIUM BEARING WASTES (A1010) AND WASTE HAVING SELENIUM; SELENIUM COMPOUNDS OR CONSTITUENTS OR CONTAMINANTS (A1020) IS PROHIBITED.
text:
56  
     THE   GAZETTE   OF  INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
             [PART II—SEC. 3(i)] 
 
 
35. 
“treatment” means a method, technique or process, designed to modify the physical, chemical or biological 
characteristics or composition of any hazardous or other waste so as to reduce its potential to cause harm;  
 
36. 
“used oil” means any oil- 
 
(i) 
derived from crude oil or mixtures containing synthetic oil including spent oil, used engine oil,   
gear oil, hydraulic oil, turbine oil, compressor oil, industrial gear oil, heat transfer oil, transformer 
oil and their tank bottom sludges; and 
(ii) 
suitable for reprocessing, if it meets the specification laid down in Part A of Schedule V but does 
not include waste oil; 
 
37. 
“utilisation” means use of hazardous or other waste as a resource; 
 
38. 
“waste” means  materials that are not products or by-products, for which the generator has no further use 
for the purposes of production, transformation or consumption. 
  
Explanation.- for the purposes of this clause,  
 
(i)       waste includes the materials that may be generated during, the extraction of raw materials, the 
processing of raw materials into intermediates and final products, the consumption of final 
products, and through other human activities and excludes residuals recycled or reused at the 
place of generation; and 
(ii)       by-product means a material that is not intended to be produced but gets produced in the production 
process of intended product and is used as such; 
 
39. 
“waste oil” means any oil which includes spills of crude oil, emulsions, tank bottom sludge and slop oil 
generated from petroleum refineries, installations or ships and can be used as fuel in furnaces for energy 
recovery, if it meets the specifications laid down in Part-B of Schedule V either as such or after 
reprocessing.  
 
(2)  Words and expressions used in these rules and not defined but defined in the Act shall have the meanings 
respectively assigned to them in the Act.  
 
CHAPTER II 
 
PROCEDURE FOR MANAGEMENT OF HAZARDOUS AND OTHER WASTES 
 
4.      Responsibilities of the occupier for management of hazardous and other wastes.- 
 
(1) For the management of hazardous and other wastes, an occupier shall follow the following steps, namely:-   
(a) prevention; 
(b) minimization;  
(c) reuse,  
(d) recycling;  
(e) recovery, utilisation including co-processing;  
(f) safe disposal.  
 
(2) The occupier shall be responsible for safe and environmentally sound management of hazardous and other wastes.  
 
(3) The hazardous and other wastes generated in the establishment of an occupier shall be sent or sold to an authorised 
actual user or shall be disposed of in an authorised disposal facility. 
 
(4) The hazardous and other wastes shall be transported from an occupier’s establishment to an authorised actual user or 
to an authorised disposal facility in accordance with the provisions of these rules. 
 
(5) The occupier who intends to get its hazardous and other wastes treated and disposed of by the operator of a treatment, 
storage and disposal facility shall give to the operator of that facility, such specific information as may be needed for safe 
storage and disposal. 
 
(6) The occupier shall take all the steps while managing hazardous and other wastes to- 
(a) contain contaminants and prevent accidents and limit their consequences on human beings and the environment;

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a registered unit in Maharashtra and a warehouse in Gujarat used exclusively for stock transfers to dealers. The Gujarat warehouse is separately registered. During an audit, the GST officer contends that stock transfers from Maharashtra to Gujarat constitute a 'supply' under Section 7(1)(c) read with Schedule I of the CGST Act and demands IGST. The company argues that since the warehouse performs no independent economic activity and all sales are booked in Maharashtra, the transfer is purely internal. Critically evaluate the officer's contention with reference to the conditions for 'principal place of business' vs. 'additional place of business', the principal-agent relationship test under Schedule I, para 3, and the impact of the Gujarat unit's GSTIN on the taxability of the stock transfer. What ITC implications arise if IGST is held payable on the transfer?

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a related foreign supplier at USD 500,000. The customs officer rejects the transaction value under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, alleging that the relationship has influenced the price, based solely on the fact that the importer holds a 30% equity stake in the foreign supplier. The importer provides a test value under Rule 5 using a contemporaneous sale to an unrelated buyer at USD 480,000 for identical goods. The officer argues that Rule 5 cannot be applied because the unrelated buyer's transaction was in a different country. Analyze: (a) the precise legal standard for 'influence on price' under Rule 3(3) and whether equity stake alone satisfies it; (b) the admissibility of the Rule 5 test value across different destination countries; (c) the sequential hierarchy of fallback methods under Rules 5–9 and which method should apply if Rule 5 is validly rejected; and (d) the importer's evidentiary burden before the CESTAT.

title: Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
text:
CHAPTER XV DEMANDS AND RECOVERY

* Section 73. Determination of tax not paid or short paid or erroneously refunded or input tax credit wrongly availed
or utilised for any reason other than fraud or any willful-misstatement or suppression of facts.-

(1) Where it appears to the proper officer that any tax has not been paid or short paid or erroneously refunded, or where input tax
credit has been wrongly availed or utilised for any reason, other than the reason of fraud or any wilful-misstatement or
suppression of facts to evade tax, he shall serve notice on the person chargeable with tax which has not been so paid or which
has been so short paid or to whom the refund has erroneously been made, or who has wrongly availed or utilised input tax credit,
requiring him to show cause as to why he should not pay the amount specified in the notice along with interest payable thereon
under section 50 and a penalty leviable under the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder.

(2) The proper officer shall issue the notice under sub-section (1) at least three months prior to the time limit specified in sub-
section (10) for issuance of order.

(3) Where a notice has been issued for any period under sub-section (1), the proper officer may serve a statement, containing
the details of tax not paid or short paid or erroneously refunded or input tax credit wrongly availed or utilised for such periods
other than those covered under sub-section (1), on the person chargeable with tax.

(4) The service of such statement shall be deemed to be service of notice on such person under sub-section (1), subject to the
condition that the grounds relied upon for such tax periods other than those covered under sub-section (1) are the same as are
mentioned in the earlier notice.

(5) The person chargeable with tax may, before service of notice under subsection (1) or, as the case may be, the statement
under sub-section (3), pay the amount of tax along with interest payable thereon under section 50 on the basis of his own
ascertainment of such tax or the tax as ascertained by the proper officer and inform the proper officer in writing of such payment.

(6) The proper officer, on receipt of such information, shall not serve any notice under sub-section (1) or, as the case may be, the
statement under sub-section (3), in respect of the tax so paid or any penalty payable under the provisions of this Act or the rules
made thereunder.

(7) Where the proper officer is of the opinion that the amount paid under sub-section (5) falls short of the amount actually
payable, he shall proceed to issue the notice as provided for in sub-section (1) in respect of such amount which falls short of the
amount actually payable.

(8) Where any person chargeable with tax under sub-section (1) or sub-section (3) pays the said tax along with interest payable
under section 50 within thirty days of issue of show cause notice, no penalty shall be payable and all proceedings in respect of
the said notice shall be deemed to be concluded.

(9) The proper officer shall, after considering the representation, if any, made by person chargeable with tax, determine the
amount of tax, interest and a penalty equivalent to ten per cent. of tax or ten thousand rupees, whichever is higher, due from such
person and issue an order.

**(10) The proper officer shall issue the order under sub-section (9) within three years from the due date for furnishing of annual
return for the financial year to which the tax not paid or short paid or input tax credit wrongly availed or utilised relates to or within
three years from the date of erroneous refund.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. Post-export, the foreign buyer rejects 30% of the shipment and it is re-imported. The re-imported goods undergo minor reprocessing and are again exported. Analyze: (1) the ITC reversal obligations at each stage, (2) whether the re-export qualifies as 'export of goods' under IGST Act triggering fresh LUT validity, (3) the customs duty treatment on re-import under Notification 45/96-Cus versus Section 74 of Customs Act, and (4) the reconciliation required in GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, and GSTR-9 across both financial years if the rejection and re-export straddle a year-end.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A holding company provides corporate guarantee to a bank securing a loan taken by its wholly-owned subsidiary. The AO invokes Section 56(2)(x) read with Rule 11UA treating the guarantee as a 'benefit' received by the subsidiary at below-market cost, while simultaneously the GST department raises a demand under Schedule II Entry 5(e) treating guarantee as a taxable supply of service, applying OM dated 26-10-2023 on related-party guarantees. Examine: (1) the constitutional validity of taxing the same transaction under both statutes without double-taxation relief, (2) the arm's length pricing methodology applicable under both regimes and whether they must converge, (3) the correct taxable value under GST when the subsidiary is insolvent and no commercial rate exists, and (4) the appellate strategy if the holding company contests both demands simultaneously before ITAT and GST Appellate Authority.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three distinct business verticals under a single GSTIN: (1) taxable supply of industrial machinery (28% GST), (2) exempt supply of agricultural equipment, and (3) a captive power plant supplying electricity exclusively to its own factory. During FY 2024-25, the company imported capital goods used across all three verticals and also received a Works Contract Service for construction of a new factory building. Detail the complete ITC reversal methodology under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of CGST Rules, specifying how to compute the 'exempt turnover' denominator when captive electricity consumption has no monetary value, and whether the Works Contract ITC blockage under Section 17(5)(c) can be overridden when the building is used for further taxable supply of goods.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US MNC provides IT-enabled services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at OP/TC = 18%. During assessment, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) proposes to include notional interest on the outstanding receivables (outstanding >180 days) as an international transaction under Section 92B, applies CUP method using LIBOR+200bps, and also recharacterizes the service agreement as a risk-bearing principal structure rather than a limited-risk distributor, triggering a separate ALP adjustment. Analyze: (a) the legal validity of notional interest as an international transaction post the coordinate bench ruling in Kusum Healthcare, (b) whether the TPO can simultaneously apply two different characterization theories to the same entity in the same year, and (c) the interplay between the proposed adjustment and IndCo's eligibility for deduction under Section 10AA if it is located in an SEZ.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) but later discovers that 15% of the export consignments were made to a related party at a price lower than the open market value. The jurisdictional officer issues a SCN demanding reversal of zero-rated IGST refunds already processed, invoking Rule 96(10) read with Section 15(4) of CGST Act. The exporter argues that since payment was received in foreign exchange and the LUT was valid at the time of export, Rule 28 valuation norms apply only to domestic supplies between related parties, not to exports. Analyse the legal merits of both positions, identify the relevant proviso under Section 70 of the Customs Act that may independently override IGST zero-rating, and determine whether the exporter can seek prospective relief under Section 159 of CGST Act without reversing past credits.

An Indian subsidiary (AssesseeCo) provides software development services to its US parent under a cost-plus 15% arrangement benchmarked using TNMM with operating margin as the PLI. During assessment for AY 2024-25, the TPO rejects the comparables selected by AssesseeCo and applies the Resale Price Method instead, arguing that the subsidiary functionally resembles a distributor of IP rather than a service provider, based on the fact that AssesseeCo holds registered patents in its own name that were subsequently licensed back to the parent. The assessee contends that the patents arose from funded R&D under a Cost Contribution Arrangement pre-dating the service agreement, and that the royalty stream is separately benchmarked. Evaluate: (a) whether the TPO's unilateral change of method requires invocation of Section 92CA(3A) and DRP approval; (b) how the existence of the CCA affects the characterisation of AssesseeCo under OECD Chapter VIII guidelines as incorporated under Rule 10B; and (c) whether the assessee can invoke MAP under Article 9 of the India-US DTAA to suspend demand recovery under Section 220(6) pending competent authority resolution.

questions.question: A holding company (H) provides brand licensing, shared IT infrastructure, and centralized treasury services to its wholly-owned subsidiary (S). Under GST, H charges S a consolidated monthly fee. The Assessing Officer contends that (a) brand licensing constitutes a 'supply' under Schedule I even without consideration since H and S are related parties, (b) the IT infrastructure sharing should be valued under Rule 28 of the CGST Valuation Rules using open market value, and (c) the treasury service interest income is exempt but proportionate ITC reversal under Rule 42/43 is mandatory. S disputes all three grounds and has filed a writ. Identify the strongest legal argument available to S on each ground, citing the relevant sections of CGST Act 2017, applicable rules, and any AAAR/AAR or High Court rulings that support S's position.

A foreign parent (FP) in the Netherlands grants its Indian subsidiary (IS) an exclusive license to manufacture and sell patented pharmaceutical compounds in South Asia. IS bears all clinical trial costs for Indian regulatory approval (INR 340 crore over 5 years), while FP holds the global patent. The TPO proposes to: (i) treat IS as a 'limited risk distributor' and benchmark using TNMM with net cost-plus 8%, disregarding IS's actual 22% operating margin; (ii) impute a 'deemed royalty' from FP to IS under Section 92CA for the DEMPE functions IS performs; and (iii) disallow the INR 340 crore R&D deduction under Section 35(1)(iv) on grounds that the resultant IP vests in FP. Draft a structured response to the TPO covering: the correct functional/risk characterization under Rule 10B, the DEMPE framework under OECD BEPS Action 8-10 and its domestic applicability, the legality of imputing a reverse royalty, and the correct treatment of contract R&D costs under Indian domestic law and relevant ITAT precedents.

questions.question: A manufacturer registered under GST in Maharashtra supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka under a works contract. The goods are dispatched from a job-worker's premises in Telangana (where the manufacturer holds an additional place of business registration). During transit, 30% of the goods are damaged and the dealer partially rejects the consignment. The manufacturer issues a credit note 45 days after the original invoice. Determine: (1) the place of supply, (2) whether the job-worker's GSTIN or the manufacturer's Maharashtra GSTIN should appear on the e-way bill, (3) the ITC reversal obligations for the dealer on the rejected portion, and (4) whether the 45-day credit note issuance falls within the time limit under Section 34 of the CGST Act for the relevant financial year, assuming the invoice was raised on March 10, 2025.
expected_answer_elements: Place of supply determination under Section 10 / Section 12 for works contract
Job-worker e-way bill rules under Rule 55A and Section 143
ITC reversal under Rule 42 / Rule 43 for rejected goods
Section 34 credit note time limit: earlier of September 30 following FY end or annual return filing date
March 10 2025 invoice → FY 2024-25 → deadline September 30 2025 → 45 days = April 24 2025 → within limit

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US multinational provides software development services to its parent (USCo) under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15%. During AY 2024-25, IndCo also receives a royalty-free license for proprietary technology from USCo. The TPO proposes an arm's length royalty charge of 8% on IndCo's net revenues under TNMM, treating the royalty as a separate international transaction. IndCo's total revenues are ₹500 crore; operating costs are ₹380 crore. Simultaneously, IndCo has accumulated losses of ₹120 crore carried forward. Compute: (1) the transfer pricing adjustment proposed by the TPO, (2) the tax impact including surcharge and cess at applicable corporate rates, (3) whether MAT liability arises and its interaction with the TP adjustment, and (4) the secondary adjustment obligation under Section 92CE and whether the deemed advance threshold is breached.
expected_answer_elements: Royalty adjustment = 8% × ₹500 cr = ₹40 crore addition to income
Taxable income = ₹120 cr operating profit + ₹40 cr TP adj − ₹120 cr b/f loss = ₹40 crore
Tax at 25.168% (domestic company, turnover threshold check) = ~₹10.07 crore
MAT under Section 115JB at 15% on book profit (TP adjustment may not flow to book profit directly)

Section 92CE secondary adjustment triggered if TP adjustment > ₹1 crore and transaction > ₹1 crore — threshold breached; deemed advance attracts interest at SBI MCLR + 325 bps or 1-year US Treasury + 300 bps for foreign currency

questions.question: A manufacturer operating under the Composition Scheme under Section 10 of the CGST Act supplies goods to an SEZ unit located in a Special Economic Zone. The supplier mistakenly charges CGST and SGST on the invoice instead of treating it as a zero-rated supply under Section 16 of the IGST Act. The SEZ unit has already availed ITC on the erroneously charged tax. Subsequently, a show-cause notice is issued to the manufacturer. Analyze: (a) the correct GST treatment for B2B supplies to SEZ units, (b) whether a Composition Scheme taxpayer can make zero-rated supplies at all, (c) the rectification mechanism under Section 77 of the CGST Act for tax wrongly charged, and (d) the liability of the SEZ unit to reverse ITC availed on an ineligible invoice, with reference to Rule 37 and Section 17(5) of the CGST Act.

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign supplier (holding 30% equity in the importer). The declared transaction value is accepted by the customs officer at the port of import. Eighteen months later, the importer receives a supplementary invoice from the foreign supplier for price adjustments based on a retroactive cost-plus pricing audit mandated by the OECD Transfer Pricing guidelines. Analyze: (a) whether the supplementary invoice amount constitutes 'additions to transaction value' under Rule 10 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007, (b) the applicability of the related-party valuation test under Rule 3(3) and whether the original acceptance survives scrutiny post-audit, (c) the mechanism and time limits for filing a re-assessment or Bill of Entry amendment under Section 149 of the Customs Act 1962, and (d) the interplay between the customs re-valuation and the corresponding Transfer Pricing adjustment already accepted by the Income Tax Authority under Section 92CA, including risk of double taxation and available remedies.

title: Anti-dumping duty on Poly-Iso-Butylene
text:
31st  October, 2002

Notification No. 123/2002-Customs

WHEREAS in the matter of import of Poly-Iso-Butylene falling under sub-heading 3902.20 of the First Schedule to the Customs
Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), originating in, or exported from, European Union, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea,
Singapore and Thailand, the designated authority in its preliminary findings vide notification No.47/1/2001-DGAD, dated the
12th December, 2001, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 12th December, 2001, had
come to the conclusion that -

(a)Poly-Iso-Butylene originating in, or exported from, European Union, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea, Singapore

and Thailand , had been exported to India below normal value, resulting in dumping;

(b)the Indian industry had suffered material injury only from exports of Poly-Iso-Butylene from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic

of Korea and Singapore;

(c)injury had been caused cumulatively by the imports from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and Singapore;

AND WHEREAS on the basis of the aforesaid findings of the designated authority, the Central Government had imposed an
anti-dumping duty on Poly-Iso-Butylene vide notification of the Government of India in the erstwhile Ministry of Finance
(Department of Revenue), No. 7/2002-Customs, dated the 16th January, 2002, [G.S.R. 32(E), dated the 16th January, 2002],
published in Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 16th January, 2002;

AND WHEREAS the designated authority, vide its final findings notification No.47/1/2001-DGAD, dated the 5th September,
2002, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 9th September, 2002 has come to the
conclusion that -

(a)Poly-Iso-Butylene originating in, or exported from, European Union, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea, Singapore

and Thailand, have been exported to India below normal value, resulting in dumping;

(b)the Indian industry has suffered material injury on account of price under-cutting, price suppression and significant increase

in the volume of dumped imports of Poly-Iso-Butylene from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and Singapore;

(c)the injury has been caused cumulatively by the dumped imports from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and

Singapore;

and has recommended the imposition of definitive anti-dumping duty on all imports of  the said Poly-Iso-Butylene originating in,
or exported from, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and Singapore;

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1), read with sub-section (5) of section 9A of the said
Customs Tariff Act, and rules 18 and 20 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty
on Dumped Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995, the Central Government, on the basis of the aforesaid final
findings of the designated authority, hereby imposes on all imports of Poly-Iso-Butylene, falling under sub-heading 3902.20 of
the First Schedule to the said Customs Tariff Act, originating in, or exported from, the country specified in column (2) of the
Table below, when exported by the exporter or producer specified in the corresponding entry in column (3) of the said Table,
and imported into India, an anti-dumping duty at a rate which is equivalent to the difference between the amount specified in the
corresponding entry in column (4) of the said Table  and  the landed value, in US $ per metric Tonne, of such imported Poly-Iso-
Butylene.

questions.question: A manufacturer procures capital goods (CNC machine) worth ₹50 lakhs in April 2025 and avails full ITC of ₹9 lakhs in the same month. In November 2025, the machine is partly used for exempt supply of goods (ratio: 40% taxable, 60% exempt). Subsequently, in February 2026, the machine suffers partial damage and its useful life is reassessed downward from 5 years to 3 years. Calculate: (a) the ITC reversal amount applicable in November 2025 under Rule 43 of CGST Rules, 2017; (b) whether the February 2026 useful-life reassessment triggers any further reversal or adjustment; and (c) the impact on the aggregate Tr value for annual reconciliation in GSTR-9C for FY 2025-26. Cite the exact sub-rules and CBIC circulars governing capital goods ITC apportionment and useful-life computation.

An importer declares CIF value of USD 1,20,000 for a consignment of cold-rolled steel coils from a related foreign supplier. The DRI investigation reveals: (i) contemporaneous imports of identical goods from unrelated suppliers at USD 1,45,000 CIF; (ii) the importer and exporter share 35% cross-shareholding; and (iii) an anti-dumping duty notification under Section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 is in force at USD 180/MT, but the consignment was shipped before the notification date though assessed after it. Determine: (a) the correct transaction value under Rule 3 read with Rules 4–9 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007; (b) whether the related-party relationship vitiates the declared value and which alternative valuation method applies; (c) the applicability and quantification of anti-dumping duty given the ship-date vs. assessment-date conflict under Section 9A(1A); and (d) the importer's remedies and procedural steps under Section 128 of the Customs Act, 1962 and WTO Anti-Dumping Agreement Article 10 on retroactivity.

title: Clarifications regarding applicability of GST on certain services
text:
which are approved by the Directorate General of Civil Aviation (DGCA). The same has
been examined.

4.2
Under GST Law, vide Sl. No. 66 of the notification No. 12/2017- Central Tax (Rate)

dated 28.06.2017, services provided by educational institutions to its students, faculty and
staff are exempt from levy of GST. In the above notification, “educational institution” has
been defined to mean an institution providing services by way of education as a part of a
curriculum for obtaining a qualification recognized by any law for the time being in force.

4.3
In exercise of the power vested by Section 5 of the Aircraft Act, 1934, the Central

Government has made the Aircraft Rules, 1937, which, inter-alia, provide for ‘approved
training’, i.e. training the curriculum of which has been approved by the DGCA, and
‘approved training organization’, i.e. a flying training organization which shall obtain the
approval of DGCA before the students are enrolled to acquire flying experience. The said
rules further state that flying experience required for the issue of private pilot and commercial
pilot licenses shall be acquired at the Flying Training Organization (FTO) approved/
recognized by the DGCA. The Civil Aviation Requirements (CAR) issued under the said
rules also provide for a completion certificate to be issued by an approved FTO to each
student who completes its approved course of training.

4.4
It is evident from the above that the DGCA not only approves FTOs but also flying

training courses and mandates the requirement of course completion certificates to be issued
to successful candidates in terms of the Aircraft Act, 1934 and the rules prescribed
thereunder. Therefore, the approved flying training courses conducted by FTOs approved by
DGCA, wherein the DGCA mandates the requirement of a completion certificate, are covered
under Sl. No. 66 of Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28.06.2017 and are
hence, exempt.

5.
Regularizing payment of GST on transport of passengers by helicopter:

5.1
54th GST Council has recommended that the GST rate on transportation of passengers,

with or without accompanied baggage, by air, in a helicopter on seat share basis may be
notified at 5%. Accordingly, notification No. 07/2024- Central Tax (Rate) dated 08.10.2024
effective from 10.10.2024 has been issued.

5.2
The Council further recommended to regularize payment of GST on transportation of

passengers, with or without accompanied baggage, by air, in a helicopter on seat share basis
on ‘as is where is’ basis.

5.3
In addition to above, the Council also recommended to clarify that charter of

helicopter would continue to attract GST at the rate of 18%.

5.4
Thus, as recommended by the 54th GST Council, payment of GST on transportation of

passengers, with or without accompanied baggage, by air, in a helicopter on seat share basis
is hereby regularized on ‘as is where is’ basis for the period from 01.07.2017 to 09.10.2024.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two registered units in different states — Unit A in Maharashtra and Unit B in Karnataka. Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit B for further processing under a job-work arrangement. Unit B then supplies the finished goods directly to an unregistered customer in Rajasthan. The invoice is raised by Unit A. Identify: (1) the correct place of supply for the B2C transaction, (2) whether IGST or CGST+SGST applies and to which unit, (3) the ITC eligibility on raw material procurement by Unit A, and (4) the documentation requirements under Rule 45 of the CGST Rules for the goods movement between the two units.

An Indian-resident HUF earns: (a) long-term capital gains of ₹18 lakhs from listed equity shares sold in March 2026, (b) short-term capital gains of ₹4.5 lakhs from equity mutual fund units sold in November 2025, (c) dividend income of ₹2.2 lakhs from domestic companies, and (d) rental income of ₹9.6 lakhs from a commercial property mortgaged to a bank (outstanding loan ₹40 lakhs at 9% p.a.). Compute the total tax liability for AY 2026-27 under the default new regime, showing: applicable surtax, set-off of losses if any, rebate eligibility, and the net payable after TDS already deducted of ₹85,000.

title: Regarding anti-dumping duty on Sodium Nitrite
text:
2.         The anti-dumping duty imposed under this notification shall be effective for a period of five years (unless revoked,
superseded or amended earlier) form the date of publication of this notification in the Gazette of India.

Explanation. - For the purposes of this notification, "landed value" means the assessable value as determined under the
Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962) and includes all duties of customs except duties levied under sections 3, 3A, 8B, 9 and 9A of
the said Customs Tariff Act.

[F.No.354/24/2002-TRU}

(Sonal Bajaj)

Under Secretary to the Government of India.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under an inter-state transaction where the place of supply shifts mid-transit due to a sale-in-transit arrangement under Section 10(1)(b) of the IGST Act. The original consignee endorses the document of title to a third party registered in a different state before the goods cross the state border. Determine: (a) who is liable to pay IGST — the original supplier, the original consignee, or the endorsee; (b) whether the original supplier can claim Input Tax Credit on the IGST paid on inputs used for this supply; and (c) how E-Way Bill obligations cascade across all three parties under Rule 138 of the CGST Rules, 2017, specifically when the endorsement occurs at a checkpost where the E-Way Bill has already been generated by the original supplier.

A closely held private limited company receives a fresh equity infusion from a foreign venture capital investor at a share premium that is 4.2x the fair market value computed under Rule 11UA(2) of the Income Tax Rules (DCF method). The company simultaneously converts outstanding compulsorily convertible debentures (CCDs) held by a resident HUF into equity at face value. Analyze: (a) applicability and quantum of Section 56(2)(viib) deemed income on the premium received from the foreign VC given the exemption under proviso (ii) and DPIIT notification conditions; (b) whether the CCD-to-equity conversion at face value triggers Section 56(2)(x) in the hands of the HUF if the shares' FMV on conversion date exceeds the CCD acquisition cost; and (c) transfer pricing implications under Section 92B if the foreign VC is an associated enterprise and the share premium departs materially from arm's length price under the CUP method.

title: Intimation to Liquidator for recovery of amount
text:
Bond for provisional assessment

[Rule 98(3) & 98(4)]

I/We.................of....................,hereinafter called "obligor(s)", am/are held and firmly bound to the President of 
India (hereinafter called "the President"/ the Governor of …………(State) (hereinafter called the ―Governor‖) in 
the sum of......................rupees to be paid to the President/ Governor for which payment will and truly to be 
made. I/We jointly and severally bind myself/ourselves and my/our respective heirs/ executors/ administrators/ 
legal representatives/successors and assigns by these presents; Dated this...................day of....................; 
 
WHEREAS final assessment of Integrated tax/ central tax/ State tax / Union territory taxon 
.................................. (name of goods/services or both-HSN:___________) supplied by the above bounded 
obligor from time to time could not be made for want of full information with regard to the value or rate of tax 
applicable thereto; 
and whereas the obligor desires that the provisional assessment in accordance with the provisions ofSection 60 
be made;

AND WHEREAS the Commissioner has required the obligor to furnish bank guarantee for an amount of 
............................................ rupees endorsed in favour of the President/ Governor and whereas the obligor has 
furnished such guarantee by depositing with the Commissioner the bank guarantee as aforementioned;  
The condition of this bond is that the obligor and his representative observe all the provisions of the Act in

And if all dues of Integrated tax/ Central tax/ State tax/ Union territory tax or other lawful charges, which shall

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a related party (wholly-owned subsidiary) in Karnataka at a price below cost. The recipient uses these goods exclusively for making exempt supplies. How should the transaction value be determined under Section 15 of the CGST Act, and what are the cascading implications for input tax credit reversal under Rule 42 of the CGST Rules at both the supplier and recipient ends? Cite the relevant valuation rules and any AAAR/AAR rulings that have addressed below-cost transfers between related parties.

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a US parent company under a long-term agreement that includes a post-importation royalty payable as 3% of net sales of goods manufactured using that machinery, plus a separate lump-sum technical know-how fee paid before shipment. The customs officer proposes to add both payments to transaction value under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007. The importer argues the royalty is not a condition of sale and relates to a post-importation activity. Analyze the legal test for 'condition of sale' under the CVR, the Supreme Court's position in Ferodo India and Commissioner of Customs v. Matsushita, and whether the lump-sum know-how fee is independently includible under Rule 10(1)(b). State the correct transaction value and any applicable safe-harbour or pre-import determination mechanism.

title: Modvat Credit - Dealers need to have proper business premises and actual business of goods for which registration is sought
text:
Circular No. 69/69/94-CX

Dated 24/10/94

F.No. 267/92/94-CX-8

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Central Board of Excise and Customs, New Delhi

Subject : Fraudulent availment of Modvat credit-preventive measures regarding

It has been brought to the notice of the Board that there is a tendency on the part of some dealers to obtain Central Excise
registration, merely for the sake of using their invoice for transfer of Modvat credit, even though the dealers do not have proper
business premises or without any arrangemnet for receipt, storage and onward dispatch of excisable goods.

2.    In this regard attention i sinvited to the provisions of Rule 57 GG which provides for registration under rule 174. A
registration certificate issued under Rule 174 is valid only for the premises specified in such certificates; therefore, registration
can not be granted to person who does not own proper premises for conducting his business.

3.    The Board therefore directs that the Central Excise Range Officers/Staff Audit and the Preventive parties shall specially
check that the registered dealers who have proper business premises and arrangement for receipt, storage and onward
despatch of excisable goods.

4.    The transport documents etc. relevant for modvat credit should be shown in the records of such dealers, both while they
receive the goods and also while they pass on the goods down  the line to others who will in due course, claim Modvat credit. It
should be ensured that dealers registered with the Central Excise Department for the purpose of passing on the Modvat credit
actually deal in the excisable goods and are not merely namelenders or, are not fictious entities with only a postal address. 
However, such checks should be carried out on a selective basis, wherever deemed necessary, with the written permission of
the jurisdictional Assistant Collector.

Sd/-

(I.P.Lal)

Deputy Secretary to the Governement of India

questions.question: A registered taxpayer under GST has an export-oriented unit (EOU) that claims refund of accumulated ITC under Section 54(3) on account of inverted duty structure. During the audit, the department contends that the taxpayer's product falls under the negative list of Rule 89(5) of the CGST Rules, thereby disqualifying the refund claim. The taxpayer argues that the restriction under the proviso to Section 54(3) applies only to goods notified by the government, and the product in question was not so notified at the time of supply. Simultaneously, a portion of the ITC was availed on capital goods used commonly for both taxable and exempt supplies. Compute the maximum admissible refund, specifying: (a) the correct formula under Rule 89(5) and whether 'Net ITC' includes capital goods ITC, (b) the treatment of common credit reversal under Rule 42/43 before computing the refund base, and (c) the legal position on whether the department's restriction is sustainable given the absence of a specific notification.

An importer brings in goods from a related-party foreign supplier under a long-term agreement that includes a royalty clause: 5% of net sales payable to the supplier post-importation. The declared transaction value is accepted by the importer as the customs value. Post-importation, the importer also receives a retrospective price reduction (credit note) covering shipments from the prior financial year, which the supplier issues as a commercial adjustment unrelated to quality defects. The department seeks to: (i) add the royalty to the transaction value under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation Rules, 2007, arguing the royalty is a condition of sale; and (ii) reject the retrospective price reduction and re-assess the earlier Bills of Entry at original invoice prices. Analyze: (a) the legal test for whether a royalty is a 'condition of sale' under Rule 10(1)(c) and cite the key judicial precedents; (b) the admissibility of retrospective price reductions in customs valuation and the procedural mechanism available to the importer; and (c) whether the related-party relationship independently triggers a valuation re-examination under Rule 3(3) and what burden of proof applies.

title: Prescribing procedure for sale of movable/immovable properties by Central Excise Officers for recovery of Central Excise dues

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under an inter-state B2B transaction and issues a tax invoice with IGST at 18%. The recipient, located in a different state, partially uses the goods for exempt supplies (40%) and taxable supplies (60%). The recipient later finds that the original supplier had an incorrect GSTIN on the invoice due to a clerical error, which was discovered only after the annual return filing deadline. Analyze the eligibility of ITC reversal under Rule 42, the mechanism for rectification of the invoice under Section 34, the impact on GSTR-3B and GSTR-9, and whether the recipient faces any interest liability under Section 50 for the period during which the incorrect credit was availed.
questions.expected_complexity_reason: Multi-layered problem requiring simultaneous application of apportionment rules, rectification procedure, annual return reconciliation, and interest liability across multiple statutory provisions

questions.question: An EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods under a bond without payment of customs duty and IGST. Midway through the 5-year export obligation period, it receives a large domestic order and wishes to convert 30% of its production to DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) sales. Determine: (i) the proportional customs duty and IGST recovery formula applicable on the capital goods under the Foreign Trade Policy read with Customs Notification, (ii) how the domestically-cleared goods will be valued for GST purposes under Section 15 versus the transaction value declared in the bond, (iii) the treatment of accumulated ITC on inputs used in both export and DTA supply under Rule 89(4) for refund eligibility, and (iv) whether the unit must re-register under a different GST category or if an amendment to the existing registration suffices.
questions.expected_complexity_reason: Cross-statute problem spanning Customs Act, Foreign Trade Policy, and CGST Act requiring sequential resolution of duty recovery, valuation, refund eligibility, and registration implications

title: Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
text:
(b) an agriculturist, to the extent of supply of produce out of cultivation of land.

(2) The Government may, on the recommendations of the Council, by notification, specify the category of persons who may be
exempted from obtaining registration under this Act.

* Enforced w.e.f. 22nd June, 2017.

* Section 24. Compulsory registration in certain cases.-

Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (1) of section 22 , the following categories of persons shall be required to be
registered under this Act,-

(i) persons making any inter-State taxable supply;

(ii) casual taxable persons making taxable supply;

(iii) persons who are required to pay tax under reverse charge;

(iv) person who are required to pay tax under sub-section (5) of section 9 ;

(v) non-resident taxable persons making taxable supply;

(vi) persons who are required to deduct tax under section 51 , whether or not separately registered under this Act;

(vii) persons who make taxable supply of goods or services or both on behalf of other taxable persons whether as an agent or
otherwise;

(viii) Input Service Distributor, whether or not separately registered under this Act;

(ix) persons who supply goods or services or both, other than supplies specified under sub-section (5) of section 9 , through such
electronic commerce operator who is required to collect tax at source under section 52 ;

(x) every electronic commerce operator 1 [who is required to collect tax at source under section 52 ;]

(xi) every person supplying online information and database access or retrieval services from a place outside India to a person
in India, other than a registered person; and

(xii) such other person or class of persons as may be notified by the Government on the recommendations of the Council.

* Enforced w.e.f. 22nd June, 2017.

1. Inserted by s. 12 of The Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018 (No. 31 of 2018) - Brought into force w.e.f. 01st February, 2019.

* Section 25. Procedure for registration.-

(1) Every person who is liable to be registered under section 22 or section 24 shall apply for registration in every such State or
Union territory in which he is so liable within thirty days from the date on which he becomes liable to registration, in such manner
and subject to such conditions as may be prescribed:

Provided that a casual taxable person or a non-resident taxable person shall apply for registration at least five days prior to the
commencement of business:

1 [ Provided further that a person having a unit, as defined in the Special Economic Zones Act, 2005 (28 of 2005), in a Special
Economic Zone or being a Special Economic Zone developer shall have to apply for a separate registration, as distinct from his
place of business located outside the Special Economic Zone in the same State or Union territory.]

Explanation .-Every person who makes a supply from the territorial waters of India shall obtain registration in the coastal State
or Union territory where the nearest point of the appropriate baseline is located.

(2) A person seeking registration under this Act shall be granted a single registration in a State or Union territory:

2 [ Provided that a person having multiple places of business in a State or Union territory may be granted a separate
registration for each such place of business, subject to such conditions as may be prescribed.]

(3) A person, though not liable to be registered under section 22 or section 24 may get himself registered voluntarily, and all

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) and claims IGST refund on zero-rated supply. The ICEGATE system rejects the refund application citing mismatch between the shipping bill FOB value (INR 48,32,000) and the GSTR-1 Table 6A export invoice value (INR 48,27,500). The exporter argues the difference is attributable to port handling charges deducted by the freight forwarder post-invoice. Under Section 16(3)(b) of IGST Act read with Rule 96 of CGST Rules, does this value discrepancy constitute a fatal defect requiring amendment of the shipping bill, or can the refund be processed on the lower of the two values? Cite the relevant circular and judicial precedent.

An Indian subsidiary (wholly owned by a US parent) provides back-office IT services to group entities in 14 countries under a cost-plus 12% arrangement. During TP assessment for AY 2023-24, the TPO proposes to treat the subsidiary as a 'captive service provider' and benchmarks using TNMM with PLI = operating profit/total cost. The assessee contends that because 3 of the 14 group entities are loss-making, the comparables selected by the TPO (all profit-making independent IT companies) create an asymmetric comparison and the arm's length range should be recomputed excluding those entities from the denominator. Under Rule 10B(1)(e) and the guidance in the Report on Actions 8-10 of BEPS, is the assessee's aggregation/exclusion argument tenable, and what documentation under Rule 10D must accompany the revised computation to withstand scrutiny at the DRP stage?

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a distributor under a principal-to-principal arrangement. Mid-year, they restructure to a del-credere agent model where the agent guarantees payment. The agent charges a 2% guarantee fee and also earns a 3% commission on sales. Simultaneously, the manufacturer runs a post-supply volume discount scheme payable at year-end via credit notes. Under GST, (a) classify the guarantee fee and commission — are they composite or mixed supply, and at what rate? (b) determine whether the year-end credit notes can reduce the manufacturer's output tax liability under Section 34, considering whether the agent's ITC reversal obligation under Rule 37A is triggered, and (c) assess the place of supply for the guarantee fee if the del-credere agent is in a different state than the manufacturer.

An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15%. During the AY, the subsidiary also sub-contracts 40% of the work to an unrelated Indian vendor and passes through those costs without markup in its cost base. The TPO argues the passthrough costs must be excluded from the cost base for markup computation, citing Explanation to Rule 10B(1)(b), reducing the arm's length price significantly. Simultaneously, the subsidiary received an interest-free loan from the parent which the AO seeks to impute interest on under Section 92. Analyze: (a) the legal merit of the TPO's passthrough cost exclusion, citing relevant tribunal rulings; (b) whether imputing interest on the interest-free loan is sustainable when the transaction is already covered under APA negotiations; and (c) the interaction between secondary adjustment provisions under Section 92CE and the dividend distribution tax abolition post-Finance Act 2020.

title: This relates to Customs valuation (Determination of Price of Imported Goods) Rules, 1988
text:
Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007

Notification No. 94/2007 - Customs (N.T.)

1. Short title, commencement and application.-

(1)These rules may be called the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007.

(2) They shall come into force on the 10 th day of October, 2007.

(3) They shall apply to imported goods.

2. Definitions.-

(1) In these rules, unless the context otherwise requires, -

(a)        "computed value" means the value of imported goods determined in accordance with rule 8.

(b)   "deductive value" means the value determined in accordance with rule 7.

(c) "goods of the same class or kind", means imported goods that are within    a group or range of imported goods produced
by a particular industry or industrial sector and includes identical goods or similar goods;

(d)    "identical goods" means imported goods -

(i) which are same in all respects, including physical characteristics, quality and reputation as the goods being valued except
for minor differences in appearance that do not affect the value of the goods;

(ii) produced in the country in which the goods being valued were produced; and

(iii)    produced by the same person who produced the goods, or where no such goods are available, goods produced by a
different person,

but shall not include imported goods where engineering, development work, art work, design work, plan or sketch undertaken in
India were completed directly or indirectly by the buyer on these imported goods free of charge or at a reduced cost for use in
connection with the production and sale for export of these imported goods;

(e)     "produced" includes grown, manufactured and mined

(f)  "similar goods" means imported goods -

(i) which although not alike in all respects, have like characteristics and like component materials which enable them to perform
the same functions and to be commercially interchangeable with the goods being valued having regard to the quality, reputation
and the existence of trade mark;

(ii) produced in the country in which the goods being valued were produced; and

(iii) produced by the same person who produced the goods being valued, or where no such goods are available, goods
produced by a different person,

but shall not include imported goods where engineering, development work, art work, design work, plan or sketch undertaken in
India were completed directly or indirectly by the buyer on these imported goods free of charge or at a reduced cost for use in
connection with the production and sale for export of these imported goods;

(g) "transaction value" means the value referred to in sub-section (1) of section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962;

(2)    For the purpose of these rules, persons shall be deemed to be "related" only if -

(i)     they are officers or directors of one another"s businesses;

(ii)    they are legally recognised partners in business;

(iii)    they are employer and employee;

(iv) any person directly or indirectly owns, controls or holds five per cent or more of the outstanding voting stock or shares of
both of them;

(v)    one of them directly or indirectly controls the other;

(vi)                   both of them are directly or indirectly controlled by a third person;

questions.question: A holding company in India (registered under GST in Maharashtra) provides corporate guarantee to its wholly-owned subsidiary (registered in Karnataka) for a term loan taken from a foreign bank. The subsidiary uses the loan proceeds to import capital goods under EPCG scheme. Analyze: (1) whether the corporate guarantee constitutes a 'supply of service' under Schedule I of CGST Act after the 2023 amendment, (2) the applicable valuation rule under Rule 28(2) of CGST Rules and how the 1% deemed value is computed on the outstanding loan amount, (3) the place of supply determination under Section 12(2)(b) for B2B guarantee services, (4) whether input tax credit on GST paid on the guarantee fee is available to the subsidiary given the EPCG capital goods restriction under Rule 6 of CCR 2017, and (5) the interaction between the deemed export benefit on capital goods and the blocked credit provisions under Section 17(5)(h).
expected_expertise: GST Schedule I related party transactions

questions.question: An Indian exporter (EOU unit in SEEPZ) sells software services to its US parent at INR 45 lakh per month under an intercompany agreement. The TPO proposes an arm's length price of INR 72 lakh using TNMM with a net cost plus markup of 18% versus the assessee's 12%. Simultaneously, the RBI AD Bank flags a potential FEMA violation because the assessee received advance payment exceeding the 12-month export realization period under FED Master Direction on Export of Goods and Services. Analyze: (1) the correct functional and comparability analysis for software EOU entities under TNMM versus CUP method hierarchy in Rule 10B, (2) whether the TPO's use of ROCE as a PLI is appropriate given EOU cost-structure and the impact of duty-free imports on the cost base, (3) how the MAP (Mutual Agreement Procedure) under the India-US DTAA Article 27 interacts with a pending DRP objection and whether the limitation period is tolled, (4) the specific FEMA compounding procedure under Section 15 of FEMA 1999 for the advance payment violation including the compounding authority jurisdiction above USD 10 million, and (5) whether the TP adjustment itself creates a deemed income under Section 92CA that must be repatriated under FEMA or whether it remains a notional adjustment.
expected_expertise: Section 92CA TP adjustment vs FEMA repatriation

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka under a works contract where 60% of the contract value is attributable to goods and 40% to services. The manufacturer also claims ITC on capital goods used exclusively for this works contract. During audit, the department disallows ITC under Section 17(5)(c) arguing the supply is 'works contract services.' The assessee contends that since the goods component dominates, the principal supply doctrine under Section 8 of CGST Act applies and the supply should be classified as goods. Analyze the merits of both positions, the applicable HSN classification, place of supply rules under Section 12(3) vs 12(14), and the consequential ITC eligibility under Section 16 read with Rule 36.
questions.expected_reasoning_depth: Multi-section statutory analysis, landmark rulings (Kone Elevator, Larsen & Toubro), composite supply vs mixed supply distinction, interstate vs intrastate implications, IGST credit chain

questions.question: An Indian subsidiary imports raw materials from its Singapore parent at a declared transaction value. Customs authorities reject the declared value invoking Rule 4 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, applying computed value under Rule 8 based on comparable uncontrolled prices. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer issues an order under Section 92CA of the Income Tax Act accepting the same transaction price as arm's length, creating a direct conflict between the two authorities on the same transaction. The assessee seeks to use the TP order to defend customs valuation. Discuss: (a) whether a TP order is binding on Customs, (b) the interplay between SVB (Special Valuation Branch) proceedings and TP assessment, (c) the applicability of WTO Customs Valuation Agreement Article 1 vs domestic override provisions, and (d) available appellate remedies and the risk of double taxation/double adjustment.
questions.expected_reasoning_depth: SVB circular analysis, CBIC vs CBDT jurisdictional conflict, WTO Agreement on Customs Valuation Articles 1-8, Section 92C/92CA TP provisions, relevant tribunal decisions (Samsung, Sony Ericsson), advance pricing agreement interplay

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two distinct units registered under the same GSTIN in Maharashtra — Unit A (engaged in taxable supply of machinery) and Unit B (engaged in exempt supply of agricultural equipment). During FY 2023-24, Unit A procures inputs worth ₹50 lakhs (GST paid ₹9 lakhs) that are used 60% for Unit A's taxable output and 40% for common overhead shared with Unit B. Unit B also directly procures raw materials worth ₹20 lakhs (GST ₹3.6 lakhs) exclusively for its exempt supplies. Under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of CGST Rules, compute the exact ITC reversal amount, specify the treatment in GSTR-3B, and explain how the Annual ITC reconciliation in GSTR-9C must reflect these reversals — including whether any prior-period short-reversal carries a 24% per annum interest liability and from which date it accrues.

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign supplier (holding 30% equity in the Indian entity) at a declared transaction value of USD 100/unit. The customs officer invokes Rule 4 rejection citing 'influence of relationship on price', and proposes valuation under Rule 5 (identical goods) using a contemporaneous import by an unrelated buyer at USD 130/unit — but that import had different Incoterms (CIF vs. the present FOB) and a 15% volume discount for a 10,000-unit order versus the current 500-unit import. Simultaneously, the importer holds a pre-import Advance Ruling (AAR) confirming acceptability of transaction value under Rule 3(3)(a) based on 'test values'. Detail the precise legal challenge strategy under Section 128 of the Customs Act, the burden-of-proof allocation between importer and department post-Eicher Motors precedent, the required adjustments under Rule 10 before applying Rule 5, and the interplay between the binding AAR and the officer's rejection authority.

questions.question: A foreign company incorporated in the UAE provides cloud-based SaaS services to Indian businesses. The Indian recipient is registered under GST. The foreign company has no PE in India, but routes billing through a related Indian entity that holds a GST registration. Under the IGST Act 2017, determine: (a) whether the supply qualifies as 'import of services' or 'intermediary services', (b) which entity bears the GST liability under reverse charge mechanism, (c) how the valuation rules under Rule 28 of CGST Rules apply given the related-party transaction, and (d) whether the Indian billing entity can claim full ITC on the RCM liability, citing the relevant circular clarifications and any Advance Authority for Advance Rulings precedents.

An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides back-office KPO services exclusively to its parent. The AO proposes to re-characterize the entity from a 'captive service provider' (cost-plus 15%) to a 'risk-bearing entrepreneur' based on functional analysis showing the Indian entity bears significant market risk post-2022. The taxpayer contests, citing OECD BEPS Action 8-10 guidance on accurate delineation of transactions. Analyze: (a) the threshold at which functional analysis overrides contractual allocation of risk under Indian TP Regulations, (b) how Sections 92C and 92CA interact when the Transfer Pricing Officer disagrees with the MAP position already agreed between India and the US under the India-US DTAA Article 27, (c) the admissibility of secret comparables used by the AO versus publicly available TNMM comparables submitted by the assessee, and (d) whether invoking the Secondary Adjustment under Section 92CE is mandatory if the primary adjustment exceeds INR 1 crore.

title: Modvat / Cenvat credit - cash refund of unutilized credit on exports
text:
Circular No. 83 /2000-Cus

F.NO. 609/116/2000-DBK

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

New Delhi, 16 th October, 2000.

Subject : Modvat / Cenvat credit - cash refund of unutilized credit on exports

In terms of the Customs & Central Excise Duties Drawback Rules, 1995, drawback is allowed to the exporters for the duties of
Customs and Central Excise suffered on the imported or indigenous inputs used in the manufacture of the export product for
which no relief is otherwise available. Accordingly, in the All Industry Rates. Drawback Table, notified by the Central
Government annually, the drawback rates for various export products are also indicated with their customs and Central Excise
allocations. The Customs allocation denotes the Basic Custom Duty, Surcharge on Basic Customs Duty and the Special
Additional Duty paid, if any. The Central Excise allocation represents the Additional Customs Duty leviable in terms of Section
3 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 or the Central Excise duty leviable in terms of Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985. It has been
specifically provided in various Notifications notifying the Annual Drawback Table that the Central Excise allocation of the
drawback would be allowed only if the exporters do not avail of the MODVAT / CENVAT Scheme.

2.        A representation has been received from the Vidarbha Industries Association, Nagpur, that its members who are
manufacturing and exporting Woven Sacks and Bags falling under SS No. 39.251 of the Drawback Table and claim only the
Customs allocation of Drawback rate are not being allowed cash refund of the unutilised CENVAT credit. This is on the
Premise that since the Drawback has been availed, the cash refund of the unutilised CENVAT credit is prohibited in terms of
Rule 57F(14) of the central Excise Rules, 1944.

3.        The issue has been examined in the Board. It is observed that Rule 57A(1) of the Central Excise Rules, 1944 specifically
provides that the Central Government by notification may specify the finished excisable goods for the purpose of allowing
Credit of any of excise or the additional duty under Section 3 of Customs Act, 1975, i.e., the specified duty paid on the goods
used in the manufacture of such final products. Rule 57F(13) expounds that where any inputs are used in the final products
which are cleared for exports under bond or used in the intermediate products cleared for exports in accordance with rule
57F(14), the credit of specified duty in respect of the inputs so used shall be allowed to be utilised by the manufacturer towards
payment of duty of Excise or any final products cleared for home consumption or for exports on payment of duty any where for
any reason such adjustment is not possible, the manufacture shall be allowed refund of such amount subject to such safeguard,
conditions anf limitations as may be specified by the Central Government. Rule 57F(14) lays down that no refund of credit
mentioned in Rule 57F(13) shall be allowed if the manufacturer avails of drawback allowed under the Customs and Central
Excise Duties Drawback Rules, 1995 or claims a rebate of duty under Rule 12, in respect of such duty.

4.        A combined and harmonious  reading of these provisions reveals that the word "duty" appearing any where in the
MODVAT rules, unless otherwise qualified should always be construed as having reference to duty of Central Excise or the
additional duty under section 3 of Customs Tariff Act, 1975. Since Rule 57F(13) mentions the wording "Credit of specified duty
in respect of inputs so used ------" and Rule 57F(14) states that no credit in sub-rule (13) shall be allowed in the exporters avail
of drawback- in respect of such duty, it is amply clear that the prohibition of Rule 57F(14) for grant of refund is only in respect of
availment of drawback as regards the Central Excise duty or Countervailing duty. There is no double benefit available to the
manufacturer where only Customs portion of All Industry Rate of Drawback is claimed, if refund of unutilised credit is given, as
no MODVAT (now CENVAT) credit facility is permissible for Customs duty suffered on imported inputs. Denial of refund of
MODVAt credit of Excise/Countervailing duty paid on inputs relating to export products, if this cannot be used otherwise, will
thus not be only act harshly on the exporters, it will not be in accordance with the provisions of the MODVAT rules.

questions.question: Your Qdrant-based RAG system ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings at ~7 chunks/sec on a single Vulkan GPU. After deployment, you observe that retrieval recall drops sharply for queries involving cross-referenced sections (e.g., 'Section 16(4) read with Rule 36(4) of CGST'). Dense vector search alone returns only the directly-cited section, missing the referenced rule. What retrieval architecture changes would you implement to capture cross-reference relationships without re-ingesting the entire corpus, and how would you measure the improvement in a domain where ground-truth QA pairs are scarce?

You are running a 4-core CPU host with two RX 6700 XT GPUs shared across: (a) a Vulkan llama-server for the primary LLM (Qwen3-14B), (b) a BGE-M3 embedding server for RAG ingestion, and (c) an OCR workload using Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Under peak load, P95 query latency spikes from 8s to 45s. CPU uptime shows load average of 11.2. Describe a resource allocation and scheduling strategy—covering GPU VRAM partitioning, process priority, async queue depth, and request batching—that brings P95 latency below 12s without adding hardware or reducing model quality.

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives a show-cause notice under Section 74 of the CGST Act alleging suppression of facts for wrongful availment of ITC on capital goods used partly for exempt supplies. The company had reversed ITC under Rule 43 in the same tax period. Can the department invoke the extended limitation period of 5 years, and what is the evidentiary threshold required to establish 'suppression of facts' when the reversal itself appears on the GSTR-3B filed on time?

A closely-held company receives a loan from its holding company (>51% shareholding) which itself received the funds from a foreign subsidiary via ECB. The AO proposes to treat the loan as deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(e) of the Income Tax Act. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer initiates proceedings under Section 92C alleging the interest rate is below arm's length. Analyze whether the deemed dividend provision and transfer pricing adjustment can be applied simultaneously to the same transaction, and which takes precedence under the domestic law hierarchy.

title: Customs Act, 1962
text:
isions of this Act, pass such order as it thinks fit on the matters covered by the
application and any other matter relating to the case not covered by the application, but referred to in the report of the Commissioner of Customs or the
Commissioner (Investigation) under sub-section (1) or sub-section (6).

(8) Subject to the provisions of section 32A of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944), the materials brought on record before the Settlement Commission shall be
considered by the Members of the concerned Bench concerned before passing any order under sub-section (7) and, in relation to the passing of such order, the
provisions of section 32D of the Central Excise Act, 1944 shall apply.

(9) Every order passed under sub-section (7) shall provide for the terms of settlement including any demand by way of duty, penalty or interest, the manner in which
any sum due under the settlement shall be paid and all other matters to make the settlement effective and shall also provide that the settlement shall be void if it is
subsequently found by the Settlement Commission that it has been obtained by fraud, or misrepresentation of facts.

(10) Where any duty payable in pursuance of an order under sub-section (7) is not paid by the applicant within thirty days of the receipt of a copy of the order by him,
then, whether or not the Settlement Commission has extended the time for payment of such duty or has allowed payment thereof by instalments, the applicant shall
be liable to pay simple interest at the rate of eighteen per cent per annum or at such other rate as notified by the Board on the amount remaining unpaid from the date
of expiry of the period of thirty days aforesaid.

(11) Where a settlement becomes void as provided under sub-section (9), the proceedings with respect to the matters covered by the settlement shall be deemed to
have been revived from the stage at which the application was allowed to be proceeded with by the Settlement Commission and the proper officer may,
notwithstanding anything contained in any other provision of this Act, complete such proceedings at any time before the expiry of two years from the date of the receipt
of communication that the settlement became void."
