questions.question: Your production RAG system serves 108k chunks of Indian tax law. Under concurrent load (8+ simultaneous queries), p95 latency spikes from 4s to 28s. You have a 4-core CPU, two AMD RX 6700 XT GPUs (one running the LLM via Vulkan, one running the embedder), and a Qdrant vector store. Profiling shows the bottleneck shifts between GPU VRAM saturation, Qdrant search threads competing for CPU cores, and async FastAPI worker queue depth. Design a concrete remediation strategy that addresses all three bottlenecks without adding hardware, specifying: (a) the exact order of interventions and why, (b) which metrics you monitor at each step to confirm improvement before proceeding, and (c) the single change most likely to regress quality if implemented incorrectly.

You are ingesting a 471-PDF corpus where approximately 40% of PDFs are image-only scans (no extractable text layer). Your current pipeline achieves 5.2–9.6 chunks/second on text PDFs using BGE-M3 embeddings via Vulkan on a single GPU. For image-only PDFs, you must choose between: (A) a Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF multimodal model on llama-server Vulkan for OCR, (B) RapidOCR on CPU as fallback, or (C) a two-pass hybrid that attempts A and falls back to B on confidence threshold. Given that the same GPU running OCR cannot simultaneously serve live RAG queries, and your SLA requires zero query downtime during ingestion, design the scheduling and resource isolation strategy for each option. Include: the failure mode unique to each approach, the minimum viable monitoring signal to detect silent OCR quality degradation, and your final recommendation with justification.

title: Medical Devices Rules
text:
68  
     THE   GAZETTE   OF  INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
             [PART II—SEC. 3(i)]

3.18.1. ᮧᳰᮓया िविधमा᭠यकरण से व᭭तुिन᳧ सा᭯य ᳇ारा यह ᭭थािपत करना अिभᮧेत है ᳰक ऐसी ᳰकसी ᮧᳰᮓया से िनर᭠तर 
पᳯरणाम िनकलता है या उ᭜पाद उसके पूवᭅअवधाᳯरत िविनदᱷशᲂ को पूरा करता है ।

3.18.2. िडजाइन िविधमा᭠यकरण से व᭭तुिन᳧ सा᭯य ᳇ारा यह ᭭थािपत करना अिभᮧेत है ᳰक युिᲦ के िविनदᱷश उपयोᲦा कᳱ 
आव᭫यकताᲐ और आशियत उपयोग (उपयोगᲂ) के अनुᱨप हᱹ ।

3.19  स᭜यापन से परीᭃा और व᭭तुिन᳧ सा᭯य के उपबंध ᳇ारा यह पुि᳥ अिभᮧेत है ᳰक िविन᳸द᳥ अपेᭃाᲐ का पालन ᳰकया गया है ।

4  वािलटी ᮧबंधन ᮧणाली.-

4.1 साधारण :

िविनमाᭅता एक Ფािलटी ᮧबंधन ᮧणाली कᳱ ᭭थापना, उसका द᭭तावेजीकरण, कायाᭅ᭠वयन करेगा और उसे बनाए रखेगाऔर इस अनुसूची 
कᳱ अपेᭃाᲐ के अनुसार उसकᳱ ᮧभावकाᳯरता बनाए रखेगा ।

िविनमाᭅता –

(क) Ფािलटी ᮧबंधन ᮧणाली के िलए आव᭫यक ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ और संपूणᭅ संगठन मᱶ उनके उपयोजन कᳱ पहचान करेगा;

(ख) इन ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ के ᮓम और पार᭭पᳯरक ᳰᮓया को अवधाᳯरत करेगा;

(ग) ऐसे मापदंड और पितयां अवधाᳯरत करेगा जो यह सुिनि᳟त करने के िलए आव᭫यक हᱹ ᳰक इन ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ का ᮧचालन और

िनयंᮢण दोनᲂ ᮧभावी हᱹ;

(घ) इन ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ के ᮧचालन और मानीटᳳरग का समथᭅन करने के िलए आव᭫यक संसाधनᲂ और जानकारी कᳱ उपल᭣धता सुिनि᳟त

करेगा;

(ङ) इन ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ को मानीटर करेगा, उनका मापमान और िव᳣ेषण करेगा; और

(च) इन ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ के योजनाब पᳯरणामᲂ कᳱ ᮧाि᳙ और ᮧभावशीलता को बनाए रखने के िलए आव᭫यक कारᭅवाइयᲂ को

कायाᭅि᭠वत करेगा ।

इन ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ का ᮧबंधन िविनमाᭅता ᳇ारा इस अनुसूची कᳱ अपेᭃाᲐ के अनुसार ᳰकया जाएगा ।जहां कोई िविनमाᭅता ᳰकसी ᮧᳰᮓया को ऐसे 
ᳰकसी बाहरी ᮲ोत से कराने का िवक᭨प अपनाता है िजससे उ᭜पाद के अपेᭃाᲐ के अनुᱨप होने पर ᮧितकूल ᮧभाव पड़ता है तो िविनमाᭅता 
ऐसी ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ पर िनयंᮢण सुिनि᳟त करेगा । ऐसी बाहरी-᮲ोत वाली ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ के िनयंᮢण कᳱ पहचान Ფािलटी ᮧबंधन ᮧणाली के भीतर 
कᳱ जाएगी ।

ᳯट᭡पण— ऊपर िन᳸द᳥ Ფािलटी ᮧबंधन ᮧणाली के िलए आव᭫यक ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ मᱶ ᮧबंध ᳰᮓयाकलाप, संसाधनᲂ का उपबंध, उ᭜पाद आपन और 
मापमान कᳱ ᮧᳰᮓयाएं भी आती हᱹ ।

4.2 द᭭तावेज़ीकरण अपेᭃाएं :

4.2.1 साधारण :

Ფािलटी ᮧबंधन ᮧणाली द᭭तावेज़ीकरण मᱶ िन᳜िलिखत होगा –

(क) ᳰकसी Ფािलटी नीित और Ფािलटी उे᭫ यᲂ के द᭭तावेज़ी िववरण;

(ख) Ფािलटी मैनुअल;

(ग) इस अनुसूची ᳇ारा अपेिᭃत द᭭तावेज़ी ᮧᳰᮓयाएं;

(घ) िविनमाᭅता ᳇ारा उसकᳱ ᮧᳰᮓयाᲐ कᳱ ᮧभावी योजना, ᮧचालन और िनयंᮢण सुिनि᳟त करने के िलए आव᭫यक द᭭तावेज़;

(ङ) इस अनुसूची ᳇ारा अपेिᭃत अिभलेख; और

जहां इस अनुसूची मᱶ यह िविन᳸द᳥ होता है ᳰक ᳰकसी अपेᭃा, ᮧᳰᮓया, ᳰᮓयाकलाप या िवशेष ᳞व᭭था का ‘’द᭭तावेज़ीकरण’’ ᳰकया जाए,

वहां, इसके अितᳯरᲦ, इसे कायाᭅि᭠वत ᳰकया जाएगा और बनाए रखा  जाएगा ।

questions.question: A manufacturing company has three units: Unit A (registered in Maharashtra, supplies taxable goods), Unit B (registered in Gujarat, engaged exclusively in exempt supplies), and Unit C (registered in Rajasthan, providing works contract services for immovable property to a government entity). All three units are part of the same legal entity under a single PAN. During FY 2024-25, Unit A imported capital goods from Germany under EPCG license, Unit B purchased inputs from Unit A under inter-unit stock transfer, and Unit C received advance payment from the government entity before execution of the contract. Determine: (i) the GST liability, ITC eligibility, and reversal obligations for each unit, (ii) whether cross-utilization of ITC between units is permissible, (iii) the place of supply for the advance received by Unit C, and (iv) the impact on Unit A's EPCG obligation fulfillment if goods supplied to Unit B are treated as exempt supplies.

An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent (USCo) under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using TNMM. During AY 2024-25: (a) IndCo also licenses self-developed IP to a Singapore affiliate (SingCo) at a royalty rate of 8%, while a contemporaneous third-party comparable charges 12%; (b) IndCo receives a corporate guarantee from USCo for a rupee-denominated ECB loan from an Indian bank—no guarantee fee is charged; (c) IndCo's employees are seconded to USCo for 6 months and their salary is recharged at cost with no markup; (d) IndCo books an expenditure of ₹15 crore as 'management fees' paid to USCo without a specific service agreement or evidence of service receipt. Analyze: (i) transfer pricing adjustments the AO is likely to make for each transaction, (ii) applicability of secondary adjustment and repatriation requirement under Section 92CE, (iii) whether the secondment constitutes a Service PE for USCo in India under the India-US DTAA, and (iv) deductibility of management fees under Section 37(1) read with Section 40A(2).

questions.question: Your CBIC RAG system ingests 108k chunks from Indian tax law PDFs using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single GPU with Vulkan backend at 5.2–9.6 chunks/second. You now need to add Income Tax and MCA corpora (estimated 200k+ additional chunks) without disrupting the live :9500 service. The rig has 4 CPU cores, shared GPU memory across 6 cards, and the ingestion worker runs at nice +19/ionice idle. Design a zero-downtime ingestion strategy that: (a) avoids OOM on the shared GPU, (b) maintains query latency SLAs during ingestion, and (c) supports atomic corpus switchover so a failed new-corpus ingest does not corrupt the existing Qdrant collection. What collection naming convention, Qdrant alias strategy, and resource scheduling approach would you use?

On a 4-core AMD GPU rig running llama-server with Vulkan backend (not ROCm), you observe that query-to-first-token latency for Qwen3-14B spikes from 800ms to 6–8 seconds under concurrent load of 3+ simultaneous RAG queries. The FastAPI app uses async endpoints, the LLM is called with /no_think mode, and uptime shows load average hovering at 3.8. Systematically diagnose whether the bottleneck is (a) CPU-bound context prefill on Vulkan, (b) Qdrant vector search under concurrent reads, (c) Python asyncio thread starvation from blocking llama-server HTTP calls, or (d) memory bandwidth saturation from multi-GPU VRAM splits. For each hypothesis, specify the exact metric to measure, the tool to collect it on a minimal Linux install, and the remediation if confirmed.

title: Medical Devices Rules
text:
¹Hkkx IIµ[k.M 3(i)º 
Hkkjr dk jkti=k % vlk/kj.k 
183

(ii) Evidence  of  the  ongoing  revalidation  of  the  process  should  also  be provided.   Typically this would 
consist of arrangements for, or evidence of, revalidation of the packaging and sterilization processes. 
7.6 Software verification and validation: 
The dossier should contain information on the software design and development process and evidence of the 
validation of the software, as used in the finished device.  This information should typically include the summary 
results of all verification, validation and testing performed both in-house and in a simulated or actual user 
environment prior to final release. It should also address all of the different hardware configurations and, where 
applicable, operating systems identified in the labelling. 
7.7  Animal studies: 
(i) Where studies in an animal model have been undertaken to provide evidence of conformity with the Essential 
Principles related to functional safety and performance, detailed information should be contained in the dossier. 
(ii) The dossier should describe the study objectives, methodology, results, analysis and conclusions and document 
conformity with Good Laboratory Practices. The rationale (and limitations) of selecting the particular animal 
model should be discussed. 
7.8 Stability data: 
If available, real-time aging data shall be submitted to support the claimed shelf life. However, if real-time data is 
not available, accelerated stability data shall be submitted to support the claimed shelf life. Such a provisional 
claimed shelf life may be approved provided that the manufacturer immediately initiates real-time stability 
testing to validate the proposed shelf life. After completion of the real time stability analysis, real-time stability 
data shall be submitted in support of the claimed shelf life. 
7.9 Clinical evidence: 
The dossier should contain the clinical evidence that demonstrates conformity of the device with the Essential 
Principles that apply to it. It needs to address the elements contained in the Clinical Investigation, as specified 
under the Seventh Schedule.  If   a predicate device is available, the manufacturer needs to submit the substantial 
equivalence evaluation along with relevant published literature in accordance with these rules. 
7.10  Post Marketing Surveillance data (Vigilance reporting): 
The dossier should contain the Post Marketing Surveillance or Vigilance Reporting procedures and data collected 
by the manufacturer encompassing the details of the complaints received and corrective and Preventive actions 
taken for the same. 
NOTE: 
1. All reports submitted as a part of the dossier should be signed and dated by the responsible person. 
2. Batch Release Certificates and Certificate of Analysis of finished product for minimum 3 consecutive batches 
should be submitted. 
3. All certificates submitted must be within the validity period. 
4. Any information which is not relevant for the subject device may be stated as ‘Not Applicable’ in the relevant 
Sections/Columns of the above format, and reasons for non-applicability should be provided. 
 
Appendix III 
DEVICE MASTER FILE FOR IN VITRO DIAGNOSTIC MEDICAL DEVICES 
 
1.   EXECUTIVE SUMMARY:  
An executive summary shall be provided by the manufacturer and shall contain: 
1.1 Introductory descriptive information on the in vitro diagnostic medical device, the intended use and risk Class of 
in vitro diagnostic medical device, novel features (if any), claimed shelf life and a synopsis on the content of the 
dossier. 
1.2 Regulatory status of the similar device in India (approved or new in vitro diagnostic medical device). 
1.3 Domestic price of the in vitro diagnostic medical device in the currency followed in the country of origin. 
1.4 Marketing history of the in vitro diagnostic medical device from the date of introducing the in vitro diagnostic 
medical device in the market. 
1.5 List of regulatory approvals or marketing clearance obtained in below format (submit respective copy of 
approval certificate).

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a Bill of Entry dated March 28th but the IGST payment is made on April 3rd of the next financial year due to a banking holiday. The company claims ITC in April. The department argues ITC should have been claimed in March under Section 16(4) of CGST Act, and issues a demand with interest under Section 50. Simultaneously, the company has pending TRAN-1 credit that was rejected due to a technical glitch on the GST portal, for which a writ petition is pending in High Court. Analyze the legal position on: (a) the correct tax period for ITC availment under Section 16(2)(b) vs 16(4), (b) applicability of interest under Section 50(3) vs 50(1), and (c) whether the pendency of the writ petition creates any estoppel against the department's demand.
questions.expected_depth: Multi-provision cross-referencing with CGST Act Sections 16, 49, 50; IGST Act Section 20; relevant circulars on Bill of Entry ITC; High Court writ jurisdiction under Article 226 vs statutory appeal hierarchy under Section 107/112/117 CGST Act
questions.practitioner_type: Senior Tax Counsel / GST Litigation Specialist

questions.question: An EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods under Notification 52/2003-Customs with full duty exemption. After 8 years of operation, the unit surrenders its EOU status and applies for debonding. Customs demands proportionate duty on the remaining useful life of capital goods. Simultaneously, GST Council has clarified that such goods imported pre-GST are not liable to IGST on debonding, but the Customs department raises a demand under Section 28 for the differential CVD that would have been payable under the pre-GST regime, arguing unjust enrichment. The company disputes this citing: (i) limitation under Section 28(1) proviso, (ii) the goods are now fully depreciated in the company's books, and (iii) the Ministry of Commerce's rehabilitation package for debonded EOUs. Evaluate the legal merits of each defense, the correct valuation methodology for depreciated imported capital goods under Customs Valuation Rules 2007, and whether the unjust enrichment doctrine under Section 27 can be invoked by the department offensively rather than defensively.
questions.expected_depth: Cross-referencing Customs Act 1962 Sections 25, 27, 28; EOU Scheme under FTP 2015-2020/2023; Customs Valuation Rules 2007 Rule 3-9; CBEC/CBIC circulars on EOU debonding; Supreme Court jurisprudence on unjust enrichment in indirect tax (Mafatlal Industries lineage)
questions.practitioner_type: Customs & Foreign Trade Policy Specialist / FEMA Counsel

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a job work arrangement and later supplies some of these goods directly to the principal's customer on the principal's instruction. The job worker's factory is in a different state than the principal. The goods were originally sent under a delivery challan. Analyze the GST implications: (1) Is this supply by the job worker or the principal? (2) What documents are required? (3) How does Section 143 of CGST Act interact with this scenario, and does the six-month return period reset? (4) What are the ITC implications for the principal if the job worker is unregistered?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A holding company in India provides management support services, IT infrastructure, and brand licensing to its wholly-owned subsidiary. The AO proposes to treat the entire arrangement as a colourable device to shift profits, disallowing the subsidiary's deductions under Section 37(1) and simultaneously the GST department issues a notice claiming the 'brand licensing' is a disguised dividend repatriation and hence not a taxable supply but a capital receipt on which GST was wrongly collected. (1) Can both positions coexist legally? (2) What transfer pricing documentation under Section 92C defends the subsidiary? (3) If the GST demand succeeds, what happens to the ITC already claimed by the subsidiary? (4) Cite the relevant circulars and case law hierarchy that governs intra-group service valuation under both regimes.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a distributor under a principal-to-principal arrangement. The distributor subsequently re-labels the goods and supplies them to retailers. The manufacturer issues a credit note to the distributor for trade discounts post-supply, which the distributor does not pass on to retailers. Analyze whether: (a) the manufacturer must reverse ITC proportionate to the credit note under Section 15(3) read with Rule 96A, (b) the distributor's margin constitutes a taxable supply requiring separate invoice issuance, and (c) whether the anti-profiteering provisions under Section 171 apply to the distributor's retention of the discount benefit, citing the relevant CBIC circulars and NAA rulings.

An EOU imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and uses them for both export-oriented production (90%) and domestic tariff area (DTA) sales (10%). During an IGST audit, the officer proposes demand for proportionate IGST on the DTA portion under Section 3(7) of the Customs Tariff Act along with interest under Section 28AA, while also alleging that the ITC claimed on IGST paid at import exceeds permissible limits under Rule 42 because the goods serve exempt and taxable supplies simultaneously. Determine: (a) the correct valuation methodology for the DTA supply, (b) whether Rule 42 apportionment applies to capital goods or Rule 43, (c) the interaction between EPCG bond obligations and GST ITC reversal demands, and (d) the applicable limitation period given the audit was initiated 38 months after the import.

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a dealer in Karnataka under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement, where the billing is to a Gujarat-based intermediary who is not involved in the movement of goods. The goods are physically shipped directly from Maharashtra to Karnataka. Determine: (1) the place of supply for the manufacturer's invoice, (2) whether the transaction is inter-state or intra-state for each leg, (3) the applicable IGST/CGST/SGST for each invoice, and (4) whether the Gujarat intermediary can claim ITC on the IGST charged to it, given it has no establishment in Karnataka.

An EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods under EPCG authorization and also procures duty-free inputs under Advance Authorization. During the same year, it makes DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) sales exceeding 50% of its NFE (Net Foreign Exchange) obligation. Analyze: (1) the customs duty and IGST liability on DTA sales of goods manufactured using duty-free inputs, (2) whether proportionate reversal of ITC is required under Rule 42/43 of CGST Rules if the EOU is also registered under GST, (3) the interplay between the Foreign Trade Policy obligations and GST ITC reversal provisions, and (4) consequences if the EOU's EPCG export obligation lapses.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. However, due to a procedural lapse, the shipping bill was not filed within the time prescribed under Rule 96A of the CGST Rules. The exporter later files a refund claim under Section 16 of the IGST Act read with Rule 89 of CGST Rules, but the jurisdictional officer rejects it citing time-bar under Section 54(1). The exporter argues that the limitation clock should run from the date of realization of foreign exchange, not the date of export. Analyze the interplay between Rule 96A(1) compliance, Section 54(1) limitation, and the GST Council clarification in Circular No. 125/44/2019, and advise on the strongest legal grounds for challenging the rejection before the Appellate Authority.

An Indian subsidiary imports raw materials from its Singapore-based parent at a declared transaction value. Customs assesses the goods under Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 (transaction value method). Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) under Section 92CA of the Income Tax Act proposes an upward adjustment to the ALP of the same transaction, finding the declared price below arm's length. The assessee argues that Customs acceptance of transaction value creates an estoppel against the TPO. The Revenue contends the two proceedings are independent. Examine: (a) whether Customs valuation acceptance binds the TPO under the principle of consistency, (b) the Supreme Court's position post-Glaxo SmithKline on cross-statute transaction value conflicts, and (c) the double-taxation risk and available remedies under MAP under the India-Singapore DTAA.

title: Regarding levy of duty of excise (including non-levy thereof) under section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944
text:
20 th May, 2003

Notification No. 50/2003 - Central Excise (N.T.)

Whereas the Central Government is satisfied that according to a practice that was generally prevalent regarding levy of duty of
excise (including non-levy thereof) under section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944) (hereinafter referred to as the
said Act), on cotton yarn falling under heading No.52.03 of the Schedule to Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985 (5 of 1986), in the
form of cops, cones and in other forms falling under said heading, sent outside the factory for conversion into plain straight reel
hanks and that such cotton yarn was liable to duty of excise which was not being levied according to the said practice during
the period commencing on the 1 st March, 1986 and ending with 28 th February, 1992;

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by section 11C of the said Act, the Central Government hereby directs that
the duty of excise payable on the said cotton yarn in the forms of cops, cones and in other forms falling under said heading,
sent outside the factory for conversion into plain straight reel hanks, but for the practice, shall not be required to be paid in
respect of such cotton yarn on which the said duty of excise was not being levied during the aforesaid period in accordance
with the said practice.

Suraksha Katiyar

Under Secretary o the Government of India

F.No.55/9/90-CX.1

questions.question: A registered supplier of goods under regular GST scheme mistakenly filed GSTR-1 declaring an inter-state supply of ₹48 lakh to an unregistered recipient in April 2023, but the actual supply was intra-state. The supplier corrected it via an amendment in GSTR-1 for June 2023, but the auto-populated GSTR-3B for April was already filed without manual correction. The recipient (unregistered) has since moved to a different state. Identify: (a) the specific sections of CGST Act and applicable IGST provisions violated, (b) the exact mechanism and timeline under Rule 37A or Rule 88C for reconciling the mismatch, (c) whether a SCN is mandatory before demand issuance under Section 73 or 74, and (d) the interest computation methodology under Section 50(3) if excess ITC was claimed by anyone downstream using the incorrect invoice data.

An importer under a long-term contract with a related foreign supplier imports specialty chemical intermediates where the declared transaction value is ₹850/kg. The customs officer invokes Rule 4 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007 to reject the declared value citing 'relationship influence', and proposes a contemporaneous imports value of ₹1,100/kg from unrelated parties for a 'similar' (not identical) good. The importer contests that: (i) a test value under Rule 7 should apply since the goods are eventually sold in India at ₹1,600/kg with a standard 30% profit margin, and (ii) the officer skipped the sequential application mandate. Analyze: (a) whether sequential hierarchy of Rules 4–9 was correctly bypassed, (b) the precise burden-of-proof standard on the importer vs. department under Rule 4(3) to demonstrate 'no influence', (c) how the 'identical'/'similar' goods distinction in Rules 5 and 6 affects the officer's use of ₹1,100/kg, and (d) the admissibility of the deductive value method under Rule 7 given the 30% margin and whether the importer must demonstrate this margin is 'usual' per Explanation to Rule 7(1).

questions.question: Your production RAG system ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU achieving 5.2–9.6 chunks/second. During peak query load, you observe that retrieval latency spikes from 120ms to 2.8 seconds when concurrent users exceed 5. Qdrant is running locally, the LLM (Qwen3-14B) uses /no_think mode, and the host is a 4-core machine. Walk through your systematic diagnosis and remediation plan, specifying which metrics you collect first, which components you isolate, and what architectural changes you consider before adding hardware.

You have a working CBIC (customs/GST) RAG pipeline and must now extend it to cover Income Tax Act, MCA filings, Labour codes, and RBI circulars — each corpus with different document structures, citation styles, and update frequencies. Your ingest throughput is 5.2–9.6 chunks/second on a single GPU, you have no additional hardware, and queries must return answers grounded only in the corpus relevant to the user's jurisdiction. Design the multi-corpus architecture: address namespace/collection strategy in Qdrant, routing logic for jurisdiction detection, incremental re-ingestion without downtime, and how you prevent cross-corpus hallucination in the LLM response.

questions.question: A manufacturer in Gujarat supplies goods to a dealer in Maharashtra under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement where the billing is to a trading company in Delhi and physical delivery is directly to the Maharashtra dealer. The Maharashtra dealer subsequently claims Input Tax Credit on the IGST paid. The Delhi trading company has not filed GSTR-3B for the relevant tax period due to a technical glitch, resulting in the ITC not reflecting in the Maharashtra dealer's GSTR-2B. Examine the legal validity of the ITC claim under Section 16(2)(aa) post the Finance Act 2021 amendment, the applicability of Circular No. 235/29/2024-GST on ITC reconciliation, and the recourse available to the Maharashtra dealer if the supplier's non-compliance is established — including whether Writ jurisdiction under Article 226 survives after the Supreme Court's ruling in Union of India v. Filco Trade Centre Pvt. Ltd. (2022).

An Indian subsidiary of a US multinational provides software development services exclusively to its parent. The TPO proposes to recharacterize the entity from a 'captive service provider' benchmarked under TNMM with a routine return, to a 'risk-bearing entrepreneur' on the ground that the subsidiary owns certain process patents developed during the course of its operations and therefore bears significant intangible development risk. The AO additionally invokes Section 9(1)(vi) to treat a lump-sum payment received from the parent for assigning these patents as royalty, taxable in India at 20% under the Act (in the absence of a PE-linked reduced treaty rate under the India-US DTAA post the MFN clause controversy following the Nestle ruling). Analyze the interplay between the OECD BEPS Action 8-10 'DEMPE functions' framework as adopted in the 2017 Indian TP regulations, the correct characterization of the payment under Article 12 of the India-US DTAA, and the impact of the Supreme Court's 2023 ruling in Engineering Analysis Centre of Excellence on the definition of royalty for software-related transactions.

(iv) However, if the normal price is insufficient to cover the supplier"s average total costs plus a reasonable profit margin
(based on sector averages), the amount of subsidy should be the difference between the preferential price and the price which
would be required to cover the above costs and profit.

(v) If the government is the monopoly supplier of  the goods or services with a specific use, e.g. television tubes, the question of
preferential pricing does not arise, and the amount of subsidy should be the difference between the price paid by the firm
involved and the price required to cover the supplier"s costs and profit margin.

(e) Purchase of goods by government

(i) In a situation where private operators purchase the kind of goods in question as well as the government body, the amount of
subsidy should be the extent to which the price paid for the like product by the government exceeds the highest price offered for
a comparable purchase of the same goods by the private sector.

(ii) If the company involved has not made comparable sales to private operators, details should be obtained of the price paid
by private operators to comparable companies in the same sector of the economy, or, if such data is not available, in the
economy as a whole. In such a case, the amount of subsidy should be calculated as above.

(iii) If the government has a monopoly for the purchase of the goods in question, the amount of subsidy as regards the purchase
of goods by the government should be the extent to which the price paid for the goods exceeds adequate remuneration.
Adequate remuneration in this situation is the average costs incurred by the firm selling the product during the investigation
period, plus a reasonable amount of profit, which will have to be determined on a case-to-case basis.

The amount of subsidy should be the difference between the price paid by the government and adequate remuneration as
defined above.

questions.question: A manufacturer imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously claims IGST credit on such imports. The goods are partly used in export-oriented production and partly in domestic supply. The company later defaults on export obligations due to force majeure (COVID-19 declared). Customs demands recovery of customs duty saved plus interest under Section 114A of the Customs Act. Simultaneously, the GST department initiates reversal of ITC under Rule 42/43 of CGST Rules for the domestic-use portion. Analyze whether the taxpayer can avail the benefit of the Sabka Vishwas Legacy Dispute Resolution Scheme 2019 for the customs duty component, whether the force majeure clause under FTP 2015-20 para 2.82 provides a complete defence, and whether double jeopardy principles under Article 20(2) of the Constitution apply given concurrent proceedings under Customs Act and CGST Act for what is essentially the same underlying transaction.
questions.expected_depth: Multi-statute analysis spanning EPCG Scheme (FTP 2015-20), Customs Act 1962 Sections 114A/125, CGST Act Sections 17(5)/18(4), Rule 42/43 CGST Rules, SVLDRS 2019 eligibility criteria, constitutional Article 20(2) applicability to civil penalty vs tax recovery proceedings, and DGFT circular on COVID force majeure.

questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement. The AO in scrutiny assessment applies the TNMM method and rejects the subsidiary's use of internal CUP, making a TP adjustment of ₹120 crore for AY 2021-22. The subsidiary simultaneously has an Advance Pricing Agreement application pending for AY 2020-21 to 2024-25 covering the same transaction. The parent company is also under a Mutual Agreement Procedure request initiated by the US Competent Authority. During the proceedings, the AO discovers that the subsidiary has an undisclosed PE of the parent in India under Article 5(5) of the India-US DTAA (dependent agent PE), which would make a portion of the parent's global profits attributable to India. Analyze: (i) the interplay between the pending APA, MAP, and the current assessment under Section 92CA; (ii) whether the AO can simultaneously pursue both TP adjustment and PE attribution for the same entity/transactions; (iii) the correct method for profit attribution to the alleged PE under Rule 10 of Income Tax Rules vs OECD AOA approach given India's position; and (iv) whether the subsidiary can seek stay of demand under Section 220(6) pending MAP resolution, and the implications of the recent Pillar Two GloBE rules if the US parent is subject to a 15% minimum tax.
questions.expected_depth: Requires mastery of: Section 92C/92CA/92CD, APA provisions under Section 92CC/92CD, MAP under Article 25 India-US DTAA, PE attribution under Article 7 + Rule 10 Income Tax Rules, OECD AOA vs formulary apportionment, CBDT circulars on stay of demand in TP cases, BEPS Pillar Two interaction with domestic TP adjustments, and Supreme Court precedent on simultaneous TP + PE proceedings.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under an inter-state contract where the invoice is raised in INR but settlement is received in USD through an authorized dealer. The supplier claims the transaction qualifies as a 'zero-rated supply' under Section 16(1)(b) of the IGST Act on the grounds of 'receipt of payment in convertible foreign exchange.' The department contends that since the place of supply is within India (goods never crossed customs frontier), IGST is applicable at the standard rate. Evaluate the legal merits of both positions, citing relevant circulars and case law, and determine the correct tax treatment including the admissibility of ITC in the hands of the recipient.

An importer brings in a capital good under an EPCG license with a specific export obligation. Before fulfilling the obligation, the importer's business is acquired through a slump sale by a successor entity. The successor seeks to transfer the EPCG license and outstanding export obligation to itself. Simultaneously, the original importer had availed ITC on the customs duty paid (via IGST at import), which now sits as unutilized credit in the original entity's electronic credit ledger. Analyze: (1) the procedure and conditions for EPCG license transfer under the FTP 2023, (2) whether the successor can claim the transferred ITC under GST law, and (3) the redemption bond and bank guarantee liability upon the original licensee post-slump sale.

title: EOUs/ Jurisdiction of Customs and Excise Defied
text:
3.    As regards the administrative/ technical control on the existing EPZs, the Board is of the view that the present
arrangement would continue. Accordingly, the commissioner of Customs, Delhi will continue to exercise control
over the Noida EPZ while Commissioner of Customs, Calcutta would continue to exercise control over the FALTA
and so on.

4.    I am directed to say the Board's aforesaid decision has been taken after examining the issue of administrative
control over the EOU/ EPZ in totality keeping in mind the requirement of law and the facilities to the trade. It is
expected that with the decision, as above, the representations of various service associations will be set at rest.

5.    It is requested that , necessary action may be taken by all concerned officers to implement the subject decision
of the Board.

Sd/-

(Ranjana Jha)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under Letter of Undertaking (LUT) to a SEZ unit, which further processes and re-exports them. The SEZ unit claims the input tax credit on the goods received but the jurisdictional officer contends that since the final export was made by the SEZ unit (not the original manufacturer), the LUT benefit cannot extend to the initial supply and the manufacturer's zero-rated status is invalidated. Simultaneously, the manufacturer receives a SCN alleging short payment of IGST for the period. Analyze the legal validity of the officer's contention, the applicable provisions under IGST Act and SEZ Act 2005, the ITC reversal implications for both entities, and the correct appellate strategy including interim relief options.

An Indian subsidiary imports raw materials from its AE (Associated Enterprise) parent at a declared transaction value. Customs authorities reject the declared value under Rule 12 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007 and apply computed value method, adding notional royalty payments made to a third-party licensor as an addition under Rule 10(1)(c). The TPO simultaneously makes an upward TP adjustment on the same import transaction under Section 92CA, treating the arm's length price as higher than declared. The assessee faces dual taxation on the same differential — once as enhanced customs duty base and once as TP adjustment income. Examine whether the doctrine of consistency applies across Customs and Income Tax proceedings, the legal remedies to avoid double taxation of the same addition, the evidentiary weight of a CBIC advance ruling versus an APA in such cross-jurisdictional disputes, and draft the key grounds for a writ petition challenging the concurrent adjustments.

title: seeks to impose anti-dumping duty on the imports of "Grinding Media Balls" (excluding Forged Grinding Media Balls) originating in or exported from China PR and Thailand.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, 
SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No. 36/2018-Customs (ADD)

New Delhi, the 13th July, 2018

G.S.R.      (E). -Whereas, the designated authority vide Notification No.7/7/2017-DGAD 
dated 4th July, 2017-DGAD, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 
1, dated the 4th July, 2017, had initiated review in terms of sub-section (5) of section 9A of 
the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) (hereinafter referred to as the said Customs Tariff 
Act) and in pursuance of rule 23 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and 
Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles and for Determination of Injury) 
Rules, 1995 (hereinafter referred to as the said rules), in the matter of continuation of anti-
dumping duty on imports of ‘Grinding Media Balls’ (excluding Forged Grinding Media 
Balls) (hereinafter referred to as subject goods), classified under Chapter 73 of the First 
Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), originating in, or exported from, 
Thailand and People's Republic of China imposed vide  notification of the Government of

dated the 16th July, 2012 -Customs, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, 
Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R.568 (E), dated the 16th July, 2012 which was 
extended vide  notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance 
(Department of Revenue), No. 34/2017-Customs (ADD) dated the 13th July, 2017, published 
in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number 
G.S.R.878 (E), dated the 13th July, 2017 and had recommended for continuation of the anti-
dumping duty vide notification No. 7/7/2017-DGAD, dated the 11th June, 2018, published in

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) read with sub-

(3) of the Table below, falling under sub-heading of the First Schedule to the said Customs

specified in the corresponding entry in column (5) and produced by the producer specified in 
the corresponding entry in column (6) and exported by the exporter as specified in column (7) 
and the corresponding amount  specified in the currency per unit of measurement as specified 
in corresponding entry in column (8), of the said Table.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two registered units in different states — Unit A in Maharashtra supplies semi-finished goods to Unit B in Gujarat under a stock transfer arrangement. Unit B then further processes and sells the finished goods to an unrelated buyer. The company has been treating the inter-unit stock transfer as a supply under Schedule I of the CGST Act and paying IGST on the deemed transaction value (cost of production + 10% markup). A GST audit now contends that since the transaction is between distinct persons, the value should be determined under Rule 28 of the CGST Rules (open market value), not cost-plus, and has raised a demand for the differential tax plus interest. Simultaneously, Unit B is claiming full ITC on the IGST paid on the inter-unit receipt. Analyze: (a) whether the audit's position on valuation is legally sustainable given existing AAR/AAAR rulings and the CBIC circular on distinct persons; (b) whether Unit B's ITC claim can be blocked if the original supply is re-valued upward; and (c) what rectification mechanism, if any, is available to the company without triggering a fresh interest liability under Section 50.

An Indian subsidiary of a US-based MNC provides IT-enabled services exclusively to its parent. For AY 2024-25, the TPO benchmarks the arrangement using the TNMM with operating profit/revenue (OP/R) as the PLI, and proposes an upward adjustment of ₹42 crore, rejecting the assessee's use of a comparables set that includes three companies flagged as 'super-profit' entities. The assessee argues that: (i) two of the flagged companies are functionally comparable under Rule 10B(2) and were accepted by the same TPO in the immediately preceding year; (ii) the TPO's fresh rejection without a change in facts violates the principle of consistency; and (iii) the working capital adjustment methodology used by the TPO applies a short-term borrowing rate to net working capital differences but ignores the asset-light nature of the assessee. Critically evaluate: (a) the legal enforceability of the consistency principle in TP proceedings under Indian domestic law versus treaty-based MAP protections; (b) whether asset-intensity filters applied at the comparables-selection stage and working capital adjustments at the margin-adjustment stage can both be invoked or whether doing so amounts to double-counting risk; and (c) the procedural strategy the assessee should adopt — DRP vs. CIT(A) vs. immediate MAP filing — given that the parent treaty contains a mandatory binding arbitration clause under Article 25(5) of the US-India DTAA.
expected_competencies: MAP / mandatory arbitration under US-India DTAA Article 25(5)
Principle of consistency in TP assessments

title: FOR HAZARDOUS WASTE LISTED IN PART A OF SCHEDULE III ENSURE ACTUAL USER, VERIFY PRIOR INFORMED CONSENT AND MOEF PERMISSION. FOR OTHER WASTE LISTED IN PART B OF SCHEDULE III ENSURE ACTUAL USER AND VERIFY MOEF PERMISSION. FOR OTHER WASTE LIST
text:
o cause disease in animals or humans.  
H 8 
Corrosives 
 
Substances or wastes which, by chemical action, will cause severe damage when in contact with living 
tissue, or, in the case of leakage, will materially damage, or even destroy, other goods or the means of 
transport; they may also cause other hazards. 
H 10 
Liberation of toxic gases in contact with air or water 
 
Substances or wastes which, by interaction with air or water, are liable to give off toxic gases in 
dangerous quantities.  
H 11 
Toxic (delayed or chronic) 
 
Substances or wastes which, if they are inhaled or ingested or if they penetrate the skin, may involve 
delayed or chronic effects, including carcinogenicity). 
H 12 
Eco-toxic  
 
Substances or wastes which if released, present or may present immediate or delayed adverse impacts 
to the environment by means of bioaccumulation or toxic effects upon biotic systems or both.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operating under the composition scheme u/s 10 of the CGST Act supplies goods to an SEZ unit. The supplier's accountant argues that since composition dealers cannot make inter-state supplies, the transaction is void and no tax is payable. The SEZ unit, however, insists it is entitled to zero-rated supply benefits u/s 16 of the IGST Act. Simultaneously, the company's CA discovers that one of their input suppliers had their GST registration cancelled retrospectively 18 months ago, invalidating ITC already availed. Analyse: (a) the legal validity of the composition dealer's supply to the SEZ unit, (b) whether the SEZ unit can claim zero-rating benefits, (c) the recourse available to the manufacturer for the retrospectively cancelled supplier's ITC, and (d) the cascading penalty exposure across all three parties under Sections 122, 73, and 74 of the CGST Act.

An exporter claims MEIS scrips (pre-FTP 2023) worth ₹2.8 crore against exports of a product now classified under a HS code that Customs re-classified post-export under a Customs Audit finding, disqualifying it from MEIS. Simultaneously, the same exporter used Advance Authorisation to import raw materials duty-free, but due to the re-classification, the actual value addition does not meet the minimum 15% NFEP condition. DGFT has issued a Show Cause Notice demanding recovery of customs duty foregone plus interest, while Customs adjudicating authority is separately proceeding under Section 28 of the Customs Act for short levy. Analyse: (a) whether dual proceedings by DGFT and Customs on the same transaction are legally sustainable, (b) the precedence of HS classification by Customs versus DGFT's accepted classification at time of Advance Authorisation issuance, (c) available estoppel or legitimate expectation defences, and (d) the interplay between Section 28 customs recovery and DGFT penalty under Para 9.8 of the FTP 2015-20.

questions.question: A manufacturer in SEZ supplies goods to a DTA unit under a bond. The DTA unit further processes the goods and re-exports 60% while clearing 30% domestically (paying applicable IGST) and claiming the remaining 10% as wastage. The SEZ unit originally availed zero-rated supply benefits. How should the DTA unit compute its ITC reversal under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of CGST Rules, and what are the implications under Section 17(2) when the exempt portion (zero-rated origin) intersects with domestic taxable supply—specifically, does the zero-rated origin of inputs affect the proportional reversal calculation for the DTA unit?

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign entity at a declared transaction value. Customs rejects the transaction value under Rule 3(3) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, citing relationship influence, and proposes Computed Value under Rule 7. The importer contests, arguing that identical goods were sold to unrelated buyers in the exporting country at the same price (Rule 5 fallback). Simultaneously, the DGFT issues a Show Cause Notice alleging misdeclaration of country of origin (goods routed through a third country) potentially triggering anti-dumping duty under a standing DGAD notification. Enumerate the sequential hierarchy of valuation methods the adjudicating authority must exhaust before landing on Computed Value, the evidentiary standard for rebutting relationship-influence, and whether the pending origin determination suspends or runs parallel to the valuation adjudication under Section 28 of the Customs Act 1962.

questions.question: A manufacturing company supplies goods to its 100% subsidiary under a cost-sharing arrangement where no invoice is raised and no consideration is paid, relying on Schedule I Entry 2 of the CGST Act. The subsidiary later re-exports these goods after value addition. The GST officer contends that (a) the inter-company supply is taxable at open market value under Rule 28, (b) the subsidiary cannot claim ITC on a deemed supply where no tax was actually paid, and (c) the re-export exemption under Bond/LUT is unavailable because the original supply was not a 'zero-rated supply' in the hands of the subsidiary. Analyze all three contentions with reference to applicable sections, rules, and circulars, and advise on the correct tax position and risk mitigation strategy.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax / Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a foreign MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using the TNMM. During assessment, the TPO rejects the tested party selection (arguing the foreign parent should be the tested party), applies a secret comparable with a margin 18 percentage points higher, and simultaneously invokes Section 92CE to demand secondary adjustment — treating the excess profit as a deemed loan and imputing interest under Rule 10CB. The assessee argues that (a) OECD guidelines and Indian TP regulations mandate choosing the less complex entity as tested party, (b) the secret comparable violates Rule 10D(2) and natural justice, and (c) secondary adjustment cannot apply because the APA entered for the prior year covered the same transaction structure and the rollback provisions should extend to this year. Evaluate each ground, cite relevant case law and CBDT circulars, and outline the litigation and settlement strategy.

title: Customs Post Clearance Audit- reg.
text:
3. 
Customs clearance over the years has undergone a paradigm shift. From a dedicated

focus on duty/ revenue, the attention has expanded to trade facilitation, regulatory compliance 
and intelligent enforcement. Even though the OSPCA provisions have served their objective, 
however, their limited scope has proved to be restrictive inasmuch as only an importer or an 
exporter could be audited. As per the scope and coverage of audit under the erstwhile 
provisions, not only transaction level details but more importantly the trader’s commercial 
systems were also to be evaluated for compliance. Therefore, it was felt that a more 
comprehensive legal framework was required. The legal framework would define the scope of 
entities that would be subjected to customs audit, the manner, rights and obligations of the 
auditee and other related issues. In the light of the revised focus, a new section 99A (under 
Chapter XIIA) was introduced in the Customs Act through the Finance Act 2018, to provide a 
statutory framework for audit. An exhaustive definition of ‘Auditee’ to include custodians or 
licensees of a warehouse, customs brokers and any other person concerned directly or indirectly 
in clearing, forwarding, stocking etc. was introduced making it distinct from the scope of the 
audit prior to these statutory changes. Along with the expanded definition of auditee, a 
complementary amendment has been made in the sub-section (2) of section 17 as well. The 
changes in the said sub-section have broadened the ambit of assessment from “assessment of 
duty” to “assessment of the entries made under section 46 or section 50 and the self-assessment 
of goods”. These statutory changes have been made given the prominent role Customs audit is 
going to play in future in the context of increasing trade facilitation.  
4. 
Board has also issued ‘Customs Audit Regulations, 2018’ vide notification No.

45/2018-Cus (NT) dated 24.5.18 in supersession of the On-site Post Clearance Audit 
regulations consequent to omission of sub-section (6) of section 17. In order to operationalize 
customs audit under the new provisions in the Customs Act, 1962, Board vide notification No. 
85/2017-Customs (N.T.), dated 7th September, 2017 has notified Audit Commissionerates in 
Chennai, Delhi and Mumbai Zone-I with all India jurisdiction. These Commissionerates have 
become functional with effect from the 1st April, 2018 under the administrative control of the 
Chief Commissioner of Customs Chennai, Delhi and Mumbai Zone-I respectively. Audit 
Commissionerate at Nhava Sheva under the administrative control of the Chief Commissioner 
of Customs, Mumbai Zone-II, with all India jurisdiction has also been notified vide amending 
notification 
No. 
100/2018-Customs 
(N.T.) 
dated 
27th 
December, 
2018. 
Audit

Commissionerates are headed by an officer of the rank of Principal Commissioner/ 
Commissioner of Customs. Board vide notification No. 39/2018-Customs (N.T.) dated 11th 
May, 2018 has appointed officers in Customs Audit commissionerates of specified ranks as 
officers of Customs for the purpose of carrying out audit under section 99A of the Customs 
Act. 
5. 
Under the new scheme, Transaction based audit (TBA) and Premises based audit (PBA)

have been prescribed. Transaction based audit (TBA) was actually introduced in 2005 (when 
the risk management system was operationalized). Transaction Based Audit is a form of the 
erstwhile PCA. It may also be noted that a TBA may subsequently be converted into a Premises 
based Audit (PBA). PBA was first introduced in the year 2011 by way of On-site Post 
Clearance Audit at the premises of Importers and Exporters Regulations, 2011 whereby it was 
laid down that audit shall also be conducted at the premises of the importers / exporters.

questions.question: A multinational company has a subsidiary in India that provides software development services to its foreign parent. The subsidiary also sub-contracts 40% of the work to a third-party Indian vendor. Under GST, determine: (a) whether the subsidiary's export of services qualifies as 'zero-rated supply' under Section 16 of the IGST Act given the sub-contracting arrangement, (b) the admissibility of ITC on input services used for the sub-contracted portion, and (c) the implications if the foreign parent remits payment in INR via a Nostro account arrangement. Cite the relevant circulars and whether the place of supply rules under Section 13 of IGST Act alter the analysis.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian company (AssessCo) holds a 26% stake in a foreign JV and provides technical know-how under a royalty agreement benchmarked at 3% of net sales using the CUP method. The AO proposes to recharacterize the royalty as a deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(e) and simultaneously invokes Section 92CA for a TP adjustment, arguing the arm's length royalty should be 1.5% using TNMM. Analyze: (a) whether Section 2(22)(e) can apply to a foreign company in which AssessCo is a shareholder (not the recipient), (b) the legal validity of applying two independent adjustment mechanisms to the same transaction, and (c) the interplay with India's tax treaty obligations under Article 12 (Royalties) and Article 10 (Dividends) if the JV is in a treaty jurisdiction with a 10% withholding cap on royalties.

questions.question: Your production RAG system ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU achieving 5.2–9.6 chunks/sec. After deployment, you observe that multi-hop queries requiring cross-referencing GST circulars with Income Tax Act provisions consistently return low-relevance results despite high cosine similarity scores. The retrieval latency is acceptable (P95 < 2s), but answer quality degrades sharply for questions involving regulatory exceptions that span 3+ source documents. Propose a complete architectural intervention: which retrieval strategy would you replace dense retrieval with or augment it with, how would you restructure the chunk schema, what re-ranking pipeline would you introduce, and how would you validate improvement without contaminating your production Qdrant collection?

questions.domain: Distributed LLM Inference on Constrained Hardware
questions.question: You operate a 4-core CPU host running multiple concurrent GPU workloads: a 14B parameter LLM served via llama-server using Vulkan (not ROCm) on GPU 2, a FastAPI RAG API with async workers, and an OCR pipeline using Qwen2.5-VL-7B on GPU 4/6 — all sharing a single NVMe boot drive with no internal SATA. Under peak load, you observe CPU steal > 40%, GPU VRAM pressure causing KV-cache evictions, and I/O wait spikes to 60% during concurrent PDF ingestion + query serving. You cannot add hardware. Design a resource isolation and scheduling strategy: specify process affinity, ionice classes, Vulkan context separation, request queue depth limits, and a load-shedding policy that preserves query SLA (P95 < 4s) while allowing background ingestion to proceed at ≥ 30% of peak throughput.

title: Exempts certain goods from the whole of the national calamity contingent duty.
text:

(E), dated the 13 th May,
2005,                                                                                                                                                                                            at page 4,-

Deputy  Secretary to the Government of India

questions.question: A foreign company incorporated in Singapore provides cloud-based SaaS services to Indian businesses. It has no physical presence in India but its servers process Indian customer data routed through a Mumbai AWS region. The company crosses the Rs. 20 lakh threshold in supplies to registered Indian persons in FY 2025-26. Analyze: (1) Whether the company is liable for GST registration under Section 24 read with Section 14 of the IGST Act, (2) Whether OIDAR classification applies given the hybrid human-assisted support component, (3) What the place of supply determination is under Section 13(12) vs 13(2), and (4) What the compliance mechanism is given the company has no GSTIN and cannot appoint an Indian agent. Cite the relevant circulars and notifications.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Interface
questions.question: A partnership firm converts to an LLP under the LLP Act, 2008 during FY 2025-26. The firm held capital goods on which ITC of Rs. 18 lakhs was availed 22 months ago. Post-conversion, the LLP receives a show-cause notice claiming: (a) ITC reversal under Rule 44 read with Section 18(4) on the grounds that conversion constitutes a 'supply' under Schedule II para 4(c), and (b) that the successor LLP cannot carry forward the electronic credit ledger balance because the PAN changes. Simultaneously, the Income Tax department argues that Section 47(xiii) exemption from capital gains does not apply because the LLP agreement does not restrict profit-sharing ratio changes for 5 years. Construct a unified legal defense addressing both the GST and Income Tax contentions, identifying where the two regimes conflict and which prevails.

title: Motor spirit mixed with methanol
text:
Circular No. 83/83/94

dated 13/12/94

F.No. 89/6/93-CX-3

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Central Board of Excise & Customs, New Delhi

Subject: Central Excise - Whether mixing of duty paid methanol with motor sprit amounts to manufacture-clarification
regarding.

This Ministry has received a reference from the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas seeking clarification whether mixing of
duty paid methanol to the extent of 3% with 97% of duty paid motor sprit amounts to "Manufacture" within the meaning of
Section 2(f) of the Central excises and Salt Act, 1944. The matter has been examined in consultation with the Chief Chemist,
CRCL, New Delhi. The opinion of the Chief Chemist. CRCL is enclosed* herewith.

2.    You are therefore requested to decide pending cases on the issue in the light of the opinion of the Chief Chemist, CRCL,
New Delhi.

3.    Trade and field formations may be advised suitably.

Sd/-

(Rajiv Bhatia)

Under Secretary (CX.3)

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. The foreign buyer later remits payment in two tranches: 60% within 90 days and 40% after 180 days from the date of export invoice. The AD bank converts both remittances at prevailing RBI reference rates, resulting in a realized export value (in INR) that differs from the invoice value declared in the shipping bill. Simultaneously, the exporter had claimed refund of ITC accumulated on inputs used in zero-rated supply. The refund was sanctioned but the IGST officer now issues a SCN alleging that the partial non-realization within 180 days violates Rule 96A(1) and demands reversal of the entire ITC refund with 18% interest. Analyze: (a) whether the demand for full reversal is legally sustainable given partial realization, (b) how exchange rate variance affects the 'realized value' threshold, and (c) what remedial steps the exporter must take to preserve the LUT benefit for future exports while contesting the SCN.

An Indian importer purchases specialty chemicals from its wholly-owned subsidiary in Singapore under a long-term supply agreement. The transaction value is accepted by Customs for three consecutive years. In Year 4, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) under the Income Tax Act rejects the arm's length price and makes an upward adjustment, increasing the declared import price by 22%. Concurrently, the SVB (Special Valuation Branch) reopens the Customs assessment citing the TP adjustment as 'new information' and proposes to re-determine assessable value under Rule 5 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, demanding differential duty plus interest for all four years. Analyze: (a) whether a TP upward adjustment legally triggers SVB re-determination and if the principle of res judicata or consistency applies to prior SVB orders, (b) the correct methodology cascade under CVR 2007 when the transaction value is impugned due to related-party influence versus TP adjustment, (c) the interplay between Section 14 of the Customs Act and the arm's length standard, and (d) how the importer should structure its legal response to avoid double taxation on the same price variance across two different statutes.
expected_knowledge_areas: Transfer Pricing Section 92C Income Tax Act
Double taxation avoidance between Customs and IT

questions.question: A manufacturing company with multiple GSTINs across states supplies goods under a works contract to a government entity. The supply involves both transfer of goods and immovable property services. The company claims Input Tax Credit on capital goods used exclusively for this supply, but the tax authority contends that Section 17(5)(c) blocks ITC since the output supply itself is a works contract service for immovable property. The company argues the goods component can be bifurcated. Analyze the legal position, cite the relevant provisions and judicial precedents, and advise on the optimal structuring of the contract to maximize allowable ITC while remaining compliant.

A closely-held Indian company receives a loan from its 100% foreign parent entity denominated in USD at an interest rate of 3% p.a. The arm's length rate determined under TNMM benchmarking is 7% p.a. The AO proposes a transfer pricing adjustment on the interest differential AND simultaneously invokes Section 56(2)(x) treating the interest waiver as income from other sources. The company contends that Section 92 is a complete code and Section 56(2) cannot apply concurrently, and additionally claims treaty relief under the India-US DTAA Article 11. Evaluate the correctness of each authority's position, the interaction between domestic TP provisions and treaty override under Section 90(2), and the company's exposure to secondary adjustment under Section 92CE.

title: The Finance Act, 2020 (12 of 2020)

questions.question: A manufacturing company with plants in Maharashtra and Gujarat operates through a single GST registration in Maharashtra (head office). During an audit, the department contends that inter-state stock transfers to the Gujarat plant — which lacks independent registration — constitute taxable supplies under Section 25(4) read with Schedule I Para 2 of the CGST Act, and raises a demand for IGST on these transfers valued at cost plus 10% markup. The company argues these are branch transfers, not supplies, as both units operate under the same PAN and GSTIN. Analyze: (a) Whether the department's position is legally tenable given that the Gujarat unit is not separately registered, (b) the applicability of Schedule I Para 2 when the recipient is not a distinct person, (c) the valuation methodology if the transfer is held taxable, and (d) the ITC implications for the Maharashtra entity on such deemed outward supply.
expected_answer_elements: Distinct person definition under Section 25(4) — requires separate registration in each state
Gujarat plant without its own GSTIN is NOT a distinct person; Schedule I Para 2 inapplicable
Department's demand is legally untenable on strict reading of the Act
Valuation under Rule 28 (related-party / distinct-person rule) — cost+10% is one permissible method but only when the provision applies
ITC eligibility: since no outward supply arises, no reversal required; blocked credit under Section 17(5) not triggered
Recommendation: company should seek Advance Ruling or contest demand at adjudication stage with AAR precedents

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) provides IT-enabled back-office services exclusively to its US parent (USCo). IndCo benchmarks its ALP using the TNMM with Operating Profit/Revenue (OP/R) as the PLI, arriving at a margin of 18% against a comparable set median of 14%. The TPO rejects two comparables IndCo selected as functionally dissimilar and adds three new comparables with margins of 8%, 11%, and 9%, shifting the median to 11.5%. The TPO then applies the arithmetic mean of the revised set (9.6%) and makes a transfer pricing adjustment of ₹42 crore. IndCo disputes: (a) the rejection of its two comparables, (b) the addition of the TPO's three comparables, and (c) the use of arithmetic mean versus median. Separately, IndCo also claims a deduction under Section 10AA for SEZ units, which the AO disallows on the ground that the TP adjustment increases the arm's-length price of exports, inflating eligible profit beyond actual profit. Evaluate each dispute point with reference to applicable Rules, judicial precedents, and the interaction between TP adjustment and Section 10AA deduction computation.
expected_answer_elements: Rule 10B(1)(e) TNMM — PLI selection; OP/R appropriate for service companies
Comparable rejection criteria: functional similarity, revenue filters (Rule 10B(2)), single-segment revenue, related-party transaction filter
TPO's three comparables — each must be tested against same filters; high-revenue IT product companies commonly rejected (e.g., Infosys, Wipro as comparables for captive BPO — Quark Media precedent)
Rule 10CA(7): use of arithmetic mean only permissible if dataset has <6 comparables; otherwise median is mandated — TPO's use of arithmetic mean is procedurally incorrect

TP adjustment does not represent actual income earned; Section 10AA deduction computed on profits as disclosed in books, not on notional ALP differential (Delhi HC in Aptara Technology; Bombay HC in WNS Global)
Interaction: AO cannot simultaneously accept TP adjustment AND add it to SEZ-eligible profit — adjustment is notional, not a cash receipt
Recommended path: DRP → ITAT, citing Rule 10CA, comparable set jurisprudence, and the notional-vs-actual income distinction for Section 10AA
source_sections: Transfer Pricing Guidelines OECD Chapter II

title: Medical Devices Rules
text:
206  
     THE   GAZETTE   OF  INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
             [PART II—SEC. 3(i)]

appropriate to begin with older children before extending the investigation to younger children and then 
infants.    
(c) If the medical device is predominantly or exclusively used in paediatric patients, clinical investigation data 
should be generated in paediatric population except for initial safety and performance data, which will 
usually be obtained in adults unless such initial safety studies in adults would yield little useful information 
or expose them to inappropriate risk. 
(d) If the medical device is intended to treat serious or life-threatening diseases, occurring in both adults and 
paediatric patients, for which there are currently no or limited therapeutic options, paediatric population 
may be included in the clinical investigation early, following assessment of initial safety data and 
reasonable evidence of potential benefit. In circumstances where this is not possible, lack of data has to be 
justified. 
(e) If the medical device has a potential for use in paediatric patient, paediatric studies may be conducted. 
These studies may be initiated at various stages of clinical development or after post-marketing 
surveillance in adults, if a safety concern exists. In cases where there is limited paediatric data at the time 
of submission of application, more data in paediatric patients would be expected after marketing 
authorization for use in children is granted.   
(f) Paediatric subjects are legally unable to provide written informed consent, and are dependent on their 
parents or legal guardian to assume responsibility for their participation in clinical investigation. Written 
informed consent shall be obtained from the parent or legal guardian. However, all paediatric participants 
shall be informed to the fullest extent possible about the study in a language and in terms that they are able 
to understand. Where appropriate, paediatric participants should additionally assent to enroll in the study. 
Mature minors and adolescents should personally sign and date a separately designed written consent form. 
Although a participant’s wish to withdraw from a study shall be respected, there may be circumstances in 
therapeutic studies for serious or life-threatening diseases in which, in the opinion of the investigator and 
parent or legal guardian, the welfare of a pediatric patient would be jeopardized by his or her failing to 
participate in the study. In this situation, continued parental or legal guardian consent will be sufficient to 
allow participation in the study. 
(g) For clinical investigations conducted in paediatric population, the reviewing Ethics Committee shall 
include members who are knowledgeable about pediatric, ethical, clinical and psychosocial issues.   
(iii)  Pregnant or nursing women: 
(a) Pregnant or nursing women shall be included in clinical investigation only when the medical device is 
intended for use by pregnant or nursing women or fetuses or nursing infants and where the data generated 
from women who are not pregnant or nursing, would not be suitable.  
(b) For medical device intended for use during pregnancy, follow-up data pertaining to a period appropriate for 
that medical device on the pregnancy, foetus and child will be required.  
3. Post Marketing Surveillance: 
(i) Subsequent to approval of an Investigational medical device, it shall be closely monitored for their clinical 
safety once they are marketed. The applicants shall furnish Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs) in order 
to,- 
(a) report all the relevant new information from appropriate sources;  
(b) relate these data to patient exposure;  
(c) summarise the market authorisation status in different countries and any significant variations related to 
safety; and  
(d) indicate whether changes will be made to product information in order to optimize the use of the product.  
 
(ii)  One medical device should be covered in one PSUR. Within the single PSUR separate presentation of data for 
different indications or separate population need to be given.  
(iii)  All relevant clinical and non-clinical safety data will cover only the period of the report (interval data). The 
PSURs shall be submitted every six months for the first two years after marketing approval of the medical 
device. For subsequent two years, the PSURs need to be submitted annually. The Central Licensing Authority 
may extend the total duration for submission of PSURs if it is considered necessary in the interest of public 
health. PSURs due for a period must be submitted within thirty calendar days of the last day of the reporting 
period. However, all cases involving suspected unexpected serious adverse event shall be reported to the 
licensing authority within fifteen days of initial receipt of information by the applicant.

questions.question: A manufacturing company in Maharashtra supplies goods to a SEZ unit in Gujarat under a zero-rated supply with payment of IGST. The supplier claims refund under Rule 96 of CGST Rules. However, the shipping bill filed on ICEGATE reflects a different HSN code than what was declared in GSTR-1, causing a mismatch in the IGST paid field on the shipping bill vs the portal. The refund application is stuck in 'Deficiency Memo' stage. The officer is citing Section 54(3) read with Rule 89 as the correct refund route instead of Rule 96. Analyse the legal merit of both positions, identify whether the taxpayer can simultaneously maintain a Rule 96 claim and a fallback Rule 89 application, and advise on the rectification pathway for the HSN mismatch without amending a filed GSTR-1 for a period whose annual return (GSTR-9) has already been filed and matched.
key_issues: Rule 96 vs Rule 89 — mutually exclusive or concurrent filing
HSN mismatch between ICEGATE shipping bill and GSTR-1 after GSTR-9 filed
Deficiency Memo response drafting under Rule 96(6)

questions.question: An Indian importer purchases goods from its wholly-owned subsidiary (WOS) in Singapore. The transaction value is accepted under Rule 3(3)(b) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007 based on a previously approved APA with the Transfer Pricing officer under the Income Tax Act. During SVB (Special Valuation Branch) re-examination, the customs officer rejects the transaction value citing that the TP-APA approval covers ALP under Section 92C of IT Act but does not constitute conclusive proof of 'no influence on price' under Customs Valuation Rules, and proposes enhancement under Rule 5 (deductive value) using domestic sale prices of identical goods by an unrelated importer. The importer argues estoppel based on the APA and challenges Rule 5 applicability since Rule 5 requires deduction of post-importation costs but the officer used CIF prices of unrelated imports as base. Critically evaluate the officer's Rule 5 methodology, assess whether an TP-APA creates any binding presumption in SVB proceedings, and outline the appellate strategy through CESTAT including interim relief options to avoid enhanced duty deposit.
key_issues: TP-APA vs SVB proceedings — legal nexus and binding effect
Rule 5 deductive value methodology errors — CIF base vs domestic sale price
Estoppel / legitimate expectation against customs enhancement
CESTAT interim stay conditions for related-party valuation disputes

Explanation 2. - For the purpose of this notification, -

(A) "Capital goods" has the same meaning as assigned to it in paragraph of 9.08 of the Foreign Trade Policy;

(B) "Common Service Provider" (CSP) means a service provider who is designated or certified as a Common Service
Provider by the Director General of Foreign Trade (DGFT), Department of Commerce or State Industrial Infrastructural
Corporation in a Town of Export Excellence;

(C)  "Export obligation",-

(I)         means obligation on the authorisation holder to export to a place outside India, goods manufactured or capable of being
manufactured or services rendered by the use of capital goods imported under the said authorisation and the export obligation
shall be over and above the average level of exports achieved by the authorisation holder in the preceding three licensing years
for the same and similar products within the export obligation period and such average shall be the arithmetic mean of export
performance in the last three years for the same and similar products:

Provided that in case of export of goods relating to handicraft, handlooms, cottage, tiny sector, agriculture, animal husbandry,
floriculture, horticulture, pisciculture, viticulture, poultry, sericulture, carpet, coir and jute, the authorisation holder shall not be
required to maintain the average level of exports:

Provided further that in case of export of goods relating to aquaculture (including fisheries), the authorisation holder shall not be
required to maintain the average level of exports subject to the condition that said authorisation has been obtained for goods
other than fishing trawlers, boats, ships and other similar items:

Provided also that the goods, excepting tools, imported under said authorisation by the aforesaid sectors, shall not be allowed
to be transferred for a period of five years from the date of imports even in cases where export obligation has been fulfilled:

Provided also that exports made to such countries as notified by Director General of Foreign Trade, shall not be counted for
fixing the average level of exports:

Provided also that exports against only such shipping bills which mention the authorisation number and date of the said
authorisation shall be counted for the fulfillment of the export obligation;

Provided also that in the case of authorisation issued to a CSP, -

(i)             the reference to 'authorisation holder' in this Explanation shall be taken to mean a reference to 'CSP and specific
users whose details are informed prior to export by CSP to the Regional Authority';

questions.question: A manufacturing company exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) to a foreign buyer. The foreign buyer's payment is routed through an intermediary country due to banking restrictions, and the funds arrive in INR via an authorized dealer after 18 months from the date of export. The GST officer contends that (a) the export is not a 'zero-rated supply' since foreign exchange was not received within the prescribed period under FEMA, and (b) Input Tax Credit of ₹2.3 crore availed on inputs used in the exported goods is liable for reversal with interest under Rule 96A(1) of CGST Rules, 2017. The company argues that the delay was beyond their control and the RBI granted post-facto approval. Analyze the validity of the officer's demand, the interplay between Rule 96A, Section 16(3) of IGST Act, and FEMA regulations, and the legal remedies available to the company including the exact timeline for filing appeal versus writ petition.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax / Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US MNC provides IT-enabled services to its parent and earns a cost-plus 15% margin. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) rejects the Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) applied by IndCo, arguing that IndCo owns significant 'location savings' and 'market premium' intangibles that must be compensated by the parent, and proposes an upward adjustment of ₹180 crore under Section 92CA. IndCo disputes this on grounds that (i) it is a routine service provider with no ownership of intangibles per the OECD BEPS Action Plan 8-10 framework adopted in Indian TP regulations post-2017, (ii) the comparables selected by TPO fail the employee cost filter, and (iii) the benefit of location savings, if any, should be shared, not entirely attributed to IndCo. Simultaneously, the parent's US tax advisors warn that any upward revision in IndCo's arm's length price will trigger a corresponding Competent Authority (CA) procedure under Article 9 of the India-US DTAA. Evaluate the legal merits of both positions, the correct application of the 'ownership vs. entitlement' distinction for intangibles under Indian TP law post-Hyundai Motor India Engineering ruling, and the procedural steps for a Mutual Agreement Procedure (MAP) filing, including the interaction with penalty proceedings under Section 271(1)(c) if the adjustment is sustained.
