questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (wholly-owned subsidiary) at a price below the open market value. The recipient is eligible for full input tax credit. Under Section 15(4) read with Rule 28 of the CGST Rules, what is the applicable valuation method, and does the proviso to Rule 28 apply to exempt the transaction from OMV requirement? If the recipient later makes an exempt supply using these inputs, how does that retrospectively affect the original supplier's valuation obligation?

A resident individual holds a 35% stake in a foreign company incorporated in Mauritius, which in turn holds assets predominantly in India. The foreign company distributes dividends from accumulated profits that include gains on sale of Indian immovable property. Under Section 9(1)(i) read with Explanation 5 (deemed accrual), the India-Mauritius DTAA Article 13, and the post-2016 amended DTAA protocol on source taxation of capital gains, determine: (a) whether the dividend is taxable in India as deemed income, (b) whether the beneficial ownership test under GAAR (Chapter X-A) applies to deny treaty benefits, and (c) how Form 67 obligations interact with DTAA relief claims for the individual shareholder.

questions.question: A manufacturer operates three distinct business verticals under a single GSTIN: (a) taxable supply of goods, (b) exempt supply of financial guarantee services, and (c) zero-rated exports under LUT. During Q3, the entity procures capital goods used exclusively for the exempt vertical, common inputs used across all three verticals, and input services attributable solely to the export vertical. The CFO seeks to maximise ITC claims while remaining compliant with Sections 17(1), 17(2), 17(3), and Rule 42/43 of CGST Rules. Critically, the entity also has a pending SCN under Section 74 for allegedly availing excess ITC in a prior period. How should the entity (i) compute the reversible ITC for the quarter, (ii) treat the capital goods ITC under Rule 43 across the useful life, (iii) structure the export refund claim under Rule 89(4) vs 89(4A), and (iv) respond to the SCN without prejudicing its ongoing ITC position?

An Indian importer purchases specialty chemicals from a related party in Germany at a transfer price set under the OECD arm's-length standard (Comparable Uncontrolled Price method). The Customs department rejects the declared transaction value under Rule 3 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, invoking Rule 10A on related-party influence, and proposes enhancement to the computed value under Rule 7. Simultaneously, DGTR has issued an anti-dumping duty notification on the same product from Germany, computed as the difference between the Normal Value and the Export Price. Additionally, the FEMA adjudicating authority is investigating whether the TP adjustment payments routed through a Netherlands holding entity constitute a capital account transaction requiring RBI approval. Advise on: (i) the sequence of legal challenges across Customs, DGTR, and FEMA forums without creating adverse admissions, (ii) whether the arm's-length TP documentation can serve as primary evidence in both the customs valuation dispute and the FEMA inquiry, and (iii) the interaction between anti-dumping duty assessment and the provisional duty deposit requirement under Section 18 of the Customs Act during the pendency of the valuation appeal.

questions.question: A multi-state manufacturing company has a principal place of business in Maharashtra and additional places of business in Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. They manufacture goods under HSN 8471 (computers) and also provide ancillary repair services under SAC 9987. During a financial year, their aggregate turnover crosses ₹1.5 crore. Explain in detail: (a) how Input Tax Credit must be apportioned across the three GSTINs under Rule 36 and Rule 42/43 of the CGST Rules, (b) the specific conditions under which the company must reverse ITC under Section 17(2) for the exempt repair services rendered free of charge to warranty customers, and (c) the interplay between cross-charge obligations under Schedule I para 2 and the actual ISD mechanism under Section 20 — when must each be used, and what are the consequences of using the wrong mechanism as per recent AAR rulings?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax & Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC enters into the following transactions with its Associated Enterprises in FY 2024-25: (i) receives a sub-license for software IP at a royalty rate of 18% of net revenues, (ii) avails intra-group IT support services under a cost-plus 10% arrangement, and (iii) issues compulsorily convertible debentures (CCDs) to the parent at 6% p.a. The TPO benchmarks the royalty using the CUT method and proposes a rate of 8%, rejects the services cost base by disallowing ₹12 crore of 'shareholder activity' costs, and recharacterizes the CCDs as equity. Analyze: (a) the evidentiary burden and documentation requirements under Rule 10D to defend each transaction, (b) how the secondary adjustment under Section 92CE and corresponding withholding tax liability under Section 195 interact if the primary adjustment exceeds ₹1 crore, and (c) the applicability of the Dispute Resolution Panel vs. APA route given that an advance pricing agreement was filed but not yet concluded — including MAP implications under the India-US DTAA Article 27.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three units: Unit A (registered in Maharashtra), Unit B (registered in Gujarat), and Unit C (unregistered job-work premises in Rajasthan). Unit A sends semi-finished goods to Unit C under a challan for job-work under Section 143 of the CGST Act. Unit C sends the finished goods directly to Unit B's customer in Karnataka under Unit B's tax invoice. Unit B's customer later raises a dispute claiming ITC was disallowed because the goods were never in Unit B's possession. Analyze the GST implications for: (i) the validity of the job-work chain across three states, (ii) whether Unit C's direct supply to the Karnataka customer triggers independent GST registration liability in Rajasthan, (iii) the ITC eligibility for Unit B and the Karnataka customer, and (iv) the principal's (Unit A's) obligations under Rule 45 if goods do not return within 1 year.

An Indian EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods duty-free under the EOU scheme and uses them to manufacture goods, 60% of which are exported and 40% sold in the DTA (Domestic Tariff Area). Three years into operations, the EOU surrenders its status and converts to a regular MSME. Simultaneously, it enters into a slump-sale agreement with a GST-registered acquirer. Evaluate: (i) the customs duty and interest liability on the capital goods at the time of exit from EOU status, considering depreciation norms under the Foreign Trade Policy versus the Customs Valuation Rules, (ii) whether the slump sale constitutes a 'supply' under GST and how ITC on the capital goods block credit under Section 18(6) read with Rule 44 applies to the transferor, (iii) the acquirer's eligibility to claim ITC on GST paid (if any) on the slump-sale consideration, and (iv) FEMA implications if the original capital goods were financed through an ECB (External Commercial Borrowing) and the ECB is being novated to the acquirer.

title: Customs Tariff (Determination of Origin of Goods under the Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement between the Republic of India and Japan) Rules, 2011
text:
(h) affixing or printing marks, labels and other like distinguishing signs on products or their packaging;

(i) simple mixing of products whether or not of different kinds;

(j) simple assembly of parts of goods to constitute a complete product;

(k) slaughter of animals;

(l) mere dilution with water or another substance that does not materially alter the characteristics of the goods; or

(m) any combination of operations referred to in clauses (a) to (l).

Explanation.- For the purposes of this rule, an operation is described as "simple" if neither special skills nor machines,
apparatus or equipment especially produced or installed for carrying it out are needed.

Rule 10. Consignment criteria. -

(1) An originating good of the other Party shall be deemed to meet the consignment criteria when it is,-

(a) transported directly from the other Party; or

(b) transported through one or more non-Parties for the purpose of transit or temporary storage in warehouses in such non-
Parties, subject to the condition that it does not undergo operations other than unloading, reloading and any other operation to
preserve it in good condition.

(2) If an originating good of the other Party does not meet the consignment criteria referred to in sub-rule (1), the good shall not
be considered as an originating good of the other Party.

Rule 11. Unassembled or disassembled goods. - 
Where a good satisfies the requirements of the relevant provisions of rule 3 to rule 9and is imported into a Party from the other
Party in an unassembled or disassembled form but is classified as an assembled good pursuant to clause (a)of rule 2 of the
General Rules for the Interpretation of the Harmonized System, such a good shall be considered as an originating good of the
other Party.

Rule 12. Fungible goods and materials. -

(1) For the purposes of determining whether a good qualifies as an originating good of a Party, where fungible originating
materials of the Party and fungible non-originating materials that are mixed in an inventory are used in the production of the
good, the origin of the materials may be determined pursuant to an inventory management method under the Generally
Accepted Accounting Principles in the Party.

(2) Where fungible originating goods of a Party and fungible non-originating goods are mixed in an inventory and, prior to
exportation do not undergo any production process or any operation in the Party where they were mixed other than unloading,
reloading or any other operation to preserve them in good condition, the origin of the good may be determined pursuant to an
inventory management method under the Generally Accepted Accounting Principles in the Party.

Rule 13. Indirect materials. -

Indirect materials shall be, without regard to where they are produced, considered to be originating materials of a Party where
the good is produced.

Rule 14. Accessories, spare parts, tools and instructional or other information materials. -

(1) In determining whether all the non-originating materials used in the production of a good undergo the applicable change in
tariff classification or a specific manufacturing or processing operation, accessories, spare parts, tools and instructional or other
information materials delivered with the good that form part of the good's standard accessories, spare parts, tools and
instructional or other information materials, shall be disregarded:

Provided that,-

(a) the accessories, spare parts, tools and instructional or other information materials are not invoiced separately from the good,
without regard to whether they are separately described in the invoice or not; and

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka under a job-work arrangement where the principal sends raw materials (exempt from GST) to the job worker, who processes and returns the finished goods. The job worker inadvertently crosses the ₹40 lakh turnover threshold mid-year. Analyze the cascading GST registration obligations, the ITC reversal requirements under Rule 44, the implications of the transitional period on already-issued challan under Form GST ITC-04, and whether the principal's ITC eligibility is affected retroactively under Section 19 read with Section 143 of the CGST Act.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a foreign MNC provides both software development services (under a cost-plus arrangement) and proprietary IP licensing (royalty-based) to its parent. The AO proposes to benchmark the bundled transaction as a single arrangement under the TNMM method, while the assessee argues for transaction-by-transaction analysis under CUP for the royalty component. Critically evaluate the TPO's authority under Rule 10B(1) to mandate aggregation, the applicability of the most appropriate method doctrine post-Maruti Suzuki v. CIT, the safe harbour provisions under Rules 10TA–10TG, and whether the BEPS Action 8–10 guidance on hard-to-value intangibles has been absorbed into Indian domestic TP regulations.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three distinct business verticals under a single GSTIN: (1) taxable supply of industrial machinery (18% GST), (2) exempt supply of agricultural equipment under Notification 2/2017-CT(Rate), and (3) zero-rated export of precision components under LUT. During FY 2024-25, the company procured a centralized ERP system used across all three verticals. Under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of the CGST Rules, how should the ITC on the ERP system be apportioned, and what adjustments are required if the exempt-to-taxable supply ratio changes by more than 10% during the annual reconciliation under Rule 42(2)? Additionally, if the company subsequently transfers the ERP license to its 100% subsidiary under Schedule I (deemed supply without consideration), how does this affect the already-claimed and apportioned ITC?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US-based MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15% using the TNMM. The AO contends that IndCo functionally qualifies as a 'entrepreneur' rather than a 'routine service provider' based on the economic substance test under the 2017 OECD BEPS Action 8-10 guidelines as adopted in Indian TP regulations, and proposes recharacterization to a profit-split method with IndCo bearing 40% of intangible development costs. Simultaneously, IndCo has received a royalty waiver from the parent which the AO treats as a deemed benefit under Section 28(iv). Analyze: (a) the legal threshold for recharacterizing a tested party from routine to entrepreneurial under Indian TP law post-2017, (b) whether the royalty waiver constitutes income under 28(iv) or a capital receipt, and (c) how the MAP provision under Article 25 of the India-US DTAA interacts with a pending domestic DRP objection under Section 144C.

title: Regarding Service Tax exemption for Janata Personal Accident Policy
text:
Circular No. 133 / 2 / 2011 - ST

F.No.332/36/2010-TRU

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Tax Research Unit

North Block, New Delhi

18 th January 2011

To

Chief Commissioners of Central Excise and Service Tax (All),

Director General (Service Tax),

Director General (Central Excise Intelligence),

Director General (Audit),

Commissioners of Service Tax (All),

Commissioners of Central Excise and Service Tax (All).

Madam/Sir,

Subject: - Service tax exemption for Janata Personal Accident Policy - reg.

A clarification has been sought as to the scope and meaning of the description namely 'Janata Personal Accident Policy
(JPAP)' appearing in the Notification No. 3/1994-ST dated 30.06.1994.

2. The issue has been examined. The representation seeking clarification arises in the context of customized group insurance
policy schemes known as JPAP, floated by various insurance companies as specified by state governments, to extend risk
cover to certain specified target populations, under varying terms of insurance. Generally, a standard JPAP is an individual
oriented policy with a fixed 'sum assured'. The sum assured in these JPAP policies is often as low as Rs. 25, 000/- , so that
even people without regular income can afford to purchase a risk cover for themselves. For the insurers, JPAP offers a vehicle
to fulfill the 'rural or social sector' obligation prescribed by the Insurance Regulatory Development Authority (IRDA). Since a
description of JPAP Policy is not available in the relevant notification, it is clarified that customized group JPAP insurance
schemes floated by various insurance companies as per the specifications of state governments concerned, to extend risk
cover to target populations, and to fulfill the prescribed 'rural or social sector' obligation, are covered by the subject service tax
exemption.

3. Trade Notice/Public Notice may be issued to the field formations accordingly.

4. Please acknowledge the receipt of this circular. Hindi version to follow.

(J. M. Kennedy)

Director, TRU

Tel: 011-23092634

questions.question: Your CBIC RAG system ingests 108k chunks using BGE-M3 embeddings at 5.2–9.6 chunks/second on a single GPU via Vulkan. When you scale to a second corpus (e.g., Income Tax Act) of comparable size, your Qdrant collection query latency degrades from 180ms to 620ms under concurrent load. Assuming your 4-core rig is already at 85% CPU during peak query time, what is your remediation strategy — do you shard by corpus into separate Qdrant collections, apply HNSW parameter tuning (ef_construction, m), or introduce a query-routing layer — and what are the specific trade-offs of each approach given your hardware constraints?

You are running qwen3-14b via llama-server on ROCm GPU 2 (port 9082) behind a LiteLLM gateway. A practitioner reports that appending `/no_think` to prompts reduces median response latency by ~40% but causes a measurable drop in multi-hop legal reasoning accuracy for queries requiring cross-section statutory interpretation. Design a routing policy that selects between thinking and no-thinking mode at query time — what signals (query length, keyword patterns, retrieval confidence scores, user session context) would you use, where in the stack would you intercept (LiteLLM middleware, FastAPI pre-handler, or client-side), and how would you validate that the policy improves the accuracy/latency Pareto frontier without introducing regression on your existing eval suite?

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives an SCN (Show Cause Notice) under Section 74 of CGST Act alleging suppression of facts for FY 2019-20. The company had availed ITC on capital goods procured under a sale-leaseback arrangement where the lessor subsequently went into CIRP under IBC. The department contends that since the lessor's GSTIN was cancelled retrospectively from the date of supply, the recipient's ITC is invalid under Rule 86A. However, the company argues that at the time of availing credit, all conditions under Section 16(2) were satisfied and the tax was reflected in GSTR-2A. Analyze the competing legal positions, identify the applicable circulars and judicial precedents, and advise on the strongest defense strategy including the limitation period argument.

A wholly-owned Indian subsidiary imports specialty chemicals from its Singapore parent. The customs department rejects the transaction value under Rule 12 of CVR 1988, invoking Rule 9 (computed value method) after exhausting Rules 4–8 sequentially, and enhances the assessable value by 40%. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer proposes a downward adjustment to the ALP under Section 92C of Income Tax Act using TNMM, which would imply the import price was HIGHER than arm's length. This creates a contradictory position: customs says price is too low, TP says price is too high. Explain the legal framework governing this conflict, which authority prevails, whether the CBIC-CBDT coordination circular provides relief, and draft the key arguments for an advance ruling application to prospectively resolve the valuation methodology.

title: Revised instruction for stuffing and sealing of reefer containers- reg.
text:
non-compliance to law, rules or regulations is noticed. The details of exporters who have 
been permitted such a facility along with the indicative list of export goods shall be sent 
to RMCC and the port of export by the jurisdictional Commissioner.

(iii) The request for supervised stuffing of reefer containers shall be given by the exporter at

least 24 hours prior to stuffing to the concerned Customs office indicating the nature of 
the temperature sensitive goods. The jurisdictional Commissioner of Customs shall 
depute officers to supervise examination, stuffing and sealing of the reefer containers.

(iv) The officers deputed for supervised stuffing would ensure that the reefer containers are

stuffed with only such export goods as are sensitive to temperature and in accordance 
with the details of the export goods list furnished by the exporter. The containers would 
be sealed with RFID e-seals procured by the exporters and the data as required shall be 
uploaded by exporter as per the already prescribed procedure.

(v) RMCC may take necessary steps to ensure that the reefer containers which have been

sealed under the supervision of the officers are not subjected to examination at the port 
of export.

(vi) The jurisdictional Commissionerate shall inform the said container details exporter wise

to RMCC on monthly basis. RMCC, in turn, shall verify the details with the facilitation 
provided to the respective exporters in respect of the said reefer containers and inform 
the jurisdictional Commissionerate in case of any discrepancy. The jurisdictional 
Commissioner of Customs shall, in the case of discrepancy, review the permission for 
supervised stuffing and sealing.

5. 
The above procedure prescribed in this circular shall be observed only at the request of

the exporter in respect of reefer cargo. In other cases, the existing self-sealing procedure with 
RFID e-seals would continue. MOT charges wherever applicable shall be paid at the prescribed 
rates. 
 
6. 
In respect of other types of sensitive cargo which may deteriorate or get contaminated

etc. upon opening of the containers, the Commissioner of Customs at the port may exercise 
suitable discretion for valid reasons to override the RMS instructions. However, while doing so, 
he/ she shall inform the RMCC of instances of overriding the instructions and reasons thereof. 
 
7. 
All the field formations are directed to make adequate arrangements for deputing CBIC

officials for supervising factory stuffing & E-sealing of reefer containers carrying export goods. 
 
8. 
Notwithstanding the above relaxation, the reefer container (s) may be selected for

scanning/ examination on specific information / intelligence or occasional random basis or where 
it has come to notice that the RFID seal has been tampered with after being sealed in the presence 
of the Customs officer.

9. 
Difficulties, if any, may be brought to the notice of the Board. Hindi version follows.

Yours faithfully,

(Zubair Riaz)

Director(Customs)

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU achieving 5.2–9.6 chunks/second. Retrieval latency under concurrent load spikes to 4–6 seconds despite async FastAPI workers. Given a 4-core CPU, a shared Vulkan GPU handling both embedding inference and LLM serving (qwen3-14b), and Qdrant as the vector store, describe the exact sequence of diagnostic steps and architectural changes you would implement to achieve sub-2-second P95 query latency without adding hardware. Include specific tool commands, configuration parameters, and the order in which you would apply changes to avoid compounding bottlenecks.

A practitioner deploys a RAG system over heterogeneous Indian tax law corpora (CBIC circulars, GST FAQs, Income Tax notifications) where 471 PDFs are image-only scans with no extractable text. The system must answer multi-hop queries (e.g., 'What is the GST rate for X under notification Y, as amended by circular Z?') with citation accuracy sufficient for legal use. The embedding model was trained on general text, not legal Hindi-English code-switching. Describe a complete trustability architecture: how you would handle OCR fallback selection (VLM vs. CPU OCR), chunk boundary strategies for multi-page legal tables, cross-document citation linking, and confidence scoring—including how you would detect and surface retrieval failures rather than hallucinating answers.

questions.question: A composite supplier provides works contract services for construction of an immovable property to a registered recipient who is also engaged in exempt supplies. The recipient has a common input tax credit reversal obligation under Rule 42. The works contract supplier has sub-contracted 40% of the job to another registered entity. Analyze the ITC eligibility chain across all three entities, identify where Section 17(5)(c) and 17(5)(d) restrictions apply, and determine the net ITC reversal quantum at the recipient level assuming total input services credit is ₹18,00,000 and the exempt-to-total supply ratio is 0.35.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Customs & GST Interface
questions.question: An Indian importer clears capital goods under the EPCG scheme with a specific export obligation. Post-import, the importer also claims IGST refund under the LUT route for zero-rated exports. During a GST audit, the department contends that the imported capital goods were used partially for domestic taxable supply (60%) and partially for export (40%), triggering proportionate ITC reversal under Rule 43 read with Section 17(2). Simultaneously, the DGFT raises a redemption fine for shortfall in export obligation. Evaluate the legal sustainability of the department's ITC reversal demand, the interaction between Customs duty exemption benefits and GST ITC, and the correct methodology to compute the net tax liability or refund entitlement.

title: Notifies that Black Pepper shall be subjected to quality control and inspection prior to export.
text:
(5) In the case of vessels which had contained products blended with leaded fluid, the regulations 
laid down by the suppliers of the fluid shall be fully observed. 
 
172 Fire Control. – 
 (1) Every crude oil refinery shall be fully protected against fire by a well organized and trained fire 
fighting service with necessary materials and fixed, mobile and portable equipments for fighting 
fires in line with the OISD Standard –116. This will apply to new crude oil refineries/gas 
processing installation approved by the Chief Controller after publication of this rule. 
(2) An adequate supply of water shall be available at all strategic points in the refinery by means 
of an independent ring main or grid provided with isolating valves. The main shall be kept 
constantly pressurized by two or more boosting pumps of adequate capacity preferably working 
automatically as any significant loss of pressure in the main occurs. At least one of the boosting 
pumps shall be independent of the normal power supply.  
(3) All mains shall be fitted with hydrants at convenient places not more than thirty metres apart in 
hazardous areas and not more than 45 meteres apart in non hazardous area. Such hydrants shall 
be of design suitable for operating conditions and for connecting mobile pumps. 
(4) Static water supply of adequate capacity shall be provided where mains water supply may be 
subject to interference. 
(5) All refinery personnel shall be practiced in the use of first-aid, fire-fighting appliances and 
selected refinery personnel shall be trained in all aspects of fire-fighting.  
(6) The Chief Controller may relax any of the provisions of sub-rule (1) to (5) or require additional 
fire-fighting provisions to be made if he deems such relaxation or additional fire-fighting provisions 
necessary in respect of any class of refinery. 
 
173. Removal of Petroleum. –  
All petroleum as it leaves the stills with the exception of such quantities as may be pumped direct 
to service tanks for fuel, shall at once be pumped out into the refinery storage tanks and shall not 
be stored in the immediate neighbourhood of stills and boilers: Provided that the Chief Controller 
may permit petroleum to be disposed of otherwise. 
 
174. Prevention of danger from static electricity. –  
Adequate provision shall be made to prevent accumulation of dangerous static charges of  
electricity. 
 
175. Warning notices. –  
(1) Warning notices regarding prevention of unauthorized persons, naked lights, smoking and 
other hazards shall be displayed prominently at strategic locations in the refinery. 
(2) No fire works of any kind shall be carried out within a radius of one Kilometre from the refinery 
except with the prior permission of District Magistrate 
 
176. Marking of pipelines and cables. –  
(1) All above-ground pipelines and cables shall be identified by taping, stenciling, colouring  
distinctively or by any other suitable methods. 
(2) All overhead pipelines and cables crossing roads shall be adequately protected against 
accidental damage. 
(3) Pipelines and valves at loading and discharging berths shall be prominently marked to identify 
the product. 
(4) The route of all underground cables shall be marked by prominent markers. At least two such 
markers shall be visible from any point on the route of the cable.  
(5) The route of all underground pipelines shall be marked by prominent markers or by any other 
effective means to prevent accidental damage to the pipelines. 
 
177. Inspections.

questions.question: A holding company provides centralized IT infrastructure, HR, and treasury management services to its wholly-owned subsidiaries under a cost-sharing arrangement. The subsidiaries are located in different states. The holding company has obtained a single GSTIN in its principal place of business state. Advise on: (a) whether cross-charge is mandatory or optional under Schedule I read with Section 7(1)(c) of CGST Act, (b) how to determine the taxable value when no invoice is raised and actual cost allocation is used, (c) the correct place of supply for each service category under IGST Act, (d) ITC eligibility in the hands of the recipient subsidiary, and (e) the consequences if the holding company has obtained ISD registration instead of cross-charging—can it switch mid-year and what transitional adjustments are needed?

An Indian manufacturer imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously uses those goods to manufacture products for both export (60%) and domestic sale (40%). The goods are also partially used for job-work done for a related foreign entity (AE) at a price benchmarked using the TNMM method. Three years into the scheme: (a) customs duty saved exceeds ₹5 crore; (b) actual export obligation fulfilled is 180% against a 600% requirement on NFE basis; (c) the AE transaction has been reopened by the TPO proposing an upward adjustment of ₹2.1 crore. Analyze: (i) proportionate duty demand and interest liability under EPCG if domestic-use goods are excluded from export-obligation calculation, (ii) whether the TP adjustment creates a deemed import under Customs Valuation Rules and triggers re-assessment of assessable value, (iii) FEMA implications if the AE adjustment results in short realization of export proceeds beyond the RBI-permitted period, and (iv) the correct sequence of adjudications across DGFT, Customs, Income Tax, and RBI to minimize cascading penalties.

title: Exempts certain goods when brought into 100% EOU / STP complex.
text:
ted 4-1-95), 10/95-Central Excise dated the 23 rd February, 1995 (G.S.R. 87(E) dated 23 rd

February, 1995), 37/99-Central Excise dated, the 15th September, 1999( G.S.R 637 (E), dated the 15 th September, 1999)
and 37/2000- Central Excise, dated the 8 th May, 2000 (G.S.R 414(E) dated the 8th May, 2000), are hereby rescinded.

13.         This notification shall come into force on the 1 st day of April, 2003.

Explanation - For the purposes of this notification,-

(i)
"Board of Approval" means the authority appointed by the Central Government in exercise of the powers conferred by
section 14 of the Industries (Development or Regulation) Act, 1951(65 of 1951) and the rules made under that Act;

(ii)
" Domestic Tariff Area" means area except special economic zone, export oriented undertakings, Software Technology
Park unit and Electronic Hardware Technology Park unit;

(iii)

"Export and Import Policy" means Export and Import Policy, 1 April 2002 - 31 March 2007, published by the Government
of India in the Ministry of Commerce and Industry vide notification No. 1 (RE-2003) /2002-07, dated 31 st March, 2003, as
amended from time to time;

(iv) "export oriented undertaking" has the same meaning as assigned to "hundred percent. export oriented undertaking" in

clause (ii) to the Explanation of sub-section (1) of section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 ( 1 of 1944);

(v)

"Electronic Hardware Technology Park (EHTP) unit" means a unit established under and in accordance with the
Electronic Hardware Technology Park Scheme notified by the Government of India in the Ministry of Commerce, vide
notification No. 5(RE-95)/92-97, dated 30 th April, 1995 and approved by the Inter Ministerial Standing Committee;

(vi)

"Handbook of Procedures, Volume I " means Handbook of Procedures Volume I, 2002-2007 published by the
Government of India, Ministry of Commerce and Industry vide Public Notice No. 1(RE-2003)/2002-07, dated 31 st March,
2003, as amended from time to time;

(vii) "Inter-Ministerial Standing Committee" means a committee appointed by the Government of India in the Ministry of

Industry (Department of Industrial Development) vide notification No. S.O. 117(E), dated the 22 nd February, 1993;

(viii)

"Software Technology Park (STP) unit" means a unit established under and in accordance with Scheme notified by the
Government of India in the Ministry of Commerce and Industry vide notification No. 4(RE-95)/92-95, dated 30 th April,
1995 and approved by the Inter- Ministerial Standing Committee;

(ix) "special economic zone" has the same meaning as assigned to it in clause (iii) to the Explanation 2 of sub-section (1) of

section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 ( 1 of 1944).

ANNEXURE - I

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. Post-export, the foreign buyer rejects 30% of the consignment due to quality defects and returns it to India under a re-import. The manufacturer has already claimed deemed export benefits on the full shipment. How should the manufacturer reverse the input tax credit, handle the re-imported goods under Customs, and reconcile the GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, and ICEGATE filings to avoid show-cause notices under Section 74 of CGST Act, 2017?

An Indian subsidiary of a multinational provides IT-enabled services exclusively to its foreign parent. The AO proposes to apply the Profit Split Method (PSM) instead of the assessee's chosen TNMM, arguing that both entities make unique and valuable contributions. The subsidiary has no comparables with operating margins above 22% in the CMIE database for the relevant AY. What is the step-by-step TP documentation strategy the assessee should build — including selection of tested party, financial indicator, multiple-year data use under Rule 10B(4), and the arguments to rebut PSM applicability — to defend before the TPO and subsequently the ITAT?

title: The Finance Act, 2014
text:
40
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY
[PART II—

Paragraph E

In the case of a company,—

Rates of income-tax

I. In the case of a domestic company
30 per cent. of the
total income;

II. In the case of a company other than a domestic company—

(i) on so much of the total income as consists of,—

(a) royalties received from Government or an Indian concern in
pursuance of an agreement made by it with the Government or the Indian
concern after the 31st day  of March, 1961 but before the 1st day of April,
1976; or

(b) fees for rendering technical services received from Government
or an Indian concern in pursuance of an agreement made by it with the
Government or the Indian concern after the 29th day of February, 1964 but
before the 1st day of April, 1976,

and  where  such  agreement  has,  in  either  case,  been approved by the Central Government

50 per cent.;

(ii) on the balance, if any, of the total income

40 per cent.

Surcharge on income-tax

The amount of income-tax computed in accordance with the preceding provisions of this Paragraph, or in
section 111A or section 112, shall, in the case of every company, be increased by a surcharge for the purposes
of the Union calculated,—

(i)
in the case of every domestic company––

(a) having a total income exceeding one crore rupees, but not exceeding ten crore rupees, at
the rate of five per cent. of such income-tax; and

(b) having a total income exceeding ten crore rupees, at the rate of ten per cent. of such
income-tax;

(ii) in the case of every company other than a domestic company––

(a) having a total income exceeding one crore rupees but not exceeding ten crore rupees, at
the rate of two per cent. of such income-tax; and

(b) having a total income exceeding ten crore rupees, at the rate of five per cent. of such
income-tax:

Provided that in the case of every company having a total income exceeding one crore rupees but not
exceeding ten crore rupees, the total amount payable as income-tax and surcharge on such income shall not
exceed the total amount payable as income-tax on a total income of one crore rupees by more than the amount
of income that exceeds one crore rupees:

Provided further that in the case of every company having a total income exceeding ten crore rupees, the
total amount payable as income-tax and surcharge on such income shall not exceed the total amount payable as
income-tax and surcharge on a total income of ten crore rupees by more than the amount of income that exceeds
ten crore rupees.

questions.question: A manufacturer operates three distinct units under the same PAN: Unit A (registered under composition scheme in Maharashtra), Unit B (regular taxpayer in Gujarat exporting goods with LUT), and Unit C (an SEZ unit in Karnataka). Unit B supplies inputs to Unit C under a tax invoice. Unit A occasionally provides job work services to Unit B. Analyze: (1) Whether ITC claimed by Unit B on inputs sent to Unit C is permissible and under which provision, (2) Whether Unit A's job work services to Unit B trigger GST liability despite composition scheme registration, and (3) How the annual aggregate turnover is computed for threshold and composition eligibility when all three units are under one PAN but different GSTINs.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A listed Indian company receives a foreign currency convertible bond (FCCB) loan from a Mauritius-based entity, converts it to equity after 3 years at a price below market value on the conversion date, and simultaneously holds a 35% stake in a domestic subsidiary that has accumulated losses exceeding its paid-up capital. Analyze: (1) The taxability of the FCCB conversion gain/loss in the hands of the Indian company under the Income Tax Act and applicable DTAA provisions, (2) Whether the subsidiary's accumulated losses trigger section 56(2)(x) deemed gift provisions for the parent upon fresh equity issuance at a discount, (3) GST implications if the Indian company charges a 'management fee' to the subsidiary for financial restructuring advisory services, and (4) Transfer pricing documentation requirements under section 92 for all three transactions.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has three distinct business verticals operating under a single GSTIN: (1) taxable supply of goods at 18%, (2) exempt supply of agricultural produce, and (3) zero-rated export of software services. During FY 2024-25, the company procures a unified ERP system worth ₹50 lakhs used across all three verticals. Under Section 17(2) read with Rule 42 of the CGST Rules, calculate the admissible ITC on the ERP system, identifying whether the apportionment must be done on a month-by-month basis or aggregate annual basis, and explain how a retrospective amendment to the exempt turnover figure (due to a revised contract) in Q3 would affect the already-filed GSTR-3B for Q1 and Q2, including the applicable interest liability under Section 50.

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a related party (holding 30% equity) in Germany under a long-term supply agreement that includes a deferred royalty clause: 2% of net sales of goods manufactured using the machinery, payable annually for 5 years. The invoice value is USD 1,00,000. Customs clears the goods under Rule 3(1) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007. Two years later, a transfer pricing audit under Section 92CA of the Income Tax Act revises the arm's length price of the machinery upward by 15%. Analyze whether: (a) the royalty payments constitute 'condition of sale' under Rule 10(1)(c) and must be added to transaction value at the time of import or each year of payment; (b) the TP adjustment triggers a re-assessment under Section 17(5) of the Customs Act; and (c) the importer can claim a refund under Section 27 if the revised TP price is lower than the enhanced customs value already paid, including the limitation period implications.

title: Publication of Daily Lists of Imports and Exports(Amendment) Rules, 2012
text:
[To be published in Gazette of India Part -II-Section 3-Sub-sections-(i), Extraordinary]

Government of India

Ministry of finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise and Customs

Notification No.18/2012 - Customs (N.T.)

New Delhi, 5 th March 2012.

GSR (E) In exercise of the powers conferred by section 156 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central Government
hereby makes the following amendments to the Publication of Daily List of Imports and Exports Rules, namely:-

1. Short title and commencement . - (1) These Rules may be called the Publication of Daily Lists of Imports and Exports
(Amendment) Rules, 2012.

(2)       They shall come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.

2 .         In the Publication of Daily List of Imports and Exports Rules (hereinafter referred to as the principal Rules), for rule 1, the
following rule shall be substituted, namely:-

"1. Short title and commencement . - (1) These Rules may be called the Publication of Daily Lists of Imports and Exports
Rules, 2004.";

3. In the principal Rules as so amended, -

(a)                in rule 2, in sub-rule (1), for the words "port functioning", the words "functioning  port, airport, inland container depot
or land customs stations" shall be substituted;

(b)                for rule 3, the following rule shall be substituted, namely:-

"3 .        Particulars of publication. - (1) The particulars referred to in sub-rule (1) of rule 2 shall be the following:-

Daily list of imports-

1.     Port or country of origin

2.     Description of goods

3.     Customs tariff heading

4.     Quantity

5.     Unit quantity code

6.     Value of goods (in rupees)

Daily list of exports-

1.     Port of destination

2.     Description of goods

3.     Customs tariff heading

4.     Quantity

5.     Unit quantity code

6.     Value of goods (in rupees)."

[ F. No.401/234/2010-Cus.III]

(Vikas)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note : The principal notification No. 128/2004-Customs(N.T.), dated the 19th November, 2004 was published in the Gazette
of India Extraordinary, Part-II, Section 3, Sub section (i) vide G.S.R.758(E), dated the 19 th November 2004 .

questions.question: A manufacturer operating under a job-work arrangement under Section 143 of the CGST Act sends semi-finished goods to a job worker located in another state. The job worker further sends the goods to a second job worker in yet another state for additional processing, before returning the finished goods directly to the principal's customer under a delivery challan. The principal has not obtained registration in either job worker's state. During this process, the first job worker inadvertently crosses the 1-year time limit for returning the goods. Analyze: (a) whether the principal must reverse ITC and on which date, (b) whether the first job worker's supply to the second job worker constitutes an independent taxable supply and who is liable to pay tax, (c) whether the direct dispatch to the customer from the second job worker's premises is permissible without the goods returning to the principal's place of business, and (d) what documentary trail — challans, e-way bills, and GST returns — must be maintained by each party to remain compliant.

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign supplier under a long-term supply agreement that includes: (i) a base transaction value, (ii) a post-import royalty payable to a third-party IP holder conditioned on the importer's domestic resale price exceeding a threshold, (iii) a volume-based year-end rebate credited to the importer's account with the supplier, and (iv) tooling costs amortized over 3 years and invoiced separately. Customs has provisionally assessed the bill of entry. Subsequently, the importer receives a transfer-pricing adjustment that retroactively increases the transaction value. Analyze: (a) which of the four elements must be added to transaction value under Rule 10 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 and the legal basis for each inclusion or exclusion, (b) how the retrospective TP adjustment interacts with the finalization of the provisional assessment under Section 18 of the Customs Act, (c) whether the royalty is includible given it is paid to a third party rather than the seller, and (d) the procedural steps and timelines the importer must follow to avoid interest and penalty exposure once the TP order is received.

Subject: Export Policy of Fertilisers under Chapter 31 of ITC(HS) Classification of Export and Import Items 2018

S.O. 86(E).—In exercise of the powers conferred by Section 3 of the Foreign Trade (Development &

(FTP), 2015-2020, the Central Government hereby makes the amendments, with immediate effect, in Chapter 31 of 
ITC(HS) Classification - Schedule 2 (Export Policy):

2. The existing entries against Chapter 31 of ITC(HS) Classification - Schedule 2 (Export Policy) will be

Chapter 31

FERTILISERS

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (wholly-owned subsidiary) at ₹800/unit while the open market value for identical goods to unrelated buyers is ₹1,200/unit. The recipient is eligible for full ITC. Under Rule 28 of CGST Rules 2017, can the supplier declare ₹800 as transaction value, and if so, what are the conditions precedent? If the recipient's ITC eligibility subsequently changes mid-year due to an exempt supply becoming predominant (aggregate exempt turnover crosses 50%), what is the supplier's retrospective valuation obligation, if any, and what mechanism under Section 17(2) applies to the recipient?

An Indian importer purchases software embedded on a physical medium (USB drives) from a US parent company. The invoice value is USD 5,000 for the physical media plus USD 95,000 declared as 'royalty for software license.' Customs proposes to include the royalty in the assessable value under Rule 10(1)(c) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007. The importer argues the royalty is for post-importation use and is not a condition of sale of the goods. Simultaneously, the software attracts NIL BCD under ITC(HS) heading 8523 80 20 but the physical medium attracts 10% BCD. Analyze (i) whether the royalty is includible, (ii) how to split the assessable value between the medium and the software, and (iii) the interaction with the IGST levy under Section 3(7) of the Customs Tariff Act where the software may also be taxable as an OIDAR service under GST.

questions.question: A holding company (H) incorporated in India holds 100% equity in a wholly-owned subsidiary (S). H provides corporate guarantee to a bank securing a term loan taken by S. The bank charges S a guarantee fee of 1.2% p.a. on the outstanding loan amount. Simultaneously, H seconds three senior executives to S under a cost-sharing arrangement, recovering only the salary and statutory benefits with no markup. GST department issues a SCN alleging: (i) H's corporate guarantee constitutes a taxable supply under Schedule II para 2(d) read with Section 7(1)(c), and the open-market value under Rule 28 proviso should be the bank's own guarantee fee, not nil; (ii) the secondment constitutes manpower supply under SAC 9985 and H must charge GST on the recovered cost. Evaluate the legal sustainability of both allegations, citing the post-Alom Extrusions amendment to Rule 28, the Board Circular 210/4/2024-GST, and the Supreme Court's holding in Northern Operating Systems. What is the correct GST treatment and what is the exposure if the SCN succeeds?

An Indian importer (I) sources specialty chemicals from its parent company (P) in Germany. The declared transaction value is €100/kg. Customs runs a contemporaneous import data query and finds identical goods imported by an unrelated Indian buyer from an unrelated German seller at €82/kg CIF. The department rejects the transaction value under Rule 3(3) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, alleging influence of the relationship, and proposes to re-assess at €82/kg under Rule 5 (identical goods). I contests: (a) the contemporaneous imports are of different commercial quantities (5 MT vs. I's 500 MT shipment), and quantity discounts should be allowed under Rule 5(1)(b); (b) I submits a transfer-pricing study accepted by the income-tax authority showing arm's-length price at €98/kg; and (c) I offers a computed value under Rule 7 showing cost-of-production plus profit at €95/kg. Analyze whether the TP study acceptance binds Customs, how the CESTAT and Supreme Court have treated the interplay of Section 14 with Rule 3(3)(b) in related-party transactions, and what valuation method and final assessable value should apply.

title: Notifies that Fruit & Vegetable Products shall be subjected to quality control and inspection prior to export.
text:

agency shall at all times have free access to all parts of the establishments and records pertaining 
to the control exercised by the establishment for hygienic handling and processing of food 
products during all stages of production, storage and transport.

(7) The Council shall maintain the list of all approved establishments, each of which shall have an 
official number.

(8)  (1) An exporter covered under clause (b) of sub-rule (1) intending to export   fruitand vegetable 
products shall give intimation in writing to the nearest office of the agency to carry out the 
consignment- wise inspection.

(2)   Every intimation under sub-rule (1) shall be given-

(i) 
not less than three days before the inspection is to be carried out    at the establishment  
situated at the same station where the office of the agency is located; and

(ii) not less than seven days before the inspection is to be carried out at the establishment 
which is not situated at the same station where the office of the agency is located.

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement where Company A (recipient) issues a purchase order to Company B (supplier), who ships directly to Company C (actual recipient of goods) on behalf of Company A. Company A's registered office is in Maharashtra, Company B is in Gujarat, and Company C's factory is in Karnataka. The invoice is raised by B on A (IGST), and A issues a tax invoice to C (IGST). During an audit, the GST officer contends that the movement of goods is from Gujarat to Karnataka and therefore the place of supply for B→A transaction should be Karnataka under Section 10(1)(b) of CGST Act, making it an inter-state supply with IGST applicable, but disputes A's ITC claim on the grounds that A never physically received the goods. Separately, the officer argues that E-way bill provisions were violated since A generated an E-way bill for B→C leg without being the consignor. Analyze the legal validity of ITC entitlement for Company A, the correct determination of place of supply for both legs, and the E-way bill compliance obligations, citing specific sections and relevant AAR/AAAR rulings.

An Indian importer purchases specialty chemicals from a related party (holding 35% equity) in Germany under a long-term supply agreement that includes: (a) a base price per MT, (b) a retrospective price adjustment clause triggered if the seller's raw material costs exceed a threshold (settled 18 months post-import via debit note), (c) a royalty of 3% of net sales payable to the same German entity for use of the manufacturing process IP, and (d) a warranty cost reimbursement of 0.5% deducted from each invoice. The customs officer rejects the transaction value under Rule 3 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007 citing relationship influence, and proposes: including the retrospective debit note amount in assessable value for the original Bills of Entry; adding the royalty as a condition of sale under Rule 10(1)(c); and rejecting the warranty deduction as an impermissible post-importation cost adjustment. The importer argues the price was settled consistent with industry benchmarks (supported by a transfer pricing study accepted by income tax authorities). Evaluate the customs officer's three contentions individually under the Customs Act 1962 and CVR 2007, address whether TP study acceptance by CBDT creates any estoppel or evidentiary weight in customs proceedings, and specify the procedural remedy if the importer disagrees with the final assessment order.

title: Seeks to impose anti-dumping duty on imports of 'Chlorinated Polyvinyl Chloride (CPVC) Resin- whether or not further processed into compound' originating in or exported from China PR and Korea RP
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, 
SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No. 05/2020-Customs (ADD)

New Delhi, the  7th March, 2020

G.S.R. --(E). – Whereas, in the matter of import of ‘Chlorinated Polyvinyl Chloride Resin

(CPVC)-whether or not further processed into compound’ (hereinafter referred to as the subject

goods), falling under heading 3904 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of

1975) (hereinafter referred to as the said Customs Tariff Act), originating in, or exported from

China PR and Korea RP (hereinafter referred to as the subject countries) and imported into India,

the designated authority vide its preliminary findings No. 6/3/2019-DGTR, dated the 12th July,

2019, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 12th July,

2019, had recommended imposition of provisional anti-dumping duty on the imports of subject

goods, originating in or exported from subject countries;

And whereas, on the basis of aforesaid findings of the designated authority, the Central

Government had imposed provisional anti-dumping duty on the subject goods vide notification

of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 33/2019-

Customs (ADD), dated the 26th August, 2019, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary,

Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G. S. R. 600 (E), dated the 26th August, 2019;

And whereas, the designated authority in its final findings vide notification No. 6/3/2019-

DGTR, dated the 19th February, 2020, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I,

Section 1, dated the 19th February, 2020, has come to conclusion that-

(i) 
the product under consideration has been exported to India from the subject countries

below normal value;

(ii) 
such dumped imports of the subject goods from the subject countries have caused

material retardation to the establishment of the domestic industry;

and has recommended imposition of definitive anti-dumping duty equal to the lesser of the

margin of dumping and the margin of injury, so as to remove the injury to the domestic industry;

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (wholly-owned subsidiary) at a price below cost. The supplier claims the transaction qualifies as an 'open market value' transaction under Rule 28 of the CGST Rules, 2017, because the recipient is eligible for full ITC. The department issues a SCN alleging undervaluation and proposes to determine value at cost plus 10% markup under Rule 30. The assessee argues that since the recipient can claim full ITC, there is no revenue loss and Rule 28 proviso applies. Analyze the legal sustainability of the department's position, the evidentiary burden on each party, and whether the 'no revenue loss' argument can override the department's power to invoke Rule 30 in a related-party transaction where invoice value is demonstrably below cost.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A private limited company receives a loan of ₹5 crore from its holding company. The loan is later converted into equity shares at a premium determined by a SEBI-registered valuer using DCF methodology. The Income Tax department treats the share premium as 'income from other sources' under Section 56(2)(viib), arguing the fair market value under Rule 11UA (net asset value method) is lower than the issue price. Simultaneously, the GST department issues a notice claiming the loan-to-equity conversion constitutes a 'supply of service' (agreeing to tolerate an act) under Entry 5(e) of Schedule II and demands GST on notional interest for the pre-conversion period. Evaluate: (a) whether the DCF-based valuation can override Rule 11UA for Section 56(2)(viib) purposes post the 2023 amendment, (b) the constitutional and statutory validity of the GST department's 'deemed supply' theory on loan conversion, and (c) how the company should structure its legal response to avoid double jeopardy across both proceedings.

title: Imposition of provisional anti-dumping duty on Cellophane Transparent Film
text:
30 th March, 2006

Notification No. 31/2006-Customs

Whereas, in the matter of import of Cellophane Transparent Film (herein after also referred to as the subject goods), falling
under the Heading 3920 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) (hereinafter referred to as the said
Customs Tariff Act), originating in or exported from the People's Republic of China, (hereinafter referred to as the subject
country), the designated authority in its preliminary findings vide notification   No.14/7/2005 -DGAD, dated the 3 rd February,
2006, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 3 rd February, 2006, has come to the
conclusion that-

(i)    the subject goods have been exported to India from the subject country below its normal value;

(ii)    the domestic industry has suffered material injury;

(iii)    the injury has been caused by the dumped imports from subject country; and

exported from the subject country.

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 9A of the said Customs Tariff Act, read with

questions.question: A registered manufacturer supplies goods to an SEZ unit under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). During an audit, the GST officer disallows the zero-rated supply claim on the grounds that the LUT was filed after the date of supply but before the due date of the relevant GSTR-1. The officer raises a demand under Section 74 alleging suppression of tax liability. The taxpayer contends that the LUT filing date should not affect the zero-rating benefit since the export intent was established by the Bill of Lading. Analyze the legal validity of the officer's demand, the relevant circular or notification governing LUT ratification, and the evidentiary weight of the Bill of Lading in establishing zero-rated supply intent under IGST Act Section 16(3).
questions.expected_reasoning_depth: Must cite IGST Act S.16(3), CGST Rule 96A, Circular 4/4/2017-GST, distinguish between procedural lapse vs. substantive non-compliance, address suppression mens rea threshold under S.74 vs. S.73, and evaluate whether a post-facto LUT cures the deficiency retroactively.

questions.question: An Indian subsidiary imports specialised machinery components from its foreign parent at a declared transaction value. Customs authorities reject the transaction value under Rule 12 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, invoking Rule 9 (computed value method) after finding that comparable uncontrolled prices are unavailable and deductive value cannot be computed due to the unique nature of the goods. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer proposes an upward adjustment under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act, treating the same transaction as under-priced for income tax purposes — a directly contradictory position. The assessee argues that the two authorities cannot simultaneously treat the same transaction as both overpriced (Customs) and underpriced (TP). Evaluate the legal framework for resolving this contradiction, the binding effect of Customs valuation on TP assessment and vice versa, the role of the Safe Harbour Rules, and which authority's determination should prevail or whether a reconciliation mechanism exists under Indian law.
questions.expected_reasoning_depth: Must address Customs Valuation Rules 2007 hierarchy (Rules 4–9), Section 92C read with Rule 10B of Income Tax Rules, the lack of a statutory reconciliation mechanism between Customs Act and Income Tax Act, CBIC Circular 5/2016-Customs on ALP alignment, the doctrine of 'one transaction two statutes', and whether the assessee can invoke estoppel against either authority.

title: Regarding prosecution provision in Finance Act, 1994
text:
7. Clause (b) of section 89(1) of Finance Act, 1994, refers to the availment and utilization of the credit of taxes paid without
actual receipt of taxable service or excisable goods. It may be noted that in order to constitute an offence under this clause the
taxpayer must both avail as well as utilize the credit without having actually received the goods or the service. The clause is not
meant to apply to situations where an invoice has been issued for a service yet to be provided on which due tax has been paid.
It is only meant for such invoices that are typically known as "fake" where the tax has not been paid at the so called service
provider's end or where the provider stated in the invoice is non-existent. It will also cover situations where the value of the
service stated in the invoice and/or tax thereon have been altered with a view to avail Cenvat credit in excess of the amount
originally stated. While calculating the monetary limit for the purpose of launching prosecution, the value shall be the amount
availed as credit in excess of the amount originally stated in the invoice.

8. Clause (c) of section 89(1) of Finance Act, 1994, is based on similar provision in the central excise law. It should be noted
that the offence in relation to maintenance of false books of accounts or failure to supply the required information or supplying
of false information, should be in material particulars have a bearing on the tax liability. Mere expression of opinions shall not
be covered by the said clause. Supplying false information, in response to summons, will also be covered under this provision.

9. Clause (d) of section 89(1) of Finance Act, 1994, will apply only when the amount has been collected as service tax. It is not
meant to apply to mere non-payment of service tax when due. This provision would be attracted when the amount was reflected
in the invoices as service tax, service receiver has already made the payment and the period of six months has elapsed from
the date on which the service provider was required to pay the tax to the Central Government. Where the service receiver has
made part payment, the service provider will be punishable to the extent he has failed to deposit the tax due to the Government.

10. Certain sections of the Central Excise Act, 1944, have been made applicable to service tax by section 83 of Finance Act,
1994. Section 9AA of the Central Excise Act provides that where an offence has been committed by a company, in addition to
the company, every person who was in charge of the company and responsible for conduct of the business, at the time when
offence was committed, can be deemed guilty of an offence and can be proceeded against. A person so charged, however
has an option to establish that offence was committed without his knowledge or he had exercised all due diligence to prevent
the commission of offence.

11. Section 9C of Central Excise Act, 1944, which is made applicable to Finance Act, 1994, provides that in any prosecution
for an offence, existence of culpable mental state shall be presumed by the court. Therefore each offence described in section
89(1) of the Finance Act, 1994, has an inherent mens rea . Delinquency by the defaulter of service tax itself establishes his
'guilt'. If the accused claims that he did not have guilty mind, it is for him to prove the same beyond reasonable doubt. Thus
"burden of proof regarding non existence of 'mens rea' is on the accused".

12. It may be noted that in terms of section 89(3) of Finance Act, 1994, the following grounds are not considered special and
adequate reasons for awarding reduced imprisonment:

questions.question: A manufacturer has a factory with three distinct production lines: Line A produces exempted goods, Line B produces taxable goods at 18% GST, and Line C produces zero-rated exports. Common overhead costs (electricity, factory rent, administrative salaries) are incurred for all three lines in a ratio that varies month-to-month based on production volumes. The manufacturer also has capital goods (machinery) used exclusively on Line B that were purchased two years ago, and new capital goods purchased this year that are used across all three lines. Separately, the manufacturer has a registered office in a different state that provides management services to the factory. Given Rule 42, Rule 43, and Section 17(2) of the CGST Act, calculate the exact methodology to determine the admissible ITC for: (a) common overhead costs for the current tax period, (b) the new common capital goods over their 60-month useful life, (c) the cross-state management services ISD distribution, and identify all compliance filings, reversal obligations, and annual reconciliation requirements under Rule 44A.

An Indian importer purchases identical goods from two related foreign suppliers: Supplier X (parent company holding 51% equity in the Indian importer) located in Country A which has an active anti-dumping duty notification under Section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, and Supplier Y (unrelated entity) in Country B which is a member of a preferential trade agreement with India. The goods from Supplier X arrive with a declared transaction value. The goods from Supplier Y arrive under a PTA with claimed preferential origin. The department proposes to reject the transaction value for Supplier X citing relationship influence under Rule 3(3) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, and separately questions the Certificate of Origin for Supplier Y alleging insufficient value addition. Analyze: (a) the burden-of-proof framework and sequential valuation methods the importer must exhaust before deductive/computed value applies, (b) whether anti-dumping duty applies on the Supplier X goods if transaction value is ultimately rejected and customs value is re-determined upward, (c) the documentary and procedural requirements to defend the preferential origin claim for Supplier Y including the substantial transformation test, and (d) the appeals pathway and provisional assessment options available simultaneously for both consignments.

questions.question: A manufacturing entity operating under a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) unit in Maharashtra supplies goods to both its DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) customers and overseas buyers. During the financial year, the unit also receives input services from both SEZ and non-SEZ suppliers. Given that the unit's Input Tax Credit (ITC) is partially attributable to zero-rated supplies (exports and SEZ supplies) and partially to taxable domestic supplies, explain the complete methodology under Rule 89 of CGST Rules for claiming refund of unutilized ITC, distinguishing between the 'inverted duty structure' refund route and the 'zero-rated supply without payment of tax' route. Additionally, address how the Annual Return reconciliation under GSTR-9 and GSTR-9C must reflect these dual refund streams, and what documentary evidence is required for each stream to survive a GST audit.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US-based multinational enters into three categories of inter-company transactions: (1) software development services provided to the parent at a cost-plus margin, (2) receipt of management fees for shared services allocated using a headcount-based cost allocation key, and (3) an intra-group loan denominated in USD carrying a LIBOR-linked interest rate now transitioning to SOFR. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) disputes all three using different methods — TNMM for (1), CUP for (2), and the SBI PLR-based internal CUP for (3). Critically analyze whether the TPO's method selection for each transaction is sustainable under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act read with Rules 10B and 10C, how the 'most appropriate method' determination should be argued before the Dispute Resolution Panel (DRP), and what specific economic and functional analysis would need to be filed in Form 3CEB to preemptively rebut a potential adjustment under each transaction category.

questions.question: A manufacturer in SEZ supplies goods to a DTA unit under a bond. The DTA unit further processes the goods and re-exports them under a Letter of Undertaking. The SEZ unit claims that the supply is zero-rated under Section 16 of the IGST Act, but the DTA unit's refund application under Rule 96 is rejected on the ground that the input tax credit chain is broken because the SEZ supply was exempt. Analyse the interaction between Section 16(1)(b) IGST Act, Notification No. 18/2017-IGST, and Rule 96 of CGST Rules to determine: (a) whether the SEZ-to-DTA supply is zero-rated or exempt, (b) whether the DTA unit is entitled to ITC on such inward supply, and (c) the correct refund route — Rule 89, Rule 96, or neither — with supporting circular references.

An importer declares transaction value for identical goods imported from a related party. The proper officer rejects transaction value under Rule 3 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, invoking Rule 10A (related-party influence) on the basis that the seller controls the Indian buyer's board. The importer argues that the transaction value should nonetheless be accepted because it closely approximates the deductive value for identical goods sold to unrelated buyers in the same period under Rule 5 read with Rule 7. The officer instead applies computed value under Rule 8. Evaluate: (a) the correct sequential hierarchy of valuation rules and whether the officer can skip to Rule 8, (b) what evidence the importer must produce to trigger the 'test values' safe harbour under the proviso to Rule 3(3), and (c) how the Supreme Court's ratio in Eicher Tractors Ltd v Commissioner of Customs applies to the burden of proof on 'influence over price'.

title: Certification Scheme for Unmanned Aircraft Systems
text:
110  
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
   [PART II—SEC. 3(ii)]

1. 
सामान्य ितें

1.1 अिेदक (जनमाथता/अयातक) िर समय योजना की अिश्यकताओं का पालन करने के जलए सिमत िै जैसा दक ितथमान

में लागू िै और समय-समय पर संिोजधत िै।  अिेदक ऄपने प्रमाणन जनकाय के माध्यम से क्यूसीअइ को प्रमाणन चि 
(5 ििथ) के जलए प्रिासन िुल्क का भुगतान करने के जलए भी सिमत िोगा।

1.2 अिेदक आस संजिदा में जनजित प्रमाणन के साथ-साथ क्यूसीअइ के साथ ऄपनी संजिदा के ऄनुसार प्रमाणन की ितों का

पालन करने के जलए सिमत िोगा।

1.3 आस योजना का ईद्देश्य यूएएस जनमाथता/अयातक को, यूएएस प्रमाणन ऄपेक्षाओं के जलए लागू प्रमाणन योजना को पूरा

करने की ईनकी क्षमता के जलए प्रमाजणत करना िै।

1.4 अिेदक प्रचार सामग्री, पैम्रलेट, लेटर िेड्स, आसी प्रकार की ऄन्य स्ट्टेिनरी में  दकसी भी संचार के अदान-प्रदान के

जलए मीजडया, योजना के बारे में जागरूकता को बढािा देने अदद कायों के जलए, प्रमाणन जचह्न का ईपयोग कर 
सकता िै। िालांदक, अिेदक यि सुजनजित करने के जलए ईपरोक्त दस्ट्तािेजों का ईपयोग करते समय ध्यान रखने के 
जलए सिमत िै दक जचह्न का ईपयोग केिल यूएएस जनमाथता/अयातक के संबंध में दकया जाता िै और यि धारणा निीं 
देगा दक प्रमाजणत जिस्ट्ताररत ईत्पादों के ऄलािा गैर-प्रमाजणत,  दायरे के ऄंतगथत अने िाले कायाथलयों या संबंजधत 
कंपनी भी प्रमाजणत िैं।

1.5 अिेदक यूएएस योजना प्रमाणन जचह्न का ईपयोग केिल यूएएस जनमाथता/अयातक के संबंध में करने के जलए सिमत

िै जो आसे ददए गए प्रमाणीकरण के तित िाजमल दकया गया िै और प्रमाणन मानदंडों का पालन करना जारी रखेगा।

1.6 अिेदक सिमत िै दक िि िमेिा मौजूदा योजना के ऄनुसार और समय-समय पर संिोजधत प्रमाणीकरण ऄपेक्षाओं को

पूरा करेगा और केिल िैधता ऄिजध के दौरान प्रमाणीकरण जचह्न का ईपयोग करेगा।

1.7 अिेदक यूएएस योजना प्रमाणन जचह्न या क्यूसीअइ के नाम का ईपयोग आस प्रकार निीं करने के जलए सिमत िै जो

क्यूसीअइ के जलए भ्रामक या ऄस्ट्िीकायथ िो सकता िै।

1.8 अिेदक केिल ईस सीमा तक प्रमाणन के दािे करने के जलए सिमत िै जो जििेि रूप से प्रमाणन के ऄंतगथत अते िैं।

1.9 अिेदक आस तरि से जचह्न का ईपयोग निीं करने के जलए सिमत िै जजससे क्यूसीअइ या योजना की प्रजतष्‍ट ठा कम िो

और/या जनता का जिश्वास समाप्त िो जाए।

1.10 
अिेदक के नाम, स्ट्िाजमत्ि या स्ट्थान, जजसके जलए अिेदक ने प्रमाणन प्राप्त दकया िै, में दकसी भी मित्िपूणथ

पररितथन के बारे में जलजखत रूप में क्यूसीअइ को सूजचत करने के जलए अिेदक सिमत िै।

1.11 
अिेदक जबना दकसी देरी के क्यूसीअइ को ईन मामलों के बारे में सूजचत करेगा जो प्रमाणन ऄपेक्षाओं के ऄनुरूप

िोने की ईसकी क्षमता को प्रभाजित कर सकते िैं।

1.12 
अिेदक योजना के संचालन के संबंध में QCI द्वारा मांगी गइ कोइ भी जानकारी देने के जलए सिमत िै

1.13 
अिेदक आस बात से सिमत िै दक ईसका नाम, स्ट्थान और प्रमाणन का दायरा QCI द्वारा ऄनुरजक्षत और प्रकाजित

जनदेजिका में िाजमल िै।

1.14 
अिेदक, ईसे अिंरटत जचह्न के ईपयोग के संदभथ में, घोजित/ऄघोजित/प्रलोभन मामलों में अिेदक की ऄनुपालना

को सत्याजपत करने के जलए और अिेदक के बारे में क्यूसीअइ द्वारा प्राप्त जिकायतों के संबंध में और समय के भीतर आस 
तरि के िुल्क का भुगतान करने के जलए सिमत िै,  जैसा दक क्यूसीअइ द्वारा सूजचत दकया गया िै।

1.15 
अिेदक ईस जतजथ से प्रमाणन जचह्न के ईपयोग को बंद करने के जलए सिमत िै जब से प्रमाणपत्र जनलंजबत, रद्द,

और िापस ले जलया गया िै या बंद िो गया िै।

1.16 
आसके प्रमाणन को जनलंजबत या िापस लेने/रद्द करने पर, अिेदक तत्काल प्रभाि से प्रमाणन जचह्नों के ईपयोग के

संदभथ में सभी जिज्ञापन सामग्री का ईपयोग बंद कर देगा और क्यूसीअइ को आस अिय की एक घोिणा प्रस्ट्तुत करेगा। 
यूएएस योजना के तित प्रमाणन के संबंध में दकसी भी रूप में दािा करने से भी बचेगा।

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a factory in Maharashtra and a warehouse in Gujarat. They transfer finished goods from the factory to the warehouse under a stock transfer arrangement. The Maharashtra GST officer contends that IGST is payable on the stock transfer as it constitutes a 'supply' under Section 7 of the CGST Act, while the company argues no consideration is involved. Subsequently, the Gujarat warehouse sells goods to an unregistered buyer at a discount exceeding 20% of MRP, and the company claims ITC on inputs used in manufacturing. Analyze: (1) whether the inter-state stock transfer attracts GST and under what valuation rules; (2) whether the discounted sale triggers any reversal of ITC under Rule 42 or 43; and (3) how the principle of 'distinct persons' under Section 25(4) interacts with the ITC chain across both registrations.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a foreign MNC provides software development services to its parent (ForeignCo) under a cost-plus arrangement. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) rejects the Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) used by IndCo and instead applies the Comparable Uncontrolled Price (CUP) method, selecting three comparables with margins 12–18% higher than IndCo's. IndCo simultaneously claims deduction under Section 10AA for its SEZ unit. The TPO's adjustment increases IndCo's ALP, but IndCo argues the comparables are functionally dissimilar as they bear entrepreneurial risk while IndCo is a 'captive' unit. Analyze: (1) the legal standard for rejecting the taxpayer's choice of TP method under Rule 10C and whether functional dissimilarity is a valid basis; (2) how a TP adjustment interacts with the Section 10AA deduction computation — specifically whether the adjusted income flows into the SEZ profit ring-fence; and (3) whether IndCo can invoke the MAP under the India-[parent country] DTAA to avoid double taxation, and what procedural steps are required under Rule 44G.
