questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) to a related party overseas at a declared transaction value. Post-export, the customs officer invokes Rule 10(1)(e) of the Customs Valuation Rules and rejects the declared value, alleging relationship influence under Section 14 of the Customs Act. Simultaneously, the GST department initiates proceedings under Section 16(2)(c) of the CGST Act denying IGST refund on grounds that the supplier failed to establish that tax was paid by the recipient. Analyze: (a) the evidentiary burden on the exporter to rebut the customs valuation rejection, (b) whether a successful customs revaluation triggers a cascading GST demand under Section 74 vs Section 73, and (c) whether the limitation period for the GST demand runs from the date of export or the date of the customs adjudication order.

An Indian subsidiary of a foreign MNC provides intra-group IT services to its parent and 14 affiliates under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using TNMM with operating profit/total cost as the PLI. The TPO rejects TNMM and applies CUP based on three internal comparables, resulting in an upward adjustment of ₹340 crore. The assessee argues: (i) the internal CUPs are not 'uncontrolled' under Rule 10B(1)(a) because they involve minority-stake associates, (ii) the FAR analysis supports TNMM as the most appropriate method, and (iii) the adjustment violates the 'range concept' under Rule 10CA since the arithmetic mean was applied instead of the 35th–65th percentile range. Evaluate the legal sustainability of each ground before the ITAT, citing the hierarchy of methods under Section 92C and the applicability of the range concept to years prior to AY 2015-16.

title: Anti Dumping duty on Partially Oriented Yarn (POY)
text:
12th September, 2002

Notification No. 97/2002-Customs

WHEREAS in the matter of import of Partially Oriented Yarn (POY) of polyesters (hereinafter referred to as 'the subject goods'),
falling under sub-heading 5402.42 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), originating in, or
exported from, Turkey and Korea RP (hereinafter referred to as 'the subject countries'), the designated authority vide its
preliminary findings, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 23rd November, 2001, had
come to the conclusion that-

(a) the exporters from the subject countries have been exporting the subject goods below normal value resulting in dumping;

(b) the Indian industry has suffered material injury;

(c) the injury has been caused cumulatively by the dumped imports of the subject goods from the subject countries;

and the designated authority had considered it necessary to impose anti-dumping duty, provisionally, pending final
determination, on all imports of the subject goods, originating in, or exported from, the subject countries;

AND WHEREAS on the basis of the aforesaid findings of the designated authority, the Central Government had imposed an
anti-dumping duty vide notification No. 131/2001-Customs, dated the 27th December, 2001, published in Part II, Section 3,
Sub-section (i) of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 27th December, 2001 vide No. G.S.R. 923 (E), dated the 27th
December, 2002;

AND WHEREAS, the designated authority, vide its final findings, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I,
Section I, dated the 16th August, 2002, has come to the conclusion that-

(a)    the exporters from the subject countries have been exporting the subject goods below normal value resulting in dumping;

(b)    the Indian industry has suffered material injury;

(c)    the injury has been caused cumulatively by the dumped imports of the subject goods from the subject countries;

NOW, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-sections (1) and (5) of section 9A of the said Customs Tariff Act,
read with rules 18 and 20 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped
Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995, the Central Government, on the basis of the above findings of the
designated authority, hereby imposes on Partially Oriented Yarn (POY) of polyesters, falling under sub-heading 5402.42 of the
First Schedule to the said Customs Tariff Act, originating in, or exported from, Turkey and Korea RP, and exported by
exporters/producers specified in column (2) of the Table given below, and imported into India, an anti-dumping duty at the rate
specified in the corresponding entry in column (3) of the said Table.

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a buyer in Karnataka under a works contract involving both goods and services. The goods attract 18% GST and the services component attracts 12% GST. The invoice does not separately itemize goods and services. The buyer claims full ITC on the composite invoice. The supplier's GSTR-1 shows a single line item at 18%. Identify all compliance failures, quantify the ITC reversal liability on the buyer, and state the applicable sections and rules under CGST Act 2017 and Karnataka SGST Act.

An Indian importer buys goods from a related party in Germany. The declared transaction value is ₹50 lakhs CIF. The customs officer rejects this value under Rule 3 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007 citing relationship influence, and proposes to apply computed value under Rule 7. The importer disputes this sequencing and argues deductive value under Rule 6 must be attempted first. Determine: (a) whether the importer's sequencing argument is legally tenable citing specific rules and the Customs Act 1962, (b) what evidence the importer must produce to defend the transaction value, and (c) the appellate remedies available and their prescribed timelines.

title: New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules
text:
192  
 THE GAZETTE OF INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
 [PART II—SEC. 3(i)]

repeated-dose study. Local signs (erythema, oedema and eschar formation) as well as histological examination of 
sites of application should be used for evaluation of results.

(ii) 
Photo-allergy or dermal photo-toxicity. - It should be tested by Armstrong or Harber test in guinea pig. 
This test should be done if the drug or a metabolite is related to an agent causing photosensitivity or the nature 
of action suggests such a potential (e.g., drugs to be used in treatment of leucoderma). Pretest in eight animals 
should screen four concentrations (patch application for two hours ±15 min.) with and without UV exposure (10 
J/cm2). Observations recorded at 24 and 48 hours should be used to ascertain highest non-irritant dose. Main 
test should be performed with 10 test animals and five controls. Induction with the dose selected from pretest 
should use 0.3 ml/patch for 2 hour ±15 min. followed by 10 J/cm2 of UV exposure. This should be repeated on 
day 0, 2,4,7,9 and 11 of the test. Animals should be challenged with the same concentration of test substance 
between day 20 to 24 of the test with a similar 2-hour application followed by exposure to 10 J/cm2 of UV light. 
Examination and grading of erythema and oedema formation at the challenge sites should be done 24 and 48 
hours after the challenge. A positive control like musk ambrett or psoralin should be used.

(iii) 
Vaginal toxicity test. - Study is to be done in rabbit or dog. Test substance should be applied topically 
(vaginal mucosa) in the form of pessary, cream or ointment. Six to ten animals per dose group should be taken. 
Higher concentrations or several daily applications of test substance should be done to achieve multiples of daily 
human dose. The minimum duration of drug treatment is seven days (more according to clinical use), subject to 
a maximum of 30 days. Observation parameters should include swelling, closure of in troit us and 
histopathology of vaginal wall.

(iv) Rectal tolerance test.- For all preparations meant for rectal administration this test may be performed in 
rabbits or dogs. Six to ten animals per dose group should be taken. Formulation in volume comparable to human 
dose (or the maximum possible volume) should be applied once or several times daily, per rectally, to achieve 
administration of multiples of daily human dose. The minimum duration of application is seven days (more 
according to clinical use), subject to a maximum of 30 days. Size of suppositories may be smaller, but the drug 
content should be several fold higher than the proposed human dose. Observation parameters should include 
clinical signs (sliding on backside), signs of pain, blood or mucus in faeces, condition of anal region or 
sphincter, gross and (if required) histological examination of rectal mucosa.

(v) 
Parenteral drugs.- For products meant for intravenous or intramuscular or subcutaneous or intradermal 
injection the sites of injection in systemic toxicity studies should be specially examined grossly and 
microscopically. If needed, reversibility of adverse effects may be determined on a case to case basis.

(vi) 
Ocular toxicity studies (for products meant for ocular instillation). - These studies should be carried 
out in two species, one of which should be the albino rabbit which has a sufficiently large conjunctival sac. Direct 
delivery of drug onto the cornea in case of animals having small conjunctival sacs should be ensured. Liquids, 
ointments, gels or soft contact lenses (saturated with drug) should be used. Initial single dose application should 
be done to decide the exposure concentrations for repeated-dose studies and the need to include a recovery 
group. Such initial toxicity studies shall be carried out by non-animal alternative tests as given in Organisation 
for Economic Cooperation and Development Guidelines. Duration of the final study will depend on the 
proposed length of human exposure subject to a maximum of 90 days. At least two different concentrations 
exceeding the human dose should be used for demonstrating the margin of safety. In acute studies, one eye 
should be used for drug administration and the other kept as control. A separate control group should be 
included in repeated-dose studies. Slit-lamp examination should be done to detect the changes in cornea, iris and 
aqueous humor. Fluorescent dyes (sodium fluorescein, 0.25 to 1.0%) should be used for detecting the defects in 
surface epithelium of cornea and conjunctiva. Changes in intra-ocular tension should be monitored by a 
tonometer. Histological examination of eyes should be done at the end of the study after fixation in Davidson's 
or Zenker's fluid.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three distinct units under a single GSTIN: Unit A (exempt supplies only), Unit B (taxable supplies only), and Unit C (both taxable and exempt supplies). During FY 2024-25, Unit C procures capital goods worth ₹50 lakhs (GST paid ₹9 lakhs) used commonly across all three units. The company also has an ITC reversal obligation under Rule 42 for common inputs worth ₹30 lakhs (GST ₹5.4 lakhs). Given that the aggregate turnover ratio of exempt to total supplies is 35%, calculate (a) the eligible ITC on capital goods under Section 17(2) read with Rule 43, (b) the common ITC reversal under Rule 42, and (c) whether the company can claim a refund of any reversed ITC if Unit A is subsequently converted to a taxable supply unit mid-year. Cite the relevant sections of CGST Act 2017 and applicable circulars.

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a related party (holding 30% equity) in Germany at a declared transaction value of USD 1,20,000. The customs officer invokes Rule 4 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007 and rejects the declared value, citing contemporaneous imports of identical goods at USD 1,55,000 by an unrelated buyer. The importer argues that the price was influenced by a long-term supply agreement and submits a transfer pricing study under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act showing the transaction is at arm's length. Additionally, the goods attract a provisional anti-dumping duty under a Customs Notification that was under challenge before CESTAT at the time of import. Analyze: (a) the hierarchy of valuation methods under Rules 4–9 and which method should apply, (b) whether an arm's length TP study is sufficient to validate transaction value under Rule 3(3)(b), (c) the importer's obligation to pay anti-dumping duty pending CESTAT adjudication, and (d) the available legal remedies and their procedural sequence including timelines under Section 128 and 130E of the Customs Act 1962.

questions.question: A multinational corporation operating in India has a subsidiary that provides software development services to its parent company abroad. The subsidiary charges the parent company a management fee for shared services (HR, IT, legal) allocated on a cost-plus basis. The parent company also licenses IP to the subsidiary, for which royalties are paid. The Indian subsidiary files GST returns treating the management fee receipt as an export of services (zero-rated) and the royalty payment as an import of services subject to RCM. During a GST audit, the officer contends that the management fee allocation lacks economic substance and should be re-characterized as a non-arm's-length transaction, while also questioning whether the IP licensing qualifies as 'supply' under Schedule II. Simultaneously, a transfer pricing assessment under the Income Tax Act proposes to benchmark both transactions separately using the TNMM method, conflicting with the GST valuation. How should the taxpayer reconcile the GST and transfer pricing positions, what documentation is required to defend both simultaneously, and what is the correct GST treatment if the transactions are re-characterized?

An SEZ unit imports capital goods under the zero-duty benefit available to SEZ operators. After three years of operation, the SEZ unit partially de-notifies and converts 40% of its area into a Domestic Tariff Area (DTA) unit. The capital goods imported under zero-duty were used for production in both the SEZ and DTA zones without separate metering. The Customs department raises a demand for proportionate customs duty on goods attributable to DTA operations, computed on the depreciated value under Rule 6 of SEZ Rules 2006. Simultaneously, the GST department raises an input tax credit reversal demand under Section 17(2) read with Rule 42, treating the DTA supply as exempt. The company also has pending IGST refund claims on exports made during the SEZ period. Analyze the correct method for computing customs duty liability on partial de-notification, whether ITC reversal under GST is independently maintainable when customs duty is already being demanded on the same goods, and the impact of de-notification on pending IGST refund claims.

title: Filing of appeals in the Supreme Court - instructions regarding forwarding of information on connected matters for proper defence of cases
text:
e Supreme Court
during the year. Chief Commissioners will coordinate with Commissioners in their jurisdiction to ensure that the concerned
officers locate the case files pertaining to cases in their charge, go through them and ascertain the latest information in respect
of relied upon/connected matters as well as any other development connected with the case, so that the Departmental counsels
can be adequately briefed before the final hearing. A copy of the list has been forwarded to all Chief Commissioners vide
Board"s F. No. 390/194/2000-JC dated 30-8-2000. Chief Commissioners who have not received the list should immediately
get in touch with Joint Secretary (Review) or Director (Review).

In addition to the Terminal List forwarded to the Chief Commissioners, Board has also forwarded a copy of list of matters
pending in the Supreme Court as ascertained from the Supreme Court Registry, vide Board's F. No. 390/195/2000-JC dated
30-8-2000 to all Chief Commissioners. All Chief Commissioners should correlate the position reflected in this list with their own
lists of pending cases, and in case there are certain cases which appear to be pending as per their own list and do not figure in
this list of cases obtained from the Supreme Court Registry, particulars of such cases (including Board"s File No. if any) should
be furnished at the earliest to the Joint Secretary (Legal) - in respect of appeals against High Court"s orders, and to Joint
Secretary (Review) regarding appeals against CEGAT"s orders. In fact it has been noticed that the figures of pendency as per
Supreme Courts records and the figures as reported in the MTR or to Legal Cell of the Board do not tally. They therefore have
to be reconciled by December 2000, latest.

Board would henceforth be sending such lists twice a year to the Chief Commissioners and Commissioners concerned should
ensure that their data is updated in accordance with pendency reflected in the Board"s Office/S.C. Registry and discrepancies,
if any, are reconciled as early as possible, if necessary by deputing officers to Delhi. They may also suggest case, which can
be bunched together, or cases, which need to be, heard on priority basis in view of the importance of the matter or the revenue
implications.

Please acknowledge receipt of this circular.

REPORT ON CONNECTED MATTERS

questions.question: Under the GST Act 2017, when a registered supplier provides both exempt and taxable supplies and the input tax credit apportionment under Section 17(1) yields a common credit pool, explain the step-by-step methodology to compute the final reversal amount under Rule 42 of CGST Rules including the treatment of blocked credits under Section 17(5), the effect of exempt turnover on the D1 and D2 components, and how this reconciles with GSTR-3B auto-population logic when the taxpayer also has zero-rated exports with and without payment of IGST.

A multinational enterprise has an Indian subsidiary that provides IT-enabled services to its foreign parent under a cost-plus arrangement. The CBIC has issued a show-cause notice alleging that the arm's length price determined under the TNMM method is understated, and simultaneously the GST department is questioning whether the cross-charge of shared services to branch offices in different states satisfies the distinct person supply rules under Schedule I Entry 2. Analyze the legal framework under which both proceedings can be resolved, the evidentiary standards required at the Advance Authority for Ruling vs. Customs Excise and Service Tax Appellate Tribunal, and whether a settlement under the Sabka Vishwas scheme extinguishes liability in both forums.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two registered units in different states sharing common input services (legal, IT, HR). The ISD distributes ITC monthly using the turnover ratio prescribed under Rule 39. During FY 2024-25 Q3, one unit was exclusively engaged in exempt supplies for 2 months and mixed supplies for 1 month. The other unit had no turnover in month 1 due to a shutdown. How should the ISD calculate and distribute eligible ITC for each month, and what reversal obligations arise under Rule 42 read with Section 17(2) for the unit with exempt supplies? Cite the relevant provisions and compute the eligible ITC if total ISD credit for the quarter is ₹18,00,000 and the unit-wise turnover figures are: Unit A — Month 1: ₹0, Month 2: ₹45L (exempt), Month 3: ₹60L (mixed, exempt ratio 40%); Unit B — Month 1: ₹80L, Month 2: ₹55L (all taxable), Month 3: ₹90L (all taxable).

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign supplier at a declared transaction value of USD 1,00,000 (CIF). The customs officer rejects the transaction value under Rule 3(3) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, citing relationship influence, and proposes to use the deductive value method under Rule 7. The importer contests, arguing that the test values under Rule 3(3)(b) — based on sales to unrelated buyers in India at the same commercial level — actually support the declared value. The importer provides: (a) a cost sheet showing the declared price covers cost + 12% profit, (b) contemporaneous sales to three unrelated Indian buyers at USD 98,000–1,03,000, and (c) a transfer pricing study accepted by the Income Tax department at arm's length. Analyze: (i) the burden of proof allocation between importer and department under Rule 3(3) and the Supreme Court's position in Eicher Tractors, (ii) whether the TP study is admissible as corroborative evidence in customs proceedings, and (iii) the sequential fallback hierarchy the officer must follow before invoking Rule 7, with citation to CBIC Circular No. 39/2016-Customs.

questions.question: You have a production RAG system serving 108k chunks of Indian tax law (CBIC corpus) on a 4-core CPU machine with ROCm GPU. Your p95 query latency is 18 seconds. Profiling shows: vector search (Qdrant) = 0.4s, LLM generation (Qwen3-14B, /no_think) = 14s, reranking (BGE-M3) = 3.1s, async overhead = 0.5s. You cannot add hardware. Describe your optimization strategy in priority order, explaining the trade-offs of each step, and estimate the latency reduction achievable at each phase without degrading answer quality below a 90% relevance threshold.

You are ingesting 471 image-only PDFs (scanned Indian government circulars, mixed Hindi/English, variable DPI 72–300) into a Qdrant vector store. Your pipeline runs on a single GPU (Vulkan, not ROCm) at 5.2–9.6 chunks/second for text PDFs. For image PDFs you must choose between: (A) Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF via llama-server for LLM-based OCR, (B) RapidOCR on CPU as fallback, or (C) Tesseract CPU. Compare these approaches across: throughput, accuracy on mixed-script scans, GPU VRAM impact on concurrent RAG serving, and operational failure modes. Which would you deploy in production and why?

questions.question: A registered supplier in Maharashtra supplies composite works contract services (civil construction + electrical installation) to a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) unit. The contract value is ₹5 crore inclusive of GST. The supplier claims zero-rated supply under LUT. The SEZ unit later uses 30% of the constructed infrastructure for non-processing zone activities. Analyze: (a) whether the original zero-rated treatment holds, (b) what reversal obligations arise under Rule 42/43 of CGST Rules, (c) whether the supplier faces any retrospective liability, and (d) what documentation the supplier must retain to defend an audit.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services (captive IT) to its parent under a cost-plus 15% arrangement. During AY 2024-25, the subsidiary also earns ₹12 crore from third-party clients (20% of total revenue). The Transfer Pricing Officer proposes to benchmark the entire entity using TNMM and rejects the segmentation of accounts on grounds that the subsidiary has no genuine functional separation. The subsidiary claims Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) on the captive segment alone, citing Rule 10B(1)(e). Analyze: (a) validity of the TPO's rejection of segmentation, (b) which method and comparables should apply, (c) the impact of the 20% third-party revenue on 'comparability analysis' under Rule 10C, and (d) available appellate remedies if the APA application is already pending.

title: The Finance Act, 2021 (13 of 2021)
text:
SEC. 1] 
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY 
91

appointment shall not be eligible for re-appointment or for fresh 
appointment as auditor for a period of five years from such completion: 
 
 
Provided further that no audit firm shall be appointed as auditor for 
a period of five years which, if appointed, as on the date of its 
appointment, would have a common partner or partners with the audit 
firm whose term as auditor in the Corporation had expired in the 
financial year immediately preceding the financial year in which fresh 
appointment is to be made, or which is associated with the same network 
of audit firms as the audit firm whose term had expired as aforesaid:

Provided also that nothing contained in this sub-section shall 
prejudice the right of the Corporation to remove an auditor or the right 
of the auditor to resign from such office of the Corporation.

38 of 1949.

Explanation.—For the purposes of this sub-section, the expression 
“same network” includes firms operating or functioning under a 
common brand name or trade name, or under common control, or which 
are network firms as defined under any guidelines for networking issued 
by the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India, constituted under 
section 3 of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949.

(3) Subject to the provisions of this Act, the Corporation may 
resolve in a general meeting to provide that—

(a) in the audit firm appointed by it, the auditing partner and his 
team shall be rotated at such intervals as may be resolved by 
members;

(b) the audit shall be conducted by more than one auditor.

(4) Any casual vacancy in the office of an auditor shall be filled by 
the Board within thirty days, but if such casual vacancy is as a result of 
the resignation of an auditor, such appointment shall also be approved 
by the Corporation in a general meeting convened within three months 
of the Board making recommendations in this behalf, and the auditor so 
appointed shall hold office till the conclusion of the next annual general 
meeting.

(5) Where at any annual general meeting, no auditor is appointed, 
the existing auditor shall continue to be the auditor of the Corporation.

(6) All appointments, including the filling of a casual vacancy of an 
auditor under this section, shall be made after taking into account the 
recommendations of the Audit Committee.

(7) The remuneration of the auditor shall be fixed in the general 
meeting or in such manner as may be determined therein.

(8) Until the first annual general meeting is held, auditors duly 
qualified to act as auditors of companies under the law for the time 
being in force relating to companies shall be appointed by the Board 
with the previous approval of the Central Government, and shall receive 
such remuneration from the Corporation as the Central Government may 
fix.

(9) Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (1), where an 
auditor has been appointed previous to the first annual general meeting, 
either under section 25 [as it stood before the coming into force of 
section 137 of the Finance Act, 2021] or thereafter under sub-section (8),

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a factory in Maharashtra and a registered warehouse in Gujarat. They transfer finished goods from the factory to the warehouse under a stock transfer arrangement. The warehouse later sells to customers across India. The company has claimed ITC on inputs used in manufacturing. Determine: (a) whether the stock transfer attracts GST and under what supply classification, (b) how ITC flows between the two GSTINs, (c) whether the warehouse GSTIN can claim ITC on the stock transfer invoice, and (d) what reversal obligations arise if goods worth ₹12 lakhs remain unsold in the warehouse at year-end and are written off as damaged.

An Indian importer brings in capital goods under the EPCG scheme with a specific export obligation of 6x the duty saved over 6 years. After 4 years, the importer: (i) fulfills 70% of the export obligation through direct exports, (ii) seeks to count deemed exports under a domestic supply contract to an EOU for the remaining 30%, and (iii) simultaneously applies for redemption of the bond. The customs authority raises a demand claiming the deemed export supplies don't qualify because the EOU's Annual Performance Report is pending. Analyze the legal position, applicable notifications, and the correct procedure to regularize the shortfall while contesting the demand, including any interest and penalty exposure under the Customs Act.

title: Regardingclassification of Fused Silica under Customs Tariff Act, 1975
text:
Circular No.03 / 2012 - Customs

F. No. 524/129/2011-STO (TU)

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

229A, North Block, New Delhi,

1 st February, 2012.

To

All Chief Commissioners / Commissioner of Customs / Customs (Prev.)

All Chief Commissioners / Commissioner of Customs & Central Excise

All Commissioners of Customs (Appeals)

All Commissioners of Customs & Central Excise (Appeals)

All Directors General under CBEC.

Subject: Classification of Fused Silica under Customs Tariff Act, 1975 - regarding.

****

Sir / Madam,

References have been received in the Board from field formations regarding divergent practices being followed in respect of
classification of 'Fused Silica' under Customs Tariff sub-heading 250610, 281122, 320740 or 700231.

2.         The matter of correct classification of fused silica under the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 was taken up
for discussion during the Conference of Chief Commissioners of Customs on Tariff and allied matters held in May 2011. After
examining the various entries in the said schedule and the technical aspects of the product in question, it was decided that the
Chief Commissioners under whose jurisdiction the import of fused silica are taking place, shall furnish the technical details of
the product to the Board; it was also decided to seek an expert opinion before arriving at a final decision regarding
classification of the product.

3.         Accordingly, reports received from various field formations where import of fused silica had taken place was examined
and an expert opinion was sought from the Central Glass & Ceramic Research Institute (CGCRI), Kolkata. The report given by
CGCRI and technical literature on the product reveals that 'Fused Silica' is type of glass containing primarily silica in
amorphous (non-crystalline) form. Fused Silica is produced using high-purity silica sand as the feedstock, and is normally
melted using an electric furnace or through continuous flame hydrolysis process, resulting in a material that is translucent or
opaque. It has also been confirmed by CGCRI that naturally occurring fused silica is available in small quantity and for
commercial use synthetically produced silica as above is generally in use.

4.1.       In terms of the headings, relative Section or Chapter Notes to the First Schedule of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 and
the General Rules for the Interpretation of the said Schedule to the tariff, it is seen that the sub-heading 2506 covers the
following:

2506 Quartz (other than natural sands); Quartzite, whether or not roughly trimmed or merely cut, by sawing or otherwise, into
blocks or slabs of a rectangular (including square) shape

In terms of chapter note 1 to chapter 25, the headings of this chapter cover only products which are in crude state or which have
been subjected to basic process such as washing, crushing, grounding etc. by physical or mechanical process. Products which
are processed beyond this basic process would not be covered under this Chapter. As explained in para 3 above, since fused
silica is produced by chemical processes beyond the basic processes mentioned above and considering that naturally
occurring fused silica is not available for commercial use, classification of synthetically produced fused silica is ruled out under
sub-heading 2506.

4.2.       Chapter 28 covers the following products:

Inorganic chemicals; organic or inorganic compounds of precious metals, of rare earth metals, of radioactive elements or of
isotopes

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU at 5.2–9.6 chunks/sec. After deploying async FastAPI with Qdrant and a 14B parameter LLM, p95 query latency is 18 seconds. Profiling shows: embedding lookup = 0.4s, Qdrant ANN search = 1.1s, LLM prefill = 6.2s, LLM decode = 9.8s. The rig has 4 CPU cores, 2 GPUs (RX 6700 XT), and OCR workers running at nice +19. Describe a complete, ordered remediation plan that brings p95 latency under 6 seconds without adding hardware, specifying which bottleneck you attack first, why, and what measurement confirms each fix before proceeding to the next.

A practitioner builds a retrieval-augmented system over CBIC circulars, GST notifications, and CETA tariff schedules. Users report that cross-document queries — e.g., 'What is the effective GST rate on imported industrial compressors after accounting for the 2023 exemption notification and the applicable BCD under Customs Tariff Heading 8414?' — return partially correct answers that miss the interaction between documents. The chunking strategy is fixed at 512 tokens with 64-token overlap, the retriever returns top-8 chunks by cosine similarity, and the LLM context window is 8192 tokens. Without changing the LLM or the embedding model, propose a retrieval architecture redesign that correctly surfaces multi-document, multi-hop legal reasoning chains, and explain how you would evaluate whether the redesign actually improves faithfulness versus baseline.

title: Appeal to CEGAT - Submission of case files to CDR by concerned office
text:
Pant, SDR, states that neither the Assistant Collector not the Collector (Appeals) has sent the relevant file to her and therefore,
she is not in a position of verify whether appellant had sent such a letter at all. To a query put by us, she stated that the files
relevant to the appeals are rarely sent by the lower authorities to the CDR"s office, unless specifically called for. We are
disturbed by this state of affairs which deprives the Departmental Representatives of the opportunity of making effective
submissions in support of the Department"s case. We have  seen it is occurring in many cases and we have to adjourn old
cases for the purpose of obtaining record or verifying the existence of record from the Department.

2.    If the Government or the Central Board of Excise and Customs is interested in ensuring that the Department interest is
properly and effectively defended, it is necessary that they must devise a system by which the Collector to whom notice of filing
the appeal is sent, takes care to collector the records and forward the same to the Chief Departmental Representative to
enable the departmental Representatives to stay all the papers and also whenever necessary, the Tribunal to peruse. It is not in
every case that we will be in a position to grant adjournment for the purpose of securing records. There must be an
arrangement by which a system is established to ensure that records reach the officer of the Chief Departmental
Representative, without the necessity of calling for such records. This is the system prevailing in courts and other tribunals. It is
necessary that the authorities concerned do something in the matter. It is equally necessary that the Department which receives
notice of filing of the appeal, evolves a system by which a Departmental officer studies the case and memorandum of appeal
and wherever appropriate, prepares instructions for the guidance of the Departmental Representatives who appear before the
Tribunal. A copy of this order will be forwarded to the Central Board of Excise and Customs, New Delhi for information and
necessary action.

3.    The appeal is adjourned to 27th March, 1996.

Sd/-

(Justice U.L. Bhat)

President

Sd/-

(K.S. Venkatramani)

Member Technical

questions.question: A manufacturer engaged in both taxable and exempt supplies purchases capital goods (plant and machinery) for ₹50 lakhs with GST of ₹9 lakhs. During the financial year, the taxable turnover is ₹2.4 crore and exempt turnover is ₹60 lakhs. Subsequently, in month 7, the manufacturer exclusively uses the machinery for taxable supply only. Calculate the ITC available for the year under Rule 43 of CGST Rules, specifying the common credit reversal methodology, the treatment of the reversal upon exclusive taxable use, and cite the relevant circular or notification governing the recalculation.

An Indian importer purchases goods from its wholly-owned foreign parent at a declared transaction value. The SVB (Special Valuation Branch) has provisionally assessed and issued a PD Bond. Simultaneously, the Income Tax Transfer Pricing Officer accepts the arm's length price but at a value 12% higher than the customs declared value. Analyze: (1) whether the TP order constitutes 'additional evidence' requiring SVB re-examination under Rule 10B of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, (2) the legal mechanism for revising the final SVB order retroactively, (3) the differential duty liability including interest under Section 28AA of the Customs Act, and (4) whether voluntary disclosure under Section 28(1) proviso before SVB closure alters the penalty exposure under Section 114A.
expected_retrieval_targets: Rule 10B Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007

title: Seeks to notify Customs (Verification of Identity and Compliance) Regulations, 2021
text:

these regulations and these regulations shall apply to such person.

(2) These regulations shall not apply to the Central Government, State Governments and Public 
Sector Undertakings.

(3) The person selected shall be informed about such selection immediately through the Common 
Portal, to the extent available, and in other cases, through such means as deemed appropriate by 
the Commissioner of Customs.

4. Verification of identity.-  (1) The person selected for verification as per regulation 3 shall 
furnish the following documents or information on the Common Portal within fifteen days of 
such intimation of selection:-

(i) document of incorporation, in case of persons other than individuals shall be the 
following, namely:-

(a) 
in case of partnership firm, partnership deed or agreement;

(b) 
in case of Limited Liability Partnership, Certification of Registration issued by

(c) 
in case of company, Certificate of Registration issued by Registrar and

(d)

Understanding and Articles of Association;

(e) 
in any other case, any document evidencing constitution.

(ii) document evidencing appointment of authorised signatories, if applicable.

(iii)  Permanent Account Number;

(iv) GST Identification Number;

(v) document such as bank statement, Income Tax Return etc. evidencing financial 
standing of the person:

Provided that a person who is newly engaging in import or export activity after the

commencement of these regulations shall furnish the said documents not later than thirty days of 
engaging in import or export activity.

(2) On furnishing of documents or information stipulated in sub-regulation (1), every individual, 
karta, managing director, whole time director, partners, member of managing committee of 
association, board of trustees, authorised representative, authorized signatory, shall, within the 
prescribed time limit, undergo on the Common Portal-

(ii) verification of Permanent Account Number:

Provided that where the authentication of Aadhaar cannot be completed due to its non-

assignment or due to technical reasons, the person to be verified shall furnish a notarised copy of 
valid passport or electoral photo identity card for verification on Common Portal, within an 
extended further period of five days from the date of such selection or failure of authentication, as 
the case may be, or within such extended period, as may be allowed by the Commissioner of 
Customs.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (subsidiary) at a price below cost. The subsidiary then re-sells the goods to unrelated customers at market price. The department contends that transaction value under Section 15(4) of CGST Act read with Rule 28 of CGST Rules must be the open market value, and raises a demand for differential GST on the manufacturer. The manufacturer argues that since the recipient (subsidiary) is eligible for full ITC, the proviso to Rule 28 applies and the invoice value should be accepted. However, the subsidiary has a partial exemption on its outward supplies (mixed supply scenario), limiting its ITC eligibility to ~60%. At what threshold of ITC ineligibility does the Rule 28 proviso cease to protect the supplier, and what valuation method — open market value, cost-plus 10%, or CBIC circular guidance — would a tribunal likely apply?

An Indian importer (wholly-owned subsidiary of a foreign MNC) declares customs value for imported components using the transfer price set under an APA (Advance Pricing Agreement) with the Income Tax authority. The customs department rejects this value under Rule 4 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, citing relationship influence, and re-determines value using Rule 7 (computed value). The re-determined customs value is 18% higher, generating a demand including BCD, IGST, and interest. The taxpayer argues that an APA-accepted transfer price satisfies the 'circumstances of sale' test and that the department cannot independently re-benchmark without referring the matter to the transfer pricing officer. Analyse: (a) whether an APA constitutes conclusive evidence of arm's length price for customs purposes under Indian law; (b) the interplay between Section 14 of the Customs Act and the Income Tax TP provisions; and (c) the correct appellate forum and burden-of-proof allocation when both customs value and TP adjustment are simultaneously disputed.

questions.question: A registered taxpayer operating as an e-commerce operator under Section 52 of the CGST Act has collected TCS on supplies made through their platform. During a GST audit, the officer discovers that certain suppliers on the platform were unregistered at the time of supply but later obtained registration retrospectively. How should the TCS already deposited by the e-commerce operator be reconciled against the suppliers' GSTR-1 and GSTR-2B for those periods, and what is the liability, if any, of the e-commerce operator under Section 122(1A) for supplies made by unregistered persons through their platform?

A closely held company has entered into a slump sale under Section 50B of the Income Tax Act, transferring an undertaking that includes self-generated goodwill, accumulated losses of the undertaking (ring-fenced under Section 72A), and a capital asset on which depreciation was claimed under the Written Down Value method for 12 years. The net worth of the undertaking is negative. Walk through the complete computation of capital gains: how is the cost of acquisition determined when net worth is negative, whether self-generated goodwill forms part of net worth post the Finance Act 2021 amendment, and whether the accumulated business losses of the transferred undertaking survive in the hands of the acquirer given the slump sale structure.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units in different states — Unit A (Maharashtra) and Unit B (Karnataka). Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit B as a stock transfer under a job-work arrangement. Unit B converts these into finished goods and sells directly to customers in Tamil Nadu. The company also has an Export Oriented Unit (EOU) registered under the Foreign Trade Policy. Trace the complete ITC eligibility, reversal obligations under Rule 42/43, and cross-charge requirements under Section 20 of the IGST Act. At what point does the principal supply arise, and how does the place of supply determination interact with the EOU's deemed export status for the purposes of refund under Section 54?

A listed Indian company (ListCo) proposes a composite scheme of arrangement under Sections 230–232 of the Companies Act, 2013 involving: (i) demerger of its NBFC subsidiary into a newly incorporated SPV, (ii) simultaneous merger of a foreign wholly-owned subsidiary into ListCo via cross-border amalgamation under Section 234 read with FEMA (Cross-Border Merger) Regulations 2018, and (iii) a preferential allotment to anchor investors upon completion of the scheme. Identify all regulatory approvals required (NCLT, RBI, SEBI, CCI, stock exchanges), the sequencing constraints among those approvals, the disclosure obligations under SEBI LODR Regulations, and the specific RBI conditions that would apply to the foreign entity's assets and liabilities being absorbed — including treatment of any ECB outstanding in the foreign subsidiary.

questions.question: A multinational company incorporated in Singapore provides cloud-based SaaS services to Indian corporate clients. The Indian clients use these services partly for their export of services (zero-rated supply) and partly for domestic taxable supply. The Singapore entity has no PE in India but has appointed a local liaison office. Under what conditions would the Singapore entity be required to obtain GST registration in India, and how should the Indian clients apportion their Input Tax Credit claim under Rule 42 of the CGST Rules, 2017, considering the partial reverse charge mechanism applicability and the cross-border nature of the supply chain?
expected_skills: GST registration thresholds for foreign entities

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Transfer Pricing + DTAA
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US parent (USCo) provides software development services to USCo under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at 15% markup using TNMM. The AO proposes to reclassify IndCo as an entrepreneur rather than a routine service provider, arguing that IndCo bears significant development risk evidenced by its employee stock option plan participation and decision-making authority. Simultaneously, USCo has claimed a deduction in the US for intercompany payments. Analyze the Indian transfer pricing implications under the OECD BEPS Action 8-10 framework as incorporated into Indian law, the risk of secondary adjustment under Section 92CE, potential MAP relief under Article 25 of the India-US DTAA, and whether IndCo can invoke the Safe Harbour Rules under Rule 10TD for software development services notwithstanding the AO's risk reclassification argument.

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) and simultaneously has domestic B2B supplies. During the same tax period, they receive a CGST demand for reversal of ITC on inputs attributable to exempt supplies under Rule 42, while also claiming a refund of accumulated ITC under Section 54(3) on zero-rated exports. The department contends that the ITC reversal must be computed before determining the refund quantum. Evaluate whether the department's sequencing is legally tenable, citing the relevant provisions of the CGST Act, Rules, and any conflicting judicial precedents on the interplay between Rule 42 reversal and Section 54(3) refund eligibility.

A closely-held domestic company receives a slump sale consideration of ₹85 crore for a business undertaking whose net worth per books is ₹12 crore, with the undertaking having been held for 8 years. The acquirer subsequently revalues the assets and claims depreciation on the stepped-up values. The vendor is simultaneously subject to a Section 56(2)(x) addition proposed by the AO on the grounds that the FMV of the undertaking, as determined under Rule 11UAE, exceeds the slump sale consideration. Analyse: (a) whether Section 56(2)(x) can apply to a slump sale given the charging provision under Section 50B; (b) the acquirer's right to claim depreciation on goodwill post the Finance Act 2021 amendment; and (c) whether the step-up basis in the acquirer's hands survives if the transaction is recharacterised as an itemised asset sale by the AO.

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline serves 108k chunks of Indian tax law. Under concurrent load (8+ simultaneous queries), you observe that embedding retrieval latency spikes from ~120ms to 4–6 seconds, but only when the LLM inference server (llama-server Vulkan) is simultaneously processing a long-context generation. Your Qdrant instance, FastAPI app, and llama-server all share the same 4-core host. Profiling shows CPU saturation at ~95% during spikes, but GPU VRAM is only 60% utilized. Walk through your diagnosis and remediation strategy, including how you would restructure process priorities, async I/O patterns, and batching to achieve sub-300ms P95 retrieval latency without adding hardware.

You are building a retrieval system over a corpus of Indian GST circulars, where queries mix formal legal citations (e.g., 'Section 16(4) ITC reversal circular 2019'), numerical thresholds ('transactions above ₹50 lakh'), and procedural intent ('how to file GSTR-9C after deadline'). Pure dense vector search (BGE-M3) achieves 71% answer relevance but misses exact citation matches. Pure BM25 keyword search finds citations reliably but fails on paraphrased procedural queries. Describe a production-grade hybrid retrieval architecture that handles all three query types with >90% relevance, specifying: re-ranking strategy, score fusion method, query classification logic, and how you would evaluate and tune the system without a labeled ground-truth dataset.

title: This relates to Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995
text:
determining individual margins of dumping for any exporters or producers in the exporting country in question who have not
exported the product to India during the period of investigation, provided that these exporters or producers show that they are
not related to any of the exporters or producers in the exporting country who are subject to the anti-dumping duties on the
product.

(2) The Central Government shall not levy anti-dumping duties under sub-section (1) of section 9A of the Act on imports from
such exporters or producers during the period  of  review  as  referred to in  sub-rule (1) of this rule :

Provided that the Central Government may resort to provisional assessment and may ask a guarantee from the importer if the
designated authority so recommends and if such a review results in a determination of dumping in respect of such products or
exporters, it may levy duty in such cases retrospectively from the date of the initiation of the review.

23. Review.-

(1) The designated authority shall, from time to time, review the need for the continued imposition of the anti-dumping duty
and shall, if it is satisfied on the basis of information received by it that there is no justification for the continued imposition of
such duty recommend to the Central Government for its withdrawal.

(2) Any review initiated under sub-rule (1) shall be concluded within a period not exceeding twelve months from the date of
initiation of such review.

(3) The provisions of rules 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 16, 17, 18, 19, and 20 shall be mutatis mutandis applicable in the case of
review.

24. Dumping causing injury to a third country.-

(1) The designated authority may initiate investigation into any dumping alleged to be taking place into India and causing
injury to the domestic industry of any third country which is a member of the World Trade Organisation.

(2) The designated authority in such cases shall follow the procedures laid down in Article 14 of the Agreement on
Implementation of Article VI of the General Agreement on Tariff and Trade, 1994, as contained in the Final Act of Uruguay
Round Multilateral Trade Negotiations.

ANNEXURE I

(See rule 8)

Principles governing the determination of normal value, export price and margin of dumping

The designated authority while determining the normal value, export price and margin of dumping shall take into account inter
alia , the following principles -

1. The elements of costs referred to in the context of determination of normal value shall normally be determined on the basis
of records kept by the exporter or producer under investigation, provided such records are in accordance with the generally
accepted accounting principles of the exporting country, and such records reasonably reflect the cost associated with
production and sale of the article under consideration.

2. Sales of the like product in the domestic market of the exporting country or sales to a third country at prices below per unit
(fixed and variable) costs of production plus administrative, selling and general costs may be treated as not being in the
ordinary course of trade by reason of price. The designated authority may disregard these sales, in determining normal value,
provided it has determined that -

(i) such sales are made within a reasonable period of time (not less than six months) in substantial quantities, i.e. when the
weighted average selling price of the article is below the weighted average per unit costs or when the volume of the sales
below per unit costs represents not less than twenty per cent of the volume sold in transactions under consideration, and

questions.question: Your production RAG system ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a 4-core machine with a single Vulkan GPU. During peak query load, you observe p95 latency degrading from 4s to 22s. CPU `uptime` shows load average of 3.8. Your embedding retrieval takes 180ms, BM25 reranking takes 400ms, and the LLM inference (Qwen3-14B with `/no_think`) takes 3.2s. Concurrent OCR workers (2 workers, nice +19, ionice idle) are running. Identify the bottleneck(s), propose a phased remediation ordered by effort-to-impact ratio, and explain what metric you would instrument first to confirm your hypothesis before making any changes.

You are building a legal RAG system over CBIC GST circulars, Finance Act amendments, and ITAT case law. A query like 'What is the ITC reversal obligation when a supplier's GSTIN is cancelled retrospectively after the buyer has already filed GSTR-3B?' returns 5 semantically similar chunks about ITC reversal but none specifically addressing retrospective cancellation. Your vector store has 108k chunks with BGE-M3 embeddings, and you have a BM25 index over the same corpus. (a) Design a retrieval pipeline that maximizes recall for this query type, specifying fusion strategy, reranker model choice, and chunk granularity. (b) A practitioner argues that query expansion via LLM hypothetical document embeddings (HyDE) would solve this — evaluate this claim given your 4-core CPU constraint and 3.2s LLM baseline latency. (c) What corpus-level intervention (beyond retrieval tuning) would most improve answer quality for amendment-chain queries?

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (holding company) at a price significantly below the open market value. The recipient is not eligible for full input tax credit due to partial exemption. The supplier contends the transaction is at 'open market value' citing an internal transfer pricing study. The tax officer proposes to redetermine the value under Rule 28(1)(b) of the CGST Rules. Analyze the interplay between Rule 28, the concept of 'open market value' under Explanation to Rule 28, and the conditions precedent for invoking each valuation sub-rule. Does the existence of a transfer pricing order under the Income Tax Act create any estoppel or binding precedent on the GST valuation?
questions.expected_depth: Rule 28 hierarchy, TP order non-binding in GST, Circular 76/2018, open market value vs cost+10% fallback, partial ITC impact on valuation choice

questions.question: An importer consistently declares transaction value for identical goods imported from an unrelated foreign supplier. The customs officer rejects the declared value under Rule 12 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007 on grounds of 'doubt', without identifying any specific reason for doubt beyond a price variance with NIDB data. The importer demands application of sequential valuation rules. At what point does the burden of proof shift from the importer to the department? Discuss the legal standard for 'reasonable doubt' as interpreted by the Supreme Court and CESTAT, the evidentiary value of NIDB data as a sole basis for rejection, and the procedural requirements under Rule 12 before rejection is valid.
questions.expected_depth: Eicher Motors ratio, Rule 12 procedural safeguards, NIDB as indicative not conclusive, SC in Sanjivani / Century Metal Recycling, burden shift mechanics, sequential rules 4–9

title: SSI unit opting for pool exemption can avail exemption on goods cleared for home consumption and can also take credit of duty paid on inputs used in the manufacture of goods exported under bond on payment of duty subject to maintaining sepa
text:
Circular No. 323/39/97-CX

dated 14/7/97

F.No. 345/17/97-TRU

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Tax Research Unit, New Delhi

Subject: Clarification regarding simultaneous availment of SSI exemption for home consumption and Modvat credit for export-
reg.

I am directed to refer to Board"s circular No. 59/88-CX-8 dated 27.10.88 (F.No. 263/32/88-CX 8) wherein it was clarified that
small scale units who have opted for full exemption under notification No. 175/86-CE., could be permitted to avail exemption on
goods cleared for home consumption under the said notification and to take credit of duty paid on inputs used in the
manufacture of goods exported under bond or on payment of duty. It was also clarified that simultaneous availment of SSI
exemption for home consumption and Modvat credit for export would be available provided the SSI units maintain separate
accounts right from raw materials stage to the final production stage for export production as well as for home consumption
production. Further, the units were allowed to take credit of duty paid on inputs used in the goods exported and utilise the same
for payment of duty on similar products after crossing the exemption limit or if the adjustment is not possible for any reason the
credit could be refunded in accordance with the provisions of Modvat rules.

2.    doubts have been raised whether Board"s above said instructions are equally applicable to units availing SSI exemption
under notification No. 16/97-CE., dated the 1st April, 1997, since the benefit of exemption and concessional rate of duty under
the notification is available subject to the condition that the assessee does not avail Modvat credit under rule 57A upto a
clearance value of Rs. 100 lakhs.

3.    The matter has been examined. It is observed that the aforesaid notification allows exemption on specified goods cleared
for home consumption only. Goods cleared for export either under bond or on payment of duty are not covered under the said
notification. Therefore, it the assessee wants to avail Modvat credit of duty paid on inputs used in the manufacture of goods
exported, such credit can not be denied.

4.    Further, the credit of duty paid on inputs used in the final products cleared for export under bond is also available under
Rule 57F(13). The credit so taken by a manufacturer can be utilised towards the payment of duty on similar final products
cleared for home consumption on payment of duty and where such adjustment is not possible, the credit can be refunded to
him in accordance with the procedure prescribed by the Government.

5.    It is, therefore, clarified that the Board"s aforesaid instructions would mutatis mutandis apply to the units, simultaneously
availing exemption under notification No. 16/97-CE., and Modvat credit on inputs used for their export production. The credit so
availed can either be utilised for payment of duty when the units start paying duty after crossing the limit of Rs. 100 lakhs or the
same can be refunded as per the provision of Modvat rules. It may kindly be ensured that all such units maintain separate
accounts properly right from raw materials stage to the final production stage for export production as well as for home
consumption production.

Applicability of Clarification

questions.question: A supplier files GSTR-1 declaring an inter-state supply to a registered recipient, but the recipient's GSTR-2B does not reflect the corresponding ITC for three consecutive months. The supplier claims the return was filed and GSTN portal shows 'submitted' but not 'processed'. Under Section 16(2)(aa) read with Rule 36(4), what is the recipient's legal exposure, what remedies are available under Section 43A and the GSTR-3B amendment mechanism, and how does the proviso to Section 16(2) interact with the two-year limitation under Section 16(4) if the credit window is about to expire?

An importer brings in goods from a related foreign party under a long-term supply agreement with a royalty clause: 5% of net sales payable post-import, conditioned on achieving minimum volume thresholds. The customs officer proposes to add the royalty to transaction value under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation Rules, 2007. The importer contests this on grounds that the royalty is contingent, post-import, and not a 'condition of sale'. Citing the Supreme Court's reasoning in Ferodo India and the CESTAT divergence on contingent royalties, construct the strongest legal argument for each side, identify which Customs Circular (if any) governs, and specify the valuation fallback sequence under Rules 4 through 9 if transaction value is ultimately rejected.

title: Seeks to prescribe Compensation cess  rate of 0.1% on supply of taxable goods by a registered supplier to a registered recipient for export subject to specified conditions.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3,

SUBSECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 01/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate)

New Delhi, the 16th January, 2025

G.S.R.
(E).-In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 11 of the Central Goods and

Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017), read with sub-section (1) of section 11 of the Goods and Services Tax

(Compensation to States) Act, 2017 (15 of 2017) and sub-section (1) of section 6 of the Integrated Goods and

Services Tax Act, 2017 (13 of 2017), read with sub-section (2) of section 11 of the Goods and Services Tax

(Compensation to States) Act, 2017 (15 of 2017), the Central Government, on being satisfied that it is

necessary in the public interest so to do, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby exempts the intra-

state and inter-state supply of taxable goods (hereafter in this notification referred to as “the said goods”) by a

registered supplier to a registered recipient for export, from so much of the compensation cess leviable thereon

under section 8 of the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017 (15 of 2017), as is in

excess of the amount calculated at the rate of 0.1 per cent., subject to fulfilment of the following conditions,

namely: -

(i)
the registered supplier shall supply the goods to the registered recipient on a tax invoice;

(ii)
the registered recipient shall export the said goods within a period of ninety days from the

date of issue of a tax invoice by the registered supplier;

(iii)
the registered recipient shall indicate the Goods and Services Tax Identification Number of

the registered supplier and the tax invoice number issued by the registered supplier in respect of the

said goods in the shipping bill or bill of export, as the case may be;

(iv)
the registered recipient
shall be registered with an Export Promotion Council or a

Commodity Board recognized by the Department of Commerce;

(v)
the registered recipient shall place an order on registered supplier for procuring goods at

concessional rate and a copy of the same shall also be provided to the jurisdictional tax officer of the

registered supplier;

(vi)
the registered recipient shall move the said goods from place of registered supplier –

(a)
directly to the Port, Inland Container Deport, Airport or Land Customs Station from

where the said goods are to be exported; or

(b)
directly to a registered warehouse from where the said goods shall be move to the

Port, Inland Container Deport, Airport or Land Customs Station from where the said goods

are to be exported;

questions.question: A registered supplier provides works contract services for construction of an immovable property to another registered person. The recipient intends to further use this immovable property exclusively for making taxable outward supplies. Can the recipient claim input tax credit on the GST charged by the works contract supplier, and if so, what conditions under Section 17(5) of the CGST Act must be satisfied? Additionally, how does the nature of the immovable property (plant and machinery vs. civil structure) affect this determination?

A non-resident taxable person NRTP operates in India for 30 days in a financial year, supplying digital services to unregistered Indian recipients. They have applied for registration 5 days before commencement of business. During this registration-pending period, they collect tax at 18% IGST and deposit it using their Temporary Reference Number TRN. Post-registration, can these advance tax deposits be adjusted against actual liability, and what is the mechanism under the CGST Rules for such adjustment? Further, what happens to unused advance tax if the NRTP does not extend registration beyond 90 days?

title:
Annual Supplement 2013 to Foreign Trade Policy (2009-14).
Regarding Exemption under Post Export EPCG Duty Credit Scrip.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two distinct units: Unit A (in a Special Economic Zone) and Unit B (in the Domestic Tariff Area). Unit B procures raw materials, partially processes them, and job-works the semi-finished goods to Unit A for further processing and export. Unit A then exports the finished goods without payment of IGST under a Letter of Undertaking. During a GST audit, the department contends that the supply from Unit B to Unit A constitutes a taxable inter-unit supply under Schedule I of the CGST Act (distinct persons), and that ITC claimed by Unit B on inputs used for such supply is ineligible under Section 17(2) since the onward supply by Unit A is exempt (zero-rated). The company argues the transaction is a job-work supply under Section 143, making ITC fully eligible. Analyze the legal merits of both positions, identify the applicable circulars and case law, and advise on the optimal structuring of this transaction to protect ITC and minimize litigation risk.

An Indian subsidiary of a US multinational provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement. During AY 2023-24, the Transfer Pricing Officer recharacterizes the arrangement, arguing that the Indian entity bears significant entrepreneurial risk (as evidenced by sustained losses over 3 years, ownership of process IP, and the presence of key decision-makers in India), and should be benchmarked as a 'limited risk entity' receiving a residual profit share rather than a routine cost-plus return. Simultaneously, the AO proposes to treat a $4M inter-company loan (at LIBOR + 150bps) as deemed equity under thin capitalization principles, disallowing interest under Section 94B. The company has a MAP application pending before CBDT for the prior AY on the same TP issue. Evaluate the TP recharacterization using the Most Appropriate Method analysis, assess the Section 94B disallowance in light of OECD BEPS Action 4 alignment in Indian law, and advise on the interplay between the ongoing MAP and the current assessment — including whether the MAP outcome can be applied prospectively or requires a fresh application.

title: Circular to clarify the procedure in respect of return of time expired drugs or medicines
text:
Circular No. 72/46/2018-GST

F. No. CBEC/20/16/04/2018-GST

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs

GST Policy Wing

New Delhi, Dated the 26th October, 2018

To,

The Principal Chief Commissioners/Chief Commissioners/Principal Commissioners/ 
Commissioners of Central Tax (All)

The Principal Directors General/Directors General (All)

Madam/Sir,

Subject: Circular to clarify the procedure in respect of return of time expired drugs or 
medicines - Reg.

Various representations have been received seeking clarification on the procedure to

be followed in respect of return of time expired drugs or medicines under the GST laws. The

issues raised in the said representations have been examined and to ensure uniformity in the

implementation of the law across the field formations, the Board, in exercise of its powers

conferred under section 168(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (hereinafter

referred to as the “CGST Act”) hereby clarifies the issue in succeeding paragraphs.

2.    The common trade practice in the pharmaceutical sector is that the drugs or medicines

(hereinafter referred to as “goods”) are sold by the manufacturer to the wholesaler and by the

wholesaler to the retailer on the basis of an invoice/bill of supply as case may be. It is

significant to mention here that such goods have a defined life term which is normally

referred to as the date of expiry. Such goods which have crossed their date of expiry are

colloquially referred to as time expired goods and are returned back to the manufacturer, on

account of expiry, through the supply chain.

3. It is clarified that the retailer/ wholesaler can follow either of the below mentioned

procedures for the return of the time expired goods:

Page 1 of 4

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two distinct units registered under the same GSTIN — Unit A (in an SEZ) and Unit B (in a DTA). Unit B procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit A for processing, then re-imports the finished goods into the DTA for domestic sale. The company has been availing ITC on inputs used in Unit B and reversing proportionate credit under Rule 42/43. The SEZ unit has also filed LUTs for zero-rated supplies. Upon audit, the department contends that: (1) the inter-unit transfer from DTA to SEZ constitutes a taxable supply requiring GST payment under Schedule I since both units share the same PAN but distinct GSTIN registrations, and (2) the ITC reversal methodology applied was incorrect because turnover of zero-rated supplies was excluded from the denominator in the common credit formula. Evaluate the department's dual contention, citing the relevant provisions of CGST Act 2017, applicable rules, and any AAR/AAAR/High Court rulings that would govern each limb of the dispute.
questions.expected_depth: Multi-provision statutory analysis with case law; requires reconciling s.7/Schedule-I, s.16/17, Rule 42/43, SEZ Act 2005 s.26, and circular clarifications

questions.question: An Indian importer purchases capital machinery from its wholly-owned parent company incorporated in Germany. The declared transaction value is €500,000. The customs department rejects this value on the grounds that (a) the related-party relationship under Rule 2(2) of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007 taints the declared price, and (b) contemporaneous imports of identical machinery by unrelated buyers were cleared at values 35% higher. The importer argues: (i) the transaction value satisfies the 'test values' under Rule 4 using its own historical imports from the same supplier at the same price, and (ii) the department's comparable transaction data involves machinery of different technical specifications and vintage. Additionally, the importer paid a lump-sum royalty to the same parent for a patent licence embedded in the machinery's operation — the department seeks to add this royalty to the customs value under Rule 10(1)(c). Analyse all three contested issues — related-party valuation, rejection methodology, and royalty addition — with reference to the Customs Act 1962, the 2007 Valuation Rules, the WTO Customs Valuation Agreement, and relevant CESTAT/Supreme Court precedent.
questions.expected_depth: Requires sequential application of Rules 3→4→5→6→7→8 of 2007 Rules, WTO CVA Articles 1–7, and jurisprudence on royalty nexus test (Matsushita, Ferodo India line)

title: Seeks to amend CGST Rules notification no 3/2017-Central Tax dt 19.06.2017
text:
(5) The fees for filing of appeal or restoration of appeal shall be one thousand rupees for 
every one lakh rupees of tax or input tax credit involved or the difference in tax or input tax 
credit involved or the amount of fine, fee or penalty determined in the order appealed 
against, subject to a maximum of twenty five thousand rupees.

(6) There shall be no fee for application made before the Appellate Tribunal for rectification 
of errors referred to in sub-section (10) of section 112.

111. Application to the Appellate Tribunal.- (1) An application to the Appellate Tribunal 
under sub-section (3) of section 112 shall be made electronically or otherwise, in FORM 
GST APL-07, along with the relevant documents on the common portal.

(2)  A certified copy of the decision or order appealed against shall be submitted within 
seven days of filing the application under sub-rule (1) and an appeal number shall be 
generated by the Registrar.

112. Production of additional evidence before the Appellate Authority or the Appellate 
Tribunal.-  (1) The appellant shall not be allowed to produce before the Appellate Authority 
or the Appellate Tribunal any evidence, whether oral or documentary, other than the 
evidence produced by him during the course of the proceedings before the adjudicating 
authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate Authority except in the following 
circumstances, namely:-

(a) where the adjudicating authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate

Authority has refused to admit evidence which ought to have been admitted; 
or

(b) where the appellant was prevented by sufficient cause from producing the

evidence which he was called upon to produce by the adjudicating authority 
or, as the case may be, the Appellate Authority; or

(c) where the appellant was prevented by sufficient cause from producing before

the adjudicating authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate Authority any 
evidence which is relevant to any ground of appeal; or

(d) where the adjudicating authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate

Authority has made the order appealed against without giving sufficient 
opportunity to the appellant to adduce evidence relevant to any ground of 
appeal.

(2) No evidence shall be admitted under sub-rule (1) unless the Appellate Authority or

the Appellate Tribunal records in writing the reasons for its admission.

(3) The Appellate Authority or the Appellate Tribunal shall not take any evidence

produced under sub-rule (1) unless the adjudicating authority or an officer 
authorised in this behalf by the said authority has been allowed a reasonable 
opportunity -

(a) to examine the evidence or document or to cross-examine any witness

produced by the appellant; or

(b) to produce any evidence or any witness in rebuttal of the evidence produced

by the appellant under sub-rule (1).

(4) Nothing contained in this rule shall affect the power of the Appellate Authority or

the Appellate Tribunal to direct the production of any document, or the examination 
of any witness, to enable it to dispose of the appeal.

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline ingests 108k chunks from Indian tax law PDFs using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU. Under sustained query load, you observe that retrieval latency spikes from 180ms to 2.8s every 45–90 minutes, but only when the LLM inference server (Qwen3-14B on GPU 2) is simultaneously processing a request. Your vector store (Qdrant) is on the same host with 4 CPU cores. Describe the full diagnostic methodology you would use to isolate whether the bottleneck is (a) PCIe bandwidth saturation between GPU 2 and CPU, (b) Qdrant HNSW graph traversal competing for CPU cycles with the async FastAPI worker pool, or (c) memory pressure causing the OS to swap embedding model weights. For each hypothesis, specify the exact tool invocations, metrics thresholds that confirm or rule out each cause, and the remediation strategy without adding hardware.

You are building a retrieval system over heterogeneous Indian statutory corpus: GST circulars (structured, short paragraphs), Income Tax Act sections (dense cross-references with sub-clauses like 'section 80C(2)(xiii)(b)'), and CBIC trade notices (semi-structured, mixed Hindi/English legalese). A practitioner reports that hybrid BM25+dense retrieval achieves 94% recall on GST queries but drops to 61% on Income Tax cross-reference queries where the user asks about a specific sub-clause. Without changing the embedding model or adding a reranker, design a chunking and metadata strategy that brings cross-reference recall above 85%. Specify: chunk boundary rules, metadata fields to index, query pre-processing transforms, and how you would validate the improvement using a held-out eval set of 200 known relevant (section, query) pairs.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a related party (wholly-owned subsidiary) at a price below cost. The subsidiary then resells to an unrelated buyer at market price. The department contends the transaction value is not acceptable under Section 15(2)(b) of the CGST Act and proposes to adopt Rule 30 (cost construction method) for valuation. The manufacturer argues that the open market value under Rule 28 should apply, and since no such supply exists between unrelated parties, Rule 29 should be skipped entirely and Rule 31 (residual method) should be applied using the subsidiary's resale price minus gross margin. (a) Trace the correct sequential application of valuation rules under the GST Valuation Rules 2017 for this transaction. (b) Analyze whether the department's invocation of Rule 30 bypassing Rule 31 is legally sustainable. (c) Advise on documentation the manufacturer must maintain to rebut a show-cause notice under Section 74 alleging suppression.

An Indian company (ICo) has a wholly-owned subsidiary in Mauritius (MCo) which holds a 12% stake in an Indian unlisted startup (StartupCo). MCo sells its stake in StartupCo to a US PE fund, realizing a gain. Simultaneously, ICo has an outstanding loan from MCo which is written off as bad debt in ICo's books. (a) Analyze whether the gain on sale of shares by MCo is taxable in India under the India-Mauritius DTAA post the 2016 Protocol amendment, specifically examining the grandfathering clause applicability given the acquisition date is March 2015 and sale date is January 2026. (b) Determine the Indian withholding tax implications, if any, on the deemed dividend arising from the loan write-off under Section 2(22)(e) of the Income Tax Act, and whether the DTAA dividend article provides relief. (c) Assess whether ICo's transfer pricing documentation must include the MCo loan under Section 92B and the consequence of non-maintenance under Section 271AA.
