questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a dealer in Gujarat under a works contract that involves both supply of goods and installation services. The invoice is raised for ₹50 lakhs inclusive of all charges. The dealer claims full ITC, but the tax authority contends that the works contract is a composite supply and the principal supply is a service, thereby restricting ITC under Section 17(5)(c). The manufacturer argues that since the goods are movable and independently marketable, it constitutes a mixed supply. Analyze the applicable classification, determine the correct tax treatment, and assess the ITC eligibility of the recipient under the CGST Act 2017 with reference to relevant circulars and judicial precedents.

An Indian subsidiary of a foreign holding company imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously receives an interest-free loan from the parent entity for funding the import. The DGFT export obligation runs for 6 years. Midway, the parent entity converts the loan into equity, triggering a downstream investment re-classification under FEMA. The subsidiary subsequently fails to meet 50% of its export obligation within the first 4 years. Identify all concurrent regulatory exposures — Customs duty redemption liability, FEMA compounding obligation, and any transfer pricing implications — and outline the priority sequence for remediation filings across DGFT, RBI, and income tax authorities.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat) operating under the same PAN. Unit A supplies semi-finished goods to Unit B for further processing, and Unit B exports the finished goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). Unit A had accumulated Input Tax Credit of ₹42 lakhs on capital goods purchased in FY 2023-24. Considering Rule 43 of CGST Rules, the restriction under Section 17(5), and the cross-state stock transfer valuation requirements under Rule 28, what is the net ITC eligible for Unit A, and how should the inter-unit supply be valued to avoid a demand under Section 74 for willful misstatement?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A software services company (turnover ₹180 crore) receives an advance of ₹15 crore in March 2025 for a multi-year SaaS contract to be executed over 36 months. The contract includes a variable pricing clause tied to usage metrics determined quarterly. Under AS-9 / Ind AS 115, revenue recognition is deferred. How should the company treat: (i) GST liability on the advance under Section 13(2) CGST Act vs. the time-of-supply rules for continuous supply of services, (ii) the deferred revenue for income tax purposes under Section 145A, and (iii) the TCS obligations under Section 206C(1H) if any part of this is re-invoiced to overseas affiliates — specifically identifying where the GST and Income Tax treatment diverge and the compliance risk arising from that divergence?

title: Regarding Arrest and Bail under Central Excise Act, 1944
text:
Circular No. 974/08/2013-CX

F. No. 4/2/2011-CX-1

Government of India

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

New Delhi

******

New Delhi, the 17 th September 2013

To

All Chief Commissioners of Central Excise & Customs,

All Chief Commissioners of Central Excise,

All Directors General,

Sir/Madam,

Subject - Arrest and Bail under Central Excise Act, 1944 - reg.

1.1 I am directed to invite your attention to the amendments to sections 9A, 20 and 21 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 vide the
Finance Act, 2013. A new sub-section (1A) has been inserted in section 9A to specify that the offences relating to excisable
goods, where the duty involvement exceeds Rs. fifty lakh and which are punishable under clause (b) or clause (bbbb) of sub-
section (1) of section 9, are cognizable and non-bailable. For ease of reference, clause (b) and clause (bbbb) of sub-section
9(1) as well as new sub-section 9A (1A) are summarized below:

(i) Clause (b) of sub-section 9(1) - Whoever evades the payment of any duty payable under this Act;

(ii) Clause (bbbb) of sub-section 9(1) - Whoever contravenes any of the provisions of this Act or the rules made there under in
relation to credit of any duty allowed to be utilised towards payment of excise duty on final product.

(iii) Sub-section 9A(1A) - The offences relating to excisable goods where the duty leviable thereon under this Act exceeds fifty
lakh rupees and punishable under clause (b) or clause (bbbb) of sub-section (1) of section 9, shall be cognizable and non-
bailable.

1.2 All other offences under section 9 are non-cognizable and bailable.

2.1 Thus the offences under Central Excise Act fall in two categories - bailable and non-bailable. Depending on the type of
offence committed, a person is liable to be arrested in either of the two category of offence. Since arrest takes away the liberty
of an individual, the power must be exercised with utmost care and caution and only when the exigencies of the situation
demand arrest.

Non-bailable offences

3.1 A person is liable to be arrested for non-bailable offence only when the offence committed by him is covered under clause
(b) or clause (bbbb) of sub-section 9(1) and the duty involvement exceeds Rs. fifty lakh. Thus, it is essential to examine
offences in each and every case with reference to each of the clauses of sub-section 9(1) and also the quantum of duty involved
prior to invoking the arrest provisions. Only where clause (b) or clause (bbbb) are the most appropriate clauses to describe the
offence and duty involved exceeds rupees fifty lakhs, these provisions should be invoked. Any person arrested for offences
under these clauses should be informed of the grounds of arrest and produced before a magistrate without unnecessary delay
and within 24 hours of arrest.

3.2 A list of non-bailable offences where decision to arrest may be taken by the Commissioner is given below:-

(a) clandestine removal of manufactured goods;

(b) removal of goods without declaring the correct assessable value and receiving a portion of sale price in cash which is in
excess of invoice price and not accounted for in the books of account;

(c) taking Cenvat Credit without the receiving the goods specified in the invoice;

(d) taking Cenvat Credit on fake invoices;

(e) issuing Cenvatable invoices without delivering the goods specified in the said invoice.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units: Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat). Unit A procures raw materials worth ₹50 lakhs under a Bill-to Ship-to arrangement where the billing is to Unit B but delivery is to Unit A's factory. Unit A then transfers the processed goods to Unit B as a stock transfer. Unit B exports these goods under LUT without payment of IGST. Subsequently, GST authorities in Gujarat issue a notice to Unit B alleging ineligibility of ITC on the grounds that the original supply chain involved a cross-state stock transfer and the export proceeds were not received within the RBI-mandated timeline. Analyze the ITC eligibility under Section 16 and Section 17 of CGST Act 2017, the implications of Rule 89 for refund, and whether the Bill-to Ship-to transaction under Section 10(1)(b) of IGST Act creates any deemed supply complications for Unit A.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A listed Indian company receives a ₹200 crore strategic investment from a Mauritius-based entity via compulsorily convertible debentures (CCDs) carrying 12% interest. The CCDs convert to equity after 3 years. During the holding period: (1) the interest payments are made quarterly, (2) the Mauritius entity sub-participates 40% of its CCD interest to a Singapore entity under a sub-participation agreement, (3) the Indian company subsequently does a rights issue at a premium, causing the pre-conversion equity value to rise significantly. Analyze: (a) withholding tax obligations under Section 195 read with India-Mauritius DTAA post-2016 protocol amendments for both the direct interest and the sub-participation leg; (b) whether CCDs constitute 'debt' or 'equity' for transfer pricing purposes under Rule 10B and the implications for arm's length interest rate determination; (c) GST applicability on the interest/financial service under Notification 9/2017-IT(Rate) exemption and whether the sub-participation creates a separate taxable supply in India.
expected_knowledge_areas: Section 195 TDS on interest to non-residents
CCD classification debt vs equity TP
Place of supply Section 13 IGST for banking/financial services
answer_complexity_indicators: GST-income tax intersection on financial instruments

And whereas, in the matter of review of anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject

goods, originating in or exported from the subject country, the designated authority in its final 
findings, published vide notification No. F. No. 7/24/2019-DGTR, dated the 18th June, 2020 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part- I, Section 1, dated the 18th June, 2020 
has come to the conclusion that: -

(i) 
there is continued dumping of the subject goods from subject country and the 
imports are likely to enter the Indian market at dumped prices in the event of expiry 
of duty;

(ii) 
the domestic industry’s performance has improved but remains vulnerable to 
dumping and consequent injury;

(iii) the information on record shows likelihood of continuation/ recurrence of dumping

and injury in case the anti-dumping duty in force is allowed to cease at this stage;

(iv) there is sufficient evidence to indicate that the cessation of anti-dumping duty at this

stage will lead to continuation of dumping and recurrence of injury to the Domestic 
Industry,

and has recommended continued imposition of the anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject 
goods, originating in or exported from the subject country;

questions.question: A manufacturing company imports capital goods under EPCG authorization and simultaneously claims ITC on the same goods under Section 16 of the CGST Act. The jurisdictional officer issues a SCN alleging double benefit, citing Rule 96(10) read with Section 54(3) restriction. The company argues that EPCG duty exemption operates under Customs Act 1962 while ITC is a separate statutory right under CGST. Analyze the interplay between EPCG scheme conditions, Rule 96(10) CGST Rules 2017, and the embargo on ITC reversal under Section 17(5)(h). Is the officer's position legally sustainable post the Finance Act 2021 amendment? Cite relevant circulars and tribunal precedents.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides IT-enabled services to its parent and benchmarks the transaction using TNMM with operating profit/operating cost as the PLI, reporting an OP/OC of 18%. The TPO rejects TNMM and applies CUP using internal comparables from a third-party contract the subsidiary holds, arriving at a deemed ALP 34% higher. The assessee challenges this on the grounds that the third-party contract involves assumption of credit risk absent in the AE transaction, making it not 'comparable uncontrolled'. The DRP upholds TPO. On appeal to ITAT, which method hierarchy argument under Rule 10B read with Rule 10C should the assessee lead, and how does the 'most appropriate method' standard interact with the FAR analysis required under OECD BEPS Action 8-10 guidelines as adopted in India's amended Transfer Pricing Rules effective AY 2024-25?

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a factory in Maharashtra and a registered office in Delhi. During an audit, the GST officer disallows input tax credit on capital goods purchased for the factory on the ground that the supplier's GSTIN was cancelled retrospectively 18 months after the transaction. The company had verified the supplier's registration at the time of purchase and holds a valid tax invoice with GSTR-2B reflection. Under Sections 16, 17, and 74 of the CGST Act read with Rule 86A, can the department recover ITC already availed and impose penalty? What is the evidentiary threshold required to establish 'knowledge' or 'reason to believe' on the part of the recipient, and how do recent Supreme Court and High Court rulings on bona fide purchaser doctrine apply here?

An importer brings in specialised CNC machinery from a German parent company under a long-term intra-group supply agreement. Customs authorities reject the transaction value under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, invoking the related-party test under Rule 2(2), and propose to re-determine value using computed value under Rule 8. The importer contends that the price was settled at arm's length per a contemporaneous transfer pricing study accepted by the Income Tax department under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act. To what extent does an APA or TP order under the Income Tax Act bind Customs valuation proceedings? Analyse the interplay between Section 14 of the Customs Act, the WTO Valuation Agreement, and the CBIC Circular on related-party imports, and identify the procedural steps to challenge the re-determination before the CESTAT.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat). Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit B as a stock transfer under the same GSTIN umbrella (distinct persons per Section 25). Unit B processes and exports finished goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). During an audit, the GST officer disallows ITC on the stock transfer at Unit A, arguing the supply is exempt since the export is zero-rated. Simultaneously, Unit B's LUT is retroactively cancelled for a prior period due to a technical default. Analyze: (1) whether the stock transfer between distinct persons constitutes a taxable supply and ITC eligibility under Section 16 read with Section 20 of the IGST Act, (2) the cascading impact of the LUT cancellation on Unit B's zero-rated supply status and whether IGST becomes payable with interest under Rule 96A, and (3) the remedies available to restore the LUT and recover wrongly blocked ITC, citing relevant circulars and GST Council recommendations.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a foreign MNC provides software development services to its parent (ForeignCo) under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using the TNMM method. The TPO rejects the TNMM and applies CUP using publicly available contract rates, resulting in an upward adjustment of ₹85 crore. Concurrently, IndCo received a royalty waiver from ForeignCo for use of IP — the TPO imputes a notional royalty income of ₹30 crore under Section 92 read with Rule 10B. IndCo also claimed a deduction under Section 10AA for its SEZ unit, which the AO partially disallows on grounds that the TP adjustment inflated export turnover artificially. Analyze: (1) the legal validity of the TPO substituting TNMM with CUP without establishing that CUP is the 'most appropriate method' under Rule 10C, (2) whether imputing notional royalty income on a waiver constitutes an 'international transaction' under Section 92B and the evidentiary standard required, and (3) the interaction between the TP adjustment and Section 10AA deduction computation, specifically whether the adjustment to ALP automatically feeds into the export turnover formula and the appellate strategy before the ITAT.

questions.question: A manufacturer registered under GST in Maharashtra supplies goods to a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) unit in Gujarat under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). The supplier also has an Input Service Distributor (ISD) registration at its head office in Delhi that distributes credit for shared services. During the same tax period, the ISD receives a credit note from a vendor reducing the ITC originally distributed to the Maharashtra unit. Simultaneously, the Maharashtra unit discovers it had claimed excess ITC in a prior period due to a vendor's GSTR-1 amendment that reduced the invoice value. Analyze: (a) the cascading adjustment obligations under Rule 39 for the ISD credit note, (b) whether the zero-rated supply under LUT affects the blocked credit computation under Section 17(5) for the Maharashtra unit, (c) the interest liability timeline under Section 50 for the excess ITC already utilised, and (d) whether rectification must flow through GSTR-3B or can be addressed via DRC-03 without triggering a scrutiny notice under Section 61.

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign seller. The transaction value is initially accepted by customs, but during a post-clearance audit under Section 17(5) of the Customs Act, the proper officer proposes re-determination of value under Rule 5 (identical goods) citing a higher contemporaneous import by an unrelated buyer. The importer argues: (i) the relationship did not influence the price per Rule 3(3)(b) as demonstrated by test values, (ii) the comparator import included assists not declared by that importer, making it an improper benchmark, and (iii) any differential duty demand is time-barred under the proviso to Section 28 because no suppression or mis-statement occurred. The importer further claims deductive value under Rule 7 as an alternative. Evaluate: (a) the burden-of-proof allocation between the importer and the proper officer under Rule 3(3) read with the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, (b) whether undisclosed assists in the comparator transaction legally disqualify it as an 'identical goods' benchmark, (c) the applicability of the extended limitation period given that the original assessment was 'self-assessment' under Section 17(1), and (d) the procedural prerequisites for the importer to validly invoke Rule 7 deductive value when Rules 4–6 have been sequentially rejected.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under a composite contract that includes both the principal supply (taxable at 18%) and an ancillary service (taxable at 12%). The recipient claims the entire transaction should be taxed at the lower rate under the 'naturally bundled' test from the CBIC circular on mixed/composite supplies. However, the supplier's ERP system has issued separate invoices for each component. The department has raised a SCN treating the arrangement as a mixed supply and applying the highest rate of 18% to the entire transaction. Analyze the evidentiary weight of the split invoices, the applicability of Section 8 vs Section 2(74) vs Section 2(30), and the precedential value of any advance rulings vs AAR appellate orders on composite supply characterization when the contractual intent and billing practice conflict.

An Indian importer declares customs value for related-party imports using the transaction value method under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, supported by a contemporaneous TP study showing the ALP under TNMM. The customs department rejects Rule 3 on the ground that the relationship influenced the price, and benchmarks under Rule 5 (deductive value) yielding a 23% upward revision. The income tax TP order for the same year, passed earlier, accepted the TNMM study and made no adjustment. The importer argues res judicata / issue estoppel based on the TP order. Evaluate: (a) whether customs valuation and TP ALP determinations operate in legally isolated silos under Indian law, (b) the binding effect of CBEC Circular 39/2016-Customs on accepted TP studies, (c) the revenue-neutrality argument under Section 17(5) IGST read with customs duty credit mechanics, and (d) the correct appellate forum sequencing if both CESTAT and ITAT have issued conflicting findings on the same transaction.

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka under a works contract that involves both supply of goods and installation services. The invoice value is ₹45 lakhs inclusive of GST. During a GST audit, the officer contends that the entire transaction should be classified as a 'composite supply' with the principal supply being services (works contract @ 18%), rather than a split between goods (12%) and services (18%) as declared by the assessee. The assessee argues that the goods component (fabricated steel structure) can be independently sold and was specifically negotiated as a separate line item. Analyze the applicable GST classification, the correct determination of principal supply under Section 2(90) read with Section 8 of the CGST Act, the place of supply implications under Section 12(3) vs Section 13(3), and the ITC eligibility for the recipient. What documentary evidence should the assessee produce to defend the split-supply treatment?

An Indian subsidiary of a German parent company imports raw pharmaceutical intermediates. Customs valued these at ₹800/kg using transaction value method. The same goods are simultaneously being benchmarked by the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) under the TNMM method, who proposes an ALP adjustment that effectively implies the imports should have been priced at ₹650/kg. The assessee faces a paradox: accepting the TPO's ALP would expose them to a customs undervaluation challenge (since they paid more than ALP), while defending the customs value would contradict the transfer pricing position. Under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, the Safe Harbour provisions under Section 92CB, and CBIC Circular No. 04/2011-Customs, what is the legally defensible unified strategy to present before both the TPO and the Customs Appellate Authority? Specifically address whether a bilateral APA covering both direct tax and customs valuation is available under Indian law, and what the assessee's exposure is under Section 114A of the Customs Act if the dual-authority conflict is not resolved.

questions.question: A manufacturer registered in Maharashtra supplies goods to a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) unit in Gujarat under a zero-rated supply. The supplier claims a refund of accumulated Input Tax Credit (ITC) under Rule 89 of CGST Rules. However, the supplier had also used a portion of the same inputs for both exempt and taxable supplies during the tax period. How should the refund amount be calculated under the proportionate reversal mechanism, and what is the impact of Section 17(2) read with Rule 42 on the refundable ITC, specifically when the exempt supply ratio changes between the original tax period and the date of refund application?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a foreign multinational provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement documented in an intercompany agreement. During an assessment, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) rejects the Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) applied by the assessee and instead applies the Comparable Uncontrolled Price (CUP) method using three comparable transactions, two of which involve geographies with materially different labor cost structures. The assessee argues that location savings adjustments must be made under Rule 10B(1)(a). Simultaneously, the TPO invokes Section 92CA(2A) to make secondary adjustments. What are the precise procedural and substantive grounds on which the assessee can challenge both the method selection and the secondary adjustment before the Dispute Resolution Panel (DRP), and how does the proviso to Section 92CE interact with the 30-day repatriation requirement when the primary adjustment itself is under appeal?

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) to a related party overseas at a declared transaction value of ₹50 lakhs. The customs authority challenges this under Rule 28 of CGST Rules alleging under-valuation, while the exporter simultaneously claims a refund of unutilized ITC under Section 54(3). The DGFT has also issued a Show Cause Notice questioning the advance authorization utilized for inputs. Analyze the interplay between GST valuation rules for related-party transactions, the customs arm's-length price determination, and the export refund eligibility under Section 54 — specifically whether a pending valuation dispute freezes the refund claim, and what procedural remedies are available if the adjudicating authority conflates the three proceedings.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a multinational group pays a 'guarantee fee' of 3% per annum to its foreign parent for a corporate guarantee on external commercial borrowings of USD 200 million. The TPO re-characterizes the arrangement as a deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(e) on grounds that the guarantee constitutes a loan equivalent, while simultaneously applying the Comparable Uncontrolled Price method using a benchmarked fee of 0.5% — resulting in both a transfer pricing addition and a deemed dividend disallowance on the same underlying transaction. Assess whether dual taxation of the same economic event is permissible under the Act, the interaction with Article 11 and 21 of the applicable DTAA, the relief mechanism available under Section 90 read with MAP provisions, and the evidentiary standard required before the ITAT to successfully challenge the re-characterization.

questions.question: In a 4-core CPU-constrained environment running a Qdrant vector store with 108k chunks, a Vulkan-accelerated BGE-M3 embedding model, and a Qwen3-14B LLM served via llama-server, query latency spikes to 45s under concurrent load despite async FastAPI workers. Profiling shows 60% of time in embedding generation, 25% in Qdrant search, and 15% in LLM prefill. You have access to 3 additional consumer GPUs (RX 6700 XT class). Design a concrete multi-phase optimization strategy that addresses embedding batching, Qdrant HNSW parameter tuning, and LLM KV-cache warming — specifying exact parameter values, worker topology, and the precise order of changes to avoid regression.

A corpus of 471 image-only PDFs in Indian tax law must be ingested into an existing Qdrant RAG system. You have: (a) a Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF model available for LLM-based OCR on a Vulkan GPU, (b) RapidOCR as CPU fallback, and (c) the constraint that the embedding pipeline is already saturating GPU 5. The PDFs vary: 30% are clean scanned text, 40% are low-contrast government stamps over printed text, and 30% are handwritten annotations alongside typed content. Design a routing and quality-validation pipeline that selects the optimal OCR path per document, specifies confidence thresholds for fallback triggers, handles chunk boundary integrity across OCR-extracted pages, and integrates with the existing BGE-M3 embedding pipeline without stalling the live query serving path.

questions.question: A manufacturing company in Maharashtra operates under a job-work arrangement under Section 143 of the CGST Act. They send semi-finished goods to a job-worker in Karnataka without payment of tax under a delivery challan. After 12 months, the goods are returned but during transit they are partially destroyed in an accident. The insurance company settles the claim and pays directly to the manufacturer. The job-worker had also incurred some additional processing costs and is claiming reimbursement. Analyze: (a) the GST implications on the insurance settlement received, (b) whether ITC originally availed on the goods is required to be reversed given the partial destruction, (c) the GST treatment on reimbursement paid to job-worker, and (d) whether the extended period of 3 years under Section 143(4) can be invoked and what documentary evidence is required.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A private limited company incorporated in India has a wholly-owned subsidiary in Singapore. The Indian parent provides management consultancy services, IP licensing, and secondment of employees to the Singapore entity. The Singapore entity also provides software development services back to the Indian parent. For AY 2025-26: (a) Determine the transfer pricing methodology applicable to each stream of transactions and identify which would be subject to the most scrutiny by the AO under Section 92CA; (b) Analyze whether the seconded employees create a Permanent Establishment risk in Singapore and the consequent impact on the profit attribution; (c) From a GST perspective, determine whether the Indian parent's receipt of software development services from the Singapore subsidiary constitutes import of services under the reverse charge mechanism, and whether the intermediary services exclusion under Place of Supply rules applies; (d) If the AO makes a TP adjustment upward by ₹5 crore on the management fee, compute the secondary adjustment implications under Section 92CE and the deemed dividend tax exposure.

questions.question: A manufacturing company registered under GST in Maharashtra supplies goods to its 100% subsidiary in Karnataka under a cost-plus arrangement without any markup, treating it as a 'related party transaction' under Schedule I of the CGST Act. The subsidiary subsequently uses these goods as inputs for zero-rated exports under LUT. Simultaneously, the parent company claims ITC on capital goods used exclusively for this inter-unit supply. Analyze: (a) whether the inter-unit supply is taxable notwithstanding absence of consideration, citing the relevant Schedule I entry and valuation rules under Rule 28; (b) whether the open market value or cost-based valuation applies given the recipient's full ITC eligibility; (c) the ITC reversal implications at the subsidiary level if export proceeds are not realized within the RBI-prescribed period; and (d) whether the parent's ITC on capital goods is blocked under Section 17(5) if any portion of the supply chain results in exempt or non-business use.

An Indian importer (wholly-owned subsidiary of a US MNC) imports specialized machinery from its US parent under a long-term agreement. Customs values the goods under Rule 4 (transaction value) but the Special Valuation Branch (SVB) initiates an inquiry under Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007 on grounds of relationship influence. Concurrently, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) proposes an upward adjustment on the same transaction under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act, arguing the ALP is higher than the declared price. Additionally, the RBI raises a query under FEMA 1999 regarding the external commercial borrowing (ECB) the parent used to finance the machinery supply, questioning whether the deferred payment terms constitute an ECB requiring prior RBI approval. Analyze: (a) the evidentiary standard the importer must meet before SVB to rebut relationship influence under Rule 4(3); (b) whether a higher TP adjustment is logically consistent with the customs authority's position that the declared price is artificially low, and how Indian jurisprudence reconciles this apparent contradiction; (c) the applicability of the Master Direction on ECB to supplier's credit arrangements and the threshold beyond which RBI approval is mandatory; and (d) whether advance pricing agreement (APA) coverage, if obtained post-assessment, can be applied retrospectively to nullify both the TP adjustment and SVB loading.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A in a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) and Unit B in the Domestic Tariff Area (DTA). Unit B procures raw materials and transfers them to Unit A under a job work arrangement. Unit A processes the goods and exports them directly. The ITC on inputs used for job work is claimed by Unit B. However, the GSTIN for both units is the same (single registration). The company receives a SCN alleging that the transfer of goods from Unit B to Unit A without payment of IGST is illegal, and that ITC claimed by Unit B on inputs ultimately used in SEZ operations should be reversed. Analyze the legal validity of the SCN with reference to Section 16(3) of the IGST Act, Rule 55 of the CGST Rules (delivery challans), and the Supreme Court's position on cross-charge vs. ISD mechanisms for inter-unit transactions within the same legal entity.

An importer declares a consignment of industrial equipment under CTH 8479 8990 at a declared transaction value of USD 4,20,000. The customs officer rejects the transaction value under Rule 12 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, citing (i) a related-party relationship between the buyer and seller under Rule 2(2), and (ii) a contemporaneous import of an identical goods shipment at USD 5,10,000 declared by an unrelated party. The importer challenges this, arguing the related-party price satisfies the 'test values' safe harbour under Rule 3(3)(b) and that the so-called identical goods comparison under Rule 5 is inapplicable because the comparator shipment was at a different commercial level (retail vs. bulk OEM). Walk through the sequenced application of valuation rules under the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 and determine whether the department's re-assessment is legally sustainable, referencing the Tribunal's standard of proof for related-party adjustments.

title: Pre and post audit of abatement orders in terms of Rule 10 of Pan Masala Packing Machine Rules, 2008- clarification regarding
text:
F.No.267/16/2009-CX-8

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Excise & Customs)

New Delhi, dated 12 th March, 2009.

To

All Chief Commissioners of Central Excise and Customs,

All Chief Commissioners of Central Excise,

All Chief Commissioners (LTU),

All Commissioners of Central Excise and Customs,

All Commissioners of Central Excise.

Sir/ Madam,

Subject: Pre and post audit of abatement orders in terms of Rule 10 of Pan Masala Packing Machine Rules, 2008- clarification
regarding.

The undersigned is directed to refer to Pan Masala Packing Machine Rules, 2008 issued under Notification No. 30/2008-CE
(NT) dated 01.07.2008. In terms of Rule 10 of the said rules, the abatement of duty is to be given, in case the factory did not
produce notified goods during any continuous period of 15 days or more. The JDC/JAC has to pass an abatement order in the
case.

2.     Representations have been received from the field formations regarding whether these abatement orders need to be
subjected to pre and post audit in the same manner as the refund/ rebate orders.

3.     The matter has been examined. Board has issued Circular No. 809/06/2005-CX dated 01.03.2005 as amended by
Circular No. 857/15/2007-CX dated 02.11.2007 in context of procedure related to sanction and pre/post audit of refund/rebate
claims. As, the abatement order referred above is in the nature of refund, they are required to be subjected to the same
administrative procedure of pre and post audit as laid down by the Board from time-to-time regarding refund.

4.      In view of above, all circulars of Board issued in context of pre and post audit of refund/ rebate claims will thus apply
mutatis mutandis to the abatement orders also.

5.      The field formations may be suitably informed.

6.      Receipt of this clarification may kindly be acknowledged.

Yours faithfully,

(Amish Kumar Gupta)

OSD(CX-8).

questions.question: Your production RAG system serves 108k chunks of Indian tax law with BGE-M3 embeddings in Qdrant. During peak load, you observe that semantic retrieval returns highly relevant chunks but the LLM's synthesized answer contradicts a specific CBIC circular that was correctly retrieved. Describe your end-to-end diagnostic process: how do you determine whether the failure is (a) retrieval ranking pushing the circular below the context window cutoff, (b) the reranker deprioritizing it due to query phrasing, (c) the LLM ignoring it due to position bias in a long context, or (d) chunking strategy splitting the circular's operative clause from its preamble? For each hypothesis, state the exact metric or log signal that would confirm or rule it out, and the corresponding fix.

You are ingesting a new corpus of 3,200 Income Tax Act PDFs on a 4-core machine with one RX 6700 XT GPU (Vulkan, not ROCm). Your current BGE-M3 Vulkan pipeline achieves 5.2–9.6 chunks/second on a single GPU at GPU 5. The new corpus includes (i) scanned image-only PDFs requiring OCR, (ii) PDFs with complex tabular data (GST rate schedules), and (iii) bilingual documents with Hindi-English mixing. Without adding hardware, design a tiered ingestion strategy that maximizes throughput while preserving semantic fidelity for all three document types. Specify: chunk sizing rationale per type, OCR routing logic, embedding model choice per type if different, Qdrant collection schema decisions, and how you validate that retrieval quality did not degrade versus a text-native baseline.

questions.question: A multi-state e-commerce operator supplies both goods and services through a single platform. In FY 2025-26, their aggregate turnover crosses ₹20 crore. They have a warehouse in Maharashtra (registered), drop-ship from unregistered suppliers in Rajasthan, and their registered office is in Karnataka. Analyse: (a) the place of supply determination for each transaction type under IGST Act sections 10 and 12, (b) the input tax credit eligibility and reversal obligations under Rule 42/43 given the mixed supply classification, and (c) the potential liability exposure if the Rajasthan suppliers are subsequently found to have suppressed turnover, including any joint liability provisions under GST law.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (wholly owned by a Singapore holding company) provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15% using TNMM. The TPO proposes an adjustment arguing the tested party should be the foreign parent, not the Indian entity, and benchmarks using the CUP method against Singapore-based comparables, resulting in a ₹85 crore addition. The assessee contests on grounds of FAR analysis, choice of tested party, and comparability under Rule 10B. Evaluate: (a) the legal validity of TPO's unilateral recharacterisation of the tested party under the Income Tax Act and OECD guidelines, (b) the admissibility of foreign comparables under Indian TP regulations, and (c) available remedies including MAP under the India-Singapore DTAA and domestic appellate relief, with reference to recent ITAT precedents post the 2023 TP safe harbour amendments.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under a composite contract that includes both the supply of customised machinery (HSN 8479) and its installation and commissioning at the buyer's premises. The machinery is designed to specification, cannot be sold separately without significant modification, and installation takes 14 months. The buyer is registered in a different state. Analyse: (a) whether this qualifies as a composite supply or a works contract under Section 2(119) of CGST Act 2017 and the applicable tax rate; (b) the place of supply determination under Section 12(3) vs Section 12(14) of IGST Act and the consequent inter-state vs intra-state character; (c) the input tax credit implications for the buyer if the composite supply is classified as immovable property works contract; and (d) the time of supply for the advance received at contract signing, milestone payments during installation, and the final payment on commissioning — with specific reference to Section 13(2) and any relevant CBIC circulars.

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign entity (holding company owns 51% of importer). The declared transaction value is USD 100/unit. The customs officer proposes to reject the transaction value under Rule 3 of Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007 on grounds of relationship influencing price, and seeks to apply Rule 5 (computed value method). The importer contends that: (i) they have made 18 identical imports at the same price from unrelated buyers in third countries in the past 12 months; (ii) the price covers a royalty of 3% payable to the foreign entity under a separate licence agreement; (iii) a deductible post-import commission of 2% is paid to an Indian agent. Evaluate: (a) the burden of proof and what 'test values' the importer must furnish under Rule 3(3) and the proviso to Rule 3(2) to satisfy the officer that relationship did not influence price; (b) whether the royalty must be included in customs value under Rule 10(1)(c) and the conditions under which it is excludable; (c) the correct treatment of the post-import commission in valuation; and (d) if the transaction value is ultimately rejected, the mandatory sequencing of valuation methods under Rules 4–9 and why the officer cannot jump directly to computed value without exhausting prior methods.

questions.question: Your RAG system indexes 108,000 chunks from Indian tax law PDFs using BGE-M3 embeddings stored in Qdrant. At query time, you retrieve the top-20 chunks by cosine similarity, but practitioners report that answers to multi-section GST questions (e.g., 'How does Section 16(4) interact with Rule 36(4) for ITC reconciliation?') are incomplete or contradictory. Hybrid BM25+dense search and reranking are already in place. What architectural changes — at the chunking, retrieval, or generation layer — would you prioritize to reliably surface cross-section legal reasoning, and how would you evaluate improvement without a labeled ground-truth set?

You are running a 14B-parameter Qwen3 model via llama-server in Vulkan mode on a 4-core CPU host with two AMD RX 6700 XT GPUs. Observed p95 query latency is 42 seconds end-to-end: ~3s for Qdrant retrieval, ~4s for reranking, and ~35s for LLM generation on a 2,800-token prompt. Scaling the GPU fleet is not an option. You have already applied `/no_think` mode and reduced context to 3,500 tokens max. Walk through a prioritized, systems-level remediation plan — covering prompt engineering, batching strategy, speculative decoding feasibility, KV-cache reuse, and request queuing — that could realistically cut p95 to under 15 seconds without degrading answer quality below 90% of baseline.

title: Payment of amount under Rule 6 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004
text:
Circular No. 870/08/2008-CX

F. No.267/91/2007-CX 8

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Excise and Customs)

New Delhi dated the May16, 2008

To

All Chief Commissioners of Customs & Central Excise,

All Chief Commissioners of Central Excise,

All Commissioners of Customs & Central Excise,

All Commissioners of Central Excise,

Sub.: Payment of amount under Rule 6 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004 - reg.

Sir /Madam,

The undersigned is directed to refer to circular No. 599/36/2001-CX dated November, 2001, wherein the issue of the
applicability of the provision of section 11D of the Central Excise Act, 1944 in cases of payments made under erstwhile rule
57CC (1) of the Central Excise Rules,  1944 was examined. It has been brought to the notice of the Board that there are some
decisions of the Tribunal contrary to the said circular. Further, rule 6 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004, has been amended
w.e.f. 01.04.2008, necessitating re-examination of the circular of the circular in the light of these developments.

2.         It is seen that the Larger Bench of the Tribunal in the case of Unison Metals Ltd. Versus Commissioner of Central
Excise, Ahmedabad-I [2006(2004) E.L.T. 323 (Tri.-LB)] has held that section 11D provides that any amount which has been
collected as excise duty and not paid to the credit of the Central Government shall be liable to be recovered.  The scheme of
the Law is that manufacturers shall not collect amounts falsely representing them as Central Excise duty and retain them, thus,
unjustly benefiting themselves.  However, in case of payments made under erstwhile rule 57CC(1),  section 11D of the Act is
not applicable  since the amount of 8% or 10% has already been paid to the revenue and no amount is retained by the
assessee.  The said order of the Tribunal has been accepted by the Department.

3.         The matter has been examined. Sub-rule (3) of rule 6 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004 has been amended w.e.f.
01.04.2008 to provide for payment of an amount equal to 10% of value of the exempted goods, instead of 10% of the price of
the exempted goods as provided earlier. The value is to be determined as per section 4 or 4A of the Central Excise Act, 1944
read with rules made thereunder.

4.         In the light of what is sated above, it is clarified that as long as the amount of 8% or 10% is paid to the Government in
terms of erstwhile rule 57CC of the Central Excise Rules, 1944 or rule 6 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, the provisions of section
11D shall not apply even if the amount is recovered from the buyers.   However, it may be noted that the CENVAT credit of the
said amount of 8% or 10% cannot be taken by the buyer since such payment is not a payment of duty in terms of rule 3(1) of the
CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004. Therefore, the said 10% amount should be shown in the invoice as "10% amount paid under
Rule 6 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004".

5.            Board's circular No. 599/36/2001-CX dated November, 2001 stands withdrawn.

6.         The field formations as well as trade and industry may be suitably informed.

7.         Hindi version will follow.

Yours faithfully,

(Rahul Nangare)

Under Secretary to the Govt. of India

questions.question: A manufacturing company exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. The foreign buyer later rejects 30% of the shipment due to quality defects and the goods are re-imported within 6 months. The company had already claimed refund of accumulated ITC on inputs used for the exported quantity. What are the GST implications on re-import, how must the earlier ITC refund be reversed, and what is the correct treatment of the remaining 70% that was accepted and paid for in foreign exchange?

An Indian subsidiary of a multinational provides software development services to its foreign parent under a cost-plus arrangement. During the assessment year, the subsidiary also receives a royalty-free license for the parent's proprietary platform used exclusively in serving third-party clients in India. The TPO benchmarks both transactions separately using TNMM for services and CUP for the license, and proposes an arm's length royalty of 8% of revenue attributable to platform use. The assessee argues the two transactions are economically interlinked and should be aggregated. Which method and aggregation approach is defensible under Rule 10B/10C, and how should the taxpayer quantify the arm's length price if aggregation is accepted?

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline ingests 108k chunks from Indian tax law PDFs using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU at 5.2–9.6 chunks/second. During peak query load, you observe that retrieval latency spikes from 180ms to 4.2 seconds. A `uptime` check shows load average at 3.8 on a 4-core machine. Profiling reveals 60% of latency is in the Qdrant HNSW search, 25% in the LLM (Qwen3-14B with `/no_think`), and 15% in FastAPI overhead. You cannot add hardware. Propose a concrete remediation plan that addresses all three bottlenecks simultaneously, specifying exact Qdrant HNSW parameters (m, ef_construct, ef), async concurrency settings, and query-time optimizations — and explain the trade-offs each change introduces to recall@10.

You have 471 image-only PDFs from CBIC that contain scanned tables with merged cells, handwritten annotations, and multi-column GST rate schedules. Your current plan uses Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF on llama-server (Vulkan, GPU 4/6) as primary OCR with RapidOCR CPU fallback. Design a confidence-gated routing architecture that: (a) determines at page-level whether to use VL model vs CPU OCR vs hybrid, (b) handles merged-cell tables without losing row/column alignment, (c) produces chunk-ready output compatible with your existing BGE-M3 + Qdrant ingestion pipeline, and (d) recovers gracefully when the VL model OOMs mid-batch. Specify exact decision thresholds, fallback triggers, and how you would validate output quality without ground-truth labels.

questions.question: A manufacturing company registered under GST in Maharashtra supplies goods to its 100% subsidiary in Karnataka under a cost-plus-5% transfer pricing arrangement. The subsidiary then exports these goods to a buyer in Germany under a zero-rated supply with LUT. The IGST officer contends that the valuation between related persons must follow Rule 28 of the CGST Rules and has rejected the cost-plus-5% method, demanding GST on the open market value. The company argues that since the recipient (subsidiary) is eligible for full ITC, the proviso to Rule 28 applies and the invoice value should be accepted. Simultaneously, a GST audit team has raised a demand under Section 74 alleging suppression of facts because the related-party arrangement was not separately disclosed in GSTR-9C. Analyze: (a) the correct valuation methodology applicable, (b) whether the Section 74 invocation (requiring proof of suppression/fraud) is sustainable given the GSTR-9C disclosure gap, and (c) how the zero-rated export at the subsidiary level affects the ITC eligibility condition in the Rule 28 proviso.
questions.expected_answer_depth: Requires integration of valuation rules, audit/adjudication procedure, and export supply classification

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A software company (Indian resident) enters into a composite contract with a US entity to: (i) develop custom enterprise software (deliverable: source code), and (ii) provide 3 years of post-delivery support and maintenance. The total contract value is USD 2 million, with no separate pricing for each component. Under Income Tax, the US entity claims the entire payment is for 'software' and thus falls under the India-USA DTAA Article 12 (Royalties), seeking a lower withholding rate of 15%. The Indian company's TDS officer treats the support component as 'Fees for Technical Services' under Article 12(4)(b) and seeks 25% withholding on an apportioned amount. Simultaneously, under GST, the Indian company has treated the entire contract as an 'export of service' under Section 2(6) of the IGST Act and claimed refund of accumulated ITC. The GST refund authority rejects the claim arguing that the maintenance component constitutes 'intermediary services' under Section 2(13) of the IGST Act because the Indian company is facilitating provision of support between the US entity and its Indian end-users. Analyze: (a) the correct characterization of the composite contract for TDS purposes and whether bundling/unbundling doctrine applies under DTAA, (b) the sustainability of the GST authority's intermediary services classification given the SC ruling in Dhruva Advisors, and (c) whether inconsistent characterization between Income Tax (service) and GST (intermediary) authorities creates an estoppel or can be challenged on constitutional grounds of taxation without jurisdiction.
questions.expected_answer_depth: Requires cross-statute analysis spanning DTAA, Income Tax Act, IGST Act, and constitutional law

title: Rebate of duty paid on processed textile fabrics under section 3A where the fabric is used for the manufacture of made-ups (export goods) - Problems arising due to absence of procedure
text:
Circular No. 487/53/99-CX

dated 30/9/1999

F.No.209/44/99-CX.6

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue,

Central Board of Excise & Customs, New Delhi

Subject : Central Excise - Rebate of duty paid on processed textile fabrics under section 3A where the fabric is used for the
manufacture of made-ups (export goods) - Problems arising due to absence of procedures - Regarding

I am directed to say that it has been brought to the notice of the Board that rebate claims in respect of duty paid on processed
textile fabric used as material for manufacture of made-up (export goods) are being denied on the ground(s) that the claimants
did not follow the prescribed  procedure for export under claim for rebate of duty on excisable materials in terms of rule 12(1)(b)
read  with notification No. 42/94-CE(NT) dated 22.9.94 as amended [AR-5 procedure] - such as, in some cases no declaration
was given  and no permission was obtained from the proper authorities prior to clearances for export, clearances were made
on AR4, in some documents it was mentioned "without availing the facility under rule 12(1)(b)" and/or other phrase etc. The
trade and industry have requested that the legitimate export benefits should not be denied only because of the procedural
deviations which occurred because there was general ambiguity about the correct procedure for export of made-ups after the
introductions of the new levy under Section 3A.

2. The board has examined the matter. In order to obviate the genuine difficulties of the exporters, especially in view of the fact
that exporters were not aware of the exact procedure in the aforementioned situation, the Board has decided that the rebate
sanctioning authority should, in exercise of the powers under second proviso to sub-rule (1) of rule 12, relax the procedural
requirements (conditions) of the notification No. 42/94-CE(NT) dated 22.9.1994, as amended. Accordingly, the procedural
deviations, including those relating to declaration/permissions prior to export, use of incorrect document (AR-4), use of any
phrase/declaration by the exporter on export documents which was not so required etc., should be ignored., subject to the
conditions that goods in question were actually exported and the duty was paid by the independent processors"  factory on
materials (processed textile fabrics) supplied to the manufacturer of made-up articles. In other words, where the rebate
sanctioning authority is satisfied that the goods manufactured out of the excisable material were actually exported, rebate
should be allowed on the basis of the input-output norms as determined (even if determined post-facto exports) in respect of
the same/similar goods, by the competent authority. Accordingly, the instructions contained in Circular No. 129/40/95-CX
dated 29.5.95 may also be applied, mutatis mutandis. It should, however, be ensured that the rebate claims are otherwise
admissible.

3. The Board also desires that all the pending rebate claims may be decided expeditiously. Any difficulty noticed in this regard
may be brought to the knowledge of the Board immediately. In future, the exporters should be directed to follow the specified
procedure.

Sd/-

(P K Sinha)

Under Secretary (CX.6)

questions.question: Your BGE-M3 embeddings are stored in Qdrant with cosine similarity. A user queries 'Input Tax Credit reversal under Rule 42 for exempt supplies', but the top-5 retrieved chunks are all from Rule 43 (capital goods), missing the directly relevant Rule 42 provisions. Retrieval score for the correct chunk is 0.71 vs 0.78 for the false positives. What is the most likely root cause, and what is the precise remediation — without reingesting the corpus?

On a 4-core host running llama-server in Vulkan mode with Qwen3-14B Q4_K_M, you observe that P50 TTFT is 1.2s but P95 TTFT spikes to 11s under concurrent load of 3 users. `uptime` shows load average 3.8. KV cache hit rate is 0%. What are the three independent interventions, in priority order, that would reduce P95 TTFT — and for each, state the expected mechanism and the tradeoff?

title: WASTE AND SCRAP OF HARD RUBBER HAVING BASEL NO. B3040; WASTE PARINGS AND SCRAP OF RUBBER HAVING BASEL NO. B3080 AND WASTE PNEUMATIC AND OTHER TYRES, EXCLUDING THOSE WHICH DO NOT LEAD TO RESOURCE RECOVERY, RECYCLING, RECLAMATION BUT NOT FOR

questions.question: A manufacturer in Gujarat supplies goods to a registered dealer in Maharashtra under a consignment arrangement where title transfers only upon sale by the consignee. The consignee sells 60% of the goods within the same month and returns the remaining 40% after 180 days. Analyze: (a) the GST treatment at each stage including e-way bill obligations and the impact of the 180-day rule under Section 10(1)(b) of the IGST Act; (b) whether the principal must reverse ITC on the returned goods and the mechanism for doing so; (c) the valuation methodology applicable under Rule 27 versus Rule 29 of the CGST Valuation Rules if the consignee is a related party; and (d) implications if the consignee is in a Special Economic Zone.

An Indian subsidiary of a US multinational provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement. During AY 2024-25, the subsidiary also receives a royalty-free perpetual license to use group IP for serving third-party clients in India, and simultaneously seconds three senior engineers to the parent for 18 months. The TPO proposes: (i) an ALP adjustment on the cost-plus margin using TNMM with a PLI of operating profit to operating cost; (ii) a notional royalty charge for the IP license based on CUT method; and (iii) recharacterization of the secondment as deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(e). Critically evaluate the TPO's three proposals, identify which are legally sustainable under Chapter X read with the OECD BEPS Action 8-10 framework as adopted in India, and advise on the documentation required under Section 92D to defend each position.

title: Appoints special adjudicators

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under a composite contract to a government entity — the contract covers both the fabrication of specialized machinery (principal supply) and its installation, commissioning, and annual maintenance for 5 years (ancillary supplies). The government entity claims the entire contract qualifies as a 'works contract' attracting 12% GST, while the supplier argues only the fabrication portion attracts 18% GST and the post-installation maintenance is an exempt service. The supplier also holds an advance ruling from State A saying the composite supply is taxable at 12%, but is now executing an identical contract in State B. Analyze the correct GST treatment, the inter-state advance ruling applicability problem, and what the supplier should do to protect themselves from differential state-wise adjudication.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Customs + GST Interface
questions.question: An Indian importer of industrial chemicals pays IGST at import under the Customs Tariff Act and claims ITC of that IGST in their GST returns. Three years later, a Customs audit determines the chemicals were misclassified — the correct HS code attracts a higher Basic Customs Duty (BCD) and a lower IGST rate than what was originally paid. The importer therefore overpaid IGST at import but underpaid BCD. The Customs department raises a demand for differential BCD plus interest, but refuses to refund excess IGST, arguing it flows to GST jurisdiction. The GST department, in turn, is seeking recovery of the excess ITC claimed (since the correct IGST liability was lower). Trace the complete legal remedy path for the importer — including which statute governs the IGST refund at import, how the excess ITC recovery demand can be contested, whether interest liability on the BCD demand can be offset against the IGST overpayment, and the limitation periods applicable at each stage.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a common input service (legal advisory) used for both taxable supplies (industrial machinery) and exempt supplies (agricultural equipment exempt under Schedule I). The company's aggregate turnover for FY 2025-26 is ₹42 crore, with taxable supplies of ₹28 crore and exempt supplies of ₹14 crore. During October 2025, ITC on the common legal service invoice is ₹3,60,000. Additionally, the company has exclusively used inputs worth ₹90,000 ITC for exempt supplies in the same month. Calculate the net ITC admissible for October 2025 under Rule 42 of CGST Rules 2017, showing the apportionment methodology, treatment of the exclusive exempt-supply inputs, and the reversal amount to be reported in GSTR-3B. Further, explain what annual adjustment, if any, is required under Rule 42(2) and the deadline for filing it.

An Indian importer receives a consignment of industrial robots (HS 8428.90) from a related German supplier under a long-term agreement. The invoice price is €2,00,000. However, the agreement also includes: (a) royalties of 4% on net domestic sales payable annually, (b) a post-importation technical support fee of €15,000 invoiced separately, and (c) the buyer provides design tooling worth €8,000 free-of-charge to the seller for use in production. The declared transaction value is ₹1,85,40,000 at ₹92.70/€. Customs has doubts about the related-party transaction under Rule 3(3) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007. Identify all adjustable elements under Rules 9 and 10, determine whether the transaction value is acceptable or must be rejected, describe the sequential alternative valuation methods under Rules 4 through 8 with their applicability conditions, and compute the assessable value including the correct treatment of the royalty clause under Rule 10(1)(c) given that royalties are not a condition of sale but are paid to the seller's parent company.

title: Guidelines for compounding of offences under Customs Act, 1962 - regarding
text:
the Customs (Compounding of Offences) Rules, 2005, any person who has made the application for
compounding of offence and has made full and true disclosure of facts relating to the case, is given immunity from prosecution
for any offence under the Customs Act, 1962 with respect to the case covered by the compounding of offence. Since the filing
of application under compounding rules is the individual option of the person to avoid prosecution, other persons involved in the
case/ offence and who have not filed the application would not be given immunity from prosecution. In such situation, remaining
persons would face regular proceedings of the department for adjudication/ prosecution/ appeal.

(iv) On the basis of the recommendations made by the Committee on Subordinate Legislation (Rajya Sabha) and to enable the
Scheme of Compounding of Offences to make a meaningful impact, the compounding amount prescribed under Rule 5 of the
said Rules has been revised downwards vide notification No.118/2008-Customs (NT) dated 12.11.2008. A new proviso has
also been inserted in this rule, which provides that if a person has, in respect of same goods, committed offences falling under
more than one category, i.e., Sl.No.1 to 8 of the table specified in this rule and where amount of duty evasion or amount of
drawback or exemption from duty, or amount of market value of the goods is same for all such offences, then the compounding
amount, in such cases, shall be the amount determined for the offence for which a higher compounding amount has been
prescribed.

(v) In terms of Rule 4, an applicant is required to pay duty, penalty, and interest before submission of an application for
compounding of offences. Correspondingly, the Application Form also contains a specific column under Sl.No.12A requiring
the applicant to declare whether he has paid the same and their details. Hence, it is clarified that the compounding of offences
shall not be allowed unless the aforesaid duty, penalty and interest thereon are paid by the applicant.

6. In order to make best of use of the scheme of compounding of offences, it is reiterated that at the time of intimation/ initiating
action for launching of prosecution itself, the assessees should be given an offer of compounding. It may, however, be clarified

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement, where the billing is to a Gujarat entity and shipping is directly to Karnataka. The goods attract 18% GST. Determine: (a) which state's SGST applies, (b) whether this constitutes an inter-state or intra-state supply, (c) what ITC entitlement the Gujarat entity has, and (d) the place of supply under Section 10 vs Section 12 of the IGST Act, citing relevant CBIC circulars or AAR rulings.

An Indian importer receives a consignment of capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously claims a refund of IGST paid on imports under Rule 96 of CGST Rules 2017, while also availing duty drawback on re-exported goods manufactured using those imported inputs. Identify the legal conflicts between simultaneous availment of EPCG, IGST refund under Rule 96, and duty drawback, referencing Notification No. 48/2015-Customs and the relevant CBIC circulars that restrict double-benefit claims, and state the compliant course of action.

title: Circular No. 52/26/2018-GST dated 09.08.2018 i.r.o. clarification regarding applicability of GST rates on various goods and services.
text:
8.3 
According to the General Rules for Interpretation [GRI- 3(b)] of the First Schedule to

the Customs Tariff Act (CTA), 1975, “Mixtures, composite goods consisting of different 
materials or made up of different components, and goods put up in sets for retail sale, which 
cannot be classified by reference to 3 (a), shall be classified as if they consisted of the material 
or component which gives them their essential character, insofar as this criterion is 
applicable.” Since primary function of the article should be taken into consideration while 
deciding the classification, it is clear that the essential character of the wipes in the instant case 
is imparted by the components which are to be mixed with the textile material. 
  
8.4 
As per the explanatory notes to the HSN, the HS code 5603 clearly excludes non-

woven, impregnated, coated or covered with substances or preparations such as perfumes or 
cosmetics, soaps or detergents, polishes, creams or similar preparations. The HSN is 
reproduced as follows : “The heading also excludes:

Nonwoven, impregnated, coated or covered with substances or preparations [i.e.

perfumes or cosmetics (Chapter 33), soaps or detergents (heading 3401), polishes, creams, or 
similar preparations (heading 3405), fabric, softeners (heading 3809)] where the textile 
material is present merely as a carrying medium. Further, HS code 3307 covers wadding, felt 
and non-woven, impregnated, coated or covered with perfumes or cosmetics. The HS code 
3401, would cover paper, wadding, felt and non-woven impregnated, coated or covered with 
soap or detergent whether or not perfumed”.

8.5 
Further, as per the explanatory notes to the HSN, the heading 3307 includes wadding,

felt and nonwovens impregnated, coated or covered with perfume or cosmetics. Similarly, as 
per explanatory notes to the HSN, the heading 3401 includes wipes made of paper, wadding, 
felt and nonwovens, impregnated, coated or covered with soap or detergent, whether or not 
perfumed or put up for retail sale.

8.6 
Thus, the wipes of various kinds (as stated above) are classifiable under heading 3307

or 3401 depending upon their constituents as discussed above. Therefore, if the baby wipes are 
impregnated with perfumes or cosmetics, then the same would fall under HS code 3307 and 
would attract 18% GST rate. Similarly, if they are coated with soap or detergent, then it would 
fall under HS code 3401 and would attract 18% GST.

9.1 
Classification and applicable GST rate on real zari Kasab (thread): Certain doubts

have been raised regarding the classification and applicable GST rate on Kasab thread (a 
metallised yarn) as yarn falling under heading 5605 attracts 12% GST, as per entry 137 of the 
Schedule-II-12% of the notification No.01/2017-Central Tax (rate) dated 28.06.2017, while 
specified embroidery product falling under 5809 and 5810 attracts GST @ 5%, as per entry no. 
220 of the Schedule-I-5% of the above-mentioned notification.

9.2 
The heading 5809 and 5810 cover embroidery and zari articles. These heading do not

cover yarn of any kinds. Hence, while these headings apply to embroidery articles, embroidery 
in piece, in strips, or in motifs, they do not apply to yarn, including Kasab yarn.

questions.question: A manufacturer of pharmaceutical intermediates (HSN 2941) supplies them to a 100% EOU under a deemed export arrangement. The EOU later converts 40% of the received goods into final formulations for domestic clearance and uses the remaining 60% for physical exports under LUT. The manufacturer claims refund of IGST paid on the deemed export supply. The jurisdictional officer argues that since the EOU made a mixed use of inputs, the manufacturer's refund eligibility is proportionally restricted. Examine the legal validity of this restriction under Rule 89(4) read with Section 147 of the CGST Act, 2017, and relevant CBIC circulars on deemed exports.

An Indian importer procures specialty chemicals from its AE (Associated Enterprise) in Germany. The customs value is declared at USD 850/MT under the transaction value method. The TPO, during an income-tax assessment, recomputes the arm's length price at USD 720/MT under TNMM and issues a downward transfer pricing adjustment, effectively accepting a lower price as ALP. The customs department simultaneously proposes enhancement of the declared value to USD 1,100/MT citing contemporaneous import data for similar goods. Analyze the interplay between Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, Section 92C of the Income Tax Act, and CBIC Circular 05/2016-Customs, addressing whether the TP order creates a binding floor or ceiling for customs valuation and what remedies the importer can pursue before both forums simultaneously.

title: Amends notification No. 21/2002-Customs, dated the 1st March, 2002
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

New Delhi dated the 14 th May, 2007.

24 Vaisakha, 1929 (Saka)

questions.question: A composite manufacturer supplies a works contract service for constructing a commercial complex and simultaneously transfers the underlying immovable property to the recipient before obtaining a completion certificate. Under Section 17(5)(d) read with Section 2(119) of the CGST Act, the ITC on inputs and input services attributable to the works contract component is blocked. However, the developer argues that since the property transfer occurred before completion certificate, the supply is of 'under-construction property' under Schedule II Entry 5(b) and not a works contract, thereby making ITC admissible on construction inputs used for the taxable supply of the apartment. Critically evaluate the legal sustainability of this position, identifying the specific circulars, AAR rulings, and AAAR precedents that would support or defeat this argument, and compute the net ITC reversal quantum if the developer has 60% commercial and 40% residential units by carpet area.

An Indian subsidiary imports specialty chemicals from its Singapore parent at a declared transaction value. Customs invokes Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, rejecting the declared value on the ground of related-party influence, and seeks to apply Rule 5 (identical goods) using a third-party import of a structurally similar but chemically distinct compound. Simultaneously, the Transfer Pricing Officer invokes Section 92C of the Income Tax Act and applies the TNMM method, arriving at an arm's length price that is 18% higher than the customs-accepted value under Rule 6 (deductive value) which was eventually used. Analyze the legal conflict between the customs valuation outcome and the TP order, the binding effect (if any) of each authority's determination on the other, the applicability of the CBEC Circular No. 05/2016-Cus on TP-customs coordination, and advise on the optimal appellate strategy given that the assessment year overlaps with an ongoing OECD Pillar Two compliance review for the group.

title: EOU/EHTP/STP/ SEZ Schemes-Amendments in Exim Policy and Handbook of Procedures Vol-1, 2002-2007.
text:
of excise leviable under Section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 and additional duites of excise leviable under Section 3 of
Additional Duty of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957 and additional duty of excise leviable thereon under the
Section 3 of Additional duty of Excise (Textiles and Textile Articles) Act, 1978.

(b).         However where the goods are cleared into DTA, duty would be levied on the goods under Section 3 of the Central
Excise Act, 1944. Since duty on goods manufactured by EOU/EHTP/STP and sold in DTA is equal to aggregates of the duty of
customs leviable on similar imported goods, the CVD would constitute all the components of excise duties viz, duty of excise
leviable under Section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 and additional duties of excise leviable under section 3 of Additional
Duty of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957 and additional duty of excise leviable thereon under the section 3 of
Additional duty of Excise (Textiles and Textile Articles) Act, 1978.

(c)         Therefore, to avoid double charging of duty leviable under section 3 of Additional Duty of Excise (Goods of Special
Importance) Act, 1957 and under the section 3 of Additional duty of Excise (Textiles and Textile Articles) Act, 1978, once as a
component of CVD and then independently under respective Acts, the exemption has been provided under respective Acts so
that duty leviable under Additional Duty of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957 and under Additional duty of Excise
(Textiles and Textile Articles) Act, 1978 is collected once only as a components of CVD. This point may be kept in mind while
calculating duty in case of goods manufactured by EOU/ETHP/STP units cleared in DTA.

8.         Difficulties, if any faced in the implementation of the above instructions may be brought to the notice of the Board at an
early date.

9.         Wide publicity may be given by issue of a Public Notice in this regard.

10.         Kindly acknowledge receipt of this circular.

11.         Hindi version will follow.

C.P. Goyal

Sr. Technical Officer

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a registered place of business in Maharashtra and a warehouse in Gujarat used exclusively for storage before inter-state supply. The Gujarat warehouse receives goods from the Maharashtra factory under a delivery challan but no tax invoice is raised at that point. The final buyer is in Rajasthan. At what point does the place of supply crystallize, who is the supplier of record for e-way bill purposes on the Maharashtra-to-Gujarat leg, and how does the valuation for that leg interact with the aggregate turnover threshold for the Gujarat establishment's GST registration obligation under Section 22 read with Section 10(1)(e) of the IGST Act?

An Indian importer is purchasing software licenses from a related US entity. The licenses are delivered electronically (no physical media). The US entity also charges a separate 'technical support and upgrade fee' billed annually. The transaction value is questioned by the assessing officer on the grounds of relationship influence under Rule 3(3) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007. The importer argues that since no goods cross the border the transaction falls outside the Customs Act entirely and the import is taxable only as OIDAR under GST. Analyze the correct classification of the transaction, which valuation methodology applies if Customs jurisdiction is upheld, and whether the annual support fee is includible in assessable value under Rule 10(1)(c) as a condition of sale.

22 nd July, 2003

inserted in the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962).
