questions.question: A manufacturer receives goods under a Bill of Entry dated March 28, 2026, but the IGST payment is reflected in ICEGATE only on April 3, 2026, after the March GSTR-3B filing deadline. The goods were used partly (60%) in zero-rated supplies without payment of tax under LUT and partly (40%) in domestic taxable supplies. The supplier's GSTR-1 for March reflects the transaction, but GSTR-2B for March does not include this import. Can the manufacturer claim ITC in April 2026 GSTR-3B, and if so, how should the apportionment between Rule 96A (LUT refund route) and domestic credit be computed, given that Rule 42 proportionate reversal may also apply?

An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a UK-headquartered MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus 15% arrangement. During FY 2024-25, IndCo's board meetings were conducted entirely via video call from India, with all strategic decisions ratified by directors physically present in India. The UK parent subsequently invoked a management fee of INR 12 crore for 'global strategy services'. The AO proposes: (a) to treat IndCo as the Place of Effective Management (POEM) of the UK parent, making the parent a domestic company liable to Indian tax on global income, AND (b) to disallow the INR 12 crore management fee as a non-arm's-length related-party expense under Section 92C. Can both actions be sustained simultaneously under the Income Tax Act, 1961, and what is the legal precedent and procedural safeguard under Section 144C before the Dispute Resolution Panel?

title: This notification imposes anti dumping duty on Acrylic fibre originating in or exported from Japan, Spain, Portugal and Italy
text:
Anti-dumping duty on Acrylic fibre originating in, or exported from Japan,

Spain, Portugal and Italy by specified exporters. Whereas in the matter of import of acrylic fibres, ranging from 1.5 denier

to 8.0 denier and falling under Chapter 55 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) originating in or

exported from Japan, Spain, Portugal or Italy, the Designated Authority vide its preliminary findings, published in Part I, Section

1 of the Gazette of India Extraordinary, dated the 20th October, 1998, had come to the conclusion that - (i) acrylic fibre of

Japan, Spain, Portugal and Italy, has been exported to India below normal value, resulting in dumping; (h) the Indian Industry

has suffered material injury; (in) the injury has been caused cumulatively by the imports from Japan, Spain, Portugal and Italy;

AND WHEREAS on the basis of the aforesaid findings of the Designated Authority, the Central Government has imposed anti-

dumping duty vide notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 90/98-

Customs, dated 17th November, 1998 [G.S.R. 683 (E), dated the 17th November, 1998] published in Part II, Section 3, sub-

section (i) of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 17th November, 1998 (hereinafter referred to as the provisional

duty); AND WHEREAS the Designated Authority vide Notification No. 32/1/97-ADD, published in Part I, Section 1 of the

Gazette of India Extraordinary, dated the 24th December, 1998 has in its final finding concluded that - (i) acrylic fibre exported

from Japan, Spain, Portugal and Italy has been exported to India below its normal value resulting in dumping of acrylic fibres;

(ii) the domestic industry has suffered material injury; and (iii) the causal link between dumping and injury is established. Now,

therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975)

read with rules 18 and 20 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped

Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995, the Central Government after considering the aforesaid final finding of the

Designated Authority, hereby imposes on the said [acrylic fibre, excluding modacrylic fibre] when originating in, or exported

from, the country specified in column (2) of the Table hereto annexed, by the exporters mentioned in the corresponding entry in

column (3) of the said Table, and imported into India, an anti-dumping duty which shall be equivalent to difference between the

amount mentioned in the corresponding entry in column (4) of the said Table and the landed value of acrylic fibre per Kg.

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a dealer in Gujarat under a works contract involving both supply of goods and installation services. The contract value is ₹2.5 crore. Midway through execution, the dealer cancels the contract and the manufacturer recovers 30% as liquidated damages. Analyze: (1) the GST classification and tax liability at each stage of the original works contract, (2) whether liquidated damages recovered on cancellation constitute a 'supply' under Section 7 of the CGST Act and attract GST, citing relevant AAR/AAAR rulings and Circular No. 178/10/2022-GST, and (3) the input tax credit reversal obligations under Rule 42/43 of CGST Rules on the manufacturer's partially used inputs.

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a related German entity at ₹80 lakh. The Special Valuation Branch (SVB) provisionally assesses the transaction value and imposes a 1% extra duty deposit under Customs Circular 11/2001. During SVB investigation, it emerges that the importer pays a 'royalty' to the same German entity equal to 4% of net sales, and that the sale is conditional upon the buyer purchasing maintenance services exclusively from the seller. Evaluate: (1) whether the royalty is includible in customs value under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 given the conditionality, distinguishing the Supreme Court's ruling in Commissioner of Customs v. Ferodo India, (2) the treatment of the maintenance service condition under Rule 3(3)(b) as a 'condition or consideration' affecting the acceptability of transaction value, and (3) the procedural rights of the importer to substitute an alternative valuation method under Rules 4–8 if transaction value is rejected, with timelines and appellate options.

questions.question: A pharmaceutical manufacturer operating under a composite supply arrangement supplies a patented drug along with post-sale technical support services. The drug attracts 12% GST and the support services attract 18% GST. The manufacturer's ERP system incorrectly bifurcates the invoice into two separate line items, each billed independently to the hospital buyer. The GST officer during scrutiny assessment under Section 61 contends this constitutes two separate supplies and raises a demand for differential tax on the support component. The taxpayer argues composite supply doctrine applies. Analyze: (a) the legal test for determining principal supply under Section 2(90) read with Section 8(a) of the CGST Act, (b) whether the invoicing practice is determinative or merely evidentiary, (c) the applicable rate on the entire transaction if composite supply is upheld, and (d) the rectification mechanism available if the returns for the relevant tax period are already time-barred under Section 39(9).

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Income Tax / Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using the TNMM at arm's length. The Transfer Pricing Officer proposes an upward adjustment of ₹47 crore citing (i) functional comparability failures in the comparables set selected by the assessee, (ii) working capital adjustment methodology being incorrect, and (iii) the use of a single-year data instead of multiple-year data. Simultaneously, the parent entity in the US triggers a corresponding adjustment request under Article 9(2) of the India-US DTAA. Analyze: (a) the legal validity of the TPO rejecting multiple-year data under Rule 10B(4) post the 2014 amendment, (b) the correct working capital adjustment formula under the OECD guidelines vs. CBDT practice, (c) whether the assessee can invoke the MAP procedure under Article 27 while simultaneously pursuing domestic appellate remedies before the ITAT, and (d) the interplay between DRP directions under Section 144C and the binding nature of APA rollback provisions if the assessee has a pending bilateral APA covering overlapping years.

title: Makes Service Tax (Settlement of Cases) Rules, 2012
text:
5. Manner of provisional attachment of property. - (1) Where the Settlement Commission orders attachment of property
under sub-section (1) of section 32G of Excise Act, made applicable to service tax vide section 83 of the Act, it shall send a
copy of such order to the Commissioner of Central Excise or Commissioner of Service Tax having jurisdiction over the place in
which the applicant owns any movable or immovable property or resides or carries on his business or has his bank account.

(2) On receipt of the order referred to in sub-rule (1), the Commissioner may authorise any officer subordinate to him and not
below the rank of an Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or Service Tax to take steps to attach such property of the
applicant.

(3) The officer authorised under sub-rule (2) shall prepare an inventory of the property attached and specify in it, in the case of
the immovable property, the description of such property sufficient to identify it and in case of the movable property, the place
where such property is lodged or kept and shall hand over a copy of the same to the applicant or to the person from whose
charge the property is attached.

(4) The officer authorised under sub-rule (2) shall send a copy of the inventory so prepared each to the Commissioner of
Central Excise and the Settlement Commission.

6. Fee for copies of reports. - Any person who makes an application under section 32J of Excise Act, made applicable to
service tax vide section 83 of the Act, for obtaining copies of reports made by any Central Excise Officer, shall pay a fee of five
rupees per page of each report or part thereof.

questions.question: A multi-state manufacturing company has an inter-state branch transfer of semi-finished goods from its Rajasthan unit to its Maharashtra unit for further processing. The goods are later exported from Maharashtra under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). The Rajasthan unit had claimed ITC on inputs used in manufacturing. Analyze (a) the GST treatment of the inter-state stock transfer, (b) the ITC reversal obligation at the Rajasthan unit, (c) the eligibility of the Maharashtra unit to claim a refund of accumulated ITC on zero-rated export supply, and (d) whether Rule 89(4) or Rule 89(4A) of the CGST Rules applies and how the refund quantum is computed.

An EOU unit imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously claims the benefit of Notification No. 52/2003-Customs for duty-free import as an EOU. The unit subsequently surrenders its EOU status before fulfilling the export obligation under EPCG. Examine (a) the legal conflict between concurrent availment of EOU and EPCG benefits on the same capital goods, (b) the customs duty demand and interest liability triggered by EOU de-bonding, (c) whether the unfulfilled EPCG export obligation survives EOU surrender and how the obligation is re-computed on Net Foreign Exchange basis, and (d) the applicability of Section 28 read with Section 142 of the Customs Act for demand finalization after the bonding period.

questions.question: A pharmaceutical manufacturer supplies active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) to a contract manufacturer under a job work arrangement. The APIs are sent under a delivery challan. After processing, the finished formulations are directly dispatched by the job worker to the principal's customers in another state. The principal has not taken ITC on the APIs sent for job work. The job worker charges processing fees and also uses some of his own inputs. Analyze the following: (a) GST liability on the job worker's processing fees and the applicable place of supply; (b) whether the direct dispatch to the principal's customer triggers any additional compliance obligation for the principal or the job worker under sections 143 and 10A of the CGST Act; (c) ITC eligibility for the principal on job worker's processing charges when the finished goods are dispatched directly to the end customer without returning to the principal's premises; and (d) consequences if the time limit under section 143(1) for return of goods/completion of job work is breached, specifically whether deemed supply provisions apply to the APIs which the principal never took ITC on.

An EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods duty-free under a Customs notification and also procures goods domestically under CT-3 without payment of GST. The unit subsequently obtains STPI registration for its IT/ITES division and files for conversion from EOU to an STPI unit. During the transition period: (a) examine the duty liability and redemption fine exposure on the imported capital goods that have not completed the prescribed export obligation period under the Foreign Trade Policy, specifically distinguishing between goods notified under the EOU scheme versus goods covered by advance authorization; (b) whether DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) clearances made prior to conversion should be recomputed under section 3(3) of the Customs Tariff Act or under the standard IGST mechanism applicable post-conversion; (c) analyze the impact on accumulated ITC standing in the GSTIN of the EOU at the time of conversion—whether it can be carried forward, must be reversed under rule 44A, or is subject to lapsing under section 54(3) restrictions; and (d) whether the STPI unit post-conversion is entitled to claim benefits under any zero-rating mechanism for its domestic IT service exports and the documentary compliance trail required to establish that the services qualify as 'export of services' under place of supply rules despite the physical premises remaining unchanged.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three distinct units under the same GSTIN: Unit A (supplying goods to SEZ developers under LUT), Unit B (making exempt supplies of agricultural produce), and Unit C (making standard-rated taxable supplies). During FY 2024-25, the company incurred ₹45 lakh in common input tax credit on overheads that cannot be directly attributed to any unit. The company also reversed ITC of ₹3.2 lakh under Rule 42 in Q2. In Q4, Unit A received a refund rejection order for accumulated ITC citing that SEZ supplies are 'zero-rated' and not 'exempt', yet the adjudicating authority applied the Rule 42 formula treating SEZ supplies as exempt for ITC reversal purposes. Analyze the legal contradiction, cite the specific provisions under the CGST Act and Rules, and determine the correct ITC apportionment and refund eligibility, with reference to any relevant circulars or appellate precedents.

An Indian importer procures identical goods from a related foreign supplier (holding 26% equity stake) under a long-term supply agreement that includes: (a) a royalty of 3% of net sales payable post-importation, (b) a year-end volume rebate clawed back if annual purchases fall below a threshold, and (c) tooling costs amortized into the unit price at ₹18/unit for the first 50,000 units. The declared transaction value was accepted by customs at port of import. Six months later, a Special Valuation Branch (SVB) inquiry is initiated. Customs proposes to add: the royalty as a condition of sale under Rule 10(1)(c), the tooling costs as assists under Rule 10(1)(b)(iv), and to reject the rebate as a post-importation price reduction not permitted under Rule 10(2). The importer argues the royalty is not a condition of sale as it is payable regardless of whether the goods are imported or domestically sourced. Evaluate each addition/rejection proposed by customs under the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007, identify which additions are legally sustainable, and prescribe the correct SVB procedure the importer should follow under the circular governing related-party transactions.

title: Regarding implementation of Service Export from India Scheme (SEIS) under FTP 2015-2020
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA MINISTRY OF FINANCE  (DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No.  21/ 2015 - Central Excise

New Delhi, the   8 th April, 2015.

G.S.R. 272 (E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 5A of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of
1944), read with sub-section (3) of section 3 of the Additional Duties of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957 (58 of
1957) and sub-section (3) of section 3 of the Additional Duties of Excise (Textiles and Textile Articles) Act, 1978 (40 of 1978),
the Central Government, on being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby exempts the goods
specified in the First Schedule and the Second Schedule to the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985 (5 of 1986), when
cleared against a Service Exports from India Scheme duty credit scrip issued by the Regional Authority under paragraph 3.10
read with paragraph 3.08 of the Foreign Trade Policy (hereinafter referred to as the said scrip) from, -

(i)   the whole of the duty of excise leviable thereon under the First Schedule and the Second Schedule to the Central Excise
Tariff Act, 1985 (5 of 1986);

(ii)   the whole of the additional duty of excise leviable thereon under section 3 of the Additional Duties of Excise (Goods of
Special Importance) Act, 1957 (58 of 1957); and

(iii)  the whole of the additional duty of excise leviable thereon under section 3 of the Additional Duties of Excise (Textiles and
Textile Articles) Act, 1978 (40 of 1978).

2.      The exemption shall be subject to the following conditions, namely:-

(1)       that the conditions (1) and (2) specified in paragraph 2 of the Notification No. 25/2015-Customs, dated the 8 th April,
2015 are complied and the said scrip has been registered with the Customs Authority at the port of registration specified on
the said scrip (hereinafter referred as the said Customs Authority);

(2)       that the holder of the scrip, who may either be the person to whom the scrip was originally issued or a transferee-holder,
presents the said scrip to the said Customs Authority along with a letter or proforma invoice from the supplier or manufacturer
indicating details of its jurisdictional Central Excise Officer (hereinafter referred as the said Officer) and the description,
quantity, value of the goods to be cleared and the duties leviable thereon, but for this exemption;

(3)       that the said Customs Authority, taking into account the debits already made towards imports under Notification No .
25/2015-Customs, dated the 8 th April, 2015, the debits made under Notification No.11/2015-Service Tax, dated the 8 th April,
2015 and this exemption, shall debit the duties leviable, but for this exemption in or on the reverse of the said scrip and also
mentions the necessary details thereon, updates its own records and sends written advice of these actions to the said Officer;

(4)       that at the time of clearance, the holder of the scrip presents the said scrip debited by the said Customs Authority to the
said Officer along with an undertaking addressed to the said Officer that in case of any amount short debited in the said scrip
he shall pay on demand an amount equal to the short debit, along with applicable interest;

(5)       that based on the said written advice and undertaking, the said Officer endorses the clearance particulars and validates,
on the reverse of the said scrip, the details of the duties leviable, but for this exemption, which were debited by the said
Customs Authority, and keeps a record of such clearances;

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) to a SEZ unit, but the SEZ unit subsequently DTA-clears a portion of the processed output without paying integrated tax. The original exporter claims the entire transaction as zero-rated under Section 16(1)(b) of IGST Act. The department issues a SCN alleging that proportionate input tax credit reversal is required under Rule 42 read with Section 17(2) of CGST Act for the DTA-cleared portion. Analyze whether the department's position is sustainable, referencing the distinction between 'zero-rated supply' and 'exempt supply' under the GST framework, and whether the recipient SEZ unit's downstream DTA clearance can trigger ITC reversal obligations on the original supplier.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Transfer Pricing & PE
questions.question: A foreign parent company seconds 12 employees to its Indian subsidiary under a cost-recharge arrangement where the subsidiary bears salary costs plus 5% markup. The AO treats the secondment as a Service PE of the foreign parent under Article 5(3)(b) of the applicable DTAA, attributing profits to the PE using the AOA (Authorized OECD Approach). Simultaneously, the TPO benchmarks the cost-recharge using TNMM and proposes an arm's-length adjustment. Examine whether the concurrent application of PE profit attribution under AOA and a TP adjustment on the same underlying transactions constitutes double taxation, how the 'significant people functions' analysis interacts with the PE determination, and what treaty override provisions under Section 90(2) vs. domestic POEM rules govern the dispute resolution mechanism.

title: Seeks to amend notification No. 2/2011-CE, dated the 1st March, 2011, so as to omit the entry relating to handmade carpets and other carpets and floor coverings of Jute and Coir

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat) under the same PAN. Unit A supplies semi-finished goods to Unit B for further processing under a job-work arrangement. Unit B then exports the finished goods to a foreign buyer under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). Unit A also simultaneously claims ITC on capital goods used partly for the inter-unit supply and partly for direct B2B domestic sales. Identify all applicable GST compliances, ITC reversal obligations under Rule 42/43, e-way bill requirements, and the treatment of the export supply in Unit B's GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B, including the zero-rated supply classification and refund eligibility under Section 54(3) vs Section 16(3) of the IGST Act.

questions.domain: Indian Customs / Import Duty / Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary imports specialised machinery components from its foreign parent at a declared transaction value of USD 1,20,000. The customs officer invokes Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, rejects the transaction value on grounds of related-party influence, and re-determines the value at USD 1,75,000 using deductive value under Rule 7. The importer contests this, arguing that the transaction value satisfies the 'circumstances of sale' test under Rule 3(3)(a) and provides a contemporaneous transfer pricing study under the Income Tax Act, 1961, accepted by the TPO, showing arm's-length price at USD 1,22,000. Analyse: (a) whether the TP study constitutes sufficient evidence to reinstate the declared transaction value under Customs law, (b) the interplay between Section 14 of the Customs Act and the CBIC Circular 39/2016-Customs on related-party imports, (c) the correct appellate remedy and evidentiary burden, and (d) any anti-avoidance exposure under GAAR if the import structure is also used to shift profits.

title: Regarding anti-dumping duty on the subject goods, originating in, or exported from, the United States of America, Japan, the European Union and Singapore
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No. 15/2008-Customs

New Delhi, the 5 th February, 2008

G.S.R. (E). - Whereas, the designated authority, vide notification No. 15/25/2006-DGAD, dated the 27 th December, 2006,
published in Part I, Section 1 of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 28 th December, 2006, had initiated review, in the
matter of continuation of anti-dumping on imports of flexible slabstock polyol or FSP (hereinafter referred to as the subject
goods) falling under sub-heading 3907 20 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act 1975, (51 of 1975) (hereinafter
referred to as the said Act), originating in, or exported from, the United States of America, Japan, the European Union and
Singapore, imposed vide notification  of the Government of India in the  Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue),  No.
120/2002-Customs, dated the 31 st October, 2002, published in Part II, Section 3, sub-section (i) of the Gazette of India,
Extraordinary, dated the 31 st October, 2002, vide number G.S.R.744 (E), dated the 31 st October, 2002;

And whereas, the Central Government had extended the anti-dumping duty on the subject goods, originating in, or exported
from, the United States of America, Japan, the European Union and Singapore upto and inclusive of the 10 th February, 2008
vide notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 12/2007 -Customs dated
the 6 th February, 2007, vide number G.S.R. 64(E), dated the 6 th February, 2007, published in Part II, Section 3, sub-section
(i) of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 6 th February, 2007;

And whereas, in the matter of review of anti-dumping on import of the subject goods, originating in, or exported from, the United
States of America, Japan, the European Union and Singapore, the designated authority vide its final findings No. 15/25/2006-
DGAD dated the 26 th December, 2007, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 26 th
December, 2007, has come to the conclusion that-

(a)                 the subject goods have entered from the United States of America at less than its normal value and the dumping
margin of the subject goods imported are significant, while current dumping margins of subject goods imported from the
European Union and Singapore are negligible;

(b)                 there is a likelihood of dumping to continue from the United States of America and of intensification or recurrence
of dumping from Singapore and Japan if the duties are revoked; there is less likelihood of intensification of imports from the
European Union if duties are withdrawn;

(c)                 the domestic industry suffers insignificant material injury at present due to the dumped imports, but there is a
likelihood of recurrence of injury if duties are withdrawn;

(d)                 therefore, continued imposition of anti dumping duty is warranted against the subject goods originating in, or
exported from, the United States of America, to offset dumping and against Singapore and Japan to prevent recurrence of
dumping;

and has recommended continued imposition of anti-dumping duty against the subject goods originating in, or exported from,
the United States of America, to offset dumping and against Singapore and Japan to prevent recurrence of dumping  in order
to remove the likelihood of injury to the domestic industry;

questions.question: A registered supplier of composite works contracts involving both goods and services is executing a long-term infrastructure project for a State Government. During the project, the GST rate on the service component was revised mid-contract. The supplier has already raised progressive invoices at the old rate. Analyze: (a) whether the supplier can claim the benefit of the concessional rate under Notification 24/2017-CT(R) for the remaining contract value, (b) how the time of supply rules under Section 13 interact with the rate-change notification date to determine applicable tax liability on retention money released post-completion, and (c) the ITC reversal implications under Rule 43 if the supplier switches from regular to composition scheme after partial execution.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a foreign MNC provides intra-group IT services to its parent and three Associated Enterprises (AEs) across different tax treaty jurisdictions. The Transfer Pricing Officer proposes to benchmark IndCo using TNMM with operating profit/total cost as the PLI, but rejects three comparables selected by IndCo on grounds of functional dissimilarity. IndCo also has a secondary adjustment triggered under Section 92CE after the primary TP adjustment. Critically evaluate: (a) the legal validity of the TPO's comparable rejection criteria under Rule 10B read with Rule 10C, (b) whether IndCo can invoke the MAP procedure under the applicable DTAA simultaneously with a domestic appeal before ITAT, and (c) the interaction between the secondary adjustment deemed loan provisions and thin-capitalisation rules under Section 94B when IndCo has outstanding ECB borrowings from the same AE.

title: RAGS---- Uniform gudeline for imprt clearance
text:
rm Guidelines for Clearance of Rags.

Issue: Divergent pracitces are being followed by Customes House with respect of clearance of rags. While at Calcutta and
Chennai, garments having 2-3 cuts are being cleared as gars, Cochin Custom Hoiuse is insisting on 6-8 cuts on the garments
for them to be consisdered as completely mutilated rags.

Improte of " worn clothing and other worn articles" falling in CTH 63.09 [ITC (HSN)63090000] is restricted under the EXIM
Policy 1997-2002. However, import of syntheric and woolen rags in completely mutilated condition is allowd freeely subject to
the condition that mutilation conforms to the requirement specified by Customs by way of Public or Trade Notice.

Mumbai Custom House had issued a Puboic Notice in 1988(PN60/880 which rquired the goods to have 6-8 cuts depending
upon the type of garment. Board had also issued instruction to all customs House vide letter Dated 26.9.88, to follow norms of
mutilation specified by mumbai Custom House. Accordingly, toher Custom Houses had also issued Public Notice along the
same lines. However, the norms specified in Mumbai custom House Public Notice were not implemented nuniformly by various
Custom House, in view of Supreme court order in case of Swastika Wollen Insustries [1990(47) ELT-216(SC)] that garments
cut into 2,3,4 pieces ' not along the seams' can be regarded as completely nutilate. The issu was placed before Conference in
view of existence of divergent practices.

The conference disucssed the issue and overved theat lack of unifrom assessment pracitce results in complaints that the same
foods which can ve freely iported as rags at one port, are treated as restricted at another. The Conference elaborately
discussed the scope of heading 63.09 and 63.10 of Customs Tariff. Heading 63.09 covers 'worn clothing and other worn
articles". According to HSN Explanatory Notes, garments to be classified under heading 63.09, must show signs of

questions.question: A manufacturing company receives goods under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement where the billing party (Company A) raises an invoice to the recipient (Company B), but physical delivery is made directly from the supplier to Company B's factory. Company A is registered in Maharashtra, the supplier is in Gujarat, and Company B's factory is in Tamil Nadu. Determine: (1) the place of supply for IGST purposes under Section 10(1)(b) vs Section 10(1)(a) of the IGST Act, (2) whether Company A can claim ITC on the inward supply from the Gujarat supplier without the goods ever entering Maharashtra, (3) the correct GST invoice structure and e-way bill requirements for each leg of the transaction, and (4) the implications if Company B subsequently rejects 30% of the goods and initiates a return — specifically whether a debit note, credit note, or fresh invoice is required and from which party.

An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent (USCo) under a cost-plus arrangement at cost+15%. The AO proposes to benchmark this using the TNMM with net cost plus margin as the PLI, referencing 6 comparables with margins ranging from 8% to 34%. IndCo argues: (a) two comparables should be excluded due to abnormally high related-party revenue (>25%), (b) a working capital adjustment of 3.2% is warranted because IndCo carries 90-day receivables versus the 30-day industry norm, and (c) a risk adjustment is due because USCo bears all market and entrepreneurial risk. Analyse: (1) the legal basis under Rule 10B(1)(e) for each proposed adjustment, (2) whether the DRP can admit fresh comparables at the appellate stage that were not part of the original TP study, (3) the interaction between the secondary adjustment under Section 92CE and the corresponding adjustment mechanism if the ALP is determined to be cost+22%, and (4) whether the 'range concept' under Rule 10CA applies and how the 35th–65th percentile band would be computed given only 4 comparables survive after exclusions.

title: Exempts goods when imported into India against a duty free entitlement credit certificate-reg.
text:
1 st April, 2003

Notification No. 53 / 2003 - Customs

In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 25 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central
Government, being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby exempts goods when imported into India
against a duty free entitlement credit certificate (hereinafter referred to as the said cerficate ) issued under paragraph 3.7.2.1
(vi) of the Export and Import Policy,-

(a)from the whole of the duty of customs leviable thereon under the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act 1975 (51 of 1975);
(b)from the whole of the additional duty leviable thereon under sub-section (1) of section 3 of the said Customs Tariff Act; and
(c)from the whole of the special additional duty of customs leviable thereon under section 3A of said Customs Tariff Act,

subject to the following conditions, namely

(1)that the said certificate has been issued by the licensing authority to a status holder specified in para 3.7.2 of the Export and

Import Policy;

(2)that the said certificate and goods imported against it shall not be transferred or sold:

Provided that where the goods are imported by a merchant exporter having supporting manufacturer(s) whose name and
address is specified on the license, the said goods may be utilised by the said supporting manufacturer(s).

(3)

that in respect of capital goods, a certificate from jurisdictional Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise, Assistant
Commissioner of Central Excise or an independent Chartered Engineer, as the case may be, is produced confirming
installation and use of capital goods in the importer's factory or premises, within the six month from the date of imports or
within such extended period as the Deputy Commissioner of Customs or Assistant Commissioner of Customs may allow:
Provided that where the capital goods are imported by a merchant exporter having supporting manufacturer(s), the capital
goods may be installed in the factory or premises of the said supporting manufacturer(s);

(4)that the said certificate shall be produced before the proper officer of customs at the time of clearance for debit of the duties

leviable on the goods but for this exemption:
Provided that exemptions from duty shall not be admissible if there is insufficient credit in the said license for debiting the
leviable on the goods but for this exemption;

(5)

that the imports against the said certificate are undertaken through sea ports at Mumbai, Kolkata, Cochin, Magdalla,
Kakinada, Kandla, Mangalore, Marmagoa, Madras, Nhava Sheva, Paradeep, Pipavav, Sikka, Tuticorin, Visakhapatnam,
Dahej, ,Nagapattinam, Mundhra and Okha or through any of the airports at Ahmedabad, Bangalore, Bhubaneswar, Mumbai,
Kolkata, Coimbatore, Delhi, Hyderabad, Jaipur, Madras, Srinagar, Trivandrum, Varanasi, Nagpur and Cochin or through
any of the Inland Container Depots at Agra, Bangalore, Coimbatore, Delhi, Faridabad, Gauhati, Guntur, Hyderabad, Jaipur,
Jallandhar, Kanpur, Ludhiana, Moradabad, Nagpur, Pimpri (Pune), Pitampur (Indore), Surat, Tirupur, Varanasi, Nasik,
Rudrapur (Nainital), Dighi (Pune), Vadodara, Daulatabad, (Wanjarwadi and Maliwada), Malanpur, Waluj (Aurangabad),
Anaparthy (Andhra Pradesh), Salem, Singanalur, Jodhpur, Kota, Udaipur, Ahmedabad, Bhiwadi, Madurai, Bhilwara,
Pondicherry and Garhi Harsaru or through the Land Customs Stations at Ranaghat, Singhabad and Raxaul.

Explanation :- In this notification,-

(i) "Capital Goods" has the same meaning as assigned to it in paragraph 9.10 of the Export and Import Policy,-
(ii) "goods" means,-
(a) capital Goods;
(b) office equipment ( including Computer systems, Software, Fax/ machine, Telephone); and
(c) raw materials, components, intermediates, consumables and parts other than agricultural and dairy products;

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) without payment of IGST. Post-export, the foreign buyer raises a debit note reducing the invoice value by 15% due to quality disputes, and the remittance received is correspondingly lower. The exporter has already filed GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B for the period. What are the obligations under Section 16(3) of the IGST Act read with Rule 96A of CGST Rules regarding reversal of ITC or payment of tax, and how does the reduced foreign exchange realization interact with the RBI's Export Data Processing and Monitoring System (EDPMS) reconciliation requirements for GST refund eligibility?

An Indian subsidiary of a foreign MNC provides back-office IT services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement. During assessment, the TPO recharacterizes the transaction as a 'unique and valuable contribution' under DEMPE analysis and proposes a profit split method instead of TNMM. The subsidiary simultaneously has a domestic transaction with an associated enterprise covered under Section 92BA. The aggregate of international transactions exceeds INR 50 crore and domestic transactions exceed INR 20 crore. Analyze the interplay between the mandatory TP documentation under Rule 10D, the application of the 'most appropriate method' under Rule 10C given the TPO's recharacterization, and whether the DRP (Dispute Resolution Panel) has jurisdiction to entertain both the international and specified domestic transaction adjustments in a single proceeding under Section 144C.

title: Cosmetics Rules, 2020
text:
[भागII—खण्‍ड‍3(i)]
भारत‍का‍राजपत्र‍:‍असाधारण‍
83

30.  Validity of licence.﹘ (1) A licence or loan licence issued in Form COS- 8 or Form COS- 9 shall remain

valid in perpetuity, subject to payment of licence or loan licence retention fee as specified in the 
Third Schedule before completion of the period of five years from the date of its issue, unless, it is 
suspended or cancelled by the State Licensing Authority.

(2) If the licensee fails to pay the required licence or loan licence retention fee on or before the due date 
as referred to in sub-rule (1), the licence or loan licence holder shall, in addition to the licence or loan 
licence retention fee, be liable to pay a late fee calculated at the rate of two per cent.of the licence or 
loan licence retention fee for every month or part thereof within one hundred and eighty days and in the 
event of non-payment of such fee during that period, the licence shall be deemed to have been cancelled.

31.  Inspection for verification of compliance.﹘(1) The premises licensed for manufacturing cosmetics

shall be inspected by Inspector appointed by the Central Government and State Government to verify 
the compliance with the conditions of licence and the provisions of the Act and these rules, not less than 
once in three years or as needed as per risk based approach.

(2) The inspectors appointed by the Central Government shall, when so required by the controlling 
officer or the Central Licensing Authority, carry out special inspection of the identified manufacturing 
facilities for ensuring compliance with the provisions of this Act and Rules thereunder.

CHAPTER V

PERMISSION FOR IMPORT OR MANUFACTURE OF NEW COSMETIC

32.  Permission for import or manufacture of new cosmetic.﹘(1) Any person who intends to import or

manufacture a new cosmetic, shall apply to the Central Licensing Authority in Form COS- 12 along 
with requisite fee and the data on safety and effectiveness of cosmetic.

(2) If the Central Licensing Authority, after being satisfied that the cosmetic if permitted to be 
manufactured or imported shall be safe and effective for use in the country, may issue a prior permission 
in Form COS-3, subject to the condition specified therein.

(3) The prior permission obtained in Form COS-3 shall be furnished along with the application for 
import under Chapter III or manufacture under Chapter IV of such new cosmetics.

(4) Methods of test or analysis to be employed for safety evaluation of new cosmetic shall be complied 
by manufacturer as specified in the IS 4011 : 2018 methods of test for safety evaluation of cosmetics, 
published by the Bureau of Indian Standards as amended from time to time.

CHAPTER VI

LABELLING, PACKING AND STANDARDS FOR SALE OR DISTRIBUTION OF COSMETICS

33.   Prohibition of sale or distribution.﹘ Subject to the other provisions of the act and these rules, no

person shall sell or distribute any cosmetic unless the cosmetic, if of Indian origin, is manufactured by a 
licensed manufacturer and labelled and packed in accordance with these rules.

34.  Manner of labelling.﹘ (1) Subject to other provisions of the rules, a cosmetic shall carry on both the

inner and outer labels:

(a) the name of the cosmetics,

(b) the name of the manufacturer and complete address of the premises of the manufacturer 
where the cosmetic has been manufactured. If the product has not been manufactured in a factory 
owned by the manufacturer, the name and address of the actual manufacturer or the name of the 
country where it has actually been manufactured as ―Made in ........ (name of country)" should be 
there on the label:

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates three distinct business verticals under a single GSTIN: (1) exempt supply of educational services, (2) zero-rated export of goods, and (3) taxable domestic supply of industrial machinery. During FY 2024-25, it incurred ₹85 lakhs in common input tax credit on overheads that cannot be exclusively attributed to any vertical. The company also has ₹12 lakhs ITC on capital goods used partially for exempt supplies. Describe the precise step-by-step methodology under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of CGST Rules for apportioning and reversing ITC, specifying the formula variables, the treatment of zero-rated supplies in the denominator, the annual reconciliation adjustment in GSTR-9, and the consequences if the provisional reversal computed monthly differs from the final annual reversal.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Customs & Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary imports specialised software licenses and embedded technical services from its foreign parent under a cost-sharing arrangement. The customs department seeks to enhance the declared transaction value by including the royalty payments made post-importation, citing Rule 10(1)(c) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, while the Transfer Pricing Officer simultaneously proposes an upward adjustment under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act on the grounds that the ALP for the same arrangement is higher than the contracted price. Analyze: (a) the legal basis for including or excluding post-importation royalties in customs value under WTO Customs Valuation Agreement Article 8 as adopted in Indian law; (b) the risk of economic double taxation when both adjustments are applied to the same underlying transaction; (c) the procedural remedy available under the Mutual Agreement Procedure in India's DTAAs and whether MAP relief can extend to indirect tax adjustments; and (d) the evidentiary standard the taxpayer must meet to rebut the customs enhancement while simultaneously defending the transfer price.

title: Intimation of difference in liability reported in statement of outward supplies and that reported in return

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) but the foreign buyer remits payment in INR through an intermediary bank account held in a third country. The exporter claims zero-rated supply under Section 16(3)(a) of the IGST Act. The department contends that 'receipt of convertible foreign exchange' is not satisfied under Rule 96A of the CGST Rules, and proposes to deny the refund of accumulated ITC while also levying IGST at 18% retrospectively. Simultaneously, the customs department initiates proceedings under Section 11 of the Foreign Exchange Management Act for failure to realise export proceeds in the prescribed manner. Analyse whether the GST department's dual action—denial of zero-rating AND levy of IGST—is legally sustainable, the inter-play between FEMA compliance and GST zero-rating eligibility, the correct forum for challenging each proceeding, and the quantum of interest/penalty exposure if both are sustained.

An Indian subsidiary (wholly owned by a Netherlands parent) provides software development services to the parent under a cost-plus arrangement. During assessment, the Transfer Pricing Officer recharacterises the transaction from a 'service' to a 'development of intangible', invoking Chapter X read with Rule 10B(1)(e), and applies a Profit Split Method instead of TNMM, attributing residual profit to the Indian entity on the ground that it performs DEMPE functions for a marketing intangible. The AO separately disallows 30% of the royalty payment received under Section 40A(2)(b) as excessive. The ITAT has remanded the matter twice on comparability. The assessee now wants to pursue an Advance Pricing Agreement while the MAP under the India-Netherlands DTAA is already pending for the same years. Advise on: (i) whether APA and MAP can run concurrently for overlapping years; (ii) the correct functional characterisation to rebut the DEMPE allegation; (iii) the evidentiary standard required to defend TNMM at the Tribunal after two remands; and (iv) the interaction between the Section 40A(2)(b) disallowance and the TP adjustment to avoid double taxation of the same income.

title:
Agreement between the India-Taipei Association in Taipei (ITA) and the Taipei Economic and Cultural Center in India (TECC) on the FICCI / TAITRA Carnet for the Temporary Admission of Goods
Appendix-2Appendix-3
text:
(d)        " FICCI/TAITRA Carnets " means the document reproduced as the Annex to the present Agreement;

(e)        " Issuing Association " means Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce & Industry (FICCI) for India-Taipei
Association in Taipei and Taiwan External Trade Development Council (TAITRA) for Taipei Economic and Cultural Center in
India approved for the issue of FICCI/TAITRA Carnets;

(f)         " Guaranteeing Association " means FICCI for India-Taipei Association in Taipei and TAITRA for Taipei Economic and
Cultural Center in India approved to guarantee the sums referred to in Article 6 of this Agreement;

(g)           " person " means natural or legal persons, unless the context otherwise requires;

(h)           " Customs Administration/authorities " means the Central Board of Excise and Customs, Department of Revenue,
Ministry of Finance, New Delhi, or the Customs Administration, Ministry of Finance, Taipei, as the case may be.

Article 2

The approval of an Issuing Association may be subject, in particular, to the condition that the price of FICCI/TAITRA Carnets
shall be commensurate with the cost of services rendered.

CHAPTER II

SCOPE

Article 3

Customs Administration in the territory of each Contracting Party may accept FICCI/TAITRA Carnets valid for its territory and
issued in accordance with the conditions laid down in the present Agreement, in lieu of its Customs documents and as due
security for the sums referred in Article 6 of the present Agreement, for temporary importation of goods for display or use at
exhibitions, international fairs, meetings or similar events as per the laws in force in its territory.

CHAPTER III

ISSUE AND USE OF FICCI/TAITRA CARNETS

Article 4

(1)        Issuing Associations shall not issue FICCI/TAITRA Carnets with a period of validity exceeding one year from the date of
issue. They shall indicate on the cover of the FICCI/TAITRA Carnet the territory in which it is valid and the names and the
addresses of the corresponding Guaranteeing Associations.

(2)        Once a FICCI/TAITRA Carnet has been issued, no extra item shall be added to the list of goods enumerated on the
reverse of the front cover of the Carnet, or on any continuation sheets annexed thereto (General List).

Article 5

The period fixed for the re-exportation of goods imported under cover of a FICCI/TAITRA Carnet shall not in any case exceed
the period of validity of that Carnet.

CHAPTER IV

GUARANTEE

Article 6

(1)        Each Guaranteeing Association shall undertake to pay to the Customs authorities of the territory in which it is
established the amount of the import duties and any other sums payable in the event of non-compliance with the conditions of
temporary admission, or of transit, in respect of goods introduced into that territory under cover of FICCI/TAITRA Carnet issued
by a corresponding Issuing Association. It shall be liable jointly and severally with the persons by whom the sums mentioned
above are due, for payment of such sums without demur or protest.

(2)        The liability of the Guaranteeing Association shall not exceed the amount of the import duties by more than ten percent.

(3)        When the Customs authorities of the territory of importation have unconditionally discharged a FICCI/TAITRA Carnet in
respect of certain goods, they can no longer claim from the Guaranteeing Association payment of the sums referred to in
paragraph (1) of this Article in respect of these goods. A claim may nevertheless still be made against the Guaranteeing
Association if it is subsequently discovered that the discharge of the Carnet was obtained improperly or fraudulently or that
there had been a breach of the conditions of temporary admission or of transit.

questions.question: A manufacturing company (ABC Ltd) has a factory that produces both taxable goods (18% GST) and exempt goods. During FY 2024-25, total ITC availed was ₹45,00,000. Taxable turnover was ₹3,20,00,000 and exempt turnover was ₹80,00,000. The company also has inputs exclusively used for taxable supplies worth ₹8,00,000 ITC and inputs exclusively for exempt supplies worth ₹3,50,000 ITC. Additionally, ₹1,20,000 ITC relates to a capital good used for both activities (useful life 5 years, purchased in Month 7 of FY). Calculate: (a) the ITC reversal under Rule 42/43, (b) the net ITC claimable, and (c) the annual adjustment required at year-end if actual D1/D2 ratio changes.
expected_answer_areas: Rule 42 common credit computation (T - T1 - T2 - T3)
Proportionate reversal using aggregate turnover ratio
Rule 43 capital goods ITC reversal (1/60 per month)

questions.question: XYZ India Pvt Ltd imports specialty chemicals from its parent company XYZ Germany GmbH. The declared transaction value is USD 500/kg. Customs SVB (Special Valuation Branch) has provisionally assessed at USD 500/kg pending final order. The ITAT has simultaneously held the ALP for the same transactions under Income Tax at USD 480/kg (buyer's perspective, meaning India paid too much). SVB is now demanding enhancement to USD 550/kg citing royalty payments of 3% of net sales that SVB treats as a condition of sale under Rule 10(1)(c) of Customs Valuation Rules 2007. The company argues: (i) royalty is post-import and not related to imported goods, (ii) ITAT ALP finding should bind SVB. Analyze: (a) SVB's jurisdiction vs ITAT finding — which prevails and why, (b) deductibility of royalty from customs value under Rule 10(1)(c) test, (c) provisional assessment implications if final SVB order exceeds bond amount, and (d) whether a DRP/MAP under DTAA can provide relief.
expected_answer_areas: Customs Valuation Rules 2007 Rule 10(1)(c) — condition of sale test for royalties
SVB Circular 11/2016-Cus independence from transfer pricing orders
Rule 3(3)(a) related party test and burden of proof
Section 18 Customs Act provisional assessment bond/bank guarantee implications
DTAA MAP applicability limited to income tax, not customs — separate regimes
Supreme Court precedent: Ferodo India / Eicher Tractors on royalty inclusion
source_sections: Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007

questions.question: A multinational company provides a composite software-as-a-service (SaaS) platform to Indian clients that bundles (i) cloud hosting, (ii) real-time analytics processing, (iii) AI-driven compliance monitoring, and (iv) dedicated customer success management. The foreign parent entity holds no PE in India but the Indian subsidiary acts as a 'facilitator' for onboarding. Under the GST framework, analyze whether each bundled component qualifies as an 'Online Information and Database Access or Retrieval (OIDAR)' service, determine the principal supply for composite supply classification, assess the reverse-charge mechanism applicability on the Indian subsidiary, and identify the specific HSN/SAC codes along with the rate differential exposure if the components are unbundled versus kept composite — citing the relevant circulars, AAR rulings, and CGST Act provisions.

An Indian importer sources specialty chemical intermediates from its wholly-owned Mauritius holding company, which in turn sources them from a third-party manufacturer in Germany. Customs valuation is done on transaction value under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, but the transfer pricing study benchmarks the intercompany price using the Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) with a tested party operating margin of 8–12%. The customs department proposes to reject transaction value under Rule 3(2)(e) alleging price influence due to related-party relationship, and shift to Computed Value under Rule 7. Simultaneously, the Income Tax transfer pricing officer proposes an upward adjustment arguing the price is below ALP. Construct a comprehensive legal and factual defense strategy that reconciles the divergent valuation methodologies across Customs and Income Tax, identifies the specific safe-harbour provisions and advance pricing agreement routes available, and addresses the anti-avoidance risk under GAAR given the Mauritius treaty structure — citing the Customs Act 1962, Income Tax Act 1961, and applicable DTAA provisions.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a factory in Gujarat and a registered office in Maharashtra. During an audit, the GST department alleges that the company has been wrongly availing Input Tax Credit (ITC) on capital goods used partially for exempt supplies without proportionate reversal under Rule 43 of CGST Rules. The company argues that the capital goods are used predominantly (>80%) for taxable supplies and that de minimis exemption applies. The department has issued SCN under Section 74 (fraud/suppression). Analyze: (a) whether Section 74 invocation is justified or should it be Section 73, (b) the correct methodology for ITC reversal on common capital goods, (c) whether the 5-year extended limitation period applies, and (d) the remedies available to the taxpayer including any recent circular clarifications.

An Indian importer is purchasing goods from a related foreign supplier (parent company holds 51% equity). The declared transaction value is ₹10 crore. Customs authorities invoke Rule 4 of Customs Valuation Rules 2007, rejecting the transaction value on grounds of relationship influencing price, and propose to use deductive value under Rule 7. The importer contests this, presenting a Transfer Pricing study under the Income Tax Act showing the price is at arm's length. The goods are also subject to an ongoing Anti-Dumping Duty investigation. Analyze: (a) the legal interplay between Customs valuation rules and Transfer Pricing documentation as evidence, (b) the burden of proof under Rule 3(3) and what constitutes 'circumstances of sale' to rebut rejection, (c) whether an arm's length TP certificate is binding on Customs authorities under any judicial precedent, and (d) the procedural strategy for challenging the valuation order before CESTAT while also managing the ADD investigation.

title: Service tax issues relating to authorized motor vehicle dealers and service stations

commission with their customers to attract them. However, this is an independent transaction between the automobile dealer
and the purchaser of the vehicle, and does not involve the service rendered by the automobile dealer to the finance company.
Therefore, the tax payable by the dealer would be on the gross amount received from the financial company and not on the
balance amount, i.e., after excluding the amount that he passes on to the customer.

5.                 As regards the applicability of service tax on the activity of servicing /repairing of the commercial vehicles, it is
clarified that as regards 'authorized service stations', the taxable service, means any service provided or to be provided, to a
customer, by an authorized service station, in relation to any service, repair, reconditioning or restoration of motor cars, light
motor vehicles or two wheeled motor vehicles, in any manner. Further, a 'light motor vehicle' means any motor vehicle
constructed or adapted to carry more than six messengers, but not more than twelve passengers, excluding driver .
Similarly, as per the 'Motor Vehicle Act', a 'motor car means any motor vehicle other than a transport vehicles, omnibus,

questions.question: A manufacturer registered under GST in Maharashtra supplies goods to an SEZ unit in Gujarat under a zero-rated supply with payment of IGST. The SEZ developer later rejects the goods and returns them after 180 days from the original invoice date. The supplier had already claimed a refund of IGST paid on the original supply. How should the supplier account for the return — in terms of ITC reversal, issuance of credit note, re-determination of place of supply, and whether the returned goods attract fresh GST liability? Cite the applicable sections of CGST Act 2017, SEZ Act 2005, and any relevant circulars.

An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using the TNMM (Transactional Net Margin Method). The Transfer Pricing Officer proposes to recharacterize the arrangement as a 'unique and valuable contribution' and applies the Profit Split Method instead, attributing 40% of the consolidated group profit to the Indian entity. The assessee argues that (a) the Indian entity bears no entrepreneurial risk, (b) all IP is legally owned by the US parent, and (c) TNMM is the most appropriate method per Rule 10C. Analyze the strength of each party's position under the Income Tax Act 1961, OECD BEPS Action 8–10 guidelines as adopted by India, and the Safe Harbour Rules under Rule 10TD, identifying the critical factual and legal pivot points that would determine the outcome.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operating as an EOU (Export Oriented Unit) under the Foreign Trade Policy procures capital goods domestically and claims IGST exemption under Notification 78/2017-Customs. Mid-year, the unit partially de-bonds 30% of its floor area for DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) operations while retaining the remaining 70% under EOU status. Determine: (a) the proportionate ITC reversal obligation on the capital goods under Rule 43 of CGST Rules, (b) whether the de-bonded portion triggers a 'deemed export' supply requiring re-determination of place of supply under Section 11 of the IGST Act, (c) the applicable interest liability under Section 50 if the reversal is made after the filing deadline, and (d) whether the company can claim a fresh ITC credit on the same capital goods for the DTA portion under Section 16(4) within the same financial year, given the proviso introduced by the Finance Act 2022.
questions.expected_skill_level: Senior Tax Consultant / GST Litigation Expert

questions.question: A multinational enterprise (MNE) imports specialised intermediary chemicals from its AE (Associated Enterprise) in Germany under a long-term supply agreement. The customs valuation is declared using the transaction value method under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) during assessment under Section 92CA of the Income Tax Act rejects the ALP declared by the assessee and makes an upward TP adjustment, thereby increasing the arm's length price of the imported goods by 18%. Analyse: (a) whether the enhanced TP-determined ALP automatically revises the assessable value for customs purposes under Section 14 of the Customs Act 1962, or whether a separate re-determination by customs authorities is required; (b) the interaction between the CBIC Circular on related-party imports and the OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines as applied by Indian courts post-Vodafone; (c) whether the assessee can invoke the Advance Pricing Agreement (APA) entered with the CBDT as a shield against customs re-valuation; and (d) the anti-avoidance implications if the assessee retrospectively amends the inter-company agreement to align with the TPO order before the customs show-cause notice is adjudicated.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to an SEZ unit under a zero-rated supply with payment of IGST and claims refund under Section 16(3)(a) of the IGST Act. The SEZ unit subsequently re-exports 60% of the goods as is, uses 30% in the authorized operations, and sells 10% in DTA after paying applicable duties. The department contends that the 10% DTA clearance retrospectively invalidates the zero-rated classification of the entire supply and issues a demand for reversal of the entire IGST refund with interest under Section 50 and penalty under Section 74. Analyze the legal tenability of the department's position, distinguishing between the point of determination of zero-rated supply status, the impact of subsequent DTA clearance on the original supply classification, and the applicability of Section 74 versus Section 73 given that the SEZ unit's DTA clearance was disclosed in its own returns.

An Indian importer purchases specialty chemicals from its wholly-owned overseas subsidiary. The declared transaction value is accepted by Customs under Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007. The same transaction is examined by the Transfer Pricing Officer who, applying TNMM, determines an arm's length price 22% lower than the declared price, resulting in a TP adjustment that reduces the importer's taxable income. The Customs department, upon learning of the TP order, proposes to re-open the Bill of Entry under Section 17(5) of the Customs Act arguing that the TP order constitutes 'additional information' proving the declared value was excessive and not at arm's length, and seeks to re-determine value under Rule 4/5 with a view to re-computing customs duty on the revised (lower) ALP. Critically evaluate whether a downward TP adjustment can legally trigger upward or downward revision of customs value, the jurisdictional overlap between CBIC and CBDT, the evidentiary weight of a TP order in customs valuation proceedings, and the taxpayer's optimal litigation strategy.
expected_skills: res judicata / issue estoppel in tax proceedings

questions.question: You have a 108k-chunk Qdrant collection built from Indian tax law PDFs using BGE-M3 embeddings. During peak query load, you observe that semantic search returns highly relevant chunks but the final LLM answer frequently contradicts the retrieved context. You suspect the issue is in the reranking or prompt construction phase. Walk through your diagnostic approach: how do you isolate whether the failure is (a) embedding retrieval returning wrong chunks despite high cosine scores, (b) the reranker demoting the correct chunks, (c) context window truncation silently dropping the decisive passage, or (d) the LLM hallucinating despite correct context? For each failure mode, specify the exact metric or log signal you would inspect and the corrective intervention.

On a 4-core CPU host running llama-server in Vulkan mode (not ROCm) across multiple RX 6700 XT GPUs, you are serving a 14B parameter model at /no_think mode for a FastAPI RAG application. Under concurrent load (8 simultaneous requests), p99 latency spikes to 40s while p50 stays at 6s. The GPU VRAM shows <70% utilization and CPU uptime load average is 3.8. Explain the exact causal chain producing this latency distribution, identify which resource is the actual bottleneck despite the misleading VRAM headroom, and prescribe a prioritized sequence of interventions covering process affinity, request queuing strategy, and Vulkan-specific parallelism limits — without adding hardware.

title: Service Tax on Clearing & Forwarding Agents and renta-cab scheme operators.
text:
NOTIFICATION

F.No. B.43/7/97-TRU

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Tax Research Unit

****

New Delhi, dated the 11th July, 1997

To  
All Commissioners of Central Excise and Customs  
Sir,

Sub: Service Tax on Clearing & Forwarding Agents and renta-cab scheme 
operators.

I am directed to invite your attention to Section 88 of the Finance Act, 1997 which, 
inter-alia, provides for levy of service tax on the services rendered by clearing and 
forwarding agents and rent-a-cab scheme operators. It has been decided to bring the 
above services under the service tax net with effect from 16th July, 1997. Notification 
Nos. 26/97-ST and 27/97-ST, both dated the 11th July, 1997 have been issued in this 
regard (copies enclosed).

2. CLEARING AND FORWARDING AGENTS

2.1     "Clearing and forwarding agent" has been defined as "any person who is engaged 
in providing any service, either directly or indirectly, connected with clearing and 
forwarding operations in any manner to any other person and includes a consigning 
agent." The taxable service has been defined as "any service provided to a client, by 
C&F agent in relation to clearing and forwarding agents are engaged/appointed by 
manufacturer of goods (both excisable and non-excisable goods),

2.2     Normally, there is a contract between the principal and the clearing and forwarding 
agent detailing the terms and conditions and also indicating the commission or 
remuneration to which the C&F agent is entitled. A clearing and Forwarding agent 
normally undertakes the following activites-  
(a) Receiving the goods from the factories or premises of the principal or his agents;  
(b) Warehousing these goods;  
(c) Receiving despatch orders from the principal;  
(d) Arranging despatch of goods as per the directions of the principal by engaging 
transport on his own or through the authorised transporters of the principal;  
(e) Maintaining records of the receipt and despatch of goods and the stock available at 
the warehouse;  
(f) Preparing invoices on behalf of the principal

2.3     It has been decided that the person responsible for collecting the service tax in the 
case of services rendered by a clearing and forwarding agent shall be the person 
engaging/appointing a clearing and forwarding agent (Notification No. 26/97-service tax 
refers). It may be noted that unlike in the case of other service tax levies where the 
service provider is the person responsible for collecting the service tax, in the case of 
services rendered by the clearing and forwarding agents the service tax liability shall be 
discharged by the person availing the service so rendered. In order words, the principal 
who engages a clearing and forwarding agent is the person responsible for collecting and 
paying the service tax to the exchequer, Commissioners of Central Exicse may therefore

questions.question: A private limited company engaged in both taxable and exempt supplies imports capital goods (CNC machinery) on which it pays IGST of ₹18 lakhs. The company's aggregate turnover for the preceding FY was ₹4.2 crores. During the current year, the proportion of exempt supply to total turnover is 35%. The company also received a show cause notice under Section 74 for alleged wrongful availment of ITC on such capital goods in a prior period, for which the adjudication order is pending. Considering the reversal obligations under Rule 43, the impact of Section 74 proceedings on ITC eligibility, and the restriction under Section 17(5), detail the step-by-step computation of admissible ITC, the reversal amount, and the interest liability if the company had provisionally availed the full ₹18 lakhs ITC in GSTR-3B for three months before making the reversal.

An EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports raw materials duty-free under a CT-3 certificate and subsequently converts them into finished goods, 70% of which are exported and 30% sold in the Domestic Tariff Area (DTA). Due to a fire, 15% of the total raw material stock is destroyed before processing. The customs authority issues a demand for recovery of duty on the destroyed stock under Section 28 of the Customs Act, 1962, along with interest under Section 28AA. The EOU disputes this, claiming force majeure and citing Rule 53 of the SEZ Rules by analogy. Simultaneously, the GST department demands reversal of IGST credit on inputs attributable to DTA sales under Rule 42 of CGST Rules. Analyze: (a) the legality of the customs duty demand on destroyed goods, (b) whether Section 28AA interest is automatic or discretionary in this scenario, (c) the correct methodology for ITC reversal on DTA sales when inputs are commingled, and (d) the jurisdictional overlap between CBIC customs and GST audit wings and how the EOU should respond to avoid contradictory adjudication outcomes.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two distinct units — Unit A (registered in Maharashtra) and Unit B (registered in Gujarat) — operating under the same PAN but separate GSTINs. Unit A procures raw materials and transfers them as stock transfers to Unit B for further processing and final sale. Unit B subsequently claims input tax credit on the GST paid on these inter-unit transfers. The department issues a notice contending that: (i) the stock transfers constitute 'supply' under Schedule I of the CGST Act since both units are 'distinct persons' under Section 25(4), (ii) the valuation must follow Rule 28 of CGST Rules (open market value / 110% of cost), and (iii) Unit B cannot claim ITC since the underlying transfer was not at arm's length. The company argues that both units share the same management, the transfer price was cost-plus 5%, and economic substance should govern. Analyze the legal validity of the department's triple-pronged challenge, the applicable valuation hierarchy under Rule 28 read with the proviso for full ITC entitlement, and whether the doctrine of 'economic substance over legal form' has any traction under the GST framework given the absence of an anti-avoidance rule equivalent to GAAR in the CGST Act.

A Category-II AIF (Alternative Investment Fund) structured as a trust invests in unlisted equity shares of a portfolio company. After 36 months, the AIF distributes the gains to its unitholders — a mix of resident HNIs, a Mauritius-based FPI, and a Singapore-based family office. The AIF manager claims pass-through status under Section 115UB of the Income Tax Act, arguing that capital gains should be taxed in the hands of each investor per their applicable treaty rates. The Assessing Officer recharacterizes the gains as 'business income' on the ground that the AIF made 12 investments and exits in 3 years, indicating a profit-motive trading pattern rather than investment, thereby denying pass-through and taxing the AIF at MMR. Simultaneously, the transfer pricing officer invokes Chapter X for the management fee paid by the AIF to its offshore investment manager, alleging it is excessive under the TNMM method. Critically evaluate: (i) the threshold between 'investment activity' and 'adventure in the nature of trade' for AIFs under Section 115UB, (ii) whether pass-through denial at AIF level creates double-taxation exposure for treaty-protected foreign investors and what remedies exist, and (iii) the jurisdictional competence of the TPO to examine intra-AIF-structure fees where the AIF itself claims pass-through (i.e., is the AIF even an 'enterprise' for TP purposes under Section 92F).

title: Wheat Import - Writ Petitions challenging duty
text:
Circular No.18/2000-Cus.

F.NO. 494/2/2000-Cus. VI 
Government of India 
Ministry of Finance 
Department of Revenue 
Central Board of Excise & Customs

New Delhi, the 13 th March, 2000

Subject: Wheat Import - Writ Petitions challenging duty

It is directed to inform that a number of writ peritions have been filed in the High court of Chennai and in the Hight court of
Kerala (Ernakulam Bench) challenging the power of the Central Government vide Section 8A of CTA'65 to impose duty on
imported wheat vide Notification NO. 127/99 Customs dated 1.12.1999. the customs duty on imported wheat was raised from
'Free' to 50%.

2.        In this connection this is to convey that in the counter-affidavits that may be filed by the Commissioneers including on
behalf on Union of India the following may be mentioned:-

1. the Notification 127/99 customs dated 1.12.1999 was issued under Section 8A of CTA that gives emergency

power to the Central Government to enhance the due rate. Further in terms of Seciton7(3) the Notification 127/99
was duly approved by the Parliament. (The Lok Sabha passed the resolution at the sitting held on 21 st December,
1999).

2. The comments of JS9TRU) to Commissioner of Customs, Cochin may also be used while preparing a suitable

counter-affidavit (Annexure "A").

The total number of similar cases pending in various courts, indication revenue involving may also be intimated to the Board.

Annexure "A"

To : Shri T.K. Jayaraman Commissioner of Customs, Cochin

From : T.R. Rustagi joint Secretary (TRU0 FINREV0 New Delhi.

F.No. 354/56/99-TRU(.) Kindly refer to your letter F.No. S32/180/99 AP(1) dated 28.12.99 regarding writ Petition No.
32741/99 filed the Hon'ble High court of Kerala challenging the authority of central Government to impose duty. On wheat
through a notification issued under Section 8A of the Customs Traiff Act, 1975(.). The main issue raised by the petitioner is that
since that rate of duty prescribed in respect of wheat in the tariff was "Free" prior to 1.12.99, it was not "leviable " to duty (.).
according to him the Central Government could have invoked the power to increase duty under Section 8A only if wheat was
leviable to duty in the first place (.). in this regard, he seeks to make a distinction between "Free" rate and "Nil" rate and argues
that while nil rate constitutes a levy free [rate] does not(.) There does not appear to be any basis for making this distinction(.)
According to Section 12 to the Custom Act, 1962 "Duties of customs shall be levied at such rates as may be specified under
the Customs Tariff Act, 1975(.)" In respect of goods falling under subheading Nos. 1001.10 and 1001.90 of the first schedule to
the Customs Tariff Act the rate of duty prescribed prior to 1.12.99 was "Free"(.) . When read in the light of Section 12, it is
evident that goods falling under these subheading were leviable to duty at "Free" rate (.) Further, Notification No. 127/99 dated
1.12.99 whereby duty on wheat was increased to 50% has already received the approval of Parliament (.) Admission of the writ
may be opposed accordingly (.) In view of the significance of the issue raised in the writ your may request Additional Solicitor
General, Chennai to appear on behalf of the Central Government.

questions.question: A manufacturing company (registered in Maharashtra) supplies goods to its 100% subsidiary (registered in Karnataka) at below-market transfer prices. The Karnataka subsidiary then exports these goods to a UAE buyer. The Maharashtra unit claims full ITC on inputs. The Karnataka unit claims export refund under LUT (zero-rated supply). The GST officer contends: (a) the inter-unit supply is between 'related persons' under Schedule I and must be valued at open market value under Rule 28, (b) the artificially low valuation inflates the Karnataka unit's export refund, and (c) the ITC chain in Maharashtra must be reversed proportionately. Analyze whether the officer's three contentions are legally sustainable under the CGST Act 2017 and rules, citing specific provisions, relevant AAR/AAAR rulings, and the Supreme Court's jurisprudence on 'related party' valuation in indirect taxes.

A Category-II AIF (Alternative Investment Fund) structured as a trust invests in unlisted equity of a startup. Three years later, the startup undergoes a court-approved merger under Section 230-232 of the Companies Act 2013, and the AIF receives shares of the resulting entity at a swap ratio determined by a SEBI-registered valuer. The AIF's unitholders include: (i) a GIFT City IFSC branch of a foreign bank, (ii) a domestic HUF, and (iii) a foreign portfolio investor (FPI) from a treaty country. Determine: (a) whether the merger constitutes a 'transfer' triggering capital gains at the AIF or pass-through level under Section 47(vii) read with Section 115UB, (b) the tax treatment for each class of unitholder given the pass-through regime, (c) whether the deemed fair market value provisions of Section 50CA apply to the swap shares received, and (d) how GIFT City IFSC exemptions interact with the pass-through attribution for the foreign bank branch.
