questions.question: A manufacturing company has a factory in Maharashtra and a warehouse in Gujarat. They transfer finished goods from factory to warehouse under a stock transfer arrangement (not a sale). The goods are later sold from the warehouse to customers in Rajasthan. The company claims ITC on inputs used in manufacturing. The GST officer contends that the inter-state stock transfer is a 'supply' under Schedule I of the CGST Act and raises a demand for IGST on the transfer value, disallowing ITC credit chain. Simultaneously, the officer raises a separate demand under Section 74 alleging suppression of facts because the company did not declare these transfers in GSTR-1 for 18 months. Analyze the legal correctness of both demands, the applicable mens rea standard under Section 74 vs Section 73, and the remedy available to the company including any limitation period considerations.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US parent (USCo) provides software development services to USCo under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15% using the TNMM method. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) rejects TNMM and proposes the Profit Split Method (PSM), arguing that IndCo contributes unique intangibles (proprietary algorithms developed by Indian engineers). The TPO attributes 40% of the combined group profit to IndCo, resulting in a TP adjustment of ₹85 crore. IndCo challenges this, arguing: (a) the algorithms are owned by USCo under the contract, (b) Indian engineers are employees-for-hire with no residual profit entitlement, and (c) PSM is inapplicable because reliable external comparables exist for TNMM. Concurrently, the Assessing Officer invokes Section 9(1)(vi) treating royalty payments from USCo to IndCo (for licensed tools) as taxable in India. Analyze the validity of the TPO's PSM selection, the legal test for 'unique intangibles' under Indian TP regulations, the interplay with the India-US DTAA Article 12 on royalties, and the MAP/APA remedies available.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under a composite contract that includes installation and commissioning services. The goods are classifiable under HSN 8479 (attracting 18% GST) while the installation service would independently attract 12% GST. The recipient argues that the dominant element test should apply, making the entire supply taxable at 12%. The supplier contends Schedule II entry 5(b) mandates treatment as a composite supply with the principal supply being the goods at 18%. Analyze the correct GST treatment, the applicable rate, the relevant circular or advance ruling precedents, and whether the recipient's ITC position changes depending on which classification prevails.
questions.source_hint: CGST Act Sections 8, 2(30), 2(74); Schedule II; CBIC Circular 47/21/2018

questions.question: An exporter claiming IGST refund under Rule 96 discovers that their Customs shipping bills were filed with incorrect GSTIN-wise breakup, causing ICEGATE-GSTN reconciliation to fail for 14 invoices across two financial years. The refund application is already pending at the jurisdictional commissionerate. Simultaneously, the supplier of the exported goods has reversed ITC under Rule 42 proportionate to an exempt supply, incorrectly treating zero-rated supply as exempt. Detail the step-by-step remediation: (a) amendment procedure for shipping bills, (b) rectification of supplier's Rule 42 reversal, (c) impact on the pending refund timeline and whether a fresh refund application is required, and (d) interest liability exposure for both parties during the pendency.
questions.source_hint: CGST Rules 42, 89, 96; IGST Act Section 16; CBIC Circular 125/44/2019; Customs Act Section 149

title: Regarding anti-dumping duty on Vitamin C, originating in, or exported from the People's Republic of China
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No.67/2009-Customs

New Delhi,  the 16th June, 2009

G.S.R. --(E).  Whereas, the designated authority vide notification No. 15/16/2008-DGAD, dated the 23 rd May, 2008, published
in Part I, section 1 of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 27 th May, 2008, had initiated review in terms of sub-section
(5) of section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) (hereinafter referred to as the said Tariff Act), and in pursuance
of rule 23 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles and for
Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995 (hereinafter referred to as the said rules), in the matter of continuation of anti-dumping duty
on Vitamin C (hereinafter referred as the subject goods) , originating in, or exported from the People's Republic of China
(hereinafter referred as the subject country), imposed vide notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance
(Department of Revenue), No. 159/2003- CUSTOMS dated the 24 th October, 2003, published in the Gazette of India,
Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R.840(E), dated the 24 th October, 2003, and had requested
for  extension of anti-dumping duty for a period of one year from the date of its expiry, in terms of sub-section (5) of section 9A
of the said Customs Tariff Act;

And whereas, the Central Government had extended the anti-dumping duty on the subject goods, originating in, or exported
from, the subject country upto and inclusive of the 23rd October, 2009 vide notification of the Government of India, in the
Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 109/2008 -Customs dated the 21st October, 2008, published in the Gazette
of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R.743(E), dated the 21st October, 2008;

And whereas, in the matter of review of anti-dumping on import of the subject goods, originating in, or exported from, the
subject country, the designated authority in its final findings issued vide notification No. 15/16/2008-DGAD, dated 21st May,
2009, published in Part I, Section 1 of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 22nd May, 2009, had come to the
conclusion that-

(a)    the subject goods originating in, or exported from, the subject country had been exported to India below their normal value,
resulting in dumping;

(b)   the performance of domestic industry had not improved during the continuation of anti-dumping duty on the subject goods;
and

(c)    discontinuation of anti-dumping duties on the subject goods from subject country would lead to the continuation of
dumping and injury to the domestic industry;

and had recommended continued imposition of definitive anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject goods, originating in, or
exported from, the subject country and imported into India, in order to remove injury to the domestic industry;

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-sections (1) and (5) of section 9A of the said Tariff Act, read with
rules 18 and 23 of the said rules, the Central Government, after considering the aforesaid final findings of the designated
authority, hereby imposes on the subject goods , the description of which is specified in column (3) of the Table below, falling
under tariff item of the First Schedule to the said Tariff Act as specified in the corresponding entry in column (2), the
specification of which is specified in column (4) of the said Table, originating in the country as specified in the corresponding
entry in column (5), and produced by the producer as specified in the corresponding entry in column (7), when exported from
the country as specified in the corresponding entry in column (6), by the exporter as specified in the corresponding entry in
column (8), and imported into India, an anti-dumping duty at a rate which is equal to the amount as specified in the
corresponding entry in column (9), in the currency as specified in the corresponding entry in column (11)  and as per unit of
measurement as specified in the corresponding entry in column (10), of the said Table.

questions.question: A registered taxpayer under GST files GSTR-1 for FY 2023-24 showing zero outward supplies for Q3, but the department's ASMT-10 notice flags a mismatch between GSTR-2B auto-populated ITC and the taxpayer's GSTR-3B for the same period. The taxpayer claims the discrepancy arose because their supplier amended invoices in their GSTR-1A after the taxpayer had already filed GSTR-3B. The department proposes to disallow ITC under Section 16(2)(c) citing non-payment of tax by the supplier. The taxpayer argues that circular 183/15/2022-GST provides protection. Analyze: (a) whether the circular's protection applies given the amendment timeline, (b) what documentary evidence is required under Rule 36(4) to sustain the ITC claim, and (c) whether the taxpayer can seek rectification under Section 161 or must proceed to adjudication under Section 73.

An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using TNMM at a net margin of 14%. During TP audit for AY 2022-23, the TPO proposes an upward adjustment claiming the comparable set is contaminated because three comparables have related-party revenue exceeding 25%. The assessee argues that the comparables were accepted in the prior year's APA. Simultaneously, the assessee entered into a secondment arrangement mid-year where US employees worked in India for 7 months—the TPO treats this as creating a service PE and taxes the deemed profits under Article 7 of the DTAA. Analyze: (a) the validity of the TPO's comparable rejection given APA precedent and Rule 10B(3) bright-line tests, (b) whether the 7-month secondment triggers a service PE under the India-US DTAA's 90-day threshold and how the MFN clause in the DTAA protocol affects the analysis, and (c) the interaction between the TP adjustment and the PE income attribution—specifically whether double taxation arises and what MAP relief is available under Section 90.

title: Central Excise - Facility of manufacturing or processing of export goods under claim of rebate of duty paid on inputs under Notification No. 41/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 26th  June, 2001- Modification of Para 8.2 of the Part-V of Cha
text:
Circular No.604/41/2001-CX

29 th November, 2001

F.No.209/29/2001-CX.6

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

Subject: Central Excise - Facility of manufacturing or processing of export goods under claim of rebate of duty
paid on inputs under Notification No. 41/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 26 th June, 2001- Modification of Para 8.2
of the Part-V of Chapter-8 of the Central Excise Manual -

I am directed to say that doubts still persist about instructions contained in Para 8.2 of the Part-V of Chapter-8 of the Central
Excise Manual (effective from 1.9.2001) notwithstanding instructions contained in Circular No. 602/39 /2001-CX dated
21.11.2001.

2.            The Board has examined the matter and decided that in supersession of Circular No. 602/39 /2001-CX dated
21.11.2001, for Para 8.2 of Part-V of Chapter-8 of Central Excise Manual (First Edition), the following shall be substituted,
namely: -

"8.2             Rebate of Input stage duty shall be allowed to manufacturer (processor)-exporter or the merchant-exporter, as
the case may be, where such inputs are used in the manufacture/processing of export goods and cleared directly from the
factory of the manufacturer/processor. The manufacturer /processor may export the goods directly himself or through merchant-
exporter. Where the goods are exported by merchant-exporter, his name shall be mentioned on ARE-2 and other conditions
prescribed in Notification No.41/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 26 th June, 2001 should be fulfilled."

3.            The Trade and the field formations may suitably be informed.

4.            Receipt of this Circular may please be acknowledged.

5.            Hindi version will follow.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has a factory in Maharashtra and a registered office in Delhi. They purchase raw materials from a supplier in Gujarat under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement where the goods are shipped directly from Gujarat to the Maharashtra factory but the invoice is raised on the Delhi registered office. The Delhi office then raises an inter-unit stock transfer to Maharashtra. Determine: (a) the place of supply for the original Gujarat-to-Delhi transaction, (b) whether ITC can be claimed by Delhi on the original purchase, (c) the GST implications of the subsequent Delhi-to-Maharashtra inter-unit transfer under Schedule I, and (d) whether the manufacturer can claim a refund of accumulated ITC if their output is zero-rated.

An Indian software company imports cloud computing services (IaaS) from a US provider under a contract valued at USD 50,000/month. The company also exports SaaS services to EU clients worth USD 80,000/month and claims LUT-based zero-rating. During an audit, the department contends that: (i) the imported cloud services attract IGST under the reverse charge mechanism and the company must register even if below threshold; (ii) the export of SaaS qualifies as 'intermediary services' and not zero-rated exports; and (iii) accumulated ITC from reverse charge on imports cannot be used to offset output liability on domestic B2B software sales. Analyze each contention with reference to the relevant IGST provisions, CBIC circulars, and any applicable Supreme Court or AAR rulings.

title: The Finance Act, 2016 (28 of 2016)
text:
46
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY
[PART II—

Insertion of
new section
270A.

98. After section 270 of the Income-tax Act [as it stood immediately before its omission
by section 105 of the Direct Tax Laws (Amendment) Act, 1987], the following section shall
be inserted with effect from the 1st day of April, 2017, namely:—

‘270A. (1) The Assessing Officer or the Commissioner (Appeals) or the Principal
Commissioner or Commissioner may, during the course of any proceedings under this
Act, direct that  any person who has under-reported his income shall be liable to pay a
penalty in addition to tax, if any, on the under-reported income.

Penalty for
under-reporting
and
misreporting of
income.

(2) A person shall be considered to have under-reported his income, if—

(a) the income assessed is greater than the income determined in the return
processed under clause (a) of sub-section (1) of section 143;

(b) the income assessed is greater than the maximum amount not chargeable
to tax, where no return of income has been furnished;

(c) the income reassessed is greater than the income assessed or reassessed
immediately before such reassessment;

(d) the amount of deemed total income assessed or reassessed as per the
provisions of section 115JB or section 115JC, as the case may be, is greater than
the deemed total income determined in the return processed under clause (a) of
sub-section (1) of section 143;

(e) the amount of deemed total income assessed as per the provisions of
section 115JB or section 115JC is greater than the maximum amount not
chargeable to tax, where no return of income has been filed;

(f) the amount of deemed total income reassessed as per the provisions of
section 115JB or section 115JC, as the case may be, is greater than the deemed
total income assessed or reassessed immediately before such reassessment;

(g) the income assessed or reassessed has the effect of reducing the loss or
converting such loss into income.

(3) The amount of under-reported income shall be,—

(i) in a case where income has been assessed for the first time,—

(a) if return has been furnished, the difference between the amount
of income assessed and the amount of income determined under clause
(a) of sub-section (1) of section 143;

(b) in a case where no return has been furnished,—

(A) the amount of income assessed, in the case of a company,
firm or local authority; and

(B) the difference between the amount of income assessed
and the maximum amount not chargeable to tax, in a case not covered
in item (A);

(ii) in any other case, the difference between the amount of income
reassessed or recomputed and the amount of income assessed, reassessed or
recomputed in a preceding order:

Provided that where under-reported income arises out of determination of deemed
total income in accordance with the provisions of section 115JB or section 115JC, the
amount of total under-reported income shall be determined in accordance with the
following formula—

(A — B) + (C — D)

where,

A = the total income assessed as per the provisions other than the provisions
contained in section 115JB or section 115JC (herein called general provisions);

questions.question: In a production RAG pipeline ingesting 108k chunks of Indian tax law (GST, Customs, Income Tax) with BGE-M3 embeddings on a 4-core Vulkan GPU and Qdrant as the vector store, query latency spikes to 18–25 seconds under concurrent load. The LLM (Qwen3-14B via llama-server Vulkan) uses /no_think mode. Profiling shows: embedding step 300ms, Qdrant retrieval 1.2s, context assembly 800ms, LLM generation 14–22s. The server has 2 available GPU VRAM slots. Propose a concrete, prioritized optimization plan addressing: (a) LLM generation throughput, (b) retrieval quality vs. speed tradeoff using hybrid sparse-dense search, (c) async request batching strategy, and (d) a reranking approach that does not add a separate GPU process. Justify each decision against the hardware constraints.

You are extending a CBIC tax-law RAG system (Qdrant, FastAPI, BGE-M3 embeddings, Qwen3-14B LLM) to support four additional corpora: Income Tax Act, MCA Company Law, Labour Codes, and RBI Circulars — each with 50k–200k chunks, distinct citation formats, and overlapping terminology (e.g., 'assessment', 'penalty', 'return' mean different things across corpora). Design a multi-corpus retrieval architecture that: (a) isolates corpus-level relevance while enabling cross-corpus queries, (b) handles citation disambiguation and entity normalization without a separate NER service, (c) supports per-corpus access control at query time, and (d) minimizes re-ingestion cost when a corpus receives incremental updates (e.g., new CBIC circulars). Specify Qdrant collection strategy, metadata schema, and retrieval routing logic.

questions.question: A manufacturing company imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously avails ITC on such goods under Section 16 of the CGST Act. The company later fails to fulfill its export obligation within the prescribed period. The customs authority demands recovery of the customs duty exemption availed, while the GST department seeks reversal of ITC with interest under Section 50. The company argues that since ITC was availed on goods that were ultimately used in taxable supplies (domestic sales), reversal under Rule 42/43 is not warranted, and the EPCG default should be treated as a customs matter only. Analyze the correctness of this position, the applicable provisions for ITC reversal, the interest computation methodology, and whether the company can claim credit of customs duty paid upon regularization of the EPCG default against its GST liability.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A private limited company receives a corporate guarantee from its holding company for securing a bank loan. Post the Supreme Court ruling in the Ld. CIT v. Glaxo SmithKline Asia case and the 2023 amendments to Section 194H, the company is uncertain whether: (a) guarantee commission notionally attributed under transfer pricing constitutes a 'taxable supply' under Schedule II of the CGST Act thereby attracting GST, (b) whether the holding company providing the guarantee must register and charge GST even if it has no other taxable turnover, (c) how the arm's length price determined under Rule 10B of the Income Tax Rules interacts with the 'open market value' concept under Rule 27 of the CGST Rules for the same transaction, and (d) whether disallowance under Section 40A(2) for excessive guarantee commission paid (if any) creates a corresponding GST demand or refund entitlement. Provide a comprehensive legal analysis citing applicable circulars, advance rulings, and the prevailing judicial position.

title: This Notification amends certain notifications
text:
In the said notification,-

(a) in paragraph 1,-

(i)   clause (e) shall be omitted;

(ii)  in condition (5), for the figures "20%", the words "fifteen per cent." shall be substituted;

(iii) for conditions (7) and (8), the following conditions shall be substituted, namely:-

"(7) the manufacturer of the said goods follows the procedures contained in rules 11 and 20 of the Central
Excise Rules, 2002;

questions.question: Your production RAG pipeline ingests 108,000 chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings on a single Vulkan GPU achieving 5.2–9.6 chunks/second. During query time, you observe that semantic retrieval returns high-similarity chunks but LLM answers still hallucinate statutory cross-references (e.g., citing Section 16(4) when the correct provision is Section 16(2)(b)). The retrieval scores are above 0.82 cosine similarity. Diagnose the failure mode and propose a retrieval architecture change—without replacing the embedding model or LLM—that specifically addresses cross-reference hallucination in dense legal corpora where multiple provisions share overlapping terminology.

You operate a 4-core CPU host running: (a) a Vulkan llama-server for embeddings on GPU 5, (b) a ROCm llama-server for Qwen3-14B inference on GPU 2 at port 9082, (c) a FastAPI RAG service with async workers, and (d) periodic OCR jobs running at nice +19 ionice idle. Under sustained concurrent query load (8 simultaneous users), you observe GPU 2 VRAM pressure causing inference timeouts, while GPU 5 sits at 40% utilization. Propose a resource scheduling strategy—using only the existing hardware topology and open-source tooling already present—that dynamically rebalances inference load without requiring model reloading or service restarts.

title: Circular on Sabka Vishwas (Legacy Dispute Resolution) Scheme, 2019
text:
Department has accepted the disputed position. Section 129(2)(b) further provides

that issue of a discharge certificate does not prevent issuance of a show cause

notice for the same matter for a subsequent period or for a different matter in the 
same period. 
It is clarified that similar position will apply in case of Department also.

In other words, a declaration under this Scheme will not be a basis for assuming that

the declarant has admitted the position, and no fresh show cause notice will be 
issued merely on that basis.

(6) 
Section 125(1)(d) mentions that the Scheme is not available to an applicant 
who has been issued a show cause notice relating to refund or erroneous refund. 
It

has potential to lead to an interpretation that such persons will not be able to opt for

the Scheme for any other dispute as well, since the restriction is on ‘the person’ in

place of ‘the case’. 
It is clarified that the exception from eligibility is for ‘the case’ and

not ‘the person’. In other words, 
if a person has been issued a show cause notice for

a refund/erroneous refund and, 
at the same time, he also has other outstanding 
disputes which are covered under this Scheme, then, he will be eligible to file a

declaration(s) for the other case(s). Same position will apply to persons covered 
under Sections 125(1)(a), (b), (c), (e) and (g).

(c) 
This Scheme provides 
for adjustment of any amount paid 
as pre-deposit

during appellate proceedings or as deposit during enquiry, investigation 
or audit 
[Sections 124(2) and 130(2) refer]. 
In certain matters, tax may have been paid by

utilising the input credit, and the matter 
is under dispute. 
In such cases, the tax

already paid through input credit shall be adjusted by the Designated Committee at

the time of determination of the final amount payable under the Scheme.

(d) 
With respect to penalty/late fee matters [Section 124(1)(b) refers], 
a doubt has 
been expressed that only show cause notices for late fee or penalty are covered 
under this Scheme as there is no mention of appellate proceedings. 
It is clarified that

the provisions apply to any show cause notice for penalty/late fee, irrespective of the

fact that 
it is under adjudication or appeal. 
Moreover, there can be a show cause

notice that originally also involved a duty demand, and the amount of duty in the said

notice became ‘nil’ whether on account of the fact that same has been paid under

this Scheme or otherwise. Such cases are also covered under Section 124(1)(b).

(e) 
In case of appeals, the applicant is ineligible to apply 
if the final hearing 
is 
concluded but the order is awaited as on 30.06.2019. The hearings in matters are

typically rescheduled even after the final hearing due to new bench, change in officer

or any other reason. 
It is clarified that this restriction will apply to only those cases, 
where the appellate forum has heard the matter finally as on 30.06.2019.

(f) 
Section 125(g) excludes the cases where an application has been filed before

the 
Settlement 
Commission 
for 
settlement. 
However, 
in 
many 
such 
cases, 
proceedings before the Commission may abate due to reasons such as rejection of

the application by the Commission or due to order of the Commission not being

questions.question: A manufacturing company imports components under EPCG authorization, uses them to manufacture goods exported under LUT (Letter of Undertaking), but subsequently discovers that 15% of the exported consignment was rejected by the foreign buyer and re-imported within 6 months. The company had already claimed IGST refund on zero-rated exports. Analyze the cascading obligations under Customs (Import of Goods at Concessional Rate of Duty) Rules 2017, GST refund reversal mechanics under Rule 96 CGST Rules, and whether the re-import triggers fresh EPCG export obligation recalculation or only proportionate duty demand under Section 143 of the Customs Act — citing specific circulars or notifications that govern each leg.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A private limited company receives a slump sale consideration of ₹80 crore for its manufacturing undertaking. Post-sale, GST authorities issue a notice arguing that the slump sale constitutes a 'supply of bundle of services' because the transferred assets include intangibles (brand license, non-compete covenant, assembled workforce) whose value exceeds the tangible asset value — relying on Schedule II para 5(e) read with Section 7(1)(c) CGST Act. Simultaneously, the Income Tax department applies Section 50B for capital gains computation. Construct the taxpayer's complete defense strategy including: (a) how to rebut the GST characterization using Schedule III entry 7 and recent AAR/AAAR rulings, (b) whether any GST paid under protest is deductible under Section 37 or 43B of the Income Tax Act, and (c) the limitation period exposure if the transfer was executed 3 years ago.

Explanation.—For the purposes of this section,—

(a) "Global Depository Receipts" means any instrument in the form of a depository

receipt or certificate (by whatever name called) created by the Overseas Depository
Bank outside India and issued to non-resident investors against the issue of ordinary
shares or Foreign Currency Convertible Bonds of issuing company;

(b) "information technology service" means any service which results from the

use of any information technology software over a system of information technology
products for realising value addition;

(c) "information technology software" means any representation of instructions,

readable form and capable of being manipulated or providing inter-activity to a user,
by means of an automatic data processing machine falling under heading information
technology products but does not include non-information technology products;

(d) "Overseas Depository Bank" means a bank authorised by the issuing company

to issue Global Depository Receipts against issue of Foreign Currency Convertible
Bonds or ordinary shares of the issuing company.'.

questions.question: A manufacturing company has three distinct GST registrations across different states. During an audit, the central GST authority discovers that Input Tax Credit (ITC) was claimed on interstate procurement of capital goods under one GSTIN, but the corresponding capital goods were physically transferred to a factory under a different GSTIN in another state without raising a stock transfer invoice. The company argues this was an internal reallocation and not a supply. Analyze the legal validity of the ITC claim under Section 16 read with Section 18(6) of the CGST Act, the applicable reversal mechanism under Rule 44A, and whether the stock transfer constitutes a 'supply' under Schedule I Entry 2, considering that both entities share the same PAN but have distinct GSTINs.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a multinational group provides software development services to its foreign parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked at cost+15% using the TNMM method. The Transfer Pricing Officer proposes an adjustment arguing that the subsidiary functionally qualifies as a 'limited risk' entity and should be benchmarked against full-fledged software product companies, citing comparables with operating margins of 25-40%. Simultaneously, the subsidiary has claimed deduction under Section 10AA for its SEZ unit on the same income. Evaluate: (a) the correctness of the TPO's functional characterization, (b) whether an upward TP adjustment to notional income can interact with Section 10AA deduction eligibility under the Explanation to Section 10AA(7), and (c) whether MAP under the India-parent-country DTAA can be invoked concurrently with a pending Section 144C DRP proceeding.

questions.question: A registered supplier provides a composite supply where the principal supply is transportation of goods (exempt under Notification 12/2017-CT(R)), but the ancillary supply includes loading/unloading and temporary warehousing at the destination. The recipient is an unregistered person. Determine: (a) whether the composite supply retains its exemption or becomes taxable due to ancillary elements, (b) the applicable place of supply under Section 12(8) vs 12(14) of IGST Act, and (c) whether reverse charge applies given the recipient's unregistered status, citing the specific notification entry and any relevant AAR/AAAR rulings.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: A wholly-owned Indian subsidiary (Newco) acquires a 74% stake in a domestic target from its foreign parent (HoldCo) at a price determined by DCF valuation. The transaction triggers: (i) Section 56(2)(x) scrutiny on the 26% minority discount applied, (ii) a potential deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(e) due to inter-company receivables exceeding accumulated profits threshold, and (iii) TP documentation requirements under Section 92C since HoldCo is an AE. Walk through the order of analysis, identify which provision takes precedence where they conflict, and specify which Form (3CEB, 56F, etc.) and transfer pricing method (CUP/TNMM/CPS) would be most defensible before the TPO given the absence of comparable uncontrolled transactions in the Indian M&A market.

questions.question: A registered manufacturer in Maharashtra exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) to a SEZ unit in Gujarat. The invoice is raised in USD, but due to a banking delay, the foreign exchange is realized 14 months after the date of export. Meanwhile, the taxpayer had claimed a refund of accumulated ITC under Rule 89(4). The jurisdictional officer is now proposing to treat the supply as a domestic supply (not zero-rated) and recover the refund with 24% interest under Section 50. Is the officer's action legally sustainable? What are the taxpayer's remedies under the CGST Act and FEMA, and how does the RBI's condonation framework interact with the zero-rated status of the supply?

An Indian subsidiary imports specialised intermediates from its German parent under a long-term supply agreement. The declared transaction value is accepted by Customs for three consecutive years. In year four, the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) under the Income Tax Act makes an upward TP adjustment on the same transactions, deeming the import price too high (ALP lower than invoice price). Simultaneously, the DGTR initiates an anti-dumping investigation on the same product category and provisionally determines a landed value lower than the declared import price. How should the importer reconcile (a) the Customs valuation already accepted under Rule 3 of CVR 2007, (b) the TPO's downward ALP determination, and (c) the DGTR's provisional normal value — and what is the correct legal hierarchy among these three parallel determinations for the purpose of computing final Customs duty liability and income tax deductions?

title: EOUs/EPZ Units---CVD on DTA sale of plastic granules manuf. out of plastic scrap & waste
text:
Circular No.38/2000-Cus

F.NO. 305/193/97-FTT

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

New Delhi, the 10th, May, 2000

Subject: EOUs/EPZ Units---CVD on DTA sale of plastic granules manuf. out of plastic scrap & waste

It is directed to invite your attention on the above subject and to state that a doubt has been raised regarding levy of additional
duty of customs (CVD) on DTA sale of reprocessed plastic agglomerates/granules (reprocessed out of plastic scrap) by
EOU/EPZ units. The doubt has arisen in relation to exemption for plastic materials falling under heading Nos. 39.01 to 39.14
under notification Nos. 5/98-CE, dated 2.6.98 (ref: S.No. 63), 5/99-CE, dated 28.2.99 (ref: S.No. 64) and 6/2000-CE, dated
1.3.2000 (ref: S.No. 63).

2.        The matter has been examined. The aforesaid notifications exempt plastic materials falling under heading Nos. 39.01 to
39.14 from excise duty, subject to the condition that they are reprocessed in India out of the scrap or the waste of goods falling
within Chapters 39, 54, 55, 56, 59, 64, 84, 85, 86, 87, 90, 91, 92, 93, 94, 95, and 96. It may be seen that the notification enjoins
fulfilment of two conditions for availing of the exemption: (I) reprocessing of plastic scrap material should be done in India; (ii)
plastic scrap or waste should be of the goods falling within the above mentioned Chapters of the Customs Tariff.

3.        In so far as levy of additional duty of customs (CVD) on imports of reprocessed plastic materials (viz. Plastic
granules/agglomerates) is concerned, condition (I) above will not be satisfied because, the reprocessing has not been done in
India. Thus, in case of normal imports of reprocessed plastic materials, the importers/domestic manufacturers are liable to pay,
among other duties, additional duty of customs (CVD). It, therefore, follows that no CVD exemption will be available when
reprocessed plastic materials are cleared in Domestic Tariff Area (DTA) by EOUs and EPZ units. In other words, the plastic
processor EOUs/EPZ units are liable to pay, among other duties, excise duty equivalent to CVD payable on imported
reprocessed plastic materials (e.g. plastic granules/agglomerates) in respect of their DTA clearances of such materials/goods.

4.        In view of the position explained above, you are requested to kindly review the DTA clearances made by plastic
processor EOUs/EPZ units to satisfy yourself as to whether the Customs authorities in charge of EOUs/EPZ units have taken
the said CVD into account in their computation of excise duty while allowing DTA clearance of reprocessed plastic materials
and initiate action to recover short levy, if any, urgently. The details of such short levy and recovery, if any, may be furnished to
the Board by 25 th May, 2000.

5.        Difficulty, if any in implementation of the above instructions may kindly be brought to the notice of the Board.

6.        These instructions supercede the individual clarifications issued earlier by the Board.

questions.question: A manufacturing company imports raw materials under an advance authorization license and later converts its factory into an SEZ unit mid-year. The imported inputs have already been partially consumed in DTA production. How should the company apportion and reverse ITC claimed under IGST on the consumed inputs, and what are the obligations under the Foreign Trade Policy regarding export obligation fulfillment for the remaining inputs now housed in the SEZ, given that SEZ supplies are treated as deemed exports under GST but zero-rated under the SEZ Act?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a foreign MNC provides software development services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement. During the assessment year, the subsidiary also sub-contracts 40% of the work to an unrelated third party at arm's length. The TPO proposes to benchmark the subsidiary as a routine service provider using TNMM, but the assessee argues that the sub-contracting arrangement creates a pass-through cost that inflates the cost base and distorts the PLI. What adjustments to the net cost base should be made under Rule 10B(1)(e) to isolate the value-added contribution of the subsidiary, and how does the recent SC ruling on economic ownership of functions affect the FAR analysis in this case?

title: DFCEC Scheme for Service Providers - whether import of all capital goods which are other than professional equipment or office equipment is also permissible - reg.
text:
Circular No. 98 /2003-Cus.

18 th November, 2003

F.NO.605/1/2003-DBK

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Sub : DFCEC Scheme for Service Providers - whether import of all capital goods which are other than professional
equipment or office equipment is also permissible - reg.

In terms of Para 3.8 of the EXIM Policy, Duty Free Credit Entitlement Certificate (DFCEC) for Service Providers has been
notified.  The said Para 3.8 of EXIM Policy permits import of spares, office equipments and furniture, professional equipments
and consumables other than agriculture and dairy products.

2.         Commissioner of Customs (Export), ACC, Sahar had raised an issue whether under DFCEC Scheme, import of all
capital goods including professional equipments could be allowed.  This doubt has also been created on account of usage of
the words "capital goods" in condition (3) of Customs Notification No.54/2003.

3.         This issue has been examined in consultation with DGFT/MOC. DGFT have confirmed that the objective of DFCEC
Scheme for Service Providers is to permit import of aforesaid goods with a view to increase the capability of the service
providers so as to enable him to render a better and efficient service.  With this in mind import of professional equipments
which are required in the profession of the service providers has been allowed.  However, in so far as capital goods are
concerned, its import to service provider has already been allowed through EPCG route.  Therefore, in so far as DFCEC
Scheme is concerned, under the category of professional equipments, import of only those equipments would be permissible
under DFCEC Scheme, which are Professional Equipments required by the Service Provider for the purpose of rendering
service and earning free foreign exchange.  It is reiterated that import of capital goods which are other than professional
equipment or office equipment shall not be allowed under DFCEC Scheme for Service Providers.  In order to remove doubts,
the words "capital goods" used in condition (3) of 54/2003-Cus. dated 1.4.2003 has also been corrected to read as
"professional equipment" by issue of corrigendum (copy enclosed).

4.         Suitable Public Notice for Trade and Standing Order for the guidance of customs field formations may be issued.

5.         Receipt of this Circular may please be acknowledged.

R.K. Talajia

Officer on Special Duty (DBK)

questions.question: Your CBIC RAG system is returning semantically correct but legally imprecise answers — for example, citing a 2018 circular that was superseded by a 2022 amendment. The embedding model (BGE-M3) has no temporal awareness, and the chunks don't consistently include document date metadata. Describe a multi-layer retrieval strategy that (a) surfaces recency signals without re-embedding the entire 108k-chunk corpus, (b) handles cases where the superseding document was ingested but the superseded one wasn't tagged as obsolete, and (c) degrades gracefully when recency metadata is missing.

You are running two concurrent inference workloads on a 4-core rig: llama-server (Vulkan, GPU 4/6) for OCR-heavy document understanding with Qwen2.5-VL-7B, and a separate qwen3-14b instance (ROCm, GPU 2) serving live RAG queries. Under peak load, OCR jobs cause GPU memory pressure that delays RAG query responses beyond acceptable latency. Without adding hardware, design a resource governance scheme that (a) prioritizes RAG query SLA over batch OCR throughput, (b) prevents VRAM OOM without killing either process, and (c) can be implemented using only process-level controls available on a Linux system with no container orchestration.

questions.question: Your production RAG system serves 108k chunks of Indian tax law with BGE-M3 embeddings on a 4-core machine. During peak load, p95 query latency spikes from 1.2s to 18s. CPU `uptime` shows load average 12.4. You have: (a) Qdrant running with HNSW ef=128, (b) a Qwen3-14B LLM on GPU via Vulkan llama-server, (c) FastAPI with sync retrieval handlers, (d) no query result cache. Describe your exact remediation sequence — what you change first, why, what metrics you check before moving to the next phase, and what failure modes you watch for at each step.

You are ingesting a 471-document corpus of scanned Indian tax circulars into Qdrant. 40% of PDFs are image-only (no text layer). Your embedding throughput is 5.2–9.6 chunks/sec on a single GPU using BGE-M3 Vulkan. You need to decide between: (A) CPU Tesseract OCR pre-pass → embed text, (B) Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF multimodal LLM on llama-server for OCR, or (C) RapidOCR CPU as fallback with LLM OCR for low-confidence pages. Compare all three on: throughput impact, accuracy on dense tabular GST rate schedules, GPU VRAM contention with the concurrent embedding process, and operational complexity. Justify a final architecture choice with quantitative reasoning.

title: Seeks to notify the esident firm as the class of persons for the purpose of sub-clause (iii) of clause (b) of section 96A of the Finance Act, 1994.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, 
SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)] 
 
GOVERNMENT OF INDIA  
MINISTRY OF FINANCE  
(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE) 
 
NOTIFICATION  
No. 9/2015 - Service Tax 
 
New Delhi, the 1st March, 2015

G.S.R.   (E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-clause (iii) of clause (b) of 
section 96A of the Finance Act, 1994 (32 of 1994), the Central Government hereby specifies 
“resident firm” as class of persons for the purposes of the said sub-clause.

Explanation. - For the purposes of this notification,-

(a) 
“firm” shall have the meaning assigned to it in section 4 of the Indian Partnership Act, 
1932 (9 of 1932) , and includes-

(i) the limited liability partnership as defined in clause (n) of sub-section (1) of 
the section 2 of the Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008 (6 of 2009); or

(ii) limited liability partnership which has no company as its partner; or

(iii) the sole proprietorship; or

(iv)  One Person Company.

(b) 
(i)  “sole proprietorship” means an individual who engages himself in an  activity as 
defined in sub-clause (a) of section 96A of the Finance Act, 1994.

(ii)  “One Person Company” means as defined in clause (62) of section 2 of the 
Companies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013).

(c) 
“resident” shall have the meaning assigned to it in clause (42) of section 2 of the 
Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) in so far as it applies to a resident firm. 
 
[F. No. 334/5/2015-TRU]  
 
 
 
(Akshay Joshi)  
Under Secretary to the Government of India

questions.question: A multinational company has a wholly-owned Indian subsidiary that provides IT-enabled services exclusively to its foreign parent. The subsidiary claims zero-rated export of services under Section 16 of the IGST Act and files for GST refund. The tax authority issues a show-cause notice arguing that since the parent and subsidiary are 'related persons' under Schedule I of the CGST Act, the supply cannot qualify as 'export of services' as it does not satisfy the condition of consideration being received in convertible foreign exchange under Section 2(6)(iv) of the IGST Act — citing that intra-group recharges do not constitute arm's length consideration. Simultaneously, the authority invokes Rule 28 of the CGST Rules to demand GST on the 'open market value' of these services. Analyze: (a) whether the related-party nature of the transaction negates the 'export of services' classification, (b) the interaction between Schedule I deemed supply provisions and the IGST Act's export definition, (c) the validity of applying Rule 28 valuation when the supply is otherwise zero-rated, and (d) the correct legal remedy and forum for the taxpayer.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian company ('ICo') holds a 60% stake in a foreign subsidiary ('FSub') incorporated in a jurisdiction with which India has a DTAA. FSub has accumulated losses carried forward over 7 years. ICo transfers its entire shareholding in FSub to an unrelated third-party buyer at a price determined by a DCF valuation that ascribes zero value to FSub's IP (on the grounds that the IP was internally generated and not recognized on FSub's books). The Indian Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) contends: (i) the IP should have been valued using the CUT/CUP method referencing comparable royalty-bearing licenses, (ii) the capital gains computation must be reopened under Section 147 read with Section 92CA since the transaction involved an Associated Enterprise, and (iii) the 'look-through' provisions under Section 9(1)(i) apply because FSub's assets are predominantly Indian-situated. The taxpayer argues treaty protection under the Capital Gains Article of the DTAA (residence-state taxation) and challenges the TPO's jurisdiction to revalue shares of a foreign company. Critically evaluate: (a) the jurisdictional limits of the TPO in recharacterizing a share sale of a foreign entity, (b) the interaction of Section 92 transfer pricing adjustments with DTAA Capital Gains Articles, (c) whether Section 9(1)(i) 'indirect transfer' provisions can override treaty protection post the 2012 retrospective amendment, and (d) the taxpayer's strongest appellate arguments before the ITAT and High Court.

questions.question: A holding company incorporated in India provides corporate guarantee to a foreign lender securing an ECB loan taken by its wholly-owned subsidiary. The subsidiary defaults; the lender invokes the guarantee and recovers from the holding company. The holding company subsequently recovers the amount from the subsidiary under a subrogation deed. Analyze the GST implications at each step: (i) the initial corporate guarantee, (ii) the invocation and payment, (iii) the subrogation recovery. Identify the applicable SAC codes, valuation rules under Rule 28 vs. Rule 32, place-of-supply determination under Section 13(8)(b) IGST Act, and whether the export-of-services exemption under Section 16 IGST applies to any leg. Also address whether CBIC Circular 204/16/2023-GST modifies the pre-existing position for guarantees issued prior to October 2023.

An Indian importer purchases specialty chemicals from its AE (Associated Enterprise) in Germany. Customs values the goods under Rule 4 (transaction value). Simultaneously, the TPO rejects the CUP method and applies TNMM, determining an arm's-length price 18% lower than the invoice price, resulting in a transfer pricing addition. Concurrently, DGTR initiates an anti-dumping investigation on the same product from Germany and provisionally assesses duty on a constructed normal value that is 30% above the customs transaction value. Analyze: (i) whether the transfer pricing adjustment creates a legal obligation to revise the customs valuation, given the conflict between Customs Act Section 14, Rule 10(1)(e) CVR, and OECD-aligned TP methodology; (ii) how provisional anti-dumping duty interacts with Rule 4 acceptance; (iii) the evidentiary and procedural strategy before the Customs Valuation Authority and ITAT to avoid double jeopardy where the same transaction is simultaneously over-valued (customs) and under-valued (TP).

questions.question: A manufacturing company has three distinct business verticals operating under a single GSTIN: (1) a factory producing exempt goods, (2) a trading division selling taxable goods, and (3) a service arm providing works contract services. During the financial year, the company received a common input service (professional consulting) used across all three verticals. The supplier charged 18% IGST. Explain in detail how the company must compute its eligible ITC under the pro-rata apportionment formula under Rule 42/43 of the CGST Rules, identify which portion becomes ineligible under Section 17(2), determine whether the works contract services create any additional ITC restriction under Section 17(5)(c), and state what reversal entries must be posted in GSTR-3B and GSTR-9 if the provisional reversal computed in monthly returns differs materially from the final annual calculation.

A domestic company is engaged in software development and claims deduction under Section 10AA for its SEZ unit. During the assessment year, the Assessing Officer proposes to invoke Section 92C transfer pricing provisions, alleging that the unit's export receipts from its foreign associated enterprise are below arm's length price, and simultaneously invokes Section 80-IA(10) on the ground that the inter-unit arrangement produces more than ordinary profits. Analyse whether both provisions can be applied concurrently to the same transactions, explain the hierarchy of adjustments if both apply, detail the impact on the Section 10AA deduction computation if the ALP adjustment increases the unit's profits (specifically whether the enhanced profit feeds back into the exemption or is taxed at full rate), and identify what safe harbour rules under Rule 10TD could the company invoke to preempt this dispute and under what conditions those safe harbours would be unavailable.

questions.question: Your dense retrieval pipeline (BGE-M3 embeddings, Qdrant, cosine similarity) consistently surfaces the correct statutory section in top-3 for well-formed queries, but fails for practitioner queries phrased as procedural exceptions — e.g., 'What happens to ITC reversal under Rule 42 when the supplier is subsequently cancelled retrospectively?' Sparse BM25 also fails because the key terms are distributed across three separate sections. Design a retrieval architecture that handles this without fine-tuning the embedding model, specifying: (a) the chunking and metadata schema changes required, (b) the query decomposition strategy and how sub-query results are merged, (c) how you prevent context window bloat when assembling the final prompt, and (d) how you evaluate retrieval quality without ground-truth QA pairs.
evaluation_criteria: Correctly identifies multi-hop retrieval as the core problem
Proposes hybrid retrieval with query decomposition (not just re-ranking)
Addresses chunk boundary problem with section-aware overlap or hierarchical indexing
Specifies a concrete merging strategy (e.g., reciprocal rank fusion, cross-encoder re-rank)
Addresses context budget with compression or map-reduce summarization
Proposes unsupervised eval (e.g., RAGAS faithfulness + answer relevancy without labels)

questions.question: You are running a 14B parameter quantized LLM (Q4_K_M GGUF) on a 4-core CPU-bottlenecked host with two AMD RX 6700 XT GPUs sharing a PCIe x4 slot. At peak load (8 concurrent RAG queries), p95 TTFT is 18 seconds and throughput collapses to 0.4 tokens/sec per request. Profiling shows: GPU utilization 34%, CPU at 100% (KV cache management + tokenization), PCIe bandwidth at 71% saturation. The model does NOT fit in a single GPU's 12GB VRAM. Prescribe a concrete remediation plan that addresses all three bottlenecks simultaneously, including: (a) specific llama.cpp / llama-server flags and their rationale, (b) how you split layers across GPUs given the PCIe constraint, (c) what you offload to CPU and why, and (d) what concurrency model change at the API layer reduces head-of-line blocking without adding hardware.
evaluation_criteria: Correctly diagnoses PCIe as the primary bottleneck (not GPU compute)
Proposes tensor-parallel or layer-split strategy with specific --tensor-split rationale
Identifies KV cache as CPU bottleneck and proposes --cache-type-k / --cache-type-v flags or Flash Attention
Recommends continuous batching over naive request queuing
Does not recommend naive GPU layer increase without accounting for PCIe bandwidth cost

title: Handling of Cargo in Customs Areas Regulations, 2009
text:
Handling of Cargo in Customs Areas Regulations, 2009

[ Notification No. 26/2009-Cus. (N.T.) , dated 17-3-2009, as amended by Corrigendum F. No. 450/55/2008-Cus-IV, dated 2-04-
2009, Notification No. 96/2010- Cus. (N.T.) , dated 12-11-2010, 43/2012-Cus. (N.T.) , dated 16-05-2012, 104/2012-Cus. (N.T.),
dated 16-11-2012 115,2016-Cus.(N.T.) dated 26-08-2016, 24/2017-Cus.(N.T.) dated 31.03.2017, 29/2019-Cus.(N.T.) dated
01.04.2019]

In exercise of powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 141 read with section 157 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962),
the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs hereby makes the following regulations, namely:-

Regulation 1. Short title and commencement.-

(1) These regulations may be called the Handling of Cargo in Customs Areas (Amendment ) Regulations, 2009.

(2) They shall come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.

Regulation 2. Definitions.-

(1) In these regulations, unless the context otherwise requires, -

(a) "Act" means the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962);

(b) "Customs Cargo Services provider" means any person responsible for receipt, storage, delivery, dispatch or otherwise
handling of imported goods and export goods and includes a custodian as referred to in section 45 of the Act and persons
as referred to in sub-section (2) of section 141 of the said Act;

(c) "specified" means specified by a notification or an order issued under the provisions of the Act;

(2) the words and expressions used herein and not defined but defined in the Act shall have the same meanings respectively
assigned to them in the Act.

Regulation 3. Application.-

These regulations shall be applicable to the handling of imported and export goods in 1 [ports, airports, inland container depots,
land customs stations and in customs areas approved or specified under section 8 ].

1. Corrected by M.F. (D.R.) Corrigendum F. No. 450/55/2008-Cus. -IV, dated 2-4-2009.

Regulation 4. Retrospective Application.-

Any action taken or anything done in respect of appointment of Customs Cargo Service providers, immediately preceding the
coming into force of these regulations, shall be deemed to have been done under the corresponding provisions of these
regulations. Customs Cargo Service providers already approved on or before the date of coming into force of these regulations
shall comply with the conditions of these regulations within a period of three months or such period not exceeding a period of
one year as the 1 [Principal Commissioner of Customs or Commissioner of Customs, as the case may be] may allow from the
date of coming into force of these Regulations.

1. Substituted by Notification No. 56/2014-Cus. (N.T.) , dated 6-8-2014.

questions.question: A manufacturing company imports raw materials under Advance Authorization and later re-exports finished goods. During an audit, the department alleges that the IGST exemption claimed at import is invalid because the export obligation was fulfilled through deemed exports to an EOU rather than physical exports. The company argues that deemed exports qualify under the FTP. Simultaneously, the Input Tax Credit availed on domestic inputs used in the manufacture of the exported goods is being disputed on the ground that the final supply was 'zero-rated' and the ITC should have been claimed only via refund route, not utilized for offsetting domestic liability. Analyze the interplay between Rule 96(10), Rule 89(4), and the Advance Authorization scheme, and state whether the company's dual position is legally tenable.

A multinational group routes a cross-border intra-group service transaction where an Indian subsidiary pays a management fee to its Singapore parent. Customs authorities invoke Rule 10(1)(e) of the Customs Valuation Rules to add the management fee to the transaction value of imported goods from the same parent, arguing it is a condition of sale. The Income Tax Transfer Pricing officer separately benchmarks the management fee using TNMM and accepts it as arm's length. The assessee argues that (a) the TP acceptance creates res judicata against customs re-characterization, and (b) Rule 10(1)(e) requires a direct nexus between the fee and the specific goods imported, which is absent here. Evaluate the strength of both arguments under Indian law, citing the Customs Act, the Customs Valuation Rules, and relevant tribunal precedents.

title: Prohibits the import of specified  goods intended for sale or use in India, subject to certain conditions and procedures
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)].

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRTY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No. 51/2010 - Customs (N.T.)

New Delhi, dated  the 30 th June, 2010

9 Asadha, 1932(SAKA)

G.S.R.___________ - In exercise of the powers conferred by section 11 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962) , and in
supersession of the notification No. 49/2007-Customs (N.T.), of the Government of India, Ministry of Finance, Department of
Revenue, Central Board of Excise and Customs, dated the 8 th May, 2007 published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part
II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 333(E), dated the 8 th May, 2007, except as respects things done or omitted
to be done before such supersession, the Central Government, being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to
do, for the purposes specified in clauses (n) and (u) of  sub-section (2) of that section, hereby prohibits the import of the
following goods intended for sale or use in India, subject to following conditions  and procedures as  specified in the Intellectual
Property Rights (Imported Goods) Enforcement Rules, 2007, namely:-

(i)      goods having applied  thereto  a  false  trade mark as specified in  section 102 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 (47 of
1999);

(ii)      goods having applied there to a false trade description within the meaning of clause (i) of sub-section (1) of section 2 of
the Trade Marks Act, 1999(47 of 1999), otherwise than in relation to any of the matters specified in sub- clauses (ii) and (iii) of
clause (za) of that sub-section;

(iii)     goods made or produced beyond the limits of India and having applied thereto a design in which copyright exists under
the Designs Act, 2000(16 of 2000), in respect of the class to which the goods belong or any fraudulent or obvious imitation of
such design except when the application of such design has been made with the licence or written consent of the registered
proprietor of the design or where such importation or use is allowed under the Designs Act, 2000(16 of 2000);

(iv)     the product made or produced beyond the limits of India for which a patent is in force under the Patents Act,1970 (39 of
1970), except in cases where the consent from the patentee in India has been obtained provided that such prohibition  is not
applicable to the cases where such importation is allowed under the Patents Act,1970(39 of 1970);

(v)      the product obtained directly by the process made or produced beyond the limits of India where patent for such process
is in force under the Patents Act,1970 (39 of 1970), except in cases where the consent from the patentee in India has been
obtained provided that such prohibition is not applicable to the case where such importation is allowed under the Patents
Act,1970 (39 of 1970);

(vi)     goods having applied thereto a false Geographical Indication within the  meaning of section 38 of the Geographical
Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999 (48 of 1999);

(vii)   goods which are  prohibited to be imported by issuance of  an order issued  by the Registrar of Copyrights under section
53 of the Copyright Act,1957 (14 of 1957).

Explanation - For the purposes of this notification, the terms and expressions used in various clauses of the notification shall
have the meanings assigned to them in the respective Acts, namely, the Trade Marks Act, 1999(47 of 1999), the Designs Act,
2000(16 of 2000), the Patents Act,1970 (39 of 1970), the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act,
1999 (48 of 1999) and  the Copyright Act,1957 (14 of 1957).

questions.question: Your Qdrant-backed RAG system ingests 108k chunks of Indian tax law using BGE-M3 embeddings at ~7 chunks/sec on a single Vulkan GPU. During peak query load, you observe that retrieval latency spikes from 120ms to 1.8s when concurrent users exceed 4. Profiling reveals 60% of spike time is in Qdrant HNSW graph traversal, 25% in embedding inference, and 15% in FastAPI overhead. You cannot add hardware. Walk through your diagnosis and optimization strategy, including which Qdrant collection parameters (ef, m, on_disk_payload) you would tune, how you would restructure the async FastAPI query pipeline to reduce contention, and what trade-offs in recall@10 you would accept.

You run a 4-core CPU rig with two AMD RX 6700 XT GPUs. GPU 1 hosts Qwen 3 14B via llama-server (Vulkan, port 9082) for RAG answer generation. GPU 2 runs BGE-M3 for embeddings. A new requirement demands concurrent OCR of 471 image-only PDFs using Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF, but loading it onto either GPU displaces the existing models. You observe that running OCR at CPU nice +19 / ionice idle causes context-switch storms that degrade GPU 1 inference latency by 40%. Describe your resource isolation strategy: how you would schedule OCR jobs relative to query traffic, whether you would use model swapping vs. persistent loading, what llama-server flags control VRAM headroom, and how you would instrument the system to detect and auto-throttle OCR when query p95 latency exceeds your SLA threshold.

questions.question: A manufacturer of pharmaceutical intermediates (HSN 2933) supplies the same product to two buyers: (a) a registered domestic formulation company under a tax invoice, and (b) a merchant exporter who furnishes Form CT-1 under Notification 41/2017-Central Tax (Rate). The manufacturer later discovers that 18% of the batch supplied under CT-1 was diverted to the domestic market by the merchant exporter. The jurisdictional officer issues a SCN under Section 74 CGST Act alleging that the manufacturer is liable for the tax short-paid on the diverted quantity, treating the transaction as a 'colourable device'. Analyze (i) whether the manufacturer's liability survives the export obligation breach by the merchant exporter, (ii) the applicability of extended limitation under Section 74 versus Section 73, (iii) the evidentiary threshold required to establish 'fraud or wilful misstatement' against the supplier in such a tripartite arrangement, and (iv) the available defenses under Section 74(5) and Rule 142(2) CGST Rules.

An Indian importer (wholly-owned subsidiary of a Japanese parent) imports specialized CNC machine components under Rule 3(3)(d) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, subject to a Special Valuation Branch (SVB) order fixing an ALP-based loading of 8.5% on the declared transaction value. The same transaction is simultaneously examined under Section 92C of the Income Tax Act by the TPO, who accepts the Comparable Uncontrolled Price (CUP) method and issues a nil adjustment order for the same AY. The importer petitions Customs to revise the SVB loading downward, citing the TPO order as corroborating evidence of arm's length pricing. The customs adjudicating authority rejects the petition. Evaluate (i) the legal inter-operability (or lack thereof) between the Customs SVB mechanism and the Income Tax transfer pricing framework for the same cross-border transaction, (ii) the evidentiary weight a TPO nil-adjustment order should carry before a SVB/Customs adjudicator, (iii) the applicable standard of review before the CESTAT on a question of valuation methodology, and (iv) whether the Tribunal's ruling in Ferodo India Pvt Ltd v. CC (2019) or the Supreme Court's observations in CC v. Aggarwal Industries (2011) provide binding guidance on this interface.
