questions.question: A manufacturer operates three distinct units under a single GSTIN: Unit A (taxable supply), Unit B (exempt supply), and Unit C (zero-rated export). During the quarter, common input services worth ₹18,00,000 (GST ₹3,24,000) were procured — of which 40% are exclusively attributable to Unit A, 25% to Unit B, and 35% are common to all three. Additionally, capital goods worth ₹50,00,000 (GST ₹9,00,000) were purchased and are used across all units in the ratio 3:1:2. The taxable turnover is ₹2.4 crore, exempt turnover ₹60 lakh, and export turnover ₹1.2 crore for the quarter. Calculate the admissible ITC, the amount to be reversed under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of the CGST Rules 2017, and determine whether a TRAN-1 adjustment is required if ₹45,000 of the common input service credit was carried forward from the pre-GST regime.
questions.source_section: Section 17(2) CGST Act; Rules 42 & 43 CGST Rules 2017

questions.question: IndoCo, an Indian company, holds 51% in SingCo (Singapore) and 49% in DIFC-Co (UAE). IndoCo licenses proprietary software to SingCo at ₹2.2 crore/year and receives management services from DIFC-Co at AED 8,00,000/year. The Transfer Pricing Officer benchmarks the software license using TNMM and concludes the arm's length price is ₹1.4 crore, while the management services are re-characterised as dividend under Section 92B(2). Simultaneously, the Assessing Officer invokes POEM (Place of Effective Management) to treat DIFC-Co as an Indian tax resident for AY 2025-26, triggering Section 6(3). Evaluate: (a) the transfer pricing adjustment quantum and penalty exposure under Section 271AA vs 270A, (b) whether POEM invocation creates a circular taxation issue on the management fee, (c) the treaty position under the India-UAE DTAA Article 9 and the applicability of the Principal Purpose Test under MLI Article 7 to the SingCo structure, and (d) the MAP recourse timeline under Article 25 if both treaty claims are contested simultaneously.
expected_skills: DTAA Article 9 interaction with domestic TP adjustment
questions.source_section: Sections 6(3), 92–92F Income Tax Act; POEM Guidelines 2017; MLI Articles 7 & 25; India-UAE DTAA

title: Application for refund by Canteen Stores Department

questions.question: Your CBIC RAG system serves 108k chunks of Indian tax law via Qdrant. During peak usage, you observe that query latency spikes from 800ms to 12s intermittently. CPU uptime shows load average of 3.8 on a 4-core rig. Qdrant reports p99 vector search at 120ms. The qwen3-14b LLM via llama-server Vulkan shows GPU utilization at 95% during spikes. Your FastAPI server uses async endpoints. Describe the exact diagnostic sequence you would follow to isolate whether the bottleneck is: (a) CPU thread starvation from concurrent async workers, (b) GPU VRAM contention between Qdrant HNSW index and llama-server KV cache, or (c) BGE-M3 embedding re-computation on cache miss. For each hypothesis, specify the exact command, metric threshold, and remediation.

You are ingesting a 471-document corpus where 40% are image-only PDFs (no text layer). Your proven pipeline achieves 5.2–9.6 chunks/second for text PDFs using single GPU Vulkan BGE-M3. For the image-only subset, you must choose between: (A) Qwen2.5-VL-7B GGUF on llama-server Vulkan for OCR, (B) RapidOCR on CPU, or (C) a two-pass hybrid routing documents by confidence score. Given your rig has 4 CPU cores, 2 RX 6700 XT GPUs already loaded with qwen3-14b and BGE-M3, and the requirement that OCR must not block the main ingestion queue, design the exact process isolation strategy, GPU memory budget allocation (in GB), and queue architecture that allows concurrent OCR + embedding without VRAM OOM. Specify the nice/ionice values, worker counts, and the confidence threshold routing logic.

title: Seeks to notify the special procedure under section 148 of the CGST Act for rectification of demand orders issued for contravention of section 16(4) of the said Act.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION  3, SUB-

SECTION (ii)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

CENTRAL BOARD OF INDIRECT TAXES AND CUSTOMS

NOTIFICATION

No. 22/2024– CENTRAL TAX

New Delhi, dated the 8th October, 2024.

S.O....(E).— In exercise of the powers conferred under the section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax 
Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) (hereinafter referred to as the said Act), the Central Government, on the 
recommendations of the Council, hereby notifies the following special procedure for rectification of order, to be 
followed by the class of registered persons (hereinafter referred to as the said person), against whom any order 
under section 73 or section 74 or section 107 or section 108 of the said Act has been issued confirming demand 
for wrong availment of input tax credit, on account of  contravention of provisions of sub-section (4) of section 
16 of the said Act, but where such input tax credit is now available as per the provisions of  sub-section (5) or 
sub-section (6) of section 16 of the said Act, and where appeal against the said order has not been filed, 
namely:–

2. 
The said person shall file, electronically on the common portal, within a period of six months from the

date of issuance of this notification, an application for rectification of an order issued under section 73 or section 
74 or section 107 or section 108 of the said Act, as the case may be, confirming demand for wrong availment of 
input tax credit, on account of  contravention of provisions of sub-section (4) of section 16 of the said Act, but 
where such input tax credit is now available as per the provisions of  sub-section (5) or sub-section (6) of section 
16 of the said Act, and where appeal against the said order has not been filed.

3. 
The said person shall, along with the said application, upload the information in the proforma in

Annexure A of this notification.

4. 
The proper officer for carrying out rectification of the said order shall be the authority who had issued

such order, and the said authority shall take a decision on the said application and issue the rectified order, as far 
as possible, within a period of three months from the date of the said application.

5.  
Where any rectification is required to be made in the order referred to in paragraph 1 and, the said

authority has issued a rectified order thereof, then the said authority shall upload a summary of the rectified 
order electronically –

(i)  in FORM GST DRC-08, in cases where rectification of an order issued under section 73 or section

74 of the said Act is made; and

(ii)  in FORM GST APL-04, in cases where rectification of an order issued under section 107 or section

108 of the said Act is made.

6. 
The rectification is required to be made only in respect of demand of such input tax credit which has

been alleged to be wrongly availed in contravention of provisions of sub-section (4) of section 16 of the said 
Act, but where such input tax credit is now available as per the provisions of sub-section (5) or sub-section (6) 
of the said section 16.

7. 
Where such rectification adversely affects the said person, the principles of natural justice shall be

followed by the authority carrying out such rectification.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates through three distinct legal entities: Entity A (Gujarat) supplies exempt goods, Entity B (Maharashtra) supplies taxable goods, and Entity C (Delhi) is an ISD (Input Service Distributor). Common input services are procured centrally by Entity C and distributed to A and B. Entity A's exempt supply ratio is 65%. Entity B receives both directly procured inputs and ISD credits. During an audit, the officer disallows the ISD credit distributed to Entity A citing Rule 39 read with Section 17(5), and also demands reversal under Rule 42 from Entity B for the proportion attributable to exempt supplies serviced by Entity A. Is the officer's dual-disallowance legally sustainable? Analyze the interplay between Section 17(2), Rule 42, Rule 43, and Rule 39 of CGST Rules, 2017, citing relevant AAR/AAAR rulings and any conflicting High Court positions.

An Indian importer purchases goods from a related foreign supplier under a long-term supply agreement. The transaction value is accepted by customs for three consecutive years. In year four, the foreign supplier restructures its transfer pricing policy under OECD arm's length guidelines, resulting in a retroactive price adjustment (upward) for the prior year's shipments, documented via a debit note. The importer voluntarily files a revised bill of entry and pays differential duty. The customs officer then issues a SCN demanding: (i) interest under Section 28AA on the differential duty from the date of original assessment, (ii) penalties under Section 114A, and (iii) re-opening of the prior three years' assessments under Section 28(1). Critically evaluate the legal merits of each demand under the Customs Act, 1962, with reference to the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, CBIC Circular 38/2016-Customs, and the Supreme Court's ruling in Eicher Tractors Ltd. v. Commissioner of Customs.

title: Changes introduced through the Customs (Import of Goods at Concessional Rate of Duty) Amendment Rules, 2021- reg.
text:
Circular No.10/2021-Customs

F.No. 450/28/2016-Cus-IV

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs)

*****

North Block, New Delhi.

Dt: 17th of  May, 2021

To,

All Principal Chief Commissioners/ Chief Commissioners of Customs/ Customs 
(Preventive), 
All Principal Chief Commissioners/ Chief Commissioners of Customs & Central tax, 
All Principal Commissioners/ Commissioners of Customs/ Customs (Preventive), 
All Principal Commissioners/ Commissioners of Customs & Central tax,

Madam/Sir,

Subject: Changes introduced through the Customs (Import of Goods at Concessional 
Rate of Duty) Amendment Rules, 2021- reg.

Reference is drawn to the Customs (Import of Goods at Concessional Rate of Duty)

Amendment Rules, 2021 notified vide Notification No. 09/2021-Customs (N.T.), dated 
02.02.2021 so as to make certain amendments in existing Customs (Import of Goods at 
Concessional Rate of Duty) Rules, 2017 (hereinafter referred to as “IGCR Rules, 2017”) that 
took effect from 2nd February, 2021.

2.   
The aforementioned Amendment Rules have been introduced in view of the demands

from the trade and industry and having regard to their changing needs as per prevalent global 
practices. The amendments are also an effort towards creating an enabling environment for 
the promoting manufacturing by domestic industry to make them competitive globally and 
also make them self-reliant in furtherance of the goal of Atmanirbhar Bharat.

3.  
The major changes brought in vide the said Amendment Rules, 2021, are highlighted

as below:

Job Work:

3.1 
The facility of carrying out job work under the ambit of IGCR has been introduced.

3.2 The scope of the job work facility has been extended to an importer who is a

manufacturer but without complete manufacturing facility. Also, 100% out-
sourcing for manufacture of goods on job-work basis has been permitted for 
importers who do not have any manufacturing facility at all. However, sensitive 
sectors such as gold, articles of jewellery and other precious metals or stones 
have been excluded from the facility of job work.

Page 1 of 5

questions.question: A manufacturing company exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) without payment of IGST. During the same tax period, the company also supplies goods domestically and claims Input Tax Credit (ITC) on common inputs. However, the export proceeds for one shipment are not realized within the prescribed time limit under FEMA, and the Customs department has raised a query on the classification of exported goods under a different HSN heading than declared. Simultaneously, the recipient of the domestic supply has reversed ITC claiming the supplier's GSTR-1 has discrepancies with GSTR-3B. Analyze the cascading legal consequences across IGST Act, FEMA regulations, Customs Act, and GST ITC provisions, and advise on the priority order of rectifications to minimize total tax exposure and penalty liability.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A private limited company engaged in SaaS services has received advance payments from foreign clients in convertible foreign exchange. The company claims these as 'export of services' exempt from GST under zero-rated supply provisions. The Income Tax department, during scrutiny assessment under Section 143(3), treats a portion of these receipts as 'fees for technical services' taxable in India under the India-USA DTAA Article 12, denying the beneficial treaty rate on grounds that the beneficial owner is a different entity. Concurrently, the GST department initiates proceedings arguing that since the income is characterized as FTS for direct tax purposes, the place of supply rules shift and IGST becomes payable. Critically evaluate whether the characterization under direct tax law is binding on GST authorities, identify the applicable safe harbor provisions under both regimes, and construct a defense strategy that reconciles conflicting departmental positions while preserving maximum tax efficiency.

title: The Central Excise (No. 2) (FirstAmendment) Rules, 2001, amending Rules 15 and 16.
text:
28 th June, 2001

Notification No. 51/2001-C.E. (N.T.)

In exercise of the powers conferred by section 37 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944), the Central Government hereby
makes the following further rules to amend the Central Excise (No. 2) Rules, 2001 (hereinafter referred to as the said rules),
namely :-

1.        (1) These rules may be called the Central Excise (No. 2) (First Amendment) Rules, 2001.

(2) They shall come into force on the 1st day of July, 2001.

2.        In rule 15 of the said rules, for sub-rule (1), the following shall be substituted, namely :-

The Central Government may, by notification, specify the

"(1) goods in respect of which an assessee shall have the option to pay the duty of excise on the basis of such factors as may
be relevant to production of such goods and at such rate as may be notified for this purpose, subject to such limitations and
conditions, including those relating to interest or penalty, as may be specified in such notification."

3.        In rule 16 of the said rules, for sub-rule (2), the following shall be substituted, namely : -

If the process to which the goods are subjected before

"(2) returning does not amount to manufacture, the manufacturer shall pay an amount equal to the CENVAT credit taken under
sub-rule (1) and in any other case the manufacturer shall pay duty on goods returned under sub-rule (1) at the rate applicable on
the date of removal and on the value determined under sub-section (2) of section 3 or section 4 or section 4A of the Act, as the
case may be."

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a registered dealer under a consignment arrangement where title transfers only upon sale by the dealer. The manufacturer also provides post-supply price adjustments (volume discounts) at the end of the financial year via credit notes. The dealer has partially availed ITC on the original invoices. How should the manufacturer determine the time of supply, calculate the GST liability adjustment on the credit notes under Section 34, and what is the dealer's obligation to reverse ITC proportionately, particularly where the credit note spans multiple tax periods and the original supply straddles a rate change notification?

An Indian entity imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and subsequently leases those goods to a foreign entity for use outside India, while retaining title. The foreign lessee sub-leases the equipment to a third country operator. The Indian entity claims the lease rentals are zero-rated exports of services. Analyse whether the transaction qualifies as 'export of services' under Section 2(6) of the IGST Act considering the 'place of supply' rules for goods temporarily sent abroad, the EPCG export obligation implications, and whether the beneficial ownership test under Customs Circular No. 33/2019 affects re-importation duty relief when the goods are eventually returned.

title: EOUs/ EPZ Units- Policy issues to be referred to CBEC before issue of Show Cause Notice
text:
Circular No. 122/-95-Cus.

dated 28/11/95

F.No. 305/113/94-FTT

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject: Issue of show cause Notice EOUs / EPZs in case involving scope of notification etc.

I am directed to say that certain instances have been brought to the notice of the Board, wherein show cause notices have
been brought to the notice of the Board, wherein show cause notices have been issued to units working under the EOU/ EPZ
scheme on matters involving interpretations of provisions and scope of exemption notification, eligibility of duty free imports
etc. without referring the issue to the Board for examination and clarification. This has led to complaints of harassment of
exporters and adverse publicity.

Board desires that in view of circular No. 21/95-Cus. (F.No. 307/2/91-FTT) dated 10.03.1995, prompt action should be taken
for issue of SCNs against units indulging in gross violations of law like illicit removal/ non-accountal/ disappearance of goods
and the like. On issues like the not-fulfilment of export obligations action has to be initiated in consultation with the Development
Commissioners or the commerce Ministry. But in matters involving interpretations of statutory provisions or the policy
provisions or the scope of notifications, the issues may be referred to the Board demi officially, (giving the details properly and
enclosing copies of documents on which doubts have arisen) for detailed examination in the Board's Officer. In such cases,
show cause notices should be issued only after the issues are examined and the legal position settled. This would avoid
situations wherein the allegations in the notices might be found unsustainable in terms of EXIM Policy/ customs law and may
have to be dropped by the department itself.

Sd/-

(Ranjana Jha)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

questions.question: A holding company incorporated in India receives management consulting services from its wholly-owned subsidiary registered in the UAE. The subsidiary charges INR 50 lakhs annually. The holding company also provides interest-free loans to the subsidiary. Under GST law, analyze: (1) whether the transaction qualifies as an 'import of services' and if the related-party nature triggers valuation under Rule 28 of CGST Rules, (2) whether the interest-free loan creates a deemed supply under Schedule I and how it should be valued under Rule 28 read with Rule 30/31, and (3) whether the holding company can claim full ITC on GST paid under reverse charge, given that its own outward supplies include both taxable and exempt financial services.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law - Customs & GST Interface
questions.question: An EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods duty-free under Customs notification 52/2003. Mid-way through the 5-year export obligation period, the unit converts to a DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) unit after fulfilling 60% of its export obligation. Analyze: (1) the customs duty demand calculation including interest under Section 61 of the Customs Act on the proportionate unfulfilled obligation, (2) whether IGST paid on the original import (if any) is recoverable by the department or adjustable against ITC already availed, (3) the GST treatment of capital goods transferred to DTA status — whether this triggers a supply under Schedule II and how depreciated value under Rule 44A of CGST Rules applies for ITC reversal, and (4) whether the unit can challenge the duty demand by arguing that the conversion itself constitutes 'export of services' via its DTA sales to SEZ units.

title: Seeks to make amendments (Twelfth Amendment, 2018) to the CGST Rules, 2017. This notification amends rule 96(10) to allow exporters who have received capital goods under the EPCG scheme to claim refund of the IGST paid on exports and align

questions.question: A manufacturing company has two units — Unit A in a SEZ and Unit B in a DTA. Unit B procures raw materials domestically and supplies finished goods to Unit A under a deemed export arrangement. Unit A then exports the goods. Simultaneously, Unit B also makes inter-state taxable supplies to third-party buyers. How should Unit B compute its ITC reversal under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of CGST Rules, 2017, given that deemed exports to SEZ units are zero-rated but not 'exempt supplies' under Section 2(47)? What proportion of common ITC is recoverable, and does the LUT filed by Unit A affect Unit B's ITC entitlement?

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a foreign MNC provides IT-enabled services to its parent (ForeignCo) under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using the TNMM. The AO rejects TNMM and applies the CUP method using internal comparables — a single third-party contract IndCo holds with an unrelated client. IndCo argues the third-party contract involves substantially different functional risk (IndCo bears credit risk, owns developed IP fragments, operates under different SLA penalties). Under Section 92C read with Rule 10B of the Income Tax Rules, 1962, what is the evidentiary burden IndCo must discharge to invalidate CUP comparability, and if CUP is upheld, how should the adjustment be computed when the third-party contract price includes embedded risk premiums that are absent from the ALP transaction?

title: This notification adds further areas to the ones mentioned in notification No. 32/99.

questions.question: A manufacturing company operating as an EOU (Export Oriented Unit) procures raw materials domestically under CT-3 certificates (nil duty), but also imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme with a specific export obligation tied to a 6-year timeline. During an audit, the department alleges that the company's DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) sales in Year 3, which exceeded 50% of NFE (Net Foreign Exchange) earnings for that year, trigger full customs duty recovery on the imported capital goods proportionate to DTA sales, irrespective of the overall EPCG obligation being on track. The company argues that EPCG obligation is measured cumulatively over 6 years, not annually. Analyze the interplay between EOU DTA sale restrictions under the FTP 2023, EPCG scheme conditions under Customs Notification 16/2015-Cus, and whether an annual NFE shortfall in isolation triggers duty recovery under Section 28 of the Customs Act, 1962, citing the jurisdictional precedents from CESTAT Mumbai and Chennai benches.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + GST Intersection
questions.question: A tech-enabled NBFC disburses loans via co-lending arrangements with a scheduled commercial bank under RBI's Co-Lending Model (CLM) guidelines. The NBFC retains 20% of the loan on its books while the bank holds 80%. The NBFC charges a 'processing facilitation fee' to the bank computed as a spread over the bank's cost of funds. The GST department contends this fee is taxable as an intermediary service (18% GST) under Schedule II of the CGST Act, while the NBFC argues it qualifies as a financial service exempt under Entry 27 of Notification 12/2017-CT(R). Simultaneously, for Income Tax purposes, the AO proposes to disallow the NBFC's interest expense deduction under Section 57(iii) read with Section 56(2)(viia) by arguing the co-lending income is 'income from other sources' rather than business income, which would also affect the NBFC's SARFAESI Act rights on the 20% retained portfolio. Provide a structured legal analysis covering: (i) GST classification and the intermediary vs. principal debate post Circular 159/15/2021; (ii) Income Tax characterization and the impact on deductibility; and (iii) whether the dual adverse treatment creates a constitutional challenge under Article 14 for discriminatory taxation of co-lending NBFCs versus direct lenders.

1. For rules 57A to 57U of the said rules, the following rules shall be substituted with effect from the 1 st day of April, 2000,

namely:-

"AA. CREDIT OF DUTY PAID ON EXCISABLE GOODS USED AS INPUTS OR CAPITAL GOODS

57A. Definitions.- For the purpose of this section,-

1. "capital goods" means-
1. All goods falling under Chapter 82, 84, or 90 of the First Schedule to the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985 and

used in the factory of manufacturer of the final products weather directly for the manufacture of the final
products or for research and develop0ment, quality control, testing, weighment or similar allied functions
related to the production of final products, except office equipment; and

2. Spare part, components and accessories of the goods mentioned at (I) above, whether falling under the

same Chapter or any other Chapter of the First Schedule to the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985;

1. "exempted goods" includes goods which are chargeable to "Nil rate of duty"
2. "final product" means finished excisable goods manufactured or produced by an assessee;
3. "input" means all goods used in or in relation to the manufacture of final products whether directly or indirectly

and whether contained in the final product or not, and whether used as packing material, or as fuel, or for
generation of electricity or steam, except high speed diesel oil and motor spirit, commonly known as petrol.

Explanation.- The high speed diesel oil or motor spirit commonly known as petrol will not be deemed to be input for any
purpose whatsoever.

57B. CENVAT credit . - A manufacturer or producer of finished excisable goods shall be allowed to take credit of duty of
excise (hereinafter referred to as the CENVAT credit) of,-

1. the duty of excise specified in the First Schedule to the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985, leviable under the

Central Excise Act, 1944;

2. the duty of excise specified in the Second Schedule to the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985, leviable under the

Central Excise Act, 1944;

3. the additional duty under section 3 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, equivalent to the duty of excise specified

under clauses (a) and (b) above,

paid on any inputs or capital goods received and used in the factory. The CENVAT credit may be utilized for payment of
duty of excise levied under the First Schedule on any final products manufactured by the manufacturer.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods under two distinct GST registrations in the same state — one for its main factory (GSTIN-A) and one for its newly commissioned captive power plant (GSTIN-B). The power plant generates electricity solely for consumption by the factory. During an audit, the officer contends that the inter-unit electricity transfer constitutes a 'supply' under Schedule I of the CGST Act (permanent transfer between distinct persons) and raises a demand for GST at 14% IGST since Schedule II entry 5(b) treats electricity generation as a service. The manufacturer argues electricity is exempt under Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) and that Schedule I applies only to goods/services with commercial intent. Analyze: (a) whether the captive electricity transfer between two GSTINs of the same legal entity constitutes a taxable supply under Schedule I, (b) the interplay between the 'distinct person' fiction and the exemption notification, and (c) the evidentiary burden and documentary safeguards the manufacturer must maintain to withstand this demand at the Appellate Authority and GST Tribunal levels.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a US MNC provides software development services to its parent under a Cost Plus 15% arrangement benchmarked using the TNMM. The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) recharacterizes the arrangement as a 'development center' rather than a 'service provider', invokes the Profit Split Method (PSM) instead, and attributes 40% of the consolidated group profit to the Indian entity — resulting in a Rs. 380 crore upward adjustment. The TPO's reasoning hinges on three findings: (i) the Indian entity holds de facto ownership of source code via version control commits, (ii) it bears economic risk through SLA penalty clauses, and (iii) it performs DEMPE functions for the underlying IP. The assessee counters that legal title, exploitation risk, and strategic decision-making remain with the US parent. Analyze: (a) the doctrinal threshold under the OECD BEPS Action 8-10 framework and Indian TP rules (Rule 10B) for when functional contribution crosses into 'economic ownership' of IP, (b) whether SLA penalties alone constitute sufficient 'risk assumption' to trigger PSM applicability, (c) the procedural rights of the assessee under Section 92CA and the DRP mechanism to challenge TPO's method selection, and (d) what MAP relief under Article 25 of the India-US DTAA is realistically achievable and within what timeline.

title: Master Circular on Show Cause Notice, Adjudication and Recovery
text:
of words “contravention of any of the provisions of this Act or Rules” are again qualified by

the immediately following words “with intent to evade payment of duty”. It is, therefore, not

correct to say that there can be a suppression or mis-statement of fact, which is not wilful and

yet constitutes a permissible ground for the purpose of the proviso to Section 11A. Mis-

statement or suppression of fact must be wilful.

3.4 Extended period in disputed areas of interpretation: There are cases where either no

duty was being levied or there was a short levy on any excisable goods on the belief that they

were not excisable or were chargeable to lower rate of duty, as the case may be. Both trade

and field formations of revenue may have operated under such understanding. Thus, the

general practice of assessment can be said to be non-payment of duty or payment at lower

rate, as the case may be. In such situations, Board may issue circular clarifying that the

general practice of assessment was erroneous and instructing field formations to correct the

practice of assessment. Consequent upon such circular, issue of demand notice for extended

period of time would be incorrect as it cannot be said that the assessee was intentionally not

paying the duty.

3.5 On the other hand, there can be Board circulars which only reiterate the correct practice

of assessment which is being followed by the compliant segment of the assessees.  In such

situations, decision to invoke extended period would depend on examination of facts of a

case and where the ingredient to invoke extended period is present, show cause notice for

extended period can be issued. In such situations it would be unfair to the compliant segment

of the assessees to not invoke the extended period of time, if active ingredients are present to

invoke extended period.

3.6 Power to invoke extended period is conditional: Power to issue notice for extended

period is restricted by presence of active ingredients which indicate an intent to evade duty as

explained above. Indiscriminate use of such restricted powers leads to fruitless adjudications,

appeals and reviews, inflates the figures of outstanding demands and above all causes

unnecessary harassment of the assessees. Therefore, before invoking extended period, it must

be ensured that the necessary and sufficient conditions to invoke extended period exists.

3.7 Second SCN invoking extended period: Issuance of a second SCN invoking extended

period after the first SCN invoking extended period of time has been issued is legally not

tenable. However, the second SCN, if issued would also need to establish the ingredients

required to invoke extended period independently. For example, in cases where clearances

are not reported by the assessee in the periodic return, second SCN invoking extended period

is quite logical whereas in cases of wilful mis-statement regarding the clearances made under

7

questions.question: A multinational company has a liaison office in India that facilitates communication between its foreign parent and Indian customers but does not directly conclude contracts or earn income. The Indian customers independently contract with the foreign parent and remit payments abroad. The CBIC has raised a show-cause notice alleging that the liaison office constitutes a 'fixed establishment' under IGST Act Section 2(50) read with Place of Supply rules, and that the services provided by the foreign parent to Indian recipients are 'import of services' attracting RCM liability on the Indian recipients. The company argues the liaison office lacks authority to conclude contracts and therefore cannot be a fixed establishment. Analyze the legal merits of both positions citing relevant CGST/IGST provisions, CBIC circulars, and AAR/AAAR precedents, and advise on the structuring of the liaison office's activities to minimize GST exposure without triggering permanent establishment risk under DTAA.

An Indian importer is purchasing specialty chemical intermediates from a related foreign supplier under a long-term agreement that includes a price adjustment clause: the declared transaction value at import is provisional, subject to retrospective revision based on the foreign supplier's actual cost of production audited at year-end. The customs authorities have rejected the transaction value under Customs Valuation Rules 2007, Rule 3(3)(a), alleging relationship-influence, and have proposed to re-determine value under Rule 5 (Transaction Value of Identical Goods). The importer contends that the year-end adjustment mechanism is a legitimate transfer-pricing true-up accepted by the Income Tax authorities under ALP documentation, and that customs cannot independently re-determine value when TP compliance is already demonstrated. Critically evaluate whether the customs valuation framework and the TP framework operate independently or must be harmonized in India, reference the Supreme Court's position on 'relationship influence' burden of proof, CBIC Circular 39/2016-Customs, and advise the importer on the evidentiary record required to defend transaction value while managing the retroactive adjustment disclosure obligation.

title: Cosmetics Rules, 2020
text:

accordance with the various rules, and regulations of the Government of India and include the following

(i) safety data sheets must be made available to staff before testing is carried out;

(iii) staff must wear laboratory coats or other protective clothing including gloves and face masks and eye 
protection wherever required;

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates under a composite supply arrangement where the principal supply is the manufacture of customized machinery (HSN 8428) and ancillary supplies include installation, commissioning, operator training, and a 5-year AMC. The buyer is an SEZ unit. The contract is a single lump-sum price. Analyze: (a) how to determine the principal supply under Section 8 of CGST Act when the AMC revenue over 5 years exceeds the machinery value, (b) whether the SEZ zero-rating under Section 16 of IGST Act applies to the entire composite supply or only the principal supply, (c) the ITC reversal implications for the supplier if the SEZ unit subsequently de-notifies, and (d) the documentary requirements under Rule 46 of CGST Rules for the tax invoice given the multi-element nature of the supply.

A related-party importer purchases pharmaceutical intermediates from its foreign parent. The declared transaction value is accepted by the importer as arm's length based on a Transfer Pricing study under the Income Tax Act. However, the customs officer invokes Rule 4(2) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, arguing that the relationship influenced the price, and proposes to reject the transaction value in favour of computed value under Rule 8. The importer argues: (i) the TP study constitutes sufficient proof under Rule 4(3)(b), (ii) the CBIC Circular on TP-Customs linkage bars independent re-determination, and (iii) the computed value method requires access to cost-of-production data held abroad. Critically evaluate the legal merits of each argument with reference to relevant case law, the WTO Customs Valuation Agreement hierarchy, and the practical burden-of-proof standards applicable at the adjudication stage.

title: This notification specifies the conditions, safeguards and procedures for Export under bond to Nepal or Bhutan.
text:
Petrol Pump located in Nepal for the bonafide use of officers and staff of the Embassy in Nepal shall be subject to the following
conditions, namely: - 
the exporter shall furnish a bond in Form as specified in Annexure-I before the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or the
Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise or such other officer as authorised by the Board on this behalf; and 
the First Secretary (Economic), Embassy of India, Nepal, certifies the signature and stamp or seal of the person authorised to
place the order for supply of excisable goods to the specified Government of India Aided Projects in Nepal; 
Export of all excisable goods without payment of duty to Kurichu Hydro Electric Project and Tala Hydro Electric
Project in Bhutan. -. Export of all excisable goods without payment of duty to Kurichu Hydro Electric Project and Tala Hydro
Electric Project in Bhutan shall be subject to the following conditions, namely: - 
the exporter shall furnish a bond in Form as specified in Annexure-I before the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or the
Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise or such other officer as authorised by the Board on this behalf; 
the goods are supplied against one or more specified contract which have been registered with the Directorate General of
Inspection, Customs and Central Excise in the manner specified in Annexure III; 
the goods are covered by a release order issued by an officer authorised by the General Manager of the concerned project
authority; 
the exporter furnishes a bond in the Form specified in Annexure-I to the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or the
Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise having jurisdiction over the factory or warehouse or the approved premises or from
where the goods are removed for export to the specified project or such other officer as authorised by the Board on this behalf. 
2. Procedure: - 
(1) Procedure at the place of despatch. - (i) Six copies of invoice in the Form specified under Annexure-IV shall be
presented to the Superintendent or Inspector of Central Excise having jurisdiction over the factory, warehouse or any other
approved premises along with the export goods; 
(ii) in case of export for supplies to Government of India Aided Projects in Nepal and the Embassy Cooperative Store and
Embassy Petrol Pump located in Nepal for the bonafide use of officers and staff of the Embassy in Nepal, the order from
Project Implementation Authority shall also be presented; 
(iii) the Superintendent or Inspector of Central Excise having jurisdiction over the factory, warehouse or any other approved
premises shall verify the identity of goods with reference to description mentioned in the invoice and the particulars of the duty
payable but for export, and if found in order, he shall seal the consignment, tank or container with Central Excise seal or in such
other the manner as may be specified by the Commissioner of Central Excise and endorse each copy of the export invoice in
token of having such verification and examination done by him; 
(iv) the goods shall be delivered to the exporter or his agent, together with the original copy of the invoice, duly completed and
registered. The said officer will also give duplicate, triplicate and quadruplicate copies of invoice in a sealed cover, to the
exporter or his agent for delivery to the Customs officer in-charge of the Land Customs Station through which the goods are
intended to be exported and will obtain acknowledgement to this effect . 
(iii) the exporter or his agent shall then be free to remove the goods for export to Nepal through the Land Customs Station
indicated on the respective invoices; 
(iv) where the goods are exported by land, the export shall take place through any of the following land customs stations,
namely, Sukhiapokhri, Panitanki, Jogbani, Jayanagar, Bairgania, Bhimnagar, Bitamore (Sursand), Raxaul, Sonauli, Barhni,
Nepalganj Road, Shohratgar (Khunwa), Jarwa, Katarniaghat, Gauriphanta, Banbasa, Jhulaghat, Dharchula, Naxalbari,
Galgalia, Kunauli, Sonabarsa, Tikonia, or such other check-post as may be specified by the Board; 
(v) the quintuplicate copy shall be forwarded to the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or the Deputy Commissioner of
Central Excise who has accepted the bond; and 
(vi) the said Superintendent or Inspector of Central Excise shall retain the sixtuplicate copy of the invoice.

questions.question: A manufacturing company imports capital goods under the EPCG scheme and simultaneously claims Input Tax Credit (ITC) on IGST paid at customs. The goods are partially used in zero-rated exports and partially in domestic taxable supplies. The company later defaults on the export obligation under EPCG. Analyze the ITC reversal obligations under Rule 42/43 of CGST Rules, the customs duty redemption fine implications, and whether the IGST ITC already utilized against output liability can be recovered under Section 73 or Section 74 of CGST Act — distinguishing fraud vs. non-fraud scenarios and applicable interest under Section 50.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian subsidiary of a foreign MNC provides back-office IT services to its parent under a cost-plus arrangement benchmarked using TNMM. The AO proposes a TP adjustment arguing the comparable set is inappropriate because the tested party has significant intangibles (proprietary process IP). Simultaneously, the AO invokes Section 9(1)(vi) to treat the service fees as royalty, subjecting them to withholding tax under Section 195. The taxpayer argues DTAA Article 7 (business profits) should prevail. Evaluate: (a) the priority of DTAA provisions over domestic law under Section 90(2), (b) the conditions under which process know-how constitutes 'royalty' per Explanation 2 to Section 9(1)(vi) post-2012 amendment, and (c) whether a TP adjustment and a royalty recharacterization can coexist on the same transaction without resulting in double taxation, citing relevant ITAT and High Court precedents.

title: Clarification regarding extension of time limit to apply for revocation of cancellation of registration in view of Notification No. 34/2021-Central Tax dated 29th August, 2021

Vide Circular No. 148/04/2021-GST, dated 18th May, 2021, detailed guidelines for

falling between 15th April, 2021 to 29th June, 2021. Government has now issued notification No.

34/2021-Central Tax dated 29th August, 2021 (hereinafter referred to as "the said notification")

questions.question: A manufacturing company operating under the composition scheme for the first three quarters of FY 2024-25 opts out mid-year and switches to the regular GST scheme in Q4. During the composition period, it had procured capital goods worth ₹50 lakhs on which ITC was blocked. Upon switching, it also discovers that certain inter-state supplies were made during Q2 — which are prohibited under composition. Advise on: (a) the ITC reversal obligations and whether any portion can be reclaimed post-switchover; (b) the penalty and interest exposure for the inter-state supplies made under composition; and (c) the correct treatment of the annual return filing, particularly whether GSTR-4 and GSTR-9 must both be filed for the same FY, and how to reconcile the two.

An Indian importer purchases goods from its wholly-owned foreign subsidiary at a declared transaction value. Customs authorities reject the transaction value invoking Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007, alleging relationship-influenced pricing, and propose to apply computed value under Rule 8. The importer holds a transfer pricing study under the Income Tax Act confirming arm's length price, but the TP study uses the TNMM method rather than CUP. The importer also has contemporaneous third-party invoices for identical goods sold by the subsidiary to an unrelated German buyer at a 12% higher price. Analyze: (a) whether the TP study under IT Act binds Customs authorities or merely constitutes persuasive evidence; (b) the legal threshold for 'relationship influence' under proviso to Section 14 of the Customs Act read with Rule 3(3); (c) the hierarchy of valuation methods and whether Customs can skip to Rule 8 without exhausting Rules 5–7; and (d) how the third-party German invoices should be presented as a CUP benchmark and what documentary requirements apply.

questions.question: A holding company in India provides corporate guarantee to its wholly-owned subsidiary for a term loan from a scheduled commercial bank. The subsidiary does not pay any consideration for this guarantee. The tax officer contends this is a 'supply of service' under Schedule I of CGST Act 2017 (related party transactions deemed as supply even without consideration) and raises demand for GST at 18% on the guarantee amount. The holding company argues the transaction is covered under Entry 5 of Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) as a service by holding company to subsidiary, hence exempt. Analyze the competing legal positions citing relevant CBIC circulars, AAR rulings, and the Supreme Court's position on related-party deemed supply. What is the correct GST treatment and what compliance should the taxpayer adopt to mitigate litigation risk?

An Indian EOU (Export Oriented Unit) imports capital goods under EPCG authorization at nil duty with an export obligation of 6x the duty saved over 6 years. Mid-way through the obligation period, the EOU converts to a DTA (Domestic Tariff Area) unit. At conversion, 40% of the export obligation remains unfulfilled. Simultaneously, the unit had availed IGST exemption on domestic procurement under EOU scheme. Analyze: (1) the customs duty liability crystallization mechanism on unfulfilled EPCG obligation post-DTA conversion including interest computation under Section 28AA of Customs Act; (2) whether the IGST input tax credit reversal under Rule 44A of CGST Rules is applicable and how it interacts with the customs demand; (3) whether the unit can apply for composition settlement under the EPCG Amnesty Scheme 2023 and the procedural prerequisites under the Foreign Trade Policy 2023 read with HBP paras 5.21–5.22. Provide a step-by-step compliance roadmap.

title: Seeks to make amendments (Thirteenth Amendment, 2018) to the CGST Rules, 2017.
text:
(d) resorting to any unfair means or practices in connection with the examination or

in connection with the result of the examination;

(e) found in possession of any paper, book, note or any other material, the use of

which is not permitted in the examination center;

(f) communicating with others or exchanging calculators, chits, papers etc. (on

which something is written);

(g) misbehaving in the examination center in any manner; 
(h) tampering with the hardware and/or software deployed; and   
(i) attempting to commit or, as the case may be, to abet in the commission of all or

any of the acts specified in the foregoing clauses.

(10)  Disqualification of person using unfair means or practice.- If any person is or 
has been found to be indulging in use of unfair means or practices, NACIN may, after 
considering his representation, if any, declare him disqualified for the examination.

(11) Declaration of result.- NACIN shall declare the results within one month of the 
conduct of examination on the official websites of the Board, NACIN,  GST Council 
Secretariat, common portal and State Tax Department of the respective States or 
Union territories, if any. The results shall also be communicated to the applicants by 
e-mail and/or by post.

(12) Handling representations.- A person not satisfied with his result may represent 
in writing, clearly specifying the reasons therein to NACIN or the jurisdictional 
Commissioner as per the procedure established by NACIN on the official websites of 
the Board, NACIN and common portal.

(13) Power to relax.- Where the Board or State Tax Commissioner is of the opinion 
that it is necessary or expedient to do so, it may, on the recommendations of the 
Council, relax any of the provisions of this rule with respect to any class or category 
of persons.

Explanation :-  For the purposes of this sub-rule, the expressions –

(a) “jurisdictional Commissioner” means the Commissioner having jurisdiction over

the place declared as address in the application for enrolment as the GST 
Practitioner in FORM GST PCT-1. It shall refer to the Commissioner of Central 
Tax if the enrolling authority in FORM GST PCT-1 has been selected as Centre, 
or the Commissioner of State Tax if the enrolling authority in FORM GST PCT-
1 has been selected as State;

(b) NACIN means as notified by notification No. 24/2018-Central Tax, dated

28.05.2018.

Annexure-A

[See sub-rule 7]

Pattern and Syllabus of the Examination

questions.question: A manufacturer exports goods under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) without payment of IGST, but the foreign buyer later rejects 30% of the shipment and ships it back. The exporter re-imports the rejected goods, pays applicable customs duty, and later re-exports the rectified goods within 6 months. How does this affect the original zero-rated supply status, the accumulated ITC claim already filed under Rule 96A, and what are the exporter's obligations under Rule 96(10) given that the re-imported goods were processed using both domestic inputs (on which ITC was availed) and imported inputs (on which SAD was paid)? Cite the relevant CGST Rules, Circular clarifications, and any applicable Advance Ruling precedents.

An Indian resident HUF holds a 45% stake in an unlisted Mauritius-incorporated company (Treaty-protected) which in turn holds commercial real estate in India through an Indian subsidiary. The Indian subsidiary declares an interim dividend in February 2026, the Mauritius company retains it, and the HUF's Karta then gifts his individual 15% direct stake in the Mauritius company to his adult son (resident) in March 2026. Analyze: (1) applicability of DTAA Article 13 vs. domestic Section 9(1)(i) on any deemed transfer; (2) whether the retained dividend in Mauritius triggers CFC-like attribution under the POEM rules or Section 9(1)(iv); (3) gift tax implications under Section 56(2)(x) given the absence of a quoted price for the unlisted foreign shares; and (4) whether the son's subsequent sale of those shares within the same FY creates a short-term capital gain taxable in India, with full citation to relevant Finance Act amendments, CBDT Circulars, and applicable AAR/ITAT rulings.

title: Clarification regarding GST rates & classification (goods)
text:
13. 
Applicability of GST on supply of cranes, rigs, tools & Spares and other

machinery when moved from one state to another by a person on his account for

there use for supply of service

13.1 As per Circular No. 21/21/2017-GST dated 22.11.2017, it was clarified that no

IGST would be applicable on such interstate movements of rigs, tools & spares and

all goods on wheels. Doubts have been raised regarding applicability of GST on

inter-state movement of   machinery like tower cranes, rigs, batching plants,

concrete pumps and mixers which are not mounted on wheels, but require regular

means of conveyance (used by companies in Infrastructure business).

13.2       Any inter-state movement of goods for provision of service on own

account by a service provider,  where no transfer of title in such goods or transfer

of goods to the distinct person by way of stock transfer is not involved, does not

constitute a supply of such goods. Hence, it is clarified that any such movement

on own account (not involving distinct person in terms of section 25), where such

movement is not intended for further supply of such goods does not constitute a

supply and would not be liable to GST.

14. 
Difficulty, if any, may be brought to the notice of the Board immediately.

Hindi version shall follow.

Yours faithfully,

(Mahipal Singh)

Technical Officer (TRU-1)

mahipal.singh1980@gov.in

questions.question: A multinational company provides cloud-based SaaS services to Indian clients from servers located outside India. The Indian subsidiary acts as a 'commissionnaire' — it solicits orders and provides local support but the contract is signed between the foreign parent and the Indian client. GST is paid by the foreign parent under OIDAR provisions. The Indian subsidiary receives a 'support fee' from the parent. Revenue authorities are contending that (a) the subsidiary constitutes a 'fixed establishment' of the foreign parent in India, making the supply a domestic B2B transaction rather than import of services, and (b) the support fee arrangement is a colourable device to suppress the true consideration for the Indian supply. Analyse the legal position under IGST Act 2017 read with Place of Supply rules, address the fixed establishment doctrine as applied in Indian GST jurisprudence, and evaluate the double-taxation risk if both contentions succeed simultaneously.

questions.domain: Indian Tax Law / Income Tax + Transfer Pricing
questions.question: An Indian company ('ICo') holds IP rights developed in India and licenses them to its Singapore subsidiary ('SCo') at an arm's length royalty. SCo sub-licenses to group entities worldwide and retains the residual profit. During a TP audit, the Indian TPO proposes: (i) recharacterisation of the transaction from 'license' to 'transfer of IP' on the ground that ICo has transferred all 'significant DEMPE functions' to SCo, triggering capital gains under Section 9(1)(vi) read with Section 45; (ii) alternatively, enhancement of royalty to capture SCo's 'location savings' and 'market premium' as comparability adjustments under Rule 10B. Simultaneously, SCo claims treaty protection under Article 12 of the India-Singapore DTAA (pre-2017 version with beneficial royalty rate). Evaluate the interplay between OECD BEPS Action 8-10 DEMPE analysis, Indian domestic TP rules, the treaty characterisation question, and whether the Principal Purpose Test under the Multilateral Instrument can be invoked to deny SCo's treaty benefit.

questions.question: A manufacturer supplies goods to a distributor under a principal-to-principal arrangement. The distributor later returns a portion of the goods citing quality defects, but the return occurs 14 months after the original supply invoice date. The manufacturer has already filed GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B for the original supply period. The distributor claims ITC reversal is not required since the return is treated as a fresh inward supply under a fresh invoice. Analyze the GST implications for both parties, citing the relevant sections of the CGST Act 2017, applicable circulars, and the time limit provisions under Section 34 for credit/debit notes.

An Indian subsidiary of a foreign parent company provides software development services exclusively to the parent under a cost-plus arrangement (cost + 15% markup). The Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) contends that the tested party should be the foreign parent, not the Indian subsidiary, and benchmarks using the Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) against foreign comparables, arriving at an arm's length markup of 22%. The assessee argues Indian comparables under TNMM yield 13–16% and the TPO has no jurisdiction to change the tested party. Evaluate the legal and procedural validity of the TPO's action, the correct application of Rule 10B and Rule 10C of the Income Tax Rules, the precedential value of relevant ITAT and High Court rulings on tested party selection, and the assessee's available remedies including APA and MAP.

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra operates under the composition scheme and supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka through an e-commerce operator. The e-commerce operator collects TCS under Section 52. Midway through the financial year, the manufacturer's aggregate turnover crosses ₹1.5 crore, making them ineligible for the composition scheme. Analyze: (a) the exact date from which the manufacturer must file GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B, (b) how the TCS already collected by the e-commerce operator gets reconciled against the new return filing obligations, (c) whether the inter-state supplies made during the composition period are subject to IGST retrospectively, and (d) the penalty exposure under Section 122 if the transition is not handled correctly.

An Indian importer purchases machinery from a related party in Germany at a declared transaction value of USD 500,000. The customs officer invokes Rule 4 of the Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules, 2007, suspecting undervaluation due to the relationship. The importer challenges this, presenting contemporaneous import data for identical goods under Rule 5 from a third-party buyer at USD 480,000. The officer then proposes sequential application through Rules 6, 7, and 8. Evaluate: (a) the burden of proof standard under Rule 3(3)(a) and whether the importer's Rule 5 data satisfies it, (b) the legal validity of bypassing Rule 5 in favor of Rule 6 when identical goods data exists, (c) how the deductive value method under Rule 7 would be computed if the goods are sold domestically after further processing, and (d) the procedural rights of the importer under Section 124 of the Customs Act before a final order is passed.

title: Seeks to continue levy of anti-dumping duty on Meta Phenylene Diamine imported from Chian PR for 5 years pursuant to Sunset Review Final Findings issued by DGTR.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-
SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

NOTIFICATION No. 01/2024-Customs (ADD)

New Delhi, the 15th January, 2024

G.S.R…(E).- Whereas, the designated authority, vide notification number 7/06/2023-DGTR, dated the

30th June, 2023, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 30th June, 2023, had 
initiated the review in terms of sub-section (5) of section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) 
(hereinafter referred to as the Customs Tariff Act), and read with rule 23 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, 
Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 
1995, in the matter of continuation of anti-dumping duty on imports of “Meta Phenylene Diamine” (hereinafter 
referred to as the subject goods) falling under tariff item(s) 2921 51 20 or 2921 51 90 of the First Schedule to 
the Customs Tariff Act, originating in or exported from China PR (hereinafter referred to as the subject country) 
imposed vide notification of the Government of India, Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), number 
5/2019-Customs (ADD), dated the 24th January, 2019, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, 
Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 46(E), dated the 24th January, 2019;

And whereas, in the matter of review of anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject goods, originating

in or exported from the subject country, the designated authority in its final findings, published vide notification 
number 7/06/2023-DGTR, dated the 23rd October, 2023, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part-I, 
Section 1, dated the 23rd October, 2023, has come to the conclusion that-

(i) 
the imports from the subject country have remained substantial in absolute terms as well as relative to 
the domestic industry’s production and in the Indian demand, even though the volume has declined 
slightly since the original investigation;

(ii) 
the production, sales, capacity utilization of the domestic industry is adversely impacted on account of 
dumped imports;

(iii) dumped imports from subject country are causing injury to the domestic industry; 
(iv) 
there is likelihood of not only continuation but also intensification of dumping and consequent injury to 
the Indian industry in the event of cessation of the existing anti-dumping duties at this stage,

and has recommended continued imposition of the anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject goods,

originating in or exported from the subject country, in order to remove injury to the domestic industry.

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-sections (1) and (5) of section 9A of the

Customs Tariff Act, 1975 read with rules 18, 20 and 23 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and 
Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995 and in 
supersession of the notification of the Government of India, Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), 
number 5/2019-Customs (ADD), dated the 24th January, 2019, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, 
Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 46(E), dated the 24th January, 2019, the Central 
Government, after considering the aforesaid final findings of the designated authority, hereby imposes on the 
subject goods, the description of which is specified in column (3) of the Table below, falling under tariff item(s) 
of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act as specified in the corresponding entry in column (2), originating 
in the country as specified in the corresponding entry in column (4), exported from the country as specified in 
the corresponding entry in column (5), produced by the producers as specified in the corresponding entry in 
column (6), an anti-dumping duty at the rate equal to the amount as indicated in the corresponding entry in 
column (7), in the currency as specified in the corresponding entry in column (8) and as per unit of measurement 
as specified in the corresponding entry in column (9) of the said Table, namely :-

questions.question: A manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to a registered dealer in Karnataka under a bill-to-ship-to arrangement where the billing party is in Gujarat. The goods are dispatched directly from the Maharashtra factory to the Karnataka consignee. During transit, the goods are inspected by a GST officer in Telangana who detains them citing a mismatch between the e-way bill consignee and the physical delivery address. The Karnataka dealer claims input tax credit on the IGST paid. Analyse: (i) the place of supply and applicable GST levy for each leg of the transaction; (ii) the validity of the e-way bill and the officer's detention authority under Section 129 read with Rule 138; (iii) the ITC eligibility of the Karnataka dealer given the bill-to-ship-to structure under Section 16(2) and Circular 235/29/2024-GST; and (iv) the recourse available to the consignor if the detained goods are perishable.
questions.expected_depth: Multi-jurisdictional analysis with statutory citations, circular reconciliation, and procedural remedy

questions.question: An Indian importer purchases software-embedded industrial IoT sensors from a related-party US subsidiary at a declared transaction value of USD 120 per unit. The embedded firmware is licensed separately under a shrink-wrap agreement at USD 18 per unit, paid to a third-party IP holding company in Ireland. The customs officer proposes to load the royalty/licence fee into the assessable value under Rule 10(1)(c) of the Customs Valuation Rules, 2007, arguing the licence is a 'condition of sale'. The importer counters that: (a) the payment flows to a third party not the seller; (b) the software constitutes 'data' exempt under the proviso to Section 14; and (c) related-party price was accepted by US customs under the identical transaction value method. Critically evaluate all three defences against the officer's position, cite relevant CBIC circulars and CESTAT precedents, and advise on the optimal valuation method if the transaction value is rejected.
questions.expected_depth: Defence-by-defence statutory rebuttal, precedent mapping, fallback valuation strategy

questions.question: A manufacturing company operates under a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) and also has a Domestic Tariff Area (DTA) unit. During FY 2024-25, the SEZ unit procures capital goods worth ₹50 crore under the zero-rated supply provision. Mid-year, the company decides to partially de-bond the SEZ unit and transfer 40% of those capital goods to the DTA unit. The ITC originally availed was ₹9 crore (18% GST). At the time of transfer, the capital goods have been used for 18 months. Calculate the exact ITC reversal liability, identify the applicable CGST/IGST provisions, determine whether interest under Section 50 applies from the original avail date or the transfer date, and explain how this interacts with the SEZ refund already claimed under Rule 89.

An Indian subsidiary (IndCo) of a US multinational provides software development services to its parent (USCo) under a cost-plus 15% arrangement. IndCo also licenses IP developed during the engagement back to USCo under a royalty agreement at 8% of net sales. The AO contends that the two transactions should be aggregated under the 'combined approach' and benchmarked as a single hybrid arrangement, rejecting the taxpayer's use of TNMM for services and CUT for royalties separately. IndCo has a profit-level indicator of 22% operating margin. Under what conditions does Rule 10B of the Income Tax Rules permit or prohibit transaction aggregation, what is the standard of proof required to rebut the AO's aggregation argument before the TPO, and how does the existence of a pre-existing APA for the services transaction (but not the royalty) affect the dispute resolution pathway under Sections 92CC and 144C?
